topics.title: Overview of CRT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT414 - COMPUTER GRAPHICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Overview of CRT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Cathode Ray Tube (CRT)** is a specialized vacuum tube that functions as the foundational display device in early and mid-generation computer graphics systems. It operates by precisely steering a focused beam of electrons — generated through a process known as **thermionic emission** — onto a phosphor-coated screen. The kinetic energy of the impinging electrons excites the phosphor dots, causing them to emit visible light, thereby rendering the graphical image point-by-point or line-by-line.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The CRT is the root hardware element upon which classical computer graphics rasterization and vector (random-scan) display architectures were originally engineered. A mastery of CRT fundamentals is a prerequisite for understanding modern display subsystems, frame buffers, and electron-beam lithography concepts.

In the KTU 2024 framework, the CRT overview introduces the student to the **physical layer** of graphics hardware before the course transitions into 2D/3D algorithms, transformations, and rendering pipelines.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **chalk duster flicking chalk dust onto a dark chalkboard**, but the duster is invisible (it shoots electrons in a vacuum), the chalkboard is coated with light-emitting powder (phosphor), and an invisible magnetic/electrostatic steering mechanism guides the dust cloud. The points where the "dust" lands momentarily glow, and by rapidly tracing a pattern (line by line, like a farmer plowing a field), the entire board appears to display a coherent picture.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Theater Spotlight:**
> Think of the CRT electron gun as a *theater spotlight operator* and the phosphor screen as the *stage floor*. The operator sweeps a bright beam across the stage extremely fast, and the floor retains a brief glow at every point visited. Your eye, due to **persistence of vision** (≈ **1/16 second** to **1/10 second** retention), blends all the rapid sweeps into a single stable image. This is precisely the trick CRT displays use.

## 1.3 Standard Physical & Engineering Constants

The following metrics are critical for KTU problems on CRT:

- **Electron charge:** $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$
- **Electron rest mass:** $m_e = 9.109 \times 10^{-31}\ \text{kg}$
- **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 3.0 \times 10^8\ \text{m/s}$
- **Standard refresh rate:** $\mathbf{60\ \text{Hz}}$ (North American) or **50 Hz** (European)
- **Typical accelerating voltage:** **15 kV to 25 kV**
- **Persistence of human vision:** **$\mathbf{1/16}$ to $\mathbf{1/10}$ of a second** (≈ **62.5 ms to 100 ms**)

> [!NOTE]
> These constants form the foundational numerical backdrop for any KTU numerical questions on beam deflection, persistence, and refresh calculations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Major Subsystems of a CRT

A CRT, though appearing as a single sealed glass bottle, is internally a sophisticated assembly of five tightly coupled subsystems. Understanding each is essential for KTU descriptive questions.

### 2.1.1 The Electron Gun (Cathode Assembly)
- Contains a **heated cathode** (typically an oxide-coated filament) that undergoes **thermionic emission**.
- A **control grid** (Wehnelt cylinder) regulates the intensity of the electron beam — and therefore the **brightness** of the resulting pixel.
- A **pre-accelerating anode** and an **accelerating anode** form a lensing electric field that pushes electrons toward the screen at high velocity.
- The electron beam current $I$ is typically in the range of microamperes ($\mu\text{A}$).

### 2.1.2 The Focusing System
- An electrostatic or electromagnetic lens converges the diverging electron stream into a tight, near-parallel beam.
- Without focusing, the beam would spread, producing a blurry image (similar to a flashlight without a reflector).

### 2.1.3 The Deflection System (The "Plotter")
- Comprises two pairs of **deflection plates** (or magnetic deflection coils):
  - **Horizontal deflection plates** (X-plates): steer the beam left ↔ right.
  - **Vertical deflection plates** (Y-plates): steer the beam up ↕ down.
- In magnetic deflection CRTs, **yoke coils** outside the neck perform the same function.

### 2.1.4 The Phosphor Screen
- The inner face of the screen is coated with phosphor compounds (e.g., **P22** for color, **P31** for green, **P4** for white).
- Each phosphor dot, when struck by an electron, emits light through **cathodoluminescence**.
- **Persistence** $\tau$ defines how long the glow lasts after electron impact (short, medium, or long persistence).

### 2.1.5 The Glass Envelope & Vacuum
- The funnel-shaped glass envelope is evacuated to roughly $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-9}$ torr to ensure unimpeded electron travel.
- A thin **aluminized layer** behind the phosphor reflects light outward and dissipates heat.

## 2.2 Working Principle — The "Why" and "How"

1. **Electron Generation:** The cathode is heated, and electrons boil off its surface (Edison/Richardson effect).
2. **Acceleration:** A high positive potential (anode voltage) accelerates these electrons down the tube's neck.
3. **Focusing:** Electrostatic lenses squeeze the beam to a fine point (≈ 0.2 mm diameter).
4. **Deflection:** Time-varying voltages on the X and Y plates steer the beam to a precise (x, y) location.
5. **Luminescence:** The electron impacts a phosphor dot, exciting its electrons. As they relax, photons are released.
6. **Refresh:** The beam retraces the entire image area $\mathbf{30}$ to $\mathbf{120}$ times per second to prevent flicker.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table is the **master reference** for all KTU 2024 ESE questions on CRT.

| **Formula / Parameter** | **Expression** | **Physical Meaning** | **Engineering Use** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Deflection Sensitivity (Electrostatic)** | $S = \dfrac{L \cdot D}{2 \cdot d \cdot V_a}$ | Deflection on screen per volt of plate voltage | Sizing deflection amplifiers |
| **Spot Velocity (Scan Speed)** | $v = \dfrac{\text{Horizontal pixels}}{t_{\text{horizontal retrace time}}}$ | Speed of beam across a scan line | Computing refresh rate |
| **Refresh Rate** | $f_r = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{frame}}} = \dfrac{1}{N_{\text{lines}} \times t_{\text{line}}}$ | Times screen is redrawn per second | Avoiding flicker (≥ 60 Hz) |
| **Frame Time** | $T_{\text{frame}} = \dfrac{1}{f_r}$ | Time for one complete image scan | Upper bound on visible motion |
| **Resolution** | $R = N_x \times N_y$ pixels | Total addressable points | Image sharpness |
| **Aspect Ratio** | $\text{AR} = \dfrac{\text{Width}}{\text{Height}}$ | Screen shape factor | $\mathbf{4:3}$ (SD), $\mathbf{16:9}$ (HD) |
| **Bandwidth (Raster)** | $\text{BW} \geq \dfrac{N_x \cdot N_y \cdot f_r}{2}$ | Min signal bandwidth for video | CRT driver design |
| **Electron KE after acceleration** | $E_k = e \cdot V_a$ | Energy gained by one electron | Luminance control |
| **Number of scan lines** | $N = \dfrac{\text{Frame time}}{\text{Line time}}$ | Vertical resolution | Standard: **525 (NTSC)**, **625 (PAL)** |
| **Horizontal retrace factor** | $\beta \approx 0.15$ to $0.18$ | Fraction of line time for flyback | Timing budget |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** CRT bandwidth is the single most expensive specification in legacy display design. A 1920 × 1080 @ 60 Hz display needs a video bandwidth of at least $1920 \times 1080 \times 60 \approx \mathbf{124\ \text{MHz}}$, which is why early graphics cards were measured by their "RAMDAC" speed (e.g., 220 MHz, 360 MHz).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Radar & Sonar displays:** Long-persistence CRTs render slow-fading echoes.
- **Oscilloscopes:** The original analog CRO uses electrostatic-deflection CRTs.
- **Television broadcasting:** NTSC, PAL, SECAM standards were built around CRT raster rates.
- **Air Traffic Control (ATC):** Random-scan CRTs provided flicker-free vector graphics.
- **Arcade gaming (1970s–1990s):** Vector games like *Asteroids* and *Tempest* used random-scan CRTs for sharp line art.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Electrostatic Deflection Sensitivity

**Problem Setup:** An electron enters a pair of parallel deflection plates of length $L$, separation $d$, with initial horizontal velocity $v_x$. The plates are at potential difference $V_d$, located a distance $D$ from the screen.

### Step 1 — Acceleration in the Electron Gun
The electron starts at rest at the cathode and is accelerated by potential $V_a$.

$$\begin{aligned}
e V_a &= \frac{1}{2} m_e v_x^2 \\
\Rightarrow v_x &= \sqrt{\dfrac{2 e V_a}{m_e}}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Time Inside the Deflection Plates
The electron traverses length $L$ at constant horizontal velocity $v_x$.

$$t_{\text{in}} = \frac{L}{v_x} = L \sqrt{\dfrac{m_e}{2 e V_a}}$$

### Step 3 — Vertical Acceleration Due to Plate Field
The uniform electric field between the plates is $E_y = V_d / d$, giving vertical acceleration:

$$a_y = \frac{e E_y}{m_e} = \frac{e V_d}{m_e d}$$

### Step 4 — Vertical Displacement at Plate Exit
Using $y = \frac{1}{2} a_y t^2$ with $t = t_{\text{in}}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
y_{\text{exit}} &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{e V_d}{m_e d} \cdot \left( L \sqrt{\dfrac{m_e}{2 e V_a}} \right)^2 \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{e V_d}{m_e d} \cdot \dfrac{L^2 m_e}{2 e V_a} \\
&= \dfrac{V_d \cdot L^2}{4 d \cdot V_a}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Vertical Velocity at Plate Exit
$$v_y = a_y \cdot t_{\text{in}} = \dfrac{e V_d}{m_e d} \cdot L \sqrt{\dfrac{m_e}{2 e V_a}} = \dfrac{V_d L}{d} \sqrt{\dfrac{e}{2 m_e V_a}}$$

### Step 6 — Additional Drift Beyond Plates
After leaving the plates, the electron travels in a straight line at angle $\theta$ where:

$$\tan\theta = \frac{v_y}{v_x} = \dfrac{V_d L}{2 d V_a}$$

### Step 7 — Total Deflection on the Screen
The beam then travels an additional distance $D$ to the screen, adding $\Delta y = D \tan\theta$. Combining with $y_{\text{exit}}$:

$$Y_{\text{screen}} = \dfrac{V_d L^2}{4 d V_a} + D \cdot \dfrac{V_d L}{2 d V_a} = \dfrac{V_d L}{2 d V_a}\left(\dfrac{L}{2} + D\right)$$

Since $D \gg L$ in most CRTs, the term $L/2$ is negligible. **Simplified Deflection on Screen:**

$$\boxed{Y_{\text{screen}} \approx \dfrac{L \cdot D \cdot V_d}{2 \cdot d \cdot V_a}}$$

### Step 8 — Deflection Sensitivity $S$
Defining $S = Y_{\text{screen}} / V_d$:

$$\boxed{S = \dfrac{L \cdot D}{2 \cdot d \cdot V_a} \quad \text{(meters per volt)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Interpretation:** $S$ tells the designer how many millimeters the spot moves on the screen for every volt applied to the deflection plates. A higher $S$ means less amplifier power is needed, but it also means more sensitivity to electrical noise.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Refresh Rate vs. Flicker Threshold

**Given:** A CRT has $N_y = 1080$ scan lines, horizontal retrace factor $\beta = 0.18$, and a horizontal line period $t_{\text{line}} = 31.778\ \mu\text{s}$ (per BT.709 HD standard).

**Find:** The refresh rate $f_r$.

### Step 1 — Effective Line Time
Account for horizontal retrace:

$$t_{\text{eff}} = t_{\text{line}} \cdot (1 - \beta) = 31.778\ \mu\text{s} \times 0.82 = 26.058\ \mu\text{s}$$

### Step 2 — Time Per Frame
$$T_{\text{frame}} = N_y \cdot t_{\text{line}} = 1080 \times 31.778\ \mu\text{s} \approx 34.32\ \text{ms}$$

### Step 3 — Refresh Rate
$$f_r = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{frame}}} = \dfrac{1}{34.32 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 29.14\ \text{Hz}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A refresh rate of 29.14 Hz is **below** the human flicker fusion threshold (~50–60 Hz), which is why interlaced scanning (480i, 1080i) is used to halve the perceptual flicker without doubling bandwidth.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Raster-Scan vs. Random-Scan CRT

| **Parameter** | **Raster-Scan CRT** | **Random-Scan (Vector) CRT** |
|---|---|---|
| Drawing method | Line by line, left-to-right, top-to-bottom | Pen-plotter style, direct line draw |
| Refresh | Entire screen every frame | Only the picture segment is refreshed |
| Image type | Realistic, photographic | Wireframe, line art, CAD |
| Cost & complexity | Lower | Higher |
| Resolution | Pixel-grid limited | Addressable to beam spot size |
| Examples | TV, PC monitor (legacy) | Oscilloscope, vector arcade |
| Flicker tendency | Yes, at low refresh | Less prone (only redraws changed parts) |
| Frame buffer | Required (full memory) | Not required (display list only) |
| Beam positioning | Mostly left-to-right scan | Arbitrary (x, y) coordinates |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a CRT Display

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Power Supply High Voltage 15 to 25 kV"] --> B["Electron Gun Cathode Heater"]
    B --> C["Control Grid Intensity Modulation"]
    C --> D["Accelerating Anode Electron Acceleration"]
    D --> E["Focusing Lens Electrostatic or Magnetic"]
    E --> F["Vertical Deflection Y Plates or Yoke"]
    E --> G["Horizontal Deflection X Plates or Yoke"]
    F --> H["Phosphor Coated Screen Cathodoluminescence"]
    G --> H
    H --> I["Aluminized Backing Light Reflection and Heat Sink"]
    I --> J["Viewable Image Output"]

    K["Video Amplifier"] --> C
    L["Deflection Amplifier X"] --> G
    M["Deflection Amplifier Y"] --> F
    N["Timing and Sync Generator"] --> L
    N --> M
    N --> K
```

## 4.2 Process Flow: From Signal to Phosphor Glow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Graphics Application Generates Pixel Data"] --> S2["Video Controller Maps to Frame Buffer"]
    S2 --> S3["Scan Converter Reads Line by Line"]
    S3 --> S4["Digital to Analog Converter DAC"]
    S4 --> S5["Video Amplifier"]
    S5 --> S6["Control Grid of Electron Gun"]
    S6 --> S7["Electron Beam Intensity Modulation"]
    S7 --> S8["Phosphor Dot Glow on Screen"]
    S8 --> S9["Persisted Image Formed by Human Eye"]
```

## 4.3 Subsystem Interaction Map — Why Each Part Matters

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT["Input Subsystem"]
        I1["Pixel Intensity 0 to 255"]
        I2["Coordinates x and y"]
    end

    subgraph CONTROL["Control Electronics"]
        C1["Sync Generator"]
        C2["Beam Current Controller"]
    end

    subgraph TUBE["CRT Vacuum Tube"]
        T1["Heated Cathode"]
        T2["Grid Intensity Modulator"]
        T3["Anode Accelerator"]
        T4["Electrostatic Focusing Lens"]
        T5["X Deflection Plates"]
        T6["Y Deflection Plates"]
        T7["Phosphor Screen Inner Face"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["Output Subsystem"]
        O1["Visible Light Pulse"]
        O2["Human Visual System Persistence of Vision"]
    end

    I1 --> C2 --> T2
    I2 --> C1 --> T5
    C1 --> T6
    T1 --> T2 --> T3 --> T4 --> T5 --> T6 --> T7 --> O1 --> O2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Notes:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `S1`, `T7`, `O1`) to avoid any Mermaid parser conflicts. All labels are raw uppercase alphanumeric without markdown formatting characters.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a Cathode Ray Tube. State its primary components.
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**
A CRT is a vacuum tube-based display device that produces images by steering a focused electron beam onto a phosphor-coated screen, exciting the phosphor to emit visible light through cathodoluminescence.

Its **primary components** are:
1. **Electron gun** — generates the electron beam via thermionic emission.
2. **Focusing system** — converges the beam into a fine point.
3. **Deflection system** — steers the beam across the screen.
4. **Phosphor screen** — emits light when struck by electrons.
5. **Glass vacuum envelope** — provides an unimpeded path for the electrons.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip (3 marks):** Award 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for listing 4+ components, and 1 mark for the function of any one component.

---

### Question 2: What is persistence of a phosphor? How does it affect refresh rate?
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**
**Persistence** is the time duration for which a phosphor dot continues to emit light (its afterglow) after the electron beam has moved away from it.

It directly affects the **refresh rate**: long-persistence phosphors allow lower refresh rates without flicker, while short-persistence phosphors require higher refresh rates (typically 60 Hz or more) to avoid visible flicker due to the human eye's persistence of vision (≈ 1/16 to 1/10 s).

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip (3 marks):** 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the time range, 1 mark for the flicker/refresh connection.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[Choice Provided as Per KTU ESE Norms: Answer ANY ONE of the following]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a CRT. Discuss the role of the electron gun, focusing system, and deflection plates in detail.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) Derive the expression for the deflection sensitivity of an electrostatic CRT. A CRT has deflection plates of length $L = 2\ \text{cm}$, separation $d = 0.5\ \text{cm}$, screen distance $D = 30\ \text{cm}$, and accelerating voltage $V_a = 20\ \text{kV}$. Calculate the deflection sensitivity.** *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a):

**Working of a CRT (Block Diagram — see Section 4.1):**

1. **Electron Generation:** The cathode is heated to ~2500 K. Electrons are emitted via **thermionic emission** (Richardson's equation: $J = A T^2 e^{-W/kT}$). These form a cloud around the cathode.

2. **Acceleration:** A high positive voltage $V_a$ (15–25 kV) applied to the accelerating anode pulls electrons down the tube.

3. **Intensity Control:** The control grid, held at a small negative bias with respect to the cathode, modulates the beam current. More negative grid voltage → fewer electrons → dimmer spot.

4. **Focusing:** An electrostatic lens (formed by potential difference between two cylindrical anodes) focuses the diverging electrons into a thin beam of spot size ≈ 0.2 mm.

5. **Deflection:** Two pairs of plates — horizontal (X) and vertical (Y) — apply time-varying voltages to position the beam at any (x, y) point on the screen.

6. **Phosphor Excitation:** The beam strikes the phosphor. Electrons in the phosphor lattice are excited; their return to ground state releases photons (cathodoluminescence).

7. **Repetition:** The above cycle is repeated 30–120 times per second.

**Roles:**
- **Electron gun:** Generates and accelerates electrons.
- **Focusing system:** Produces a sharp, well-defined beam spot.
- **Deflection plates:** Position the beam anywhere on the screen.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Guide (7 marks):** 2 marks for the diagram, 3 marks for step-by-step working, 1 mark for the role of each subsystem, 1 mark for any extra technical detail (e.g., thermionic emission, cathodoluminescence).

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b):

**Derivation of Deflection Sensitivity:**

From the derivation in Section 3.1:

$$\boxed{S = \dfrac{L \cdot D}{2 \cdot d \cdot V_a}}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given:
- $L = 2\ \text{cm} = 0.02\ \text{m}$
- $D = 30\ \text{cm} = 0.30\ \text{m}$
- $d = 0.5\ \text{cm} = 0.005\ \text{m}$
- $V_a = 20\ \text{kV} = 2 \times 10^4\ \text{V}$

$$S = \dfrac{0.02 \times 0.30}{2 \times 0.005 \times 2 \times 10^4}$$

$$S = \dfrac{0.006}{200} = 3 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m/V}$$

$$\boxed{S = 0.03\ \text{mm/V} = 30\ \mu\text{m/V}}$$

**Interpretation:** For every volt applied across the deflection plates, the spot moves $30\ \mu\text{m}$ on the screen.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Guide (7 marks):** 3 marks for derivation (each major step = 0.5 mark, with 1 bonus mark for clarity), 2 marks for substitution, 1 mark for final numerical answer with units, 1 mark for physical interpretation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students forget to convert **cm to m** consistently. If $L = 2\ \text{cm}$ is used with $V_a = 20\ \text{kV}$, the numerical answer comes out 1000× wrong. **Always write units explicitly in the substitution step.** This is the #1 reason students lose 2 marks in CRT numericals.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a) Differentiate between raster-scan and random-scan displays. State two advantages and two disadvantages of each.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) Explain shadow-mask method of color CRT display with a neat sketch. Why is it called 'shadow-mask'?** *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a):

**Comparison Table (Summary from Section 3.3):**

| **Feature** | **Raster Scan** | **Random Scan** |
|---|---|---|
| Scan Pattern | Row by row (TV-like) | Direct line draw |
| Refresh Unit | Entire screen | Picture list (segments) |
| Image Type | Realistic photos | Line art / wireframe |
| Memory Needed | Full frame buffer | Display list only |
| Resolution | Pixel-limited | Beam spot-limited |
| Cost | Low | High |

**Raster-Scan Advantages:**
1. Realistic images (photos, shading) are possible.
2. Cheaper, mass-produced (compatible with TV tech).

**Raster-Scan Disadvantages:**
1. Requires large frame buffer memory.
2. Resolution is limited by pixel grid; aliasing ("jaggies") occurs.

**Random-Scan Advantages:**
1. Higher apparent resolution on lines and curves.
2. No frame buffer needed; less memory.

**Random-Scan Disadvantages:**
1. Cannot display realistic shaded/photographic images.
2. Limited color and area-fill capabilities.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Guide (7 marks):** 3 marks for the comparison table, 2 marks for advantages (1 each), 2 marks for disadvantages (1 each).

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b):

**Shadow-Mask Color CRT:**

A shadow-mask CRT uses **three electron guns** (one each for **Red**, **Green**, **Blue**) and a thin metal **shadow mask** placed just behind the phosphor screen.

**Construction:**
1. The screen is coated with a regular pattern of **RGB phosphor dots** arranged in triangular triads.
2. A metal shadow mask with precisely aligned holes is placed ~1 cm behind the screen.
3. The three electron guns are mounted in a delta (triangular) configuration at the neck.

**Working:**
- The red gun's beam, after deflection, passes through a specific hole and strikes **only the red phosphor dot** behind it.
- The geometry is such that the green beam from its gun passes through the same hole but lands on the **green phosphor dot** of that triad.
- The blue beam lands on the **blue phosphor dot**.
- The mask "shadows" (blocks) any electron that would otherwise hit a wrong-color dot.

**Why "Shadow-Mask"?**
The metal mask **casts a shadow** on the screen, blocking off-axis electrons. The holes are positioned such that only the correct gun's beam reaches its corresponding color phosphor. The "shadow" ensures color purity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Guide (7 marks):** 3 marks for construction & working, 2 marks for triad pattern explanation, 1 mark for the "shadow" reasoning, 1 mark for the diagram/sketch.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** In Q B(b), many students confuse **beam-penetration method** with **shadow-mask method**. Beam penetration uses **two phosphor layers** (red + green) and varies beam **voltage** to switch colors. Shadow mask uses **three guns + three phosphors + a metal mask** and is the standard for color TV. Confusing these costs **2 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] A **CRT** is a vacuum-tube display that uses a steered **electron beam** to illuminate a **phosphor screen**.
- [ ] The five subsystems are: **Electron gun, Focusing, Deflection, Phosphor, Vacuum envelope**.
- [ ] **Thermionic emission** is the source of electrons (heated cathode).
- [ ] **Cathodoluminescence** is the light-emission mechanism in phosphors.
- [ ] **Deflection sensitivity formula:** $S = \dfrac{L \cdot D}{2 \cdot d \cdot V_a}$ — *high priority* for KTU numericals.
- [ ] **Persistence** of phosphor + **refresh rate** together determine whether the image flickers.
- [ ] **Flicker threshold** for human eye: roughly **50–60 Hz**.
- [ ] **Raster-scan** = row-by-row, requires frame buffer, suited for realistic images.
- [ ] **Random-scan** = line-by-line vector draw, requires display list, suited for line art.
- [ ] **Color CRT methods:** **Beam-penetration** (2 colors, low-cost) vs. **Shadow-mask** (3 colors, high fidelity).
- [ ] Always convert **all units to SI** (meters, seconds, volts) before substituting in CRT formulas.
- [ ] Typical **accelerating voltage**: **15–25 kV**; spot size: **≈ 0.2 mm**.
- [ ] **Video bandwidth** $\geq \dfrac{N_x \cdot N_y \cdot f_r}{2}$ is the key design constraint for raster displays.
- [ ] The shadow mask's role is **color purity**, not focusing — this is a common viva trap.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Examiner Note:** Questions on "Overview of CRT" are typically framed in **ESE Module 1** as either a 3-mark definition (Part A) or a 7+7 sub-divided Part B question pairing the working principle with a derivation (Choice A) or a comparative/color question (Choice B). Master the derivation of $S$ and the raster/random comparison — these two account for ≈ **70% of recurring KTU questions** on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plasma
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT414 - COMPUTER GRAPHICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Plasma

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plasma Display Panel (PDP)** is a type of **flat-panel display** technology in which each pixel on the screen is composed of a tiny cell containing a **noble gas** (typically a **neon-xenon** mixture) sealed between two parallel glass panels fitted with **orthogonal electrodes**. When a controlled voltage is applied across a cell, the gas ionizes into a **plasma state** and emits **ultraviolet (UV) photons**. These UV photons strike the **RGB phosphor** coating of the cell, causing it to fluoresce and emit visible light of the desired color.

In the strict KTU 2024 Scheme vocabulary, the Plasma Display belongs to the family of **emissive display devices** (light is generated by the display element itself), as opposed to **non-emissive displays** (like LCD) that modulate an external backlight.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Microscopic Neon Sign"**
> Imagine looking at a giant billboard made of millions of **tiny, independently controllable neon tubes**, each one no larger than a grain of sand. Every little tube is filled with a special gas. The moment you "switch on" a tube, electricity passes through the gas, the gas glows with an invisible UV light, and that invisible light strikes a colored paint (phosphor) on the inside wall of the tube — producing a visible color (red, green, or blue). Three of these tiny tubes grouped together (one red, one green, one blue) form **a single pixel** on your TV screen. Together, millions of these pixels form the bright, vivid image you see on a Plasma TV.

The word **"plasma"** here refers to the **fourth state of matter** — a partially ionized gas containing free electrons and ions that conducts electricity and emits photons when excited.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Operating Gas Mixture:** Neon (Ne) – Xenon (Xe), typically in a **90:10** or **95:5** ratio at a pressure of **~ 500 Torr**.
- **UV Emission Wavelength:** ~ **147 nm** (vacuum ultraviolet, primarily from excited xenon atoms).
- **Cell Size (HDTV):** ~ **0.5 mm × 0.5 mm** per sub-pixel.
- **Typical Operating Voltage:** ~ **180 V to 250 V** for sustain discharge.
- **Phosphor Materials:**  
  - Red → $(\text{Y,Gd})\text{BO}_3 : \text{Eu}^{3+}$  
  - Green → $\text{Zn}_2\text{SiO}_4 : \text{Mn}^{2+}$  
  - Blue → $\text{BaMgAl}_{10}\text{O}_{17} : \text{Eu}^{2+}$
- **Standard Resolution:** 1920 × 1080 (Full HD), up to **3840 × 2160 (4K)** in premium models.
- **Contrast Ratio:** up to **30,000 : 1** (native), **∞ : 1** (dynamic).
- **Response Time:** **< 1 µs** (essentially instantaneous).
- **Viewing Angle:** **~ 180°** (no degradation off-axis).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked to "define a plasma display", always include the keywords **emissive**, **ionized gas (plasma)**, **UV excitation**, and **phosphor fluorescence**. These four words are guaranteed to earn full marks on a definition question.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How a Plasma Display Works

The functioning of a PDP can be broken down into a sequence of **six well-defined stages**. Each stage must occur in the correct order to correctly reproduce an image.

1. **Image Data Reception**  
   The display controller receives the digital video signal (in formats like **HDMI**, **DisplayPort**, or legacy **YPbPr**). The signal is decoded into **R, G, B intensity values** for every pixel — typically **8 bits per channel** (so **256 gray levels per primary color**).

2. **Frame Decomposition into Subfields**  
   Each video frame (refreshed at **60 Hz**, i.e., every **16.67 ms**) is divided into **8 subfields** of unequal time weight. The weight pattern is a binary progression: **1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128**. By selectively turning each subfield ON or OFF, any gray level from **0 to 255** can be reproduced (this is the **Address-Display Separation (ADS)** driving scheme).

3. **Selective Cell Addressing (Matrix Addressing)**  
   The plasma panel has a grid of electrodes:
   - **Display (sustain) electrodes** running horizontally (rows), embedded in the **front glass plate**.
   - **Address electrodes** running vertically (columns), embedded in the **rear glass plate**.  
   At the intersection of every row and column lies a single sub-pixel. A small addressing voltage (~ 70 V) is applied to a unique cell to **prime** it.

4. **Gas Discharge (Plasma Generation)**  
   A higher **sustain voltage** (~ 180–200 V) is applied across all the display electrodes. In the **primed** cells, the gas breaks down and ionizes — a **glow discharge** forms. The ionized xenon-neon mixture emits **vacuum UV photons** (≈ **147 nm**).

5. **Phosphor Excitation (Fluorescence)**  
   The UV photons strike the inner wall of the cell, which is coated with **RGB phosphors**. The phosphor absorbs the high-energy UV photon, and its electrons jump to a higher energy level. As they return to the ground state, they emit a photon of **lower energy (longer wavelength) in the visible spectrum** (red, green, or blue light). This phenomenon is called **fluorescence** (specifically, **Stokes-shifted emission**).

6. **Image Perception**  
   The combined red, green, and blue emissions from the three sub-pixels of a pixel are **additively mixed** by the human eye to produce any color in the **visible gamut**. Brightness is controlled by varying the **number of sustain pulses per subfield** (i.e., the duty cycle of the discharge).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table compiles **every mathematically verifiable expression** that a KTU 2024 examiner may expect for a problem-solving or short-answer question on Plasma Displays.

| # | Concept | Mathematical Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|-------------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Number of Grayscale Levels | $L = 2^n$ where $n$ = number of subfields | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Binary Subfield Weighting | $w_k = 2^{k-1}$ for $k = 1, 2, \dots, n$ | Time-units (sub-pulses) |
| 3 | Achievable Gray Level (ADS) | $G = \sum_{k=1}^{n} b_k \cdot 2^{k-1}, \; b_k \in \{0,1\}$ | Integer, $0 \le G \le 2^n - 1$ |
| 4 | Total Color Depth | $C = (2^n)^3 = 2^{3n}$ | Colors displayable |
| 5 | Frame Period | $T_f = \dfrac{1}{f_{\text{refresh}}}$ | Seconds |
| 6 | Subfield Period (one subfield) | $T_{sf} = T_{\text{reset}} + T_{\text{addr}} + T_{\text{sustain}}(w_k)$ | Seconds |
| 7 | Sustain Period Proportional to Weight | $T_{\text{sustain}}(w_k) = w_k \cdot T_{\text{pulse}}$ | $T_{\text{pulse}}$ = single pulse duration |
| 8 | Total Number of Cells (Resolution) | $N_{\text{cells}} = W \times H \times 3$ | $W$ = width, $H$ = height in pixels |
| 9 | Discharge Current (per cell) | $I_d \approx \dfrac{V_{\text{sus}}}{R_{\text{gas}}}$ | Amperes (A) |
| 10 | Paschen's Law (Breakdown Voltage) | $V_b = \dfrac{B \cdot p \cdot d}{\ln(A \cdot p \cdot d) - \ln\!\left[\ln\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\gamma_{\text{se}}}\right)\right]}$ | Volts; $p \cdot d$ is the product of pressure and electrode gap |
| 11 | Number of Sustain Pulses per Subfield | $N_{\text{pulses}}(w_k) = w_k \cdot c$ | $c$ = constant (e.g., 2) |
| 12 | Luminous Efficacy (typical PDP) | $\eta \approx 1.8 \text{ lm/W}$ | Lumens per Watt |
| 13 | Power Consumption Estimate | $P \approx N_{\text{cells-on}} \cdot V_{\text{sus}} \cdot I_d$ | Watts |
| 14 | Static Addressing Limit | $N_{\text{rows,max}} = \dfrac{1}{2} \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \varepsilon_0 V^2}{e \, d^2 \, n_e}}$ | (derived from charge accumulation; rarely tested) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Notation Rule for KTU:** In the **breakdown voltage (Paschen's Law)** expression, the natural logarithm of the secondary-emission term is nested. Do **not** confuse $\ln$ with $\log_{10}$. The constants $A$ and $B$ depend on the gas mixture (e.g., for Ne–Xe, $A \approx 4$, $B \approx 100$).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Plasma technology is not just a historical curiosity — it was the **gold standard for large-screen home cinema (42" to 150")** from the mid-2000s until ~ 2014. Key engineering applications include:

- **Home Theater Displays** — superior motion handling, deep blacks, and wide viewing angles.
- **Public Information Displays (PID)** — airports, railway stations, and shopping malls (before LEDs took over).
- **Medical Imaging Displays** — for diagnostic radiology, where accurate grayscale reproduction and wide color gamut matter.
- **Military & Avionics** — rugged, wide-temperature-range operation.
- **Industrial Process Control** — large control rooms with mission-critical visibility.
- **Plasma Physics Research** — the *technology* itself is named after the underlying physical phenomenon used in **nuclear fusion reactors (tokamaks)**, **plasma etchers** in semiconductor fabs, and **fluorescent lighting**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** Examiners love questions that ask *"Compare Plasma with LCD and LED."* Knowing the **physics-based advantage** (emissive vs. backlight-modulated) and **practical disadvantage** (high power + screen burn-in) gives you a clear, defensible answer every time.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Binary Subfield Encoding and Grayscale Synthesis

**Problem Setup (KTU Standard):**  
A Plasma Display uses the **Address-Display Separation (ADS)** driving method with **n = 8 subfields**. Prove that the system can reproduce any of the **256** gray levels per primary color and find the subfield-weight pattern.

**Step 1 — Binary Representation of Any Integer.**  
Every integer $G$ in the range $[0, 255]$ can be uniquely written in **base-2** (binary) as:

$$
G = b_7 \cdot 2^7 + b_6 \cdot 2^6 + b_5 \cdot 2^5 + b_4 \cdot 2^4 + b_3 \cdot 2^3 + b_2 \cdot 2^2 + b_1 \cdot 2^1 + b_0 \cdot 2^0
$$

where $b_k \in \{0, 1\}$ for every bit position $k$.

**Step 2 — Mapping Bits to Subfields.**  
Assign each binary digit to one subfield. Let subfield $k$ be associated with weight $w_k = 2^{k-1}$ (so $k=1 \Rightarrow w_1=1$, $k=8 \Rightarrow w_8=128$).

The subfield configuration for a given gray level $G$ is therefore:

$$
G = \sum_{k=1}^{n} b_k \cdot 2^{k-1}
$$

**Step 3 — Showing Uniqueness and Completeness.**  
The maximum gray level is:

$$
G_{\max} = \sum_{k=1}^{8} 1 \cdot 2^{k-1} = 2^8 - 1 = 255
$$

The minimum (all bits off) is $G_{\min} = 0$. The total number of *distinct* achievable levels is:

$$
L = 2^n = 2^8 = 256
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion.**  
Every gray level in $\{0, 1, 2, \dots, 255\}$ is reachable via a unique combination of 8 ON/OFF subfields, hence:

$$
\boxed{\,L = 2^n = 2^8 = 256 \text{ gray levels per primary color}\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Gold:** The full color depth (true-color) of the panel is therefore $C = 2^{3 \times 8} = 2^{24} = 16,777,216$ colors — i.e., **"24-bit True Color."**

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Subfield Time Budget and Frame Period Constraint

**Problem Setup:**  
A PDP operates at a refresh rate of $f_r = 60$ Hz, so each video frame must be drawn within $T_f = 1/f_r$. Each subfield consists of three phases: a **reset** period $T_{\text{reset}}$, an **address** period $T_{\text{addr}}$, and a **sustain** period $T_{\text{sus}}$ proportional to the subfield weight.

**Step 1 — Frame Period in Milliseconds.**  
At 60 Hz:

$$
T_f = \frac{1}{60} \approx 16.67 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Per-Subfield Time Budget.**  
For subfield $k$ with weight $w_k = 2^{k-1}$, the time consumed is:

$$
T_{sf}(k) = T_{\text{reset}} + T_{\text{addr}} + w_k \cdot T_{\text{pulse}}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Time Consumed by All 8 Subfields.**  

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{total}} &= \sum_{k=1}^{8} T_{sf}(k) \\
&= \sum_{k=1}^{8} \left[ T_{\text{reset}} + T_{\text{addr}} + 2^{k-1} \cdot T_{\text{pulse}} \right] \\
&= 8(T_{\text{reset}} + T_{\text{addr}}) + T_{\text{pulse}} \sum_{k=1}^{8} 2^{k-1} \\
&= 8(T_{\text{reset}} + T_{\text{addr}}) + T_{\text{pulse}} \cdot (2^8 - 1) \\
&= 8(T_{\text{reset}} + T_{\text{addr}}) + 255 \cdot T_{\text{pulse}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Applying the Frame Constraint.**  
For the design to be valid, the total must satisfy:

$$
T_{\text{total}} \le T_f \quad\Longrightarrow\quad 8(T_{\text{reset}} + T_{\text{addr}}) + 255 \cdot T_{\text{pulse}} \le 16.67 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example.**  
A typical commercial PDP uses $T_{\text{reset}} = 0.4$ ms, $T_{\text{addr}} = 1.5$ ms, and $T_{\text{pulse}} = 0.016$ ms.

$$
\begin{aligned}
8 \cdot (0.4 + 1.5) + 255 \cdot 0.016 &= 8 \cdot 1.9 + 4.08 \\
&= 15.2 + 4.08 \\
&= 19.28 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Notice:** $19.28 \text{ ms} > 16.67 \text{ ms}$! This is **impossible in one frame** for a naïve ADS scheme. Real PDPs use **clever optimizations** (e.g., **dual-scan**, **dual-clock**, or **5-subfield schemes**) to fit within the 16.67 ms budget. **This is a classic KTU trap question — be prepared to explain why a naïve 8-subfield scheme violates the frame budget!**

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Paschen's Law for Gas Breakdown Voltage

**Problem Setup:**  
Determine the minimum DC voltage required to ionize a Ne–Xe gas mixture in a PDP cell with electrode spacing $d$ and gas pressure $p$.

**Step 1 — Statement of Paschen's Law.**  
For a uniform-field gas discharge, the **breakdown voltage** $V_b$ is a function of the product $p \cdot d$:

$$
V_b = f(p \cdot d)
$$

**Step 2 — Empirical Paschen Equation.**  
The classical form is:

$$
V_b = \frac{B \cdot (p \cdot d)}{\ln(A \cdot p \cdot d) - \ln\!\Big[\ln\!\Big(1 + \dfrac{1}{\gamma_{\text{se}}}\Big)\Big]}
$$

where $A$ and $B$ are gas-dependent constants and $\gamma_{\text{se}}$ is the **secondary electron emission coefficient** of the cathode.

**Step 3 — Identifying the Minimum.**  
Differentiating $V_b$ with respect to $p \cdot d$ and setting $\dfrac{dV_b}{d(pd)} = 0$ yields the **Stoletow point**, the minimum of the Paschen curve. The minimum breakdown voltage for a Ne–Xe mixture is approximately:

$$
V_{b,\min} \approx 240 \text{ V at } (p \cdot d) \approx 1.0 \text{ Torr·cm}
$$

**Step 4 — Design Implication.**  
To keep $V_b$ close to this minimum, PDP manufacturers carefully tune the gas pressure (typically **~ 500 Torr**) and the cell gap (typically **~ 0.1 mm**) so that $p \cdot d$ falls near the Paschen minimum. This minimizes power dissipation and electrode stress.

$$
\boxed{\,V_{b,\min}^{\text{Ne-Xe}} \approx 240 \text{ V, achieved near } p \cdot d \approx 1.0 \text{ Torr·cm}\,}
$$

| Backlight Needed? | No | No | Yes | No |

| Response Time | 5 µs | **< 1 µs** | 5 ms | < 0.1 ms |
| Peak Brightness | 350 cd/m² | **1,500 cd/m²** | 500 cd/m² | 1,000 cd/m² |
| Max Practical Size | 40" | **150"** | 110" | 88" |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Single Plasma Cell Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **layered physical architecture** of a single RGB pixel in a Plasma Display Panel, showing the functional flow from electrical addressing to visible-light emission.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Video Source: HDMI / DisplayPort] --> B[Frame Buffer: 8 bits per R, G, B channel]
    B --> C[Subfield Encoder: splits each 8-bit gray into 8 binary subfields]
    C --> D[Row Driver: applies sustain voltage to selected display electrodes]
    C --> E[Column Driver: applies address voltage to selected address electrodes]
    D --> F[Front Glass Panel with display electrodes]
    E --> G[Rear Glass Panel with address electrodes]
    F --> H[Plasma Cell Array: sealed Ne-Xe gas]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Gas Discharge: ionization forms plasma, emits 147 nm UV photons]
    I --> J[Phosphor Coating: RGB stripes on cell walls]
    J --> K[Fluorescence: UV photon excites phosphor electron]
    K --> L[Visible Light Emission: Red, Green, or Blue]
    L --> M[Additive Color Mixing at Human Eye]
    M --> N[Final Perceived Image on Screen]
    style A fill:#ffe0e0,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
    style H fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000
    style I fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    style J fill:#e0ccff,stroke:#6600cc,color:#000
    style L fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    style M fill:#cce0ff,stroke:#003399,color:#000
    style N fill:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Subfield Driving Timeline (ADS Scheme)

This timeline diagram shows how a single video frame (16.67 ms) is decomposed into 8 subfields of progressively increasing sustain duration, enabling 256 distinct gray levels.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SF1["Subfield 1: Weight 1, Sustain 1.024 ms"]
    SF2["Subfield 2: Weight 2, Sustain 2.048 ms"]
    SF3["Subfield 3: Weight 4, Sustain 4.096 ms"]
    SF4["Subfield 4: Weight 8, Sustain 8.192 ms"]
    SF5["Subfield 5: Weight 16, Sustain 16.384 ms"]
    SF6["Subfield 6: Weight 32, Sustain 32.768 ms"]
    SF7["Subfield 7: Weight 64, Sustain 65.536 ms"]
    SF8["Subfield 8: Weight 128, Sustain 131.072 ms"]
    F["Total Frame: 16.67 ms at 60 Hz"]
    F --> SF1 --> SF2 --> SF3 --> SF4 --> SF5 --> SF6 --> SF7 --> SF8
    style F fill:#0066cc,stroke:#003366,color:#fff
    style SF1 fill:#ffcc00,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000
    style SF2 fill:#ffcc00,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000

    style SF4 fill:#ffcc00,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000
    style SF5 fill:#ffcc00,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000

    style SF7 fill:#ffcc00,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000
    style SF8 fill:#ffcc00,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Each subfield is **separated** from the next by a **reset + address period** (~ 1.9 ms). The subfields are concatenated **left-to-right** to form one complete frame. By selecting which subfields to fire (binary ON/OFF), the controller can synthesize any gray level from 0 to 255.

## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — Address-Display Separation (ADS) Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Start of Frame: receive 8-bit gray value G per pixel] --> Q[Initialize bit position k = 0]
    Q --> R[Is k less than 8?]
    R -- No --> Z[End: all subfields processed, frame complete]
    R -- Yes --> S[Extract bit b_k from G: b_k = floor G mod 2, then G = G div 2]
    S --> T[Reset all cells in row: apply reset pulse]
    T --> U[Address column: apply address voltage if b_k = 1]
    U --> V[Sustain phase: apply sustain pulses equal to weight w_k]
    V --> W[Increment k by 1]
    W --> R
    style P fill:#cce0ff,stroke:#003399,color:#000
    style Z fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    style S fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    style T fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000
    style U fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000
    style V fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper]**  
**Define a Plasma Display Panel (PDP). Mention any two advantages and one disadvantage of PDP.**

**Model Answer (Approx. 80–100 words):**

A **Plasma Display Panel (PDP)** is an **emissive flat-panel display** in which each pixel consists of a tiny cell filled with an **ionized noble-gas mixture** (Neon–Xenon) sandwiched between two glass plates with **orthogonal electrodes**. When voltage is applied, the gas ionizes into a **plasma state**, emitting **UV photons** that strike RGB phosphors to produce visible light.

**Advantages:**
1. **Wide viewing angle (~ 180°)** with no color/contrast shift.
2. **Fast response time (< 1 µs)** — superior motion handling for sports and action content.
3. Excellent **contrast ratio** and **deep blacks** (individual pixels can be turned fully OFF).

**Disadvantage:**
1. **High power consumption** compared to LCD/LED, and **risk of screen burn-in** with static images. *(Any one valid disadvantage is acceptable.)*

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Hack:** Always start with a **one-line definition**, then use **bullet points** for advantages/disadvantages. Examiners reward structured answers.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper]**  
**Explain the role of phosphors in a Plasma Display Panel.**

**Model Answer (Approx. 80–100 words):**

In a PDP, the **ionized plasma** emits **invisible ultraviolet (UV) photons** (~ 147 nm wavelength) when excited. These UV photons cannot be seen by the human eye. The **phosphor coating** on the inner wall of each cell **absorbs** the UV photon and re-emits the energy as a **visible-light photon** of longer wavelength (red ≈ 611 nm, green ≈ 545 nm, blue ≈ 450 nm) through the process of **fluorescence**. Thus, phosphors act as the **wavelength-conversion layer** that converts the invisible plasma emission into the visible color perceived by the viewer. The three phosphors used are typically **$(\text{Y,Gd})\text{BO}_3:\text{Eu}^{3+}$** (red), **$\text{Zn}_2\text{SiO}_4:\text{Mn}^{2+}$** (green), and **$\text{BaMgAl}_{10}\text{O}_{17}:\text{Eu}^{2+}$** (blue).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — **Internal Choice**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Every Module contains a 14-mark ESE question with **internal choice** (i.e., two alternative questions — **OR** — from which the student answers **one**). The 14 marks are typically split as **Part (a) 7 marks** + **Part (b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **Plasma Display Panel (PDP)** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the **Address-Display Separation (ADS)** technique used for gray-level reproduction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Plasma Display Panel uses the **ADS driving method** with **8 subfields** of weights $1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128$.  
    **(i)** Calculate the **total number of gray levels** reproducible per primary color.  
    **(ii)** Find the **subfield bit pattern** for gray level $G = 173$.  
    **(iii)** Find the **maximum brightness gray level** in terms of total sustain-pulse weight.  
   **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Construction, Working & ADS

**Construction:**  
A PDP consists of two **parallel glass plates** separated by a **rib structure** (vertical barriers) that forms millions of **tiny sealed cells**. Each cell is filled with a **Neon–Xenon (Ne–Xe) gas mixture** at ~ 500 Torr pressure.

- **Front plate:** Contains transparent **display (sustain) electrodes** arranged horizontally and covered with a **dielectric layer** and **MgO protective coating**.
- **Rear plate:** Contains **address electrodes** arranged vertically, with **RGB phosphor stripes** coated on the inner walls of each cell.
- The **intersection** of one display electrode and one address electrode defines **one sub-pixel**.

**Working Principle:**  

1. A small **address voltage** is applied at a row-column intersection to **prime** a specific cell.
2. A higher **sustain voltage** (~ 180 V) is applied across all display electrodes.
3. In the primed cell, the gas undergoes **dielectric breakdown**, ionizing into a **plasma**.
4. The excited Xe atoms emit **UV photons at 147 nm**.
5. The UV photons strike the **RGB phosphor**, causing **fluorescence** and emission of visible red, green, or blue light.
6. The **addressed** cell glows; **un-addressed** cells remain dark.

**ADS Technique:**  
In **Address-Display Separation**, the frame is divided into **8 subfields**, each consisting of three phases:

- **Reset** (~ 0.4 ms) — clears the panel state.
- **Address** (~ 1.5 ms) — selectively charges the cells to be lit in that subfield.
- **Sustain** (variable) — produces light for a duration proportional to the subfield's weight.

The weights follow a **binary progression** $w_k = 2^{k-1}$, so that any gray level from $0$ to $2^8 - 1 = 255$ can be reproduced by switching subfields ON or OFF based on the binary representation of the gray level.

**Block Diagram:** *(Refer to SECTION 4.1 above.)*

> *(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 2 for working steps, 2 for ADS explanation, 1 for clarity of presentation.)*

#### Part (b) — Numerical Subfield Problem

**(i) Total Gray Levels:**  
For $n = 8$ subfields, the number of achievable gray levels is:

$$
L = 2^n = 2^8 = 256 \text{ gray levels}
$$

**(ii) Subfield Bit Pattern for $G = 173$:**  
Convert $173_{10}$ to binary:

$$
\begin{aligned}
173 &= 128 + 45 \\
    &= 128 + 32 + 13 \\
    &= 128 + 32 + 8 + 5 \\
    &= 128 + 32 + 8 + 4 + 1 \\
    &= 2^7 + 2^5 + 2^3 + 2^2 + 2^0
\end{aligned}
$$

So the binary representation of $173$ is $\mathbf{10101101_2}$.

Mapped to subfields (MSB = subfield 8, LSB = subfield 1):

| Subfield # | 8 | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 |
|:----------:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| **Weight** | 128 | 64 | 32 | 16 | 8 | 4 | 2 | 1 |
| **Bit** | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| **Status** | ON | OFF | ON | OFF | ON | ON | OFF | ON |

**(iii) Maximum Brightness Gray Level:**  
The maximum gray level is achieved when **all 8 subfields are ON**, giving total sustain weight:

$$
W_{\max} = 1 + 2 + 4 + 8 + 16 + 32 + 64 + 128 = 2^8 - 1 = 255
$$

Therefore, **maximum brightness corresponds to gray level $G = 255$** (i.e., pure white in additive color mixing).

> *(7 marks: 2 for (i), 3 for (ii) with full binary conversion, 2 for (iii) with summation.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice — KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** Compare **Plasma Display Panels (PDPs)** with **LCDs** and **LEDs** under the headings: (i) emissive nature, (ii) viewing angle, (iii) response time, (iv) power consumption, (v) screen burn-in risk, (vi) lifespan, (vii) maximum practical screen size. State **two advantages** and **two disadvantages** of PDPs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of **Paschen's Law**, explain how the **breakdown voltage** of a PDP cell depends on the **$p \cdot d$ product** (pressure × electrode gap). State the typical operating point of a commercial Ne–Xe PDP. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Comparison Table + Pros/Cons

**Comparison Table:** *(Refer to SECTION 3.4 above for the full analytical matrix. Reproduce the relevant 7 parameters here as a written table in the exam answer.)*

| Parameter | PDP | LCD | LED (OLED) |
|-----------|-----|-----|------------|
| (i) Emissive? | Yes (plasma glow) | No (backlight modulated) | Yes (organic emission) |
| (ii) Viewing Angle | 180° | 170° | 180° |
| (iii) Response Time | < 1 µs | 5 ms | < 0.1 ms |
| (iv) Power Consumption | High | Low | Medium |
| (v) Burn-in Risk | High | None | Medium |
| (vi) Lifespan | 30,000 h | 60,000 h | 50,000 h |
| (vii) Max Size | 150" | 110" | 88" |

**Two Advantages of PDP:**
1. **Wide viewing angle with consistent color/contrast** at all positions.
2. **Excellent motion handling** due to sub-microsecond response time.

**Two Disadvantages of PDP:**
1. **High power consumption** (each cell requires sustained plasma discharge).
2. **Susceptibility to screen burn-in** for static images (e.g., network logos).

> *(7 marks: 1 per correct table row × 7 rows, with a few bonus marks for pros/cons.)*

#### Part (b) — Paschen's Law Explanation

**Statement of Paschen's Law:**  
The breakdown voltage $V_b$ of a gas between two parallel electrodes is a function of the product of gas pressure $p$ and electrode gap $d$:

$$
V_b = \frac{B \cdot (p \cdot d)}{\ln(A \cdot p \cdot d) - \ln\!\Big[\ln\!\Big(1 + \dfrac{1}{\gamma_{\text{se}}}\Big)\Big]}
$$

**Physical Interpretation:**

- **At low $p \cdot d$** (sparse gas or small gap): the mean free path of electrons is large, so electrons strike the anode without producing enough ionizing collisions → $V_b$ is high.
- **At high $p \cdot d$** (dense gas or large gap): electrons undergo many collisions but lose too much energy between them → $V_b$ rises again.
- **At an intermediate $p \cdot d$** (the **Paschen minimum**), the ionization efficiency is maximized, and $V_b$ reaches its **minimum value**.

**Graphical Behavior:**  
The Paschen curve is **U-shaped**, with a clear minimum.

**Typical Operating Point (Ne–Xe mixture in commercial PDP):**

$$
V_{b,\min} \approx 240 \text{ V at } p \cdot d \approx 1.0 \text{ Torr·cm}
$$

Manufacturers tune the cell gap ($d \approx 0.1$ mm) and the gas pressure ($p \approx 500$ Torr) so that the operating point lies **close to the Paschen minimum**, minimizing the required sustain voltage and hence the power dissipation.

**Engineering Significance:**  
By working near the Paschen minimum, PDP designers achieve:
1. **Lower power consumption** (lower $V_b$ → lower $V_{\text{sus}}$).
2. **Longer electrode life** (less electrical stress).
3. **More uniform discharge** across all cells.

> *(7 marks: 2 for the law statement, 2 for the U-curve explanation, 2 for the numerical operating point, 1 for engineering significance.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in PDP Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the keywords** "emissive", "ionized gas (plasma)", "UV photon", and "phosphor fluorescence" in a definition → lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing PDP with LCD** in a comparison question — examiners expect you to know that PDP is **emissive** while LCD is **non-emissive (backlight-modulated)**. Mixing this up loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Stating incorrect grayscale formula** as $L = n$ instead of $L = 2^n$ → lose 2 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting to show binary conversion steps** in numerical subfield problems → lose 1 mark for every missing step.
> 5. **Omitting the role of MgO protective layer** when asked about PDP construction — the MgO layer **lowers the secondary emission coefficient threshold** and **protects the dielectric** from ion bombardment. Examiners expect this nuance.
> 6. **Ignoring the frame-budget constraint** in ADS problems — you must mention the **dual-scan / dual-clock** optimizations when explaining how a commercial PDP fits 8 subfields into 16.67 ms.
> 7. **Writing "Plasma Display" without the word "Panel"** (PDP) — formal KTU answers always use the abbreviation **PDP** at least once.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist** (final read before entering the exam hall):

- ✅ A **Plasma Display Panel (PDP)** is an **emissive flat-panel display** using **ionized noble gas (Ne–Xe)**.
- ✅ The "plasma" in PDP refers to the **fourth state of matter** — partially ionized gas with free electrons and ions.
- ✅ The **physical process** is: **Electrical excitation → Plasma discharge → UV photon emission → Phosphor fluorescence → Visible light.**
- ✅ **UV emission wavelength** from Xe atoms is ~ **147 nm** (vacuum UV).
- ✅ The three phosphors are: **red** $(\text{Y,Gd})\text{BO}_3:\text{Eu}^{3+}$, **green** $\text{Zn}_2\text{SiO}_4:\text{Mn}^{2+}$, **blue** $\text{BaMgAl}_{10}\text{O}_{17}:\text{Eu}^{2+}$.
- ✅ The **MgO protective layer** on the front dielectric **lowers the discharge voltage** and **prevents sputtering damage**.
- ✅ **ADS = Address-Display Separation** — divides each frame into **8 subfields** with binary weights $1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128$.
- ✅ **Number of gray levels:** $L = 2^n = 256$ per primary color for $n=8$ subfields.
- ✅ **Total true-color depth:** $C = 2^{3n} = 2^{24} = 16,777,216$ colors.
- ✅ **Frame budget at 60 Hz:** $T_f = 16.67$ ms — the naïve 8-subfield ADS scheme exceeds this, so **dual-scan** is used in practice.
- ✅ **Paschen's Law:** $V_b$ is a U-shaped function of $p \cdot d$, with a minimum near $p \cdot d \approx 1.0$ Torr·cm for Ne–Xe.
- ✅ **Typical sustain voltage:** $180$–$250$ V; **address voltage:** $\sim 70$ V.
- ✅ **PDP advantages:** wide viewing angle, fast response, deep blacks, large screen sizes.
- ✅ **PDP disadvantages:** high power consumption, screen burn-in, heavier than LCD.
- ✅ **PDP is emissive** (no backlight); **LCD is non-emissive** (needs backlight). **This is the most-tested comparison point.**
- ✅ **Response time of PDP:** < 1 µs — one of the **fastest** among all display technologies.
- ✅ **Contrast ratio of PDP:** up to **30,000:1** native; **infinity:1** dynamic.
- ✅ **Lifespan of PDP:** ~ **30,000 hours** (shorter than LCD's 60,000 h).
- ✅ **Applications:** home cinema, public information displays, medical imaging, military/avionics.
- ✅ **Comparison with CRT:** PDP is thinner, lighter, and can be made much larger, but CRT has **lower power consumption** and **no burn-in**.
- ✅ **Comparison with OLED:** PDP is cheaper at large sizes, but OLED has **better blacks, lower power, no burn-in, and slimmer profile** — which is why PDPs have been **discontinued for consumer use since ~ 2014**.
- ✅ **Exam one-liner to memorize:** *"A PDP is an emissive display that uses plasma-state noble gas to emit UV, which excites RGB phosphors to produce visible light."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: OLED
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT414 - COMPUTER GRAPHICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** OLED

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Organic Light Emitting Diode (OLED)** is a solid-state display and lighting device in which an emissive electroluminescent layer is composed of an organic compound film that emits light in response to an electric current. In the context of the **PEITT414 – Computer Graphics** syllabus, OLED is classified as a *flat-panel emissive display technology* (alongside Plasma and FED) as opposed to *non-emissive/transmissive* technologies (such as LCD).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 – Display Devices):**
> OLED belongs to the **Self-Emissive Display Category**. Each pixel contains organic semiconductors (typically small molecules or polymers) sandwiched between two electrodes. When current flows, electrons and holes recombine in the emissive layer, releasing energy as **photons (light)** directly — no backlight is required.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **glowing sandwich**:
- The **two slices of bread** are the electrodes (anode on top, cathode at the bottom).
- The **fillings** are ultra-thin organic layers that emit different colors of light.
- The moment a small current passes through the sandwich, the fillings *illuminate themselves* — like a glow-stick that turns on the instant electricity touches it.

Unlike an **LCD** (which is like a stained-glass window needing sunlight to show its colors), an OLED is like a *window that generates its own light from within*. That is precisely why OLED displays deliver **true blacks, infinite contrast, and curved/flexible form factors** — features exploited heavily in modern smartphones, televisions, and VR headsets.

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities & Industry Metrics

The following standard parameters are used by the display industry and frequently appear in KTU questions:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Emissive layer thickness | $d$ | $100 - 200 \ \text{nm}$ |
| Operating voltage (drive) | $V$ | $2 - 10 \ \text{V (DC)}$ |
| Luminance | $L$ | $100 - 1000 \ \text{cd/m}^2$ |
| Luminous efficacy | $\eta$ | $50 - 90 \ \text{lm/W}$ |
| Color gamut | $NTSC\%$ | $>100\%$ (DCI-P3) |
| Refresh rate | $f$ | $60 - 240 \ \text{Hz}$ |
| Viewing angle | $\theta$ | $\approx 180^\circ$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The **light output** in an OLED is governed by the electroluminescence equation:
>
> $$L = \frac{\Phi_v}{A_s}$$
>
> where $L$ is luminance (cd/m²), $\Phi_v$ is luminous flux (lm), and $A_s$ is the projected emitting area (m²).

## 1.4 Classification of OLEDs (KTU Taxonomy)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners expect the student to know the **two structural variants** and the **two driving variants**:

**By Molecular Structure:**
- **SM-OLED** — Small Molecule OLED (vacuum-deposited, used in TVs)
- **PLED / P-OLED** — Polymer OLED (solution-processed, used in displays)

**By Driving Scheme:**
- **PMOLED** — Passive Matrix OLED (line-addressed)
- **AMOLED** — Active Matrix OLED (each pixel has a TFT driver)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Architecture of an OLED

An OLED is a multi-layer thin-film structure. From top to bottom:

1. **Substrate** — Glass or flexible plastic (PET, PI). Carries the entire device.
2. **Anode** — Transparent conductor, typically **Indium Tin Oxide (ITO)**. High work function.
3. **Hole Injection Layer (HIL)** — Facilitates hole injection from the anode (e.g., PEDOT:PSS, CuPc).
4. **Hole Transport Layer (HTL)** — Transports holes toward the emissive layer (e.g., NPB, TPD).
5. **Emissive Layer (EML)** — The heart of the device. Contains dopant emitters (e.g., Ir(ppy)₃, DCJTB).
6. **Electron Transport Layer (ETL)** — Transports electrons (e.g., Alq₃, TPBi).
7. **Electron Injection Layer (EIL)** — Facilitates electron injection from the cathode (e.g., LiF, CsF).
8. **Cathode** — Low work-function metal, often **Al / Mg:Ag alloy** (and may be reflective).

> [!NOTE]
> The acronym **HIL–HTL–EML–ETL–EIL** is a standard KTU short-answer question. Memorize the order: **H**oles flow in first, **E**mission happens in the middle, **E**lectrons flow in last.

## 2.2 Operational Physics — Step-by-Step

When a forward DC bias is applied across the anode and cathode:

- **Step 1 — Charge Injection:** Holes are injected from the ITO anode into the HOMO of the HTL; electrons are injected from the low-work-function cathode into the LUMO of the ETL.
- **Step 2 — Charge Transport:** Holes migrate through the HTL; electrons migrate through the ETL. They are driven by the internal electric field $E$ produced by the applied voltage $V$.
- **Step 3 —Exciton Formation:** When an electron and a hole meet inside the EML, they form a bound electron–hole pair known as an **exciton** (energy $\approx E_g$).
- **Step 4 — Radiative Recombination:** The exciton relaxes to the ground state. Statistically:
  - **25%** are **singlet** excitons (spin-allowed) → **fluorescence** (fast, $\sim$ns).
  - **75%** are **triplet** excitons (spin-forbidden) → harvested via **phosphorescence** (heavy-metal dopants like Iridium) for $\sim 100\%$ internal efficiency.
- **Step 5 — Photon Ejection:** The emitted photons either escape through the transparent ITO anode or get waveguided sideways — escape cone is governed by total internal reflection.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Luminance | $L = \dfrac{\Phi_v}{A_s}$ | cd/m² |
| Luminous Efficacy | $\eta = \dfrac{\Phi_v}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{\Phi_v}{V \cdot I}$ | lm/W |
| Quantum Efficiency (Internal) | $\eta_{int} = \eta_{S/T} \cdot \eta_{PL} \cdot \gamma$ | product of spin factor, PL yield, charge balance |
| External Quantum Efficiency | $\eta_{ext} = \eta_{int} \cdot \eta_{out}$ | $\eta_{out} \approx 1/(2n^2)$ |
| Photon Energy | $E_{ph} = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | $hc \approx 1240 \ \text{eV·nm}$ |
| Bandgap–Wavelength | $\lambda = \dfrac{1240}{E_g(\text{eV})} \ \text{nm}$ | for color engineering |
| Contrast Ratio | $CR = \dfrac{L_{white}}{L_{black}}$ | OLED achieves $L_{black} \to 0$ |
| Refresh Rate | $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{\tau_{response}}$ | OLED $\tau \approx \mu s$ |
| Viewing Angle Uniformity | $\Delta L / L < 5\%$ at $\theta = 80^\circ$ | LCD drops $>50\%$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Conversion Logic for KTU numericals:**
> Wavelength $\lambda$ (in nm) to photon energy $E_{ph}$ (in eV):
>
> $$E_{ph}(\text{eV}) = \frac{1240}{\lambda(\text{nm})}$$
>
> For example, a **red** emitter at $\lambda = 620$ nm requires a bandgap:
>
> $$E_g = \frac{1240}{620} \approx 2.00 \ \text{eV}$$

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Displays:** Smartphones (Samsung AMOLED, Apple Retina XDR), TVs (LG OLED evo), VR/AR microdisplays.
- **Lighting:** OLED panels by LG, OSRAM — surface-emitting soft lighting with no glare.
- **Wearable & Foldable:** Flexible OLEDs on PI substrate enable rollable phones.
- **Automotive HUDs:** Transparent OLED windshields.
- **Biomedical:** OLED-based pulse oximeters and phototherapy patches.

> [!NOTE]
> Compared to LCD, OLED eliminates the **backlight, color filter loss, and liquid crystal alignment** steps. Power savings of 30–60% are typical at typical content (since dark pixels consume **zero power**).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Outcoupling Efficiency Limit

A fundamental constraint of OLEDs is that only a fraction of generated photons can escape the device. The remaining photons are trapped by **total internal reflection** at the ITO–air interface.

**Step 1:** By Snell's law at the ITO–air boundary, total internal reflection occurs when:

$$\sin\theta_c = \frac{n_{air}}{n_{org}} = \frac{1}{n}$$

where $n$ is the refractive index of the organic stack ($\approx 1.7$ typical).

**Step 2:** The escape cone solid angle in a medium of refractive index $n$ is:

$$\Omega = 2\pi (1 - \cos\theta_c)$$

**Step 3:** Substituting $\cos\theta_c = \sqrt{1 - \sin^2\theta_c} = \sqrt{1 - 1/n^2}$:

$$\Omega = 2\pi \left(1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{1}{n^2}}\right)$$

**Step 4:** The total solid angle in a full sphere is $4\pi$. Therefore the **outcoupling efficiency** is:

$$\eta_{out} = \frac{\Omega}{4\pi} = \frac{1}{2}\left(1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{1}{n^2}}\right)$$

**Step 5:** For a typical organic stack with $n = 1.7$:

$$\eta_{out} = \frac{1}{2}\left(1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{1}{1.7^2}}\right)$$

$$= \frac{1}{2}\left(1 - \sqrt{1 - 0.346}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\left(1 - \sqrt{0.654}\right)$$

$$= \frac{1}{2}\left(1 - 0.809\right) = \frac{1}{2}(0.191) = 0.0955$$

**Conclusion:**

$$\boxed{\eta_{out} \approx 9.55\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** This is the *classical Lambertian outcoupling limit*. The famous $1/(2n^2)$ approximation gives $\eta_{out} \approx 1/(2 \cdot 1.7^2) \approx 0.173$ (17.3%) which overestimates; the exact integration above (9.55%) is the **rigorous KTU answer**.

**Step 6:** Hence the maximum **external quantum efficiency (EQE)** of a single-layer OLED, assuming 100% internal efficiency, is:

$$\eta_{EQE, max} = \eta_{int} \cdot \eta_{out} = 1.0 \cdot 0.0955 = 9.55\%$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pixel Addressing Time in PMOLED

In a **Passive Matrix OLED (PMOLED)**, pixels are addressed **row by row** by scanning. If the display has $N$ rows, each row is illuminated for only a fraction of the frame time.

**Step 1:** Total frame time:

$$T_{frame} = \frac{1}{f_{refresh}}$$

**Step 2:** Row addressing time (row duty cycle):

$$T_{row} = \frac{T_{frame}}{N} = \frac{1}{f_{refresh} \cdot N}$$

**Step 3:** To maintain a perceived luminance $L_{avg}$, the instantaneous luminance during row-on time must be amplified:

$$L_{peak} = N \cdot L_{avg}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting values for a $1080$-row (Full HD) PMOLED at $f_{refresh} = 60$ Hz:

$$T_{row} = \frac{1}{60 \cdot 1080} \approx 15.4 \ \mu s$$

$$L_{peak} = 1080 \cdot L_{avg}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** A common mistake is computing $L_{peak} = N / 2 \cdot L_{avg}$. The correct factor is $N$, not $N/2$, because the *off-time* is $(N-1)T_{row}$. Always use the full $N$ multiplier unless an active blanking period is specified.

**Step 5:** This $L_{peak}$ scaling is the reason **AMOLED is preferred for high-resolution displays** — each pixel has its own TFT capacitor and is held in the on-state for the full frame, eliminating the $L_{peak} = N \cdot L_{avg}$ burden.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Color Emission from Bandgap

To obtain a desired emission peak $\lambda$, the emissive-layer material must be engineered with a bandgap:

$$E_g = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$

**Step 1:** Substitute $hc = 1240$ eV·nm.

**Step 2:** For RGB primaries:

| Color | Target $\lambda$ (nm) | Required $E_g$ (eV) |
|---|---|---|
| Red | 620 | 2.00 |
| Green | 530 | 2.34 |
| Blue | 470 | 2.64 |

**Step 3:** As an example, the derivation for a green pixel at $\lambda = 530$ nm:

$$E_g = \frac{1240 \ \text{eV·nm}}{530 \ \text{nm}} = 2.3396 \ \text{eV}$$

$$\boxed{E_g \approx 2.34 \ \text{eV (green OLED)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Favourite Numerical:**
> A blue OLED material emits at 460 nm. Its bandgap is:
>
> $$E_g = \frac{1240}{460} = 2.696 \ \text{eV} \approx 2.70 \ \text{eV}$$
>
> The **higher the bandgap, the harder it is to find stable organic materials** — this is why **blue OLEDs have the shortest operational lifetime**, a common KTU essay question.

---

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix — OLED vs LCD vs Plasma

| Property | OLED | LCD (IPS) | Plasma |
|---|---|---|---|
| Backlight | None (self-emissive) | Required (LED/CCFL) | None (gas discharge) |
| Contrast Ratio | $\infty:1$ (true black) | $1000:1$ – $1500:1$ | $3000:1$ |
| Viewing Angle | $\approx 180^\circ$ | $178^\circ$ typical | $170^\circ$ |
| Response Time | $\mu s$ | $1-5$ ms | $\mu s$ |
| Thickness | $1-3$ mm | $3-5$ mm | $>30$ mm |
| Power (black screen) | $\approx 0$ | High (backlight ON) | High |
| Lifespan (Blue) | $10{,}000-20{,}000$ hr | $>50{,}000$ hr | $>60{,}000$ hr |
| Burn-in Risk | Mild | None | High |
| Flexibility | Yes (PI substrate) | No | No |
| Manufacturing Cost | High | Low | High |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Multi-Layer Architecture of an OLED

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OLED_STACK["OLED DEVICE CROSS-SECTION"]
        direction TB
        A1["SUBSTRATE - Glass or PI"]
        A2["ANODE - ITO Transparent Conductor"]
        A3["HIL - Hole Injection Layer PEDOT:PSS"]
        A4["HTL - Hole Transport Layer NPB"]
        A5["EML - Emissive Layer Dopant Iridium complex"]
        A6["ETL - Electron Transport Layer Alq3"]
        A7["EIL - Electron Injection Layer LiF"]
        A8["CATHODE - Al or MgAg Reflective"]
    end
    HOLE["HOLES plus"] --> A3
    ELECT["ELECTRONS minus"] --> A7
    A5 --> PHOTON["PHOTON OUTPUT - Visible Light"]
```

## 4.2 Operational Flow — Electroluminescence Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["DC Bias Applied"] --> S2["Hole Injection from Anode"]
    S2 --> S3["Electron Injection from Cathode"]
    S3 --> S4["Charge Transport HTL and ETL"]
    S4 --> S5["Exciton Formation in EML"]
    S5 --> S6{"Spin State"}
    S6 -- Singlet 25 percent --> S7["Fluorescence - Fast ns"]
    S6 -- Triplet 75 percent --> S8["Phosphorescence - Heavy Metal Dopant"]
    S7 --> S9["Photon Emission"]
    S8 --> S9
    S9 --> S10["Outcoupling through ITO"]
```

## 4.3 PMOLED vs AMOLED — Driving Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PMO["PMOLED - Passive Matrix"]
        direction TB
        R1["Row Drivers Sequential Scan"] --> R2["One Row Active at a Time"]
        R2 --> R3["Peak L equals N times L avg"]
        R3 --> R4["Limited to Small Displays Below 3 inch"]
    end
    subgraph AMO["AMOLED - Active Matrix"]
        direction TB
        T1["TFT Backplane per Pixel"] --> T2["Capacitor Holds Charge for Full Frame"]
        T2 --> T3["L peak equals L avg"]
        T3 --> T4["Used in Smartphones TVs Monitors"]
    end
    PMO -- Comparison --> AMO
```

## 4.4 Functional Block Architecture of an OLED Display System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    GPU["GPU Frame Buffer"] --> CTRL["Timing Controller TCON"]
    CTRL --> SDA["Source Driver Column Data"]
    CTRL --> GDA["Gate Driver Row Select"]
    SDA --> TFT["TFT Backplane AMOLED"]
    GDA --> TFT
    TFT --> OLED["OLED Pixel Array RGB"]
    OLED --> OPT["Optical Stack Polarizer Encapsulation"]
    OPT --> OUT["Display Output to Viewer"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this Mermaid representation?**
> The actual physical cross-section of a 7-layer OLED cannot be rendered as a true photograph in text. The flowchart above therefore documents the *functional layering*, charge-injection, and light-emission pipeline — the **KTU-acceptable schematic substitution**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define OLED. Why is it called a self-emissive display?**

**Model Answer:**
OLED stands for **Organic Light Emitting Diode**. It is a solid-state emissive display in which a thin film of organic semiconductor (carbon-based) emits light when a current passes through it. It is termed *self-emissive* because each pixel generates its own light directly via electroluminescence, eliminating the need for a backlight unit — a key contrast to LCD technology.

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List the functional layers of a standard OLED stack in order.**

**Model Answer (Top-to-Bottom):**
1. Substrate
2. Anode (ITO)
3. Hole Injection Layer (HIL)
4. Hole Transport Layer (HTL)
5. Emissive Layer (EML)
6. Electron Transport Layer (ETL)
7. Electron Injection Layer (EIL)
8. Cathode (Al / Mg:Ag)

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**(a)** Explain the working principle of an OLED with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of HIL, HTL, EML, ETL, and EIL layers. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

An OLED is a current-driven, multi-layer organic device that emits light through the process of **electroluminescence**. The structure comprises eight functional layers: substrate, anode, HIL, HTL, EML, ETL, EIL, and cathode.

- The **anode**, usually ITO, is transparent and injects **holes** (positive carriers) into the organic stack.
- The **HIL (Hole Injection Layer)** reduces the energy barrier between ITO and HTL by aligning work functions (using materials like PEDOT:PSS or CuPc). It improves hole injection efficiency.
- The **HTL (Hole Transport Layer)** has a high hole mobility and an appropriate HOMO level (e.g., NPB, TPD). It transports holes into the EML.
- The **EML (Emissive Layer)** is the active region where holes and electrons recombine to form excitons. Phosphorescent dopants (e.g., Ir(ppy)₃) harvest both singlet and triplet states, achieving near-100% internal quantum efficiency.
- The **ETL (Electron Transport Layer)** (e.g., Alq₃, TPBi) transports electrons from the cathode to the EML.
- The **EIL (Electron Injection Layer)** (e.g., LiF, CsF) reduces the cathode–ETL energy barrier.
- The **cathode** (Al or Mg:Ag) injects electrons and is typically reflective.

When a forward DC voltage ($2$–$10$ V) is applied, holes and electrons migrate toward the EML, recombine, and produce photons whose wavelength is governed by the bandgap of the emissive material: $\lambda = 1240 / E_g$ nm. The standard block diagram is provided in **Section 4.1**.

**(b)** Differentiate between PMOLED and AMOLED. Why is AMOLED preferred for high-resolution smartphone displays? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | PMOLED | AMOLED |
|---|---|---|
| Driving | Row-by-row passive scan | TFT active backplane per pixel |
| Pixel Memory | None (latches off when not addressed) | Storage capacitor (holds state for full frame) |
| Peak Luminance | $L_{peak} = N \cdot L_{avg}$ | $L_{peak} = L_{avg}$ |
| Resolution Limit | Small displays only | Large / high-resolution |
| Power Efficiency | Low for large panels | High |
| Cost | Low for small displays | Higher, but scalable |

**Why AMOLED is preferred for smartphones:**
1. **High Resolution** — Each pixel has its own TFT switch, eliminating the row-time bottleneck. A Full HD+ smartphone with $\sim 2400$ rows would need a $L_{peak} = 2400 \cdot L_{avg}$ in PMOLED, which is impractical and degrades lifetime.
2. **Lower Power** — Capacitor holds the pixel state, so the OLED is driven with a constant current, not a pulse. Power is reduced by $30$–$60\%$ at typical mixed content.
3. **Uniform Brightness** — No row-by-row brightness pulsing, hence no flicker.
4. **Scalability** — AMOLED fabs use LTPS or IGZO backplanes that scale to large Gen-8 substrates for TVs.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Valuation Warning:** Students frequently confuse **PMOLED with AMOLED** by writing *"AMOLED uses passive scanning"* — this is incorrect. **A**ctive means each pixel has an **A**ctive driver (TFT), whereas **P**assive means rows are simply scanned without per-pixel memory. Loss: 2 marks. Also avoid writing *"OLED cannot be made flexible"* — flexible OLEDs are a *primary* advantage, not a limitation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Compare OLED with LCD and Plasma displays. Highlight why OLED is preferred for VR headsets and curved TVs. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | OLED | LCD | Plasma |
|---|---|---|---|
| Backlight | Not required | Required | Not required |
| Contrast | $\infty : 1$ | $1000:1$ | $3000:1$ |
| Response Time | $\mu s$ | $1$–$5$ ms | $\mu s$ |
| Viewing Angle | $\approx 180^\circ$ | Limited | Limited |
| Form Factor | Flexible / curved | Rigid | Rigid, thick |
| Power @ Black | $\approx 0$ | High | High |
| Lifespan | Lower for blue | Highest | High |

**Why OLED is preferred for VR headsets:**
1. **Sub-millisecond response** eliminates motion blur, critical at 90–120 Hz refresh.
2. **Self-emissive pixels** allow extreme pixel density (e.g., 3000+ PPI in micro-OLEDs) without backlight bleeding.
3. **Lighter and thinner** — fewer optical stack elements.
4. **True blacks** prevent the "screen door effect" being masked by backlight glow.

**Why OLED is preferred for curved TVs:**
1. OLEDs can be deposited on flexible **PI (polyimide)** substrates — LCDs require rigid glass TFT arrays.
2. Self-emissive pixels give uniform brightness across the curve, whereas edge-lit LCDs show uneven luminance.
