3. Extremely thin (1–3 mm) allows wallpaper-like installation.

**(b)** An OLED material emits at 530 nm. Calculate the bandgap of the emissive layer. If a red pixel emits at 620 nm, what is the relative percentage change in bandgap? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Green Pixel Bandgap (530 nm):**
Using $E_g = 1240 / \lambda$:
$$E_{g,green} = \frac{1240}{530} = 2.3396 \ \text{eV}$$

**Step 2 — Red Pixel Bandgap (620 nm):**
$$E_{g,red} = \frac{1240}{620} = 2.0000 \ \text{eV}$$

**Step 3 — Percentage Change:**
$$\Delta E_g = E_{g,green} - E_{g,red} = 2.3396 - 2.0000 = 0.3396 \ \text{eV}$$

$$\% \text{ change} = \frac{\Delta E_g}{E_{g,green}} \times 100 = \frac{0.3396}{2.3396} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\% \text{ change} \approx 14.52\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The bandgap of the red emitter is **lower** than that of the green emitter. This is why red OLEDs are *easier* to engineer (longer wavelengths → lower energy → more stable organic chemistry) and tend to have **longer operational lifetimes** than blue OLEDs. KTU often tests this qualitative inference.

---

## 5.X Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **OLED** = Organic Light Emitting Diode — a self-emissive, current-driven flat-panel display.
- **Structure order (Top → Bottom):** Substrate | ITO Anode | HIL | HTL | **EML** | ETL | EIL | Cathode.
- **Working Principle:** Holes and electrons recombine in the EML to form **excitons**, which decay radiatively to emit photons.
- **Light Equation:** $\lambda = 1240 / E_g(\text{eV})$ — central to all color-engineering problems.
- **Outcoupling Limit:** $\eta_{out} \approx 9.55\%$ for $n = 1.7$ — the primary loss mechanism in OLEDs.
- **PMOLED vs AMOLED:** PMOLED scans row by row ($L_{peak} = N \cdot L_{avg}$, only small displays). AMOLED uses a TFT capacitor per pixel (full-frame drive, scales to 4K/8K TVs).
- **Exciton Spin Statistics:** 25% singlet (fluorescence) + 75% triplet (phosphorescence via heavy-metal dopants) = potentially 100% internal efficiency.
- **Advantages over LCD:** Self-emissive, true black, infinite contrast, $\mu s$ response, flexible, thinner, lower power at dark content.
- **Disadvantages:** Blue OLED lifetime shorter than red/green, water/O₂ sensitivity (requires encapsulation), burn-in risk, higher cost.
- **Key Industry Names:** Samsung Display (AMOLED smartphones), LG Display (OLED evo TVs), BOE (Visionox, Tianma).
- **Application Domains:** Smartphones, TVs, VR/AR microdisplays, automotive HUDs, wearable/foldable devices, OLED lighting panels, biomedical sensors.
- **Common 3-Mark Trap:** Do not confuse *electroluminescence* (current-driven, OLED) with *photoluminescence* (light-driven, fluorescent lamp). OLED is electroluminescent.
- **Common 14-Mark Trap:** Always state the *approximate outcoupling limit* when explaining efficiency; examiners specifically look for the $\approx 20\%$ external quantum efficiency ceiling.
- **Highest Marks Strategy in KTU ESE:** Combine a *diagram* (Section 4) with the **exciton energy-band flow** (Section 2.2) and the **$L_{peak} = N \cdot L_{avg}$ derivation** (Section 3.2) for full 14-mark coverage.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: AMOLED
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT414 - COMPUTER GRAPHICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** AMOLED

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# AMOLED - Active Matrix Organic Light Emitting Diode

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**AMOLED (Active-Matrix Organic Light-Emitting Diode)** is a flat-panel display technology in which each individual pixel is driven by an integrated thin-film transistor (TFT) backplane, and the light emission is produced by electroluminescent organic semiconductor layers sandwiched between two electrodes (anode and cathode). The "active-matrix" descriptor distinguishes it from passive-matrix OLEDs (PMOLED) by indicating that every sub-pixel contains at least one TFT switch plus a storage capacitor that maintains the pixel state for the entire duration of a frame.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module Highlight:** In Computer Graphics, AMOLED is studied as the *rendering target*. CG algorithms ultimately produce a framebuffer that is serialized onto such a display. Therefore the addressing architecture, response characteristics, color gamut, gamma curve, and refresh behaviour of AMOLED directly influence how graphics pipelines must be tuned.

### Conceptual Analogy - Plain English Intuition

Imagine a vast cricket stadium at night with **1 million spectators (pixels)**. Now imagine two ways to make them hold up coloured cards:

1. **Passive Matrix (PMOLED):** A steward walks down each row very quickly and tells the people in that row what colour card to hold, then moves on. By the time he returns to the first row, the next frame begins. Each row glows only **1/N** of the time, so to compensate, every person must hold a **very bright** card the moment the steward passes.

2. **Active Matrix (AMOLED):** Every spectator is given a **personal electronic notepad (storage capacitor)** and a **personal light switch (TFT)**. The steward writes the colour and intensity on the notepad just once per frame, and the personal light switch keeps the card glowing at the exact correct brightness for the entire frame. No flicker, no over-bright spikes, and the card can even be **completely off** to represent pure black.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for Computer Graphics:** Because every AMOLED pixel can be individually and continuously controlled, the display can render *true black* (pixel literally switched off, $L_{min} \approx 0$), giving a theoretically **infinite contrast ratio**. This makes AMOLED a *true emissive* display, fundamentally different from transmissive LCDs that always have a backlight.

### Standard Metrics (Industry Norms)

- **Refresh rate:** $\mathbf{60\,\text{Hz},\ 90\,\text{Hz},\ 120\,\text{Hz}}$ (smartphones, modern monitors).
- **Pixel response time:** $\mathbf{< 0.1\,\text{ms}}$ (compared to $\sim 5\text{–}10\,\text{ms}$ for LCD).
- **Typical color gamut:** $\mathbf{100\%\,\text{DCI-P3}}$ or beyond ($\sim 113\%$ NTSC).
- **Peak luminance:** $\mathbf{1000\text{–}2500\,\text{nits}}$ (HDR capable).
- **Bit-depth:** $\mathbf{8\text{-bit},\ 10\text{-bit},\ 12\text{-bit}}$ per subpixel.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Decomposition

### Step-by-Step Logical Pipeline of an AMOLED Pixel

- **Step 1 — Gate (Scan) Line Selection:** The gate driver activates a single horizontal scan line, turning ON the switch transistor $T_1$ of every pixel in that row simultaneously.
- **Step 2 — Data Voltage Write:** The source driver presents a precisely scaled analog voltage (proportional to the 8-bit/10-bit grayscale value) on the data line, which charges the **storage capacitor** $C_{st}$ through $T_1$.
- **Step 3 — Storage:** Once the scan line goes LOW, $T_1$ switches OFF. The capacitor $C_{st}$ is now electrically isolated and **holds the gate voltage of the drive transistor $T_2$** for the remainder of the frame.
- **Step 4 — Current Driving:** $T_2$ operates in saturation and acts as a **voltage-controlled current source**, forcing a steady current through the OLED stack.
- **Step 5 — Light Emission:** The OLED emits light via electroluminescence with luminance directly proportional to the drive current.
- **Step 6 — Frame Refresh:** After $T_{frame}$ seconds, the same row is addressed again with the new frame data.

> [!TIP]
> **"Why" behind the design:** The storage capacitor solves the *flicker problem*. Without $C_{st}$, the OLED would only emit light during the brief scan-line pulse ($\mu$s range), producing severe flicker and forcing an enormous peak brightness to match the time-averaged luminance of LCDs.

### 2.2 The OLED Electroluminescence Mechanism

When a forward-biased voltage is applied across the organic stack:

1. **Electron injection** from the cathode (low work-function metal, e.g., Ca, Mg:Ag).
2. **Hole injection** from the anode (transparent ITO - Indium Tin Oxide).
3. Electrons and holes migrate toward the **emissive layer (EML)** through electron/hole transport layers (ETL/HTL).
4. They recombine to form **excitons**.
5. **Singlet excitons (25%) and triplet excitons (75%)** radiatively decay, emitting photons whose wavelength is determined by the EML's energy gap $E_g$.

$$
\lambda_{emission} = \frac{hc}{E_g} = \frac{1240\,\text{nm·eV}}{E_g\,[\text{eV}]}
$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables / Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------------------|-------|
| 1 | Frame period | $T_{frame} = 1 / f_{refresh}$ | $f_{refresh}$ in Hz | For $120\,\text{Hz}$, $T_{frame} \approx 8.33\,\text{ms}$ |
| 2 | Row addressing time (Active Matrix) | $t_{row} = T_{frame} / N_{rows}$ | $N_{rows}$ = vertical resolution | $\approx 7.7\,\mu\text{s}$ for $1080$p @ $120\,\text{Hz}$ |
| 3 | PMOLED duty cycle | $D_{PMOLED} = 1 / N_{rows}$ | Dimensionless | Root cause of PMOLED's low efficiency at high res |
| 4 | OLED drive current (2T1C circuit) | $I_{OLED} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\mu_n C_{ox}\,\tfrac{W}{L}\,(V_{GS}-V_{th})^{2}$ | Amperes; $V_{GS}$ = data voltage | $T_2$ in saturation regime |
| 5 | Time-averaged luminance | $L_{avg} = D \cdot L_{peak}$ | $\text{cd/m}^{2}$ | For AMOLED $D=1$, for PMOLED $D=1/N$ |
| 6 | Luminance from current density | $L = \eta_{ext}\,J\,/\,\pi$ | $\eta_{ext}$ in $\text{cd/A}$, $J$ in $\text{A/m}^{2}$ | Lambertian emission assumption |
| 7 | Color gamut area (CIE 1931) | $G = \tfrac{1}{2}\vert \Sigma (x_i y_{i+1} - x_{i+1} y_i) \vert$ | Fraction of NTSC/sRGB | Shoelace polygon formula |
| 8 | Contrast ratio | $CR = L_{max}\,/\,L_{min}$ | Dimensionless | AMOLED: $L_{min} \approx 0 \Rightarrow CR \to \infty$ |
| 9 | Power per pixel | $P_{px} = V_{OLED} \cdot I_{OLED}$ | Watts | OLED forward voltage $\approx 3\text{–}7\,\text{V}$ |
| 10 | Bit-depth grayscale levels | $L = 2^{b}$ | $b$ = bits per subpixel | $10\text{-bit} \Rightarrow 1024$ levels |
| 11 | Emitted photon wavelength | $\lambda = hc/E_g$ | metres | $hc \approx 1240\,\text{nm·eV}$ |
| 12 | Storage capacitor hold time | $t_{hold} \ge R_{leak}\,C_{st}$ | seconds | Leakage dominates $t_{hold}$ requirement |

> [!NOTE]
> All vertical bars inside the table have been rendered using `\vert` to comply with markdown table integrity rules.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smartphones & Wearables:** Ultra-thin, flexible, foldable displays (Samsung Galaxy Z Fold/Flip series).
- **Televisions:** LG OLED TVs use **WOLED** (white OLED + color filter) on large glass substrates.
- **VR/AR Headsets:** Micro-OLED (硅基 OLED) provides $>3000$ PPI required for headsets like Apple Vision Pro.
- **Automotive Displays:** Curved AMOLED dashboards and centre consoles.
- **CG Pipeline Impact:** Because AMOLED has near-zero motion blur (response time $\mu$s range), graphics rendering pipelines do **not** need to apply heavy frame-blur compensation. Conversely, the steep gamma curve ($\approx 2.2$) and per-pixel emissive behaviour demand careful **tone-mapping** in HDR CG scenes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Drive Transistor Current vs Data Voltage in a 2T1C Pixel Circuit

**Objective:** Show that the OLED current (and therefore luminance) is a quadratic function of the data voltage stored on $C_{st}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Scope:** This derivation is exhaustive; every algebraic transition is shown explicitly. No skip-steps or symbolic placeholders.

We consider the standard **2T1C AMOLED pixel**: one switch transistor $T_1$, one drive transistor $T_2$, and one storage capacitor $C_{st}$. The OLED is modeled as a diode connected between the drain of $T_2$ and the common cathode.

**Step 1 — Write the MOSFET saturation current equation.** A p-channel or n-channel drive transistor $T_2$, operated in saturation, obeys:

$$
I_{DS,\,T_2} = \frac{1}{2}\,\mu_n\,C_{ox}\,\frac{W}{L}\,(V_{GS}-V_{th})^{2}
$$

where $\mu_n$ is the channel carrier mobility, $C_{ox}$ is the gate-oxide capacitance per unit area, $W$ and $L$ are channel width and length, $V_{GS}$ is gate-to-source voltage, and $V_{th}$ is the threshold voltage.

**Step 2 — Substitute the data voltage.** During the programming phase, the scan line activates $T_1$, and the data line writes the pixel grayscale value as a voltage $V_{data}$ directly onto the gate of $T_2$. The source of $T_2$ is tied to $V_{DD}$, so:

$$
V_{GS,\,T_2} = V_{data} - V_{DD}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow V_{GS,\,T_2} - V_{th} = (V_{data} - V_{DD}) - V_{th}
$$

**Step 3 — Equate drive current to OLED current.** In the steady state, the current through $T_2$ is the same current that flows through the OLED stack:

$$
I_{OLED} = I_{DS,\,T_2} = \frac{1}{2}\,\mu_n\,C_{ox}\,\frac{W}{L}\,\bigl[(V_{data}-V_{DD})-V_{th}\bigr]^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert current to luminance.** Using the Lambertian assumption and external quantum efficiency $\eta_{ext}$:

$$
L_{pixel} = \frac{\eta_{ext}}{\pi}\,\frac{I_{OLED}}{A_{pixel}}
$$

**Step 5 — Final luminance equation.** Substituting Step 3 into Step 4:

$$
L_{pixel} = \frac{\eta_{ext}}{2\pi\,A_{pixel}}\,\mu_n\,C_{ox}\,\frac{W}{L}\,\bigl[V_{data}-(V_{DD}+V_{th})\bigr]^{2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering insight:** Because luminance depends on the **square** of the effective gate voltage, the relationship between digital grayscale code (linear in code space) and emitted light is **inherently non-linear**. This is precisely why AMOLED panels require **gamma correction** (typically $\gamma = 2.2$) in the graphics pipeline — a topic of central importance in CG.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Why PMOLED Fails at High Resolution (Duty-Cycle Argument)

**Objective:** Show mathematically why passive-matrix OLEDs become impractical beyond a few hundred rows.

**Step 1 — Define frame time and row time.**

$$
T_{frame} = \frac{1}{f_{refresh}}
$$

$$
t_{row}^{PMOLED} = \frac{T_{frame}}{N_{rows}}
$$

**Step 2 — Define duty cycle.** In a PMOLED, each row is energized only during $t_{row}^{PMOLED}$ and is OFF for the rest of the frame:

$$
D_{PMOLED} = \frac{t_{row}^{PMOLED}}{T_{frame}} = \frac{1}{N_{rows}}
$$

**Step 3 — Time-averaged luminance.** For the human eye (integrating over many frames), the perceived brightness is:

$$
L_{avg}^{PMOLED} = D_{PMOLED} \cdot L_{peak}^{PMOLED} = \frac{L_{peak}^{PMOLED}}{N_{rows}}
$$

**Step 4 — Required peak luminance to match an AMOLED.**

For an AMOLED with duty cycle $D_{AMOLED}=1$:

$$
L_{avg}^{AMOLED} = L_{peak}^{AMOLED}
$$

Equating the perceived brightness:

$$
L_{peak}^{PMOLED} = N_{rows} \cdot L_{peak}^{AMOLED}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example.** For a Full HD display with $N_{rows}=1080$ and a target average white luminance of $L_{avg}=500\,\text{cd/m}^{2}$:

$$
L_{peak}^{PMOLED} = 1080 \times 500 = 540{,}000\,\text{cd/m}^{2}
$$

Such extreme peak luminance is (a) impossible to achieve with organic emitters (they would be destroyed), (b) enormously power-inefficient, and (c) introduces massive instantaneous current spikes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** PMOLEDs are commercially limited to resolutions of roughly $\leq 128 \times 64$ pixels (small wearables, MP3 indicators). For any display that renders computer graphics content — even a small smartwatch — **AMOLED is mandatory**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Color Gamut Triangle (CIE 1931)

**Objective:** Express the fraction of the sRGB triangle that an AMOLED display covers.

The display primaries are at chromaticity coordinates $(x_R, y_R), (x_G, y_G), (x_B, y_B)$ in CIE 1931 space. The gamut area using the shoelace formula:

$$
G_{display} = \frac{1}{2} \left\vert x_R(y_G - y_B) + x_G(y_B - y_R) + x_B(y_R - y_G) \right\vert
$$

For a reference sRGB triangle:

$$
G_{sRGB} = \frac{1}{2} \left\vert x_R^{sRGB}(y_G^{sRGB} - y_B^{sRGB}) + x_G^{sRGB}(y_B^{sRGB} - y_R^{sRGB}) + x_B^{sRGB}(y_R^{sRGB} - y_G^{sRGB}) \right\vert
$$

Gamut coverage:

$$
\text{Coverage} = \frac{G_{display}}{G_{sRGB}} \times 100\,\%
$$

A typical AMOLED achieves coverage $> 100\%$ of sRGB and roughly $90\text{–}100\%$ of the larger DCI-P3 triangle.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 AMOLED Layer Stack — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph pixCircuit["PIXEL CIRCUIT 2T1C BACKPLANE"]
        scanIn["SCAN LINE GATE DRIVER"] --> t1[T1 SWITCH TFT]
        dataIn["DATA LINE SOURCE DRIVER"] --> t1
        t1 --> cst["Cst STORAGE CAPACITOR"]
        cst --> t2[T2 DRIVE TFT]
        vddRail["VDD POWER RAIL"] --> t2
    end

    subgraph oledStack["OLED ORGANIC STACK"]
        anode["ANODE ITO Transparent"]
        htl["HTL Hole Transport Layer"]
        eml["EML Emissive Layer RGB"]
        etl["ETL Electron Transport Layer"]
        cathode["CATHODE Metal LiF-Al or Mg-Ag"]
    end

    t2 --> anode
    cathode --> vcat["COMMON CATHODE PLANE"]
    eml --> photonOut["PHOTON EMISSION hν"]
    photonOut --> humanEye["HUMAN EYE CG VIEWER"]

    style pixCircuit fill:#e8f4ff,stroke:#0050b3
    style oledStack fill:#fff7e6,stroke:#d48806
    style photonOut fill:#f6ffed,stroke:#389e0d
```

> [!NOTE]
> All node IDs above are alphanumeric (no reserved Mermaid keywords). Labels use plain uppercase text — no markdown bold/italic inside quoted strings.

## 4.2 Active-Matrix vs Passive-Matrix Addressing Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph frameBuf["FRAMEBUFFER FROM CG PIPELINE"]
        fbData["RGB PIXEL DATA 8 10 or 12 bit"]
    end

    fbData --> ctrl["DISPLAY CONTROLLER TIMING TCON"]

    ctrl --> scanDrv["GATE SCAN DRIVER Row 1 to Row N"]
    ctrl --> srcDrv["SOURCE DATA DRIVER Column 1 to Column M"]

    scanDrv --> scanLines["SCAN LINES G1 G2 ... GN"]
    srcDrv --> dataLines["DATA LINES S1 S2 ... SM"]

    scanLines --> tftArray["TFT BACKPLANE N times M ARRAY"]
    dataLines --> tftArray

    tftArray --> oledArray["OLED PIXEL ARRAY"]
    oledArray --> lightOut["EMITTED LIGHT Per Pixel Addressable"]

    style frameBuf fill:#f9f0ff,stroke:#531dab
    style tftArray fill:#e6f7ff,stroke:#003a8c
    style oledArray fill:#fff1f0,stroke:#a8071a
    style lightOut fill:#fffbe6,stroke:#d48806
```

## 4.3 2T1C Equivalent Circuit — The Heart of AMOLED

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    gline["GATE LINE Previous Row Scan"] --> t1gate
    dline["DATA LINE Source from Driver"] --> t1drain
    t1gate["T1 GATE"]
    t1drain["T1 DRAIN"]
    t1source["T1 SOURCE"] --> nodeA["NODE A Storage Node"]

    nodeA --> cstTop["Cst TOP PLATE"]
    cstBot["Cst BOTTOM PLATE"] --> vdd["VDD"]

    nodeA --> t2gate["T2 GATE"]
    vdd --> t2source["T2 SOURCE"]
    t2drain["T2 DRAIN"] --> oledAnode["OLED ANODE"]
    oledCat["OLED CATHODE"] --> vcat["VSS / COMMON CATHODE"]
    oledAnode -. light .-> photonEmit["hν LAMBDA = hc E_g"]

    style nodeA fill:#fff2e8,stroke:#d4380d,stroke-width:2px
    style photonEmit fill:#f6ffed,stroke:#389e0d
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the schematic:** The voltage on **NODE A** is held by $C_{st}$ for the entire frame. It controls $T_2$'s gate, which in turn sets the OLED current — and therefore the pixel's luminance — for the *full* frame duration. This is the central reason AMOLED avoids flicker and can represent true black.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — [KTU University Exam — Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]
**"Compare AMOLED and PMOLED technologies in terms of pixel driving and resolution suitability."** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
AMOLED uses a TFT + storage capacitor (2T1C) per pixel, so each pixel maintains its programmed luminance for the entire frame ($D=1$). This eliminates flicker and enables true black since the OLED can be turned off. PMOLED addresses one row at a time without per-pixel memory, giving a duty cycle $D=1/N$, where $N$ is the row count. At high $N$ (e.g., 1080), PMOLED would require a peak luminance $\sim 10^{6}\,\text{cd/m}^{2}$, which is impossible. Hence PMOLED is limited to $\le 128\times 64$ resolution while AMOLED scales to 4K/8K TVs. AMOLED also has lower power at high resolution and supports wider color gamuts. *(3 marks — split: 1 for 2T1C, 1 for duty cycle, 1 for resolution)*

---

### Question 2 — [KTU University Exam — Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]
**"Explain why AMOLED displays require gamma correction in the computer graphics pipeline."** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
In the 2T1C circuit, the OLED luminance is proportional to the *square* of the data voltage $V_{data}$ (from MOSFET saturation). Therefore, a linear increase in $V_{data}$ produces a non-linear (quadratic) increase in luminance. Human vision itself is also non-linear (Weber-Fechner law, perceived brightness $\propto \log$ of intensity). To make a linear grayscale code appear perceptually uniform and to compensate for the device's quadratic response, the CG pipeline applies gamma correction: $V_{out} = V_{in}^{\gamma}$ with $\gamma \approx 2.2$ (sRGB standard). Without gamma correction, midtones would appear washed out or crushed. *(3 marks — split: 1 for quadratic physics, 1 for human perception, 1 for pipeline solution)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently lose marks by **(a)** confusing AMOLED with LCD backlight operation, **(b)** omitting the storage capacitor's role in eliminating flicker, **(c)** failing to mention gamma correction when explaining color/grayscale, and **(d)** drawing the 2T1C circuit with the OLED at the source side instead of the drain side (causing $V_{GS}$ instability). Always specify "OLED connected to drain of $T_2$ with common cathode."

---

### QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam — Model as per 2024 Scheme]

**(a) With a neat schematic, explain the structure and working of a 2T1C pixel circuit in an AMOLED display. Show how the storage capacitor maintains the pixel state. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Components of the 2T1C circuit (2 marks):**
   - $T_1$: Switch (select) transistor whose gate is tied to the scan line.
   - $T_2$: Drive transistor whose gate is tied to NODE A (storage node).
   - $C_{st}$: Storage capacitor between NODE A and $V_{DD}$.
   - OLED: Anode at drain of $T_2$, cathode at $V_{SS}$ (common cathode).
   - Interconnects: Scan line (gate), data line (drain of $T_1$), $V_{DD}$ rail.

2. **Programming phase (2 marks):**
   - Scan driver activates row $i$, turning ON $T_1$ for every pixel in that row.
   - Source driver presents $V_{data}$ (proportional to grayscale code) on the data line.
   - $C_{st}$ charges to $V_{data} - V_{DD}$ through $T_1$.

3. **Hold / Emission phase (2 marks):**
   - Scan line goes LOW; $T_1$ turns OFF, isolating NODE A.
   - $C_{st}$ retains the gate voltage of $T_2$ for the entire frame ($\approx 8.33\,\text{ms}$ at $120\,\text{Hz}$).
   - $T_2$ in saturation sources a constant current $I_{OLED}=\tfrac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\tfrac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{th})^2$ through the OLED.
   - Luminance $L = \eta_{ext}\,I_{OLED}/(\pi A_{pixel})$ is therefore stable.

4. **Significance of $C_{st}$ (1 mark):**
   - Prevents flicker (otherwise OLED would emit only for $\mu$s during scan).
   - Enables true black (data voltage $= V_{DD}+V_{th}$ ⇒ $V_{GS}-V_{th}=0$ ⇒ $I_{OLED}=0$).
   - Decouples display time from pixel addressing time.

---

**(b) Discuss the various sub-pixel arrangements used in AMOLED displays and their effect on color reproduction. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **RGB Stripe (Real RGB) — (2 marks):** Each pixel has three full-sized rectangular subpixels R, G, B side by side. Highest effective resolution and color accuracy; used in Samsung's earlier AMOLEDs. Higher power consumption because blue and red sub-pixels are larger.

2. **PenTile (RGBG) — (2 marks):** Shares subpixels between adjacent pixels — pattern is RGBG. Reduces subpixel count by $\sim 33\%$, lowers power, but causes visible fringing on text and fine geometry. Used in many smartphones for power efficiency.

3. **Diamond / S-Stripe — (2 marks):** Subpixels are diamond-shaped (or octagonal) to maximize aperture ratio and improve viewing angles; reduces off-axis color shift. Common in high-end smartphone AMOLEDs.

4. **WOLED + Color Filter (LG method) — (1 mark):** Uses a stack of white OLED layers plus RGBW color filters. Easier to manufacture on large substrates, longer lifetime, but consumes more power (filter absorbs $\sim 2/3$ of white light).

5. **Impact on CG rendering — summary (bonus 1 mark):** CG antialiasing and subpixel rendering algorithms (e.g., ClearType, LCD vs OLED subpixel layouts) must be tuned to the physical subpixel arrangement; otherwise text and thin lines exhibit color fringing.

---

### QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam — Model as per 2024 Scheme — Alternative Choice]

**(a) Derive the relationship between pixel data voltage and OLED luminance in an AMOLED 2T1C circuit. Why is gamma correction essential? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **MOSFET saturation current derivation (3 marks):**

$$
I_{DS} = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{th})^{2}
$$

With $V_{GS} = V_{data} - V_{DD}$:

$$
I_{OLED} = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}\bigl[V_{data}-(V_{DD}+V_{th})\bigr]^{2}
$$

2. **Luminance conversion (1 mark):**

$$
L_{pixel} = \frac{\eta_{ext}}{\pi A_{pixel}} I_{OLED} = \frac{\eta_{ext}\,\mu_n C_{ox}\,W}{2\pi A_{pixel} L}\bigl[V_{data}-(V_{DD}+V_{th})\bigr]^{2}
$$

3. **Quadratic dependency (1 mark):** Thus $L \propto (V_{data}-V_{offset})^{2}$ — light output is quadratic in input voltage. For linear increments in grayscale code, the output luminance jumps non-uniformly.

4. **Need for gamma correction (2 marks):**
   - Human visual perception is logarithmic (Weber-Fechner / Stevens' power law with exponent $\approx 1/3$).
   - Perceived brightness $B \propto L^{1/\gamma_{vision}}$ with $\gamma_{vision}\approx 2.2$.
   - To make a uniform 8-bit grayscale code produce perceptually uniform brightness steps on an AMOLED with its quadratic electro-optic response, the CG pipeline applies $V_{data} = V_{linear}^{1/\gamma}$ where $\gamma \approx 2.2$.
   - Without this, mid-tones (around code 128) would appear too dim, and shadows would be crushed.

---

**(b) Explain with a block diagram the AMOLED display driver architecture. How does the timing controller coordinate gate and source drivers? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Major blocks (2 marks):** Timing Controller (TCON), Gate Driver (scan), Source Driver (data), TFT + OLED pixel array, power management IC (PMIC), common cathode driver.

2. **Input stage (1 mark):** The TCON receives the digital video stream (e.g., MIPI DSI, eDP) from the host CG application processor. It demultiplexes into (i) sequential row scan pulses and (ii) parallel column data words.

3. **Line-by-line addressing (2 marks):**
   - For frame period $T_{frame}$, TCON generates a scan pulse for row 1, lasting $t_{row} = T_{frame}/N$.
   - During $t_{row}$, the source driver latches one full row of pixel data (M pixels) and presents analog voltages on the M data lines.
   - $T_1$ in every pixel of row 1 turns ON, charging $C_{st}$ to the new grayscale values.
   - The TCON then advances to row 2, and so on, line-at-a-time.

4. **Synchronization signals (1 mark):** HSYNC, VSYNC, pixel clock, and Data Enable signals ensure the gate and source drivers stay aligned with the incoming framebuffer.

5. **Refresh and blanking (1 mark):** Vertical blanking intervals allow the gate driver to reset to row 1. Horizontal blanking allows the source driver to reload the next row's data. In variable refresh rate (VRR) mode, $T_{frame}$ itself varies — important for CG because it synchronizes with GPU render completion.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **AMOLED** = Active-Matrix Organic Light-Emitting Diode; uses TFT + storage capacitor at every pixel.
- **Core circuit is 2T1C:** two transistors + one capacitor per subpixel.
- **OLED = current-driven device:** $L \propto I_{OLED}$, not voltage-driven like LCD.
- **$T_1$ = switch** (gate tied to scan line); **$T_2$ = drive** (source follower / current source).
- **$C_{st}$** holds data voltage for the full frame — eliminates flicker, enables true black.
- **$I_{OLED} = \tfrac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\tfrac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{th})^{2}$** — quadratic, hence **gamma correction** is mandatory.
- **PMOLED duty cycle** $= 1/N_{rows}$; AMOLED duty cycle $= 1$ ⇒ AMOLED scales to high resolution.
- **True black ⇒ $V_{data} = V_{DD}+V_{th}$** ⇒ $I_{OLED}=0$ ⇒ $L_{min}=0$ ⇒ contrast ratio $\to \infty$.
- **Color generation:** RGB OLED stripes, PenTile RGBG, diamond, or WOLED+color-filter architectures.
- **Response time:** $\mu$s range — eliminates motion blur in CG rendering.
- **Refresh rates:** $60$ / $90$ / $120\,\text{Hz}$; supports VRR (variable refresh rate) for tear-free gaming.
- **Color gamut:** $\sim 100\%$ DCI-P3; HDR with peak luminance $1000\text{–}2500\,\text{nits}$.
- **Electroluminescence wavelength:** $\lambda = hc/E_g = 1240/E_g\,[\text{nm·eV/eV}]$.
- **CG implications:** gamma $2.2$ pipeline, tone-mapping for HDR, subpixel-aware antialiasing, no need for frame-blur compensation due to fast response.
- **Examiner red flags to avoid:** confusing AMOLED with LCD, forgetting $C_{st}$, drawing OLED at the source of $T_2$, omitting gamma, ignoring duty-cycle argument.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: E-Paper Displays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT414 - COMPUTER GRAPHICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** E-Paper Displays # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **E-Paper (Electronic Paper)**, also known as **e-ink** or **electronic ink display**, is a non-emissive display technology engineered to replicate the visual appearance of ordinary ink on printed paper. Unlike traditional emissive displays (LCD, LED, OLED) that produce light to form images, E-Paper operates on the principle of **reflective display technology**, where ambient light is reflected off the display surface to create visible content. The technology was pioneered by **E Ink Corporation** (founded 1997) and Gyricon LLC (founded at Xerox PARC, 2000). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** E-Paper Displays are classified under *non-emissive / reflective display technologies* and form a critical comparison point against conventional raster-scan displays and LCDs in the Computer Graphics curriculum. The formal definition aligned with KTU terminology: > **E-Paper Display:** A bistable, reflective display device that uses microencapsulated charged particles suspended in a dielectric fluid, controlled by an applied electric field, to produce stable, paper-like images with extremely low power consumption. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Magnetic Shaker Plate"** > > Imagine a transparent tray filled with clear oil, containing thousands of tiny black and white beads, each bead carrying a tiny magnet inside. When you place a magnet underneath the tray and slide it around, the black beads rise to the top and the white beads sink. Now the surface looks black. Reverse the magnet — the white beads pop up and the surface looks white. **Cut the power entirely** — and the beads *stay exactly where they are*, frozen in place, requiring **zero electricity** to maintain the image. This is precisely how E-Paper works: charged pigment particles in fluid, manipulated by electric fields, and "frozen" by surface chemistry. ## 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics (Bold-Formatted) The performance of any E-Paper display is quantified by the following standardized metrics: - **Reflectance (R):** Typically **35%–45%** for white state (vs. ~90% for real paper). - **Contrast Ratio (CR):** Usually **10:1 to 15:1** (vs. 1000:1 for LCD). - **Bistability:** Image retention without power — the **defining property**. - **Power Consumption:** **< 0.1 W** during display (vs. 1–5 W for LCD). - **Refresh Rate:** **100–500 ms** (vs. 1–16 ms for LCD/LED). - **Viewing Angle:** **> 180°** (near-Lambertian reflectance). - **Operating Temperature:** **0°C to 50°C** (consumer grade). ## 1.4 Classification of E-Paper Technologies | Technology | Physical Principle | Commercial Status | |---|---|---| | **Electrophoretic (E-ink)** | Charged pigment migration | Dominant (Kindle, Kobo) | | **Electrowetting** | Fluid lens voltage control | R&D / Liquavista | | **Electrochromic** | Redox color change | Niche (smart windows) | | **Cholesteric LCD (ChLCD)** | Bistable liquid crystal | Limited (signage) | | **Gyricon** | Rotating bichromal balls | Defunct (Xerox) | | **Interferometric Modulator (Mirasol)** | MEMS interference | Discontinued (Qualcomm) | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Working Principle — Electrophoretic E-Paper (Step-by-Step) > [!NOTE] > This is the **dominant commercial E-Paper technology** and is the primary focus for KTU examination purposes. ### Step 1: Microcapsule Architecture Each pixel of the display is composed of thousands of **microcapsules** (typical diameter: **30–100 μm**). Each microcapsule contains: - A **dielectric fluid** (transparent hydrocarbon oil). - **Negatively charged white pigment particles** (TiO₂ — titanium dioxide). - **Positively charged black pigment particles** (carbon black). ### Step 2: Pixel Substrate The microcapsules are sandwiched between: - A **top transparent electrode** (ITO — Indium Tin Oxide). - A **bottom matrix of pixel electrodes** (one per pixel, addressed by TFT backplane). ### Step 3: Addressable Field Application The TFT backplane applies a controlled **voltage differential** (typically **±15 V**) between the top electrode and individual bottom pixel electrodes: - **+15 V at bottom:** Positive black particles attracted downward (toward the back), negative white particles pushed upward (toward the viewer) → **WHITE pixel**. - **−15 V at bottom:** Positive black particles pulled upward, white particles sink → **BLACK pixel**. - **0 V (no field):** Particles remain frozen by **surface adhesion** → **Bistable state maintained**. ### Step 4: Light Reflection & Image Formation Ambient light passes through the ITO, hits the topmost pigment layer, and is **reflected back** to the viewer's eye. No backlight is required. ## 2.2 Operational Theory — The "Why" Behind Each Step - **Why microcapsules?** They physically isolate each pigment droplet, preventing agglomeration and enabling uniform switching. - **Why charged particles?** Electric fields (per Coulomb's Law) exert force on charged bodies, enabling directional control. - **Why bistability?** Van der Waals and electrostatic adhesion between pigments and capsule walls lock particles in place when no field is applied. - **Why reflective, not emissive?** Mimics paper — readable in direct sunlight, no eye strain, dramatically lower power. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is **examination-critical**. Memorize the equations, units, and typical value ranges. **No pipe symbol `|` is used** in any cell — all absolute value / magnitude notations use `\vert` or `\mid` rendering. | # | Parameter / Concept | Equation / Definition | Units | Typical Value | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Force on charged particle | $F = qE$ | N (Newton) | — | | 2 | Electric field in capacitor gap | $E = V / d$ | V/m | $15 / 50 \times 10^{-6}$ | | 3 | Capacitance of pixel | $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$ | F (Farad) | $\sim 10^{-12}$ F | | 4 | Stored energy per pixel | $U = \frac{1}{2} C V^2$ | J (Joule) | $\sim 10^{-10}$ J | | 5 | Reflectance (Lambertian) | $R = \frac{L}{E_{incident}}$ | dimensionless (ratio) | $0.35$–$0.45$ | | 6 | Contrast Ratio | $CR = \frac{R_{white}}{R_{black}}$ | dimensionless | $10\!:\!1$ to $15\!:\!1$ | | 7 | Optical Response Time | $\tau = \frac{\eta d^2}{V \zeta \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}$ | s (seconds) | $0.1$–$0.5$ s | | 8 | Power during transition | $P_{active} = f \cdot C \cdot V^2$ | W (Watt) | $0.05$–$0.1$ W | | 9 | Power in hold state | $P_{hold} \approx 0$ | W | $\sim 0$ W | | 10 | Resolution metric | $PPI = \frac{\sqrt{W^2 + H^2}}{d_{screen}}$ | ppi | $300$ ppi (Kindle) | **Where:** - $q$ = charge on particle (C) - $V$ = applied voltage (V) - $d$ = capsule/cell gap (m) - $\varepsilon_0$ = vacuum permittivity = $8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m - $\varepsilon_r$ = relative permittivity of dielectric fluid - $\eta$ = viscosity of fluid (Pa·s) - $\zeta$ = zeta potential of particle (V) - $L$ = luminance reflected, $E_{incident}$ = incident illuminance ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Why E-Paper matters in engineering applications:** 1. **E-Readers (Kindle, Kobo, Nook):** Weeks of battery life, sunlight-readable. 2. **Electronic Shelf Labels (ESL):** Retail pricing — 5+ year battery life on coin cell. 3. **Smart Cards & Passports:** Biometric display, zero power when static. 4. **Digital Signage:** Bus stops, transit info — solar-powered. 5. **Wearable Devices:** Smartwatches with always-on displays. 6. **IoT Indicators:** Status displays on low-power edge devices. The technology is **inherently aligned with green computing** — global ESL deployment saves an estimated **30,000 tons of paper per year** according to E Ink's 2022 sustainability report. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Optical Switching Time of an Electrophoretic Particle We derive the characteristic time $\tau$ for a charged pigment particle to traverse the capsule gap under an applied field. ### Step 1: Forces Acting on the Particle The particle experiences: 1. **Electric force:** $F_e = qE = qV / d$ 2. **Viscous drag (Stokes' Law):** $F_d = 6 \pi \eta r v$ 3. **Electrostatic interaction with electrode/double layer:** Captured via the **zeta potential** $\zeta$. ### Step 2: Force Balance at Terminal Velocity At terminal velocity, the particle's acceleration is zero, so: $$F_e = F_d$$ Substituting: $$\frac{qV}{d} = 6 \pi \eta r v$$ ### Step 3: Expressing Charge via Zeta Potential For a particle in a dielectric medium, the effective surface charge relates to the zeta potential by: $$q = 4 \pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r r \zeta$$ ### Step 4: Solve for Terminal Velocity Substitute the expression for $q$ into the force balance: $$\frac{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r r \zeta \cdot V}{d} = 6 \pi \eta r v$$ Cancel $4 \pi r$ from both sides (assuming $r \neq 0$): $$\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \zeta V}{d} = \frac{3}{2} \eta v$$ Solving for $v$: $$v = \frac{2 \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \zeta V}{3 \eta d}$$ ### Step 5: Compute the Transit Time Across the Gap Since $v = d / \tau$, we get: $$\tau = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{d \cdot 3 \eta d}{2 \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \zeta V}$$ $$\boxed{\tau = \frac{3 \eta d^2}{2 \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \zeta V}}$$ This is the canonical form of the E-Paper response time equation. Note: Some textbooks absorb the factor of $3/2$ differently based on the slip-condition assumption (stick vs. slip boundary); the KTU-examined form typically uses: $$\tau = \frac{\eta d^2}{V \zeta \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}$$ ### Step 6: Numerical Sanity Check Assume: - $d = 50 \times 10^{-6}$ m (50 μm capsule) - $V = 15$ V - $\zeta = 0.05$ V - $\varepsilon_r = 3$ (hydrocarbon oil) - $\eta = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s Plugging into the simplified form: $$\tau = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3} \times (50 \times 10^{-6})^2}{15 \times 0.05 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 3}$$ Numerator: $$5 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.5 \times 10^{-9} = 1.25 \times 10^{-11}$$ Denominator: $$15 \times 0.05 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 3 = 1.992 \times 10^{-11}$$ Therefore: $$\tau = \frac{1.25 \times 10^{-11}}{1.992 \times 10^{-11}} \approx 0.627 \text{ s}$$ This matches the empirical refresh time of typical E-ink displays (**~500 ms**), confirming the model is physically valid. ## 3.2 Derivation: Power Consumption in Bistable Mode ### Step 1: Energy Per Switching Event The energy to charge a single pixel capacitor is: $$U_{pixel} = \frac{1}{2} C V^2$$ ### Step 2: Total Energy for an N-pixel Display $$U_{total} = N \cdot \frac{1}{2} C V^2$$ ### Step 3: If only a fraction $f$ of pixels change per refresh: $$U_{refresh} = f \cdot N \cdot \frac{1}{2} C V^2$$ ### Step 4: Average Power at Refresh Rate $r$ (Hz): $$P_{avg} = f \cdot N \cdot \frac{1}{2} C V^2 \cdot r$$ ### Step 5: Numerical Estimate for a 6" E-reader - $N = 800 \times 600 = 480{,}000$ pixels - $C = 1$ pF $= 10^{-12}$ F per pixel - $V = 15$ V - $f = 0.5$ (half the pixels change) - $r = 0.5$ Hz (one page turn per 2 seconds) $$P_{avg} = 0.5 \times 4.8 \times 10^5 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-12} \times 225 \times 0.5$$ $$P_{avg} \approx 1.35 \times 10^{-5} \text{ W} = 13.5 \text{ μW}$$ This extraordinarily low power figure explains why a Kindle lasts **weeks** on a single charge. ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — E-Paper vs. LCD vs. OLED | Parameter | E-Paper (E-ink) | LCD (TFT) | OLED | |---|---|---|---| | **Display Mode** | Reflective | Transmissive / Backlit | Emissive | | **Power (Static Image)** | $\sim 0$ W | $1$–$5$ W | $0.5$–$3$ W | | **Contrast Ratio** | $10\!:\!1$–$15\!:\!1$ | $1000\!:\!1$ | $1{,}000{,}000\!:\!1$ | | **Sunlight Readability** | Excellent | Poor | Poor | | **Refresh Rate** | $100$–$500$ ms | $1$–$16$ ms | $<1$ ms | | **Viewing Angle** | $>180°$ | $160°$ | $>170°$ | | **Bistability** | **Yes** | No | No | | **Eye Strain** | Very Low | Moderate | Moderate | | **Color Capability** | Limited (CFA used) | Full | Full | | **Cost per inch²** | Medium | Low | High | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 E-Paper Pixel Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow > [!NOTE] > Mermaid block diagram showing the layered structure and signal flow of a single E-Paper pixel. All node IDs are alphanumeric; no reserved keywords used. ```mermaid graph TD A[Top ITO Electrode
Common transparent layer] --> B[Microcapsule Array
Diameter 30 to 100 micrometer] B --> C[Dielectric Fluid
Hydrocarbon oil medium] C --> D1[White Pigment TiO2
Negatively charged] C --> D2[Black Pigment Carbon
Positively charged] E[TFT Backplane
Pixel address matrix] --> F[Bottom Pixel Electrode
One per subpixel] F --> B G[Column Driver IC] --> E H[Row Driver IC] --> E I[Controller FPGA or MCU] --> G I --> H J[Ambient Light Source] --> K[Reflected Light to Viewer Eye] D1 --> K D2 --> K style A fill:#d0e8ff,stroke:#003366 style B fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#806600 style C fill:#e6f5e6,stroke:#1a4d1a style D1 fill:#ffffff,stroke:#000000 style D2 fill:#1a1a1a,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff style E fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#660000 style I fill:#d6d6ff,stroke:#000066 style J fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#663300 style K fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#333333 ``` ## 4.2 E-Paper Display System — Functional Architecture Flow ```mermaid graph LR P1[Host Processor
Application CPU] --> P2[Display Controller
E-ink timing IC] P2 --> P3[Waveform LUT
Lookup table] P3 --> P4[Column Driver] P3 --> P5[Row Driver] P4 --> P6[TFT Gate Lines] P5 --> P7[TFT Source Lines] P6 --> P8[Pixel Matrix
Electrophoretic cells] P7 --> P8 P8 --> P9[Image Output
Reflected ambient light] P10[Temperature Sensor] --> P2 P11[Power Management Unit] --> P2 P11 --> P4 P11 --> P5 style P1 fill:#cce0ff,stroke:#003399 style P2 fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#804000 style P3 fill:#d9b3ff,stroke:#330099 style P8 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806600 style P9 fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600 ``` ## 4.3 E-Paper Technology Decision Tree ```mermaid graph TD Q1{Need paper-like readability?} -->|Yes| Q2{Need video refresh rate?} Q1 -->|No| LCD[Use LCD or LED] Q2 -->|No| EP[E-Paper E-ink] Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{Color required?} Q3 -->|No| EP Q3 -->|Yes| CFA[Color Filter Array E-Paper] style Q1 fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#003366 style Q2 fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#003366 style Q3 fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#003366 style EP fill:#b3ffb3,stroke:#006600 style LCD fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000 style CFA fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#993300 ``` ## 4.4 Particle Migration Behavior — Voltage Polarity Map ```mermaid graph TD V1[Voltage Plus 15V at bottom] --> W1[White particles rise to top] V1 --> B1[Black particles sink to bottom] W1 --> R1[Pixel appears WHITE] V2[Voltage Minus 15V at bottom] --> W2[White particles sink] V2 --> B2[Black particles rise to top] B2 --> R2[Pixel appears BLACK] V3[Voltage 0V Hold] --> HOLD[Bistable state locked] HOLD --> R3[Image persists with zero power] style V1 fill:#ccffff,stroke:#003366 style V2 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000 style V3 fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#666600 style R1 fill:#ffffff,stroke:#000000 style R2 fill:#1a1a1a,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff style R3 fill:#e6e6e6,stroke:#333333 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > These are direct short-answer conceptual questions of the type asked in **KTU University Exam Part A** (2-mark and 3-mark slots). --- ### **Question A1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` **Q: Define E-Paper Display. List any four advantages of E-Paper over conventional LCD displays.** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** E-Paper (Electronic Paper) is a **bistable, reflective display technology** that uses charged pigment particles suspended in a microencapsulated dielectric fluid to form images by reflecting ambient light, mimicking the appearance of ink on paper. **Four Advantages:** 1. **Ultra-low power consumption** — consumes power only during image transition; holds image with zero power. 2. **Sunlight readability** — reflective principle yields excellent contrast in bright ambient light. 3. **Wide viewing angle** — near-Lambertian reflectance provides $>180°$ viewing. 4. **Reduced eye strain** — no flicker, no backlight, paper-like appearance reduces visual fatigue. --- ### **Question A2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` **Q: What is meant by *bistability* in E-Paper displays? Why is it important?** **Model Answer:** **Bistability** is the property of a display to **retain a pixel's state (black or white) indefinitely without any continuous power supply**, relying on the surface adhesion of pigment particles to microcapsule walls. **Importance:** - Enables **zero-power static image retention**, the key to E-Paper's ultra-low energy profile. - Allows devices like **e-readers and electronic shelf labels** to operate for months or years on small batteries. - Reduces overall energy footprint, making it ideal for **sustainable and IoT applications**. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice > [!NOTE] > KTU ESE (End Semester Evaluation) Module questions carry **14 marks** with internal choice between two questions. The pattern below mirrors this structure: each question has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]` #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Q: Explain the working principle of an Electrophoretic E-Paper Display with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of microcapsules, charged pigments, and the TFT backplane.** **Model Solution:** **1. Microcapsule Architecture (3 marks):** Each pixel consists of thousands of microcapsules (30–100 μm in diameter) sandwiched between two electrode layers. Each microcapsule contains: - A transparent **dielectric fluid** (hydrocarbon oil). - **Negatively charged white pigment** (TiO₂) — high refractive index, scatters light efficiently. - **Positively charged black pigment** (carbon black) — absorbs light. **2. TFT Backplane and Addressing (2 marks):** The bottom layer is a **Thin-Film Transistor (TFT) array**, identical in concept to LCD addressing, where each pixel is controlled by a transistor. The top layer is a continuous **ITO (Indium Tin Oxide)** transparent electrode. Voltage is applied differentially between the ITO and individual bottom electrodes. **3. Particle Migration and Image Formation (2 marks):** - **+15 V at pixel electrode:** White pigments migrate to the top, black sinks → WHITE state. - **−15 V at pixel electrode:** Black pigments rise to the top, white sinks → BLACK state. - **0 V:** Particles remain locked by van der Waals adhesion → Bistable hold. Ambient light enters through ITO, hits the top pigment layer, and is reflected back to the viewer, forming the image. --- #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Q: Derive an expression for the switching time $\tau$ of an electrophoretic particle. Using typical values, compute the response time.** **Model Solution:** **Derivation (4 marks):** [Refer to §3.1 above for the full derivation.] At terminal velocity, electric force balances viscous drag: $$qE = 6 \pi \eta r v$$ With $E = V/d$ and $q = 4\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r r\zeta$: $$v = \frac{2\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r\zeta V}{3\eta d}$$ Therefore, the transit time $\tau = d / v$: $$\tau = \frac{3\eta d^2}{2\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r\zeta V}$$ **Numerical Computation (3 marks):** Given: $\eta = 5\times10^{-3}$ Pa·s, $d = 50\times10^{-6}$ m, $V = 15$ V, $\zeta = 0.05$ V, $\varepsilon_r = 3$: $$\tau = \frac{3 \times 5\times10^{-3} \times 2.5\times10^{-9}}{2 \times 8.854\times10^{-12} \times 3 \times 0.05 \times 15}$$ Numerator: $3.75 \times 10^{-11}$ Denominator: $3.186 \times 10^{-11}$ $$\tau \approx 1.18 \text{ s}$$ **Result:** Switching time is on the order of **hundreds of milliseconds to ~1 second**, which is consistent with the observed E-ink refresh behavior (typically 100–500 ms with optimized waveforms). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model — Internal Choice Alternative]` #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Q: Compare E-Paper, LCD, and OLED display technologies across the following parameters: power consumption, contrast ratio, refresh rate, sunlight readability, and bistability. Mention two specific real-world applications where E-Paper is preferred.** **Model Solution:** **Comparison Table (5 marks):** | Parameter | E-Paper (E-ink) | LCD (TFT) | OLED | |---|---|---|---| | **Power Consumption (Static)** | $\sim 0$ W | $1$–$5$ W | $0.5$–$3$ W | | **Contrast Ratio** | $10\!:\!1$–$15\!:\!1$ | $1000\!:\!1$ | $>1{,}000{,}000\!:\!1$ | | **Refresh Rate** | $100$–$500$ ms | $1$–$16$ ms | $<1$ ms | | **Sunlight Readability** | **Excellent** | Poor | Poor | | **Bistability** | **Yes** | No | No | | **Emissive / Reflective** | Reflective | Transmissive (backlit) | Emissive | **Two Real-World Applications where E-Paper is preferred (2 marks):** 1. **E-Readers (Amazon Kindle, Kobo):** Weeks of battery life, paper-like readability, no eye strain. 2. **Electronic Shelf Labels (ESL) in Retail:** 5+ year battery life, solar-compatible, remotely updatable pricing. --- #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Q: Explain the following E-Paper technologies in brief: (i) Electrophoretic, (ii) Electrowetting, (iii) Cholesteric LCD, (iv) Electrochromic.** **Model Solution:** **(i) Electrophoretic (2 marks):** Uses charged pigment particles (black & white) suspended in a dielectric fluid within microcapsules. Electric field drives particles to display surface. **Most commercially dominant** (E Ink). Used in Kindle e-readers. **(ii) Electrowetting (2 marks):** Uses voltage to control the **wetting properties** of a colored oil film on a hydrophobic surface. Applied voltage changes the oil's contact angle, altering its light-transmission characteristics. Faster than electrophoretic, but historically not commercialized at scale (Liquavista). **(iii) Cholesteric LCD — ChLCD (1.5 marks):** Uses **cholesteric (chiral nematic) liquid crystals** that have a natural helical structure. Two stable states (planar and focal-conic) are bistable, requiring no power to maintain. Used in low-power signage and shelf labels. **(iv) Electrochromic (1.5 marks):** Materials change color or opacity through a **reversible redox reaction** driven by a small DC voltage. Color change is slow (seconds to minutes), but very low power. Used in **smart windows** (e.g., SageGlass) and anti-glare rear-view mirrors. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Conflating E-Paper with LCD/OLED:** Do NOT describe E-Paper as "emissive" or "backlit." This is a **fundamental conceptual error** worth 2–3 marks deduction. > 2. **Omitting the word "bistable":** Bistability is the *defining* property of E-Paper. Any answer lacking this term is incomplete. > 3. **Wrong polarity convention:** When explaining particle migration, be explicit about which voltage polarity drives which color to the viewer. **+V at bottom → white up; −V at bottom → black up.** > 4. **Skipping the diagram in 7-mark questions:** KTU mandates a "neat diagram" — not including one costs 1–2 marks even if the explanation is correct. > 5. **Wrong units in numerical problems:** Response time $\tau$ is in **seconds**, not milliseconds, unless explicitly converted. Power is in **Watts**. > 6. **Forgetting to mention ambient light:** E-Paper is a *reflective* technology — emphasize that the display does not emit light; it reflects ambient light. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for E-Paper Displays:** - ⭐ **E-Paper = Bistable + Reflective + Charged Pigment Microcapsules.** - ⭐ **Core equation:** $\tau = \dfrac{\eta d^2}{V\zeta\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}$ — high voltage → faster switching; high viscosity → slower switching. - ⭐ **Dominant technology:** Electrophoretic (E Ink Corporation, founded 1997). - ⭐ **Key advantage:** Zero power in static state — bistable. - ⭐ **Key limitation:** Slow refresh rate (100–500 ms) — unsuitable for video. - ⭐ **Contrast ratio:** $10\!:\!1$ to $15\!:\!1$ — much lower than LCD/OLED. - ⭐ **Sunlight readability:** Excellent (reflective, not emissive). - ⭐ **Microcapsule contents:** TiO₂ (white, negative), carbon (black, positive), hydrocarbon oil. - ⭐ **TFT backplane** used for pixel addressing — same fabrication concept as LCD. - ⭐ **Color E-Paper:** Achieved via Color Filter Array (CFA) overlay on monochrome E-ink panel. - ⭐ **Real-world apps:** Kindle e-readers, retail ESLs, smart cards, IoT indicators, solar-powered signage. - ⭐ **Comparison triad:** E-Paper (low power, paper-like) vs. LCD (video, backlit) vs. OLED (high contrast, emissive, expensive). - ⭐ **Environmental impact:** ESL deployment saves an estimated **30,000+ tons of paper annually**. - ⭐ **Power equation:** $P_{avg} = f \cdot N \cdot \frac{1}{2}CV^2 \cdot r$ — typically **micro-watts** for a full E-reader. - ⭐ **For 14-mark answers:** Always include a labeled block diagram and derive the response time equation with numerical substitution.

topics.title: Output Primitives - Line Drawing Algorithms - DDA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT414 - COMPUTER GRAPHICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Output Primitives - Line Drawing Algorithms - DDA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Output Primitives** are the fundamental geometric entities (points, lines, circles, polygons, text, and filled areas) that form the basic building blocks for constructing any graphical image on a raster display device. The process of generating these primitives on a pixel-based screen is called **Rasterization** or **Scan Conversion**.

Among all output primitives, **lines** are the most elementary. Since a raster screen is a discrete grid of pixels (with integer coordinates), drawing a perfectly straight line between two arbitrary endpoints requires a deterministic mathematical procedure. Such a procedure is called a **Line Drawing Algorithm**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DDA (Digital Differential Analyzer) Algorithm** is an incremental line generation procedure based on the principle of sampling the straight line at regular unit (or fractional) intervals along the **major axis** (the axis with the larger projected length) and computing the corresponding value along the minor axis using the differential equation of a straight line:
> $$\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = m \quad \text{(slope of the line)}$$

The DDA algorithm was originally derived from the concept of a **Differential Analyzer**, a mechanical analog computer used to solve differential equations. In computer graphics, the same incremental evaluation principle is applied to traverse the line one step at a time.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are walking from the **bottom-left corner** of a tiled floor to the **top-right corner**. The floor is made of square tiles (pixels). You want to step on tiles that look like a perfectly straight line when seen from above.

- If the floor were tilted only slightly, you would step **forward (right) on every step**, and your **upward movement would be very small** (less than one tile per step).
- If the floor were tilted steeply, you would **rise by one whole tile (or more) for every horizontal step**.

The DDA algorithm does exactly this:

- It **measures the slope** of the required line.
- It decides whether the line is "more horizontal" or "more vertical" — whichever is the **larger distance** becomes the **number of steps**.
- At each step, it moves a small fractional amount in the x-direction and a corresponding fractional amount in the y-direction, computed from the slope.
- The continuous (real-valued) coordinates are then **rounded to the nearest integer pixel** so that a pixel can be lit on the screen.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Intuition:** DDA works on the principle of *finite differences* — it never recomputes the slope from scratch. Instead, it **adds a small constant increment** at every step, which is why it is called an *incremental algorithm*.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Value / Convention |
|---|---|
| Pixel coordinate origin | **Top-left corner** of the screen, with x increasing rightward and y increasing downward (standard KTU convention for raster systems) |
| Rounding rule | **Round-to-nearest integer** (typically `floor(x + 0.5)`) |
| Unit increment | $\Delta x_{\text{inc}} = \dfrac{\vert x_2 - x_1 \vert}{\text{steps}}$, $\Delta y_{\text{inc}} = \dfrac{\vert y_2 - y_1 \vert}{\text{steps}}$ |
| Major axis selection | $\text{steps} = \max\!\big(\lvert x_2 - x_1 \rvert,\ \lvert y_2 - y_1 \rvert\big)$ |
| Time complexity | **O(steps)** ≈ O(max(\|Δx\|, \|Δy\|)) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 explicitly lists *Line drawing algorithms — DDA, Bresenham's* as a mandatory sub-topic. DDA is the easier of the two and is asked frequently in **Part A (3-mark)** questions and as a sub-part in **Part B**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Broken Down Step by Step

The DDA algorithm converts the continuous line equation $y = mx + c$ into a discrete sequence of pixel coordinates. Its operational logic is as follows:

### **Step 1 — Input Acquisition**
Accept the two endpoints of the line in absolute screen coordinates:
$$(x_1, y_1) \quad \text{and} \quad (x_2, y_2)$$

### **Step 2 — Compute the Differential Lengths (Δx, Δy)**
Calculate the total horizontal and vertical extents of the line:
$$\Delta x = x_2 - x_1, \qquad \Delta y = y_2 - y_1$$

> **Why?** These are the total "distances" we need to cover along x and y, respectively.

### **Step 3 — Determine the Number of Steps**
The line must look smooth even when its slope is steep or shallow. The number of discrete steps is governed by the **larger** of the two differentials so that the denser axis gets at least one pixel per step:
$$\text{steps} = \max\!\big(\lvert \Delta x \rvert,\ \lvert \Delta y \rvert\big)$$

> **Why not the smaller one?** If we used $\min(\lvert \Delta x \rvert, \lvert \Delta y \rvert)$, then for a steep vertical line ($\lvert \Delta y \rvert \gg \lvert \Delta x \rvert$), we would have too few horizontal pixels and the line would appear disconnected (gaps along x).

### **Step 4 — Compute the Per-Step Increments**
The continuous slope is $m = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$. When normalised by the number of steps, the per-step increments become:
$$X_{\text{inc}} = \frac{\Delta x}{\text{steps}}, \qquad Y_{\text{inc}} = \frac{\Delta y}{\text{steps}}$$

> **Why divide by steps?** Because we want to spread the total change $\Delta x$ and $\Delta y$ evenly across the chosen number of steps.

### **Step 5 — Initialise the Plotting Position**
Set the current pixel position to the starting endpoint:
$$x = x_1, \qquad y = y_1$$

### **Step 6 — Plot and Iterate**
For $i = 0$ to $\text{steps}$:
1. Round the current $(x, y)$ to the nearest integer: $\big(\text{round}(x),\ \text{round}(y)\big)$ and plot this pixel.
2. Increment: $x = x + X_{\text{inc}}$ and $y = y + Y_{\text{inc}}$.

### **Step 7 — Termination**
After $\text{steps} + 1$ plots, the line is fully rasterised.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Observation:** $X_{\text{inc}}$ and $Y_{\text{inc}}$ are often **floating-point numbers** (e.g., 0.667, 1.5). This is the single biggest weakness of DDA — it suffers from **rounding accumulation error** because the floating-point values keep accumulating as we add them step after step.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every formula you must memorise for the KTU exam on this topic. **No vertical pipe `|` characters are used inside table cells** to avoid breaking markdown rendering.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Purpose / When to Use |
|:-:|---|---|---|
| 1 | Horizontal differential | $\Delta x = x_2 - x_1$ | Total horizontal span |
| 2 | Vertical differential | $\Delta y = y_2 - y_1$ | Total vertical span |
| 3 | Number of steps | $\text{steps} = \max\!\big(\lvert \Delta x \rvert,\ \lvert \Delta y \rvert\big)$ | Major-axis selection |
| 4 | Per-step X-increment | $X_{\text{inc}} = \dfrac{\Delta x}{\text{steps}}$ | Horizontal jump per iteration |
| 5 | Per-step Y-increment | $Y_{\text{inc}} = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\text{steps}}$ | Vertical jump per iteration |
| 6 | Slope of the line | $m = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$ | Line direction; required for analytical verification |
| 7 | Position update | $x_{k+1} = x_k + X_{\text{inc}},\quad y_{k+1} = y_k + Y_{\text{inc}}$ | Core recurrence relation |
| 8 | Rounding rule | $\text{pixel}_x = \text{round}(x_k) = \lfloor x_k + 0.5 \rfloor$ | Map real values to integer screen grid |
| 9 | Time complexity | $O(\text{steps}) = O\!\big(\max(\lvert \Delta x \rvert, \lvert \Delta y \rvert)\big)$ | Computational cost |
| 10 | Special case — vertical | $\Delta x = 0 \Rightarrow \text{steps} = \lvert \Delta y \rvert$ | Avoid divide-by-zero |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CRT/LCD Display Controllers:** DDA-style interpolation is used in display pipelines to draw anti-aliased lines and edges in real-time 2D rendering.
- **CNC Machines and Plotters:** Pen plotters use the DDA concept to step motors along a linear path.
- **2D Game Engines:** Early 2D games used DDA for terrain outlines and wireframe models.
- **Computer-Aided Design (CAD):** Wireframe drafts of mechanical parts rely on incremental line rasterisation.
- **Embedded Graphics:** Low-cost microcontrollers (e.g., on OLED displays) use DDA because it requires **only addition** (no multiplication inside the loop), saving CPU cycles.

> [!TIP]
> **Why "incremental" matters in hardware:** A naive line algorithm would compute $y_k = m \cdot x_k + c$ at every step — that's one multiplication and one addition per pixel. DDA replaces this with **two additions** per pixel, which is dramatically faster when rasterising millions of lines per frame.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Worked Examples

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation of the DDA Recurrence

The DDA algorithm is derived directly from the first-principles differential equation of a straight line.

### **Starting Point — Equation of a Straight Line**

The standard point–slope form of a line passing through $(x_1, y_1)$ with slope $m$ is:

$$
y = m \cdot x + c
$$

Differentiating both sides with respect to $x$:

$$
\frac{dy}{dx} = m = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \frac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1}
$$

In a **finite-difference** (discrete) approximation, the differentials $dx$ and $dy$ become small finite increments $\delta x$ and $\delta y$:

$$
\delta y = m \cdot \delta x
$$

If we choose a uniform step size in $x$ equal to $X_{\text{inc}}$, then the corresponding step in $y$ is:

$$
\delta y = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} \cdot X_{\text{inc}}
$$

To keep the line sampled uniformly along its **major axis**, we set $X_{\text{inc}} = \dfrac{\Delta x}{\text{steps}}$. Substituting:

$$
\delta y = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} \cdot \frac{\Delta x}{\text{steps}} = \frac{\Delta y}{\text{steps}} = Y_{\text{inc}}
$$

Hence the recurrence relations used in the DDA loop are:

$$
\boxed{\ x_{k+1} = x_k + X_{\text{inc}} = x_k + \frac{\Delta x}{\text{steps}}, \qquad y_{k+1} = y_k + Y_{\text{inc}} = y_k + \frac{\Delta y}{\text{steps}} \ }
$$

> **Interpretation:** Each successive point on the line is obtained by **adding a constant** to the previous one. This is precisely the digital analog of integrating $dy = m\,dx$ over a small interval.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Standard Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU examiners frequently test a 7-mark numerical problem** of this exact format. You are expected to tabulate the iterations and show the plotted integer pixels. **Skipping the rounding step loses marks.**

### **Problem Statement**
Using the DDA algorithm, rasterise the line segment from $A(2, 3)$ to $B(8, 7)$. List all pixel coordinates generated.

### **Solution — Step 1: Compute the Differentials**

$$
\Delta x = x_2 - x_1 = 8 - 2 = 6
$$

$$
\Delta y = y_2 - y_1 = 7 - 3 = 4
$$

### **Solution — Step 2: Determine the Number of Steps**

$$
\text{steps} = \max\!\big(\lvert 6 \rvert,\ \lvert 4 \rvert\big) = \max(6, 4) = 6
$$

### **Solution — Step 3: Compute the Per-Step Increments**

$$
X_{\text{inc}} = \frac{\Delta x}{\text{steps}} = \frac{6}{6} = 1.000
$$

$$
Y_{\text{inc}} = \frac{\Delta y}{\text{steps}} = \frac{4}{6} = 0.6667
$$

### **Solution — Step 4: Iterative Plotting Table**

For each iteration $i$, compute $x_i = x_1 + i \cdot X_{\text{inc}}$ and $y_i = y_1 + i \cdot Y_{\text{inc}}$, then round to the nearest integer using $\lfloor \cdot + 0.5 \rfloor$.

| Iteration $i$ | $x_i = 2 + i \cdot 1.0$ | $y_i = 3 + i \cdot 0.6667$ | Rounded Pixel $(\text{round}(x_i),\ \text{round}(y_i))$ | Calculation of $\text{round}(y_i)$ |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|---|
| 0 | $2.000$ | $3.0000$ | **(2, 3)** | $\lfloor 3.0 + 0.5 \rfloor = 3$ |
| 1 | $3.000$ | $3.6667$ | **(3, 4)** | $\lfloor 3.6667 + 0.5 \rfloor = \lfloor 4.1667 \rfloor = 4$ |
| 2 | $4.000$ | $4.3333$ | **(4, 4)** | $\lfloor 4.3333 + 0.5 \rfloor = \lfloor 4.8333 \rfloor = 4$ |
| 3 | $5.000$ | $5.0000$ | **(5, 5)** | $\lfloor 5.0 + 0.5 \rfloor = 5$ |
| 4 | $6.000$ | $5.6667$ | **(6, 6)** | $\lfloor 5.6667 + 0.5 \rfloor = \lfloor 6.1667 \rfloor = 6$ |
| 5 | $7.000$ | $6.3333$ | **(7, 6)** | $\lfloor 6.3333 + 0.5 \rfloor = \lfloor 6.8333 \rfloor = 6$ |
| 6 | $8.000$ | $7.0000$ | **(8, 7)** | $\lfloor 7.0 + 0.5 \rfloor = 7$ |

### **Solution — Step 5: Final Rasterised Pixel Set**

$$
\boxed{\ \{ (2,3),\ (3,4),\ (4,4),\ (5,5),\ (6,6),\ (7,6),\ (8,7) \} \ }
$$

### **Solution — Step 6: Verification via Slope**

The expected slope is $m = \dfrac{4}{6} = 0.6667$. The final pixel set should approximate this slope when checked between consecutive integer points. For example, from $(2,3)$ to $(5,5)$, we have $\dfrac{5-3}{5-2} = \dfrac{2}{3} \approx 0.6667$. ✓

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Note on Rounding:** Always use `floor(v + 0.5)`. Some texts use `round-half-up`, but the KTU 2024 valuation key accepts either as long as the rule is **stated explicitly** in your answer.

---

## 3.3 Second Example — Negative Slope Case (All Quadrants)

### **Problem Statement**
Rasterise the line from $P(8, 10)$ to $Q(2, 4)$ using DDA.

### **Solution — Step 1: Differentials**

$$
\Delta x = 2 - 8 = -6, \qquad \Delta y = 4 - 10 = -6
$$

### **Solution — Step 2: Steps**

$$
\text{steps} = \max(\lvert -6 \rvert,\ \lvert -6 \rvert) = 6
$$

### **Solution — Step 3: Increments**

$$
X_{\text{inc}} = \frac{-6}{6} = -1.000, \qquad Y_{\text{inc}} = \frac{-6}{6} = -1.000
$$

### **Solution — Step 4: Iteration Table**

| $i$ | $x_i = 8 + i(-1)$ | $y_i = 10 + i(-1)$ | Rounded Pixel |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 0 | 8 | 10 | **(8, 10)** |
| 1 | 7 | 9  | **(7, 9)** |
| 2 | 6 | 8  | **(6, 8)** |
| 3 | 5 | 7  | **(5, 7)** |
| 4 | 4 | 6  | **(4, 6)** |
| 5 | 3 | 5  | **(3, 5)** |
| 6 | 2 | 4  | **(2, 4)** |

> **Observation:** This is a perfect 45° line (slope = 1) drawn in the reverse direction, and DDA handles it cleanly because both increments are integral.

---

## 3.4 Pitfall Case — Steep Vertical-Dominant Line

### **Problem Statement**
Rasterise $A(5, 1)$ to $B(6, 8)$.

### **Solution**

$$
\Delta x = 1, \qquad \Delta y = 7, \qquad \text{steps} = \max(1, 7) = 7
$$

$$
X_{\text{inc}} = \frac{1}{7} = 0.1429, \qquad Y_{\text{inc}} = \frac{7}{7} = 1.000
$$

| $i$ | $x_i$ | $y_i$ | Rounded Pixel |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 0 | 5.0000 | 1.00 | **(5, 1)** |
| 1 | 5.1429 | 2.00 | **(5, 2)** |
| 2 | 5.2857 | 3.00 | **(5, 3)** |
| 3 | 5.4286 | 4.00 | **(5, 4)** |
| 4 | 5.5714 | 5.00 | **(6, 5)** |
| 5 | 5.7143 | 6.00 | **(6, 6)** |
| 6 | 5.8571 | 7.00 | **(6, 7)** |
| 7 | 6.0000 | 8.00 | **(6, 8)** |

> **Critical observation:** $X_{\text{inc}} = 0.1429$ is a *recurring* fraction. After 7 additions, the accumulated floating-point error can shift the pixel choice. This is exactly why **Bresenham's algorithm** was invented — it uses only integer arithmetic.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Top-Level Algorithm Flow (Mermaid Flowchart)

The following Mermaid block captures the complete logical control flow of the DDA algorithm. All node IDs are alphanumeric (no reserved keywords), and all labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric text inside double quotes.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([START])
    nodeB[/"Read endpoints X1 Y1 X2 Y2"/]
    nodeC["Compute dx = X2 minus X1"]
    nodeD["Compute dy = Y2 minus Y1"]
    nodeE{"abs dx greater than abs dy"}
    nodeF["steps = abs dx"]
    nodeG["steps = abs dy"]
    nodeH["Compute Xinc = dx divided by steps"]
    nodeI["Compute Yinc = dy divided by steps"]
    nodeJ["Set X = X1, Y = Y1"]
    nodeK[/"Plot pixel round X round Y"/]
    nodeL{"i less than steps"}
    nodeM["X = X plus Xinc"]
    nodeN["Y = Y plus Yinc"]
    nodeO[/"Plot pixel round X round Y"/]
    nodeP[/"Increment i"/]
    nodeZ([END])

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE -- YES --> nodeF
    nodeE -- NO --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL
    nodeL -- YES --> nodeM
    nodeM --> nodeN
    nodeN --> nodeO
    nodeO --> nodeP
    nodeP --> nodeL
    nodeL -- NO --> nodeZ
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture

This Mermaid block represents the **block-level functional architecture** of the DDA subsystem inside a typical graphics pipeline. Each block corresponds to a hardware/software module that performs one stage of the rasterisation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inA["INPUT ENDPOINT REGISTERS X1 Y1 X2 Y2"]
    subA["DIFFERENTIAL COMPUTATION UNIT"]
    inA --> subA
    subC["MAJOR AXIS SELECTOR max abs dx abs dy"]
    subA --> subC
    subD["INCREMENT GENERATOR div dx dy by steps"]
    subC --> subD
    subE["INTEGER ROUNDER floor plus 0.5"]
    subD --> subE
    subF["PIXEL PLOTTER WRITER"]
    subE --> subF
    subG["FRAME BUFFER VRAM"]
    subF --> subG
    subH["DISPLAY CONTROLLER SCAN OUT"]
    subG --> subH
```

---

## 4.3 Conceptual Pixel-by-Pixel State Transition

This Mermaid diagram illustrates the **state transition of the running coordinates** as the algorithm iterates. It is useful for visualising how $(x, y)$ evolves step by step.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> S0
    S0 : STATE 0 X equals X1 Y equals Y1
    S1 : STATE 1 X equals X1 plus 1 Xinc Y equals Y1 plus 1 Yinc
    S2 : STATE 2 X equals X1 plus 2 Xinc Y equals Y1 plus 2 Yinc
    SN : STATE N X equals X2 Y equals Y2
    S0 --> S1 : ADD INCREMENT
    S1 --> S2 : ADD INCREMENT
    S2 --> SN : REPEAT STEPS MINUS 1 TIMES
    SN --> [*]
```

---

## 4.4 Comparative Architecture — DDA vs. Bresenham

The following block-level diagram compares the two line-drawing algorithms architecturally, highlighting where DDA's floating-point unit becomes a bottleneck relative to Bresenham's purely integer pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DDA["DDA PIPELINE"]
        dda1["FLOATING POINT ADDER"]
        dda2["DIVIDER FOR INCREMENTS"]
        dda3["ROUND TO NEAREST INTEGER"]
        dda4["WRITE PIXEL TO VRAM"]
        dda1 --> dda2 --> dda3 --> dda4
    end
    subgraph BRES["BRESENHAM PIPELINE"]
        br1["INTEGER ADDER"]
        br2["DECISION VARIABLE TEST"]
        br3["INTEGER ROUNDING TRIVIAL"]
        br4["WRITE PIXEL TO VRAM"]
        br1 --> br2 --> br3 --> br4
    end
    DDA -. "SLOWER ON INTEGER ONLY HARDWARE" .- BRES
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the DDA line drawing algorithm? Why is it called a "digital differential analyzer"?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks — Distribution Shown)

- **Definition (1 mark):** DDA is an incremental scan-conversion algorithm that uses the differential equation of a line, $dy/dx = m$, to generate successive pixel coordinates by adding small constant increments $X_{\text{inc}}$ and $Y_{\text{inc}}$ at each step.
- **Origin of the name (1 mark):** It is called a "Digital Differential Analyzer" because the algorithmic procedure is a **discrete (digital) simulation** of the **Differential Analyzer**, a mechanical/analog computer that solves differential equations by integration.
- **Salient property (1 mark):** The algorithm samples the line at uniform intervals along the major axis and is therefore **faster than the direct-slope method** because the inner loop performs only **additions**, not multiplications.

---

### **Question A2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any three advantages and two disadvantages of the DDA algorithm over the direct slope method $y = mx + c$.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Advantages (any three, ½ mark each = 1.5 marks):**

1. **Faster inner loop** — only two additions per pixel, no multiplication.
2. **No need to handle special cases** for vertical lines separately in the slope equation.
3. **Uniform sampling** along the major axis gives smoother lines for steep/shallow slopes.
4. **Simple to implement** in both hardware and software.

**Disadvantages (any two, ¾ mark each = 1.5 marks):**

1. **Floating-point arithmetic** required → slower on integer-only CPUs and susceptible to rounding errors.
2. **Accumulated rounding error** after many steps can shift pixels away from the true line.
3. **Rounding operation itself is costly** on some hardware.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to use absolute values** in the steps formula. Students often write `steps = max(dx, dy)` instead of `max(|dx|, |dy|)`. This **loses 1 mark** every single time.
> 2. **Not showing the rounding step explicitly.** If you just write the floating-point values, the examiner cannot award the mark for the integer pixel plot. **Always show `floor(v + 0.5)`.**
> 3. **Mixing up the order of endpoints** $(x_1, y_1)$ vs. $(x_2, y_2)$. Although the rasterised line is the same, the table headers change — inconsistent tables are penalised.
> 4. **Skipping the verification step** with the slope formula. Examiners often allocate 1 mark for "verification using $m = \Delta y / \Delta x$". Don't leave it on the table.

---

### **Question B-A (14 Marks) — Numerical + Theory Combo** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks:**
Using the DDA algorithm, rasterise the line segment from $(10, 2)$ to $(15, 9)$. Plot the integer pixels generated and verify your answer using the slope formula.

**Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks:**
Explain why $\text{steps} = \max\!\big(\lvert \Delta x \rvert,\ \lvert \Delta y \rvert\big)$ is chosen as the iteration count. What happens if we mistakenly use $\min$ instead of $\max$? Illustrate with an example where $\lvert \Delta x \rvert = 1$ and $\lvert \Delta y \rvert = 7$.

---

#### Model Solution — Sub-part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Differentials (1 mark):**
$$
\Delta x = 15 - 10 = 5, \qquad \Delta y = 9 - 2 = 7
$$

**Step 2 — Steps (1 mark):**
$$
\text{steps} = \max(\lvert 5 \rvert,\ \lvert 7 \rvert) = 7
$$

**Step 3 — Increments (1 mark):**
$$
X_{\text{inc}} = \frac{5}{7} \approx 0.7143, \qquad Y_{\text{inc}} = \frac{7}{7} = 1.000
$$

**Step 4 — Iteration Table (3 marks):**

| $i$ | $x_i$ | $y_i$ | Rounded Pixel | Rounding of $x_i$ |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|---|
| 0 | 10.0000 | 2.00 | (10, 2) | $\lfloor 10.0 + 0.5 \rfloor = 10$ |
| 1 | 10.7143 | 3.00 | (11, 3) | $\lfloor 10.7143 + 0.5 \rfloor = 11$ |
| 2 | 11.4286 | 4.00 | (11, 4) | $\lfloor 11.4286 + 0.5 \rfloor = 11$ |
| 3 | 12.1429 | 5.00 | (12, 5) | $\lfloor 12.1429 + 0.5 \rfloor = 12$ |
| 4 | 12.8571 | 6.00 | (13, 6) | $\lfloor 12.8571 + 0.5 \rfloor = 13$ |
| 5 | 13.5714 | 7.00 | (14, 7) | $\lfloor 13.5714 + 0.5 \rfloor = 14$ |
| 6 | 14.2857 | 8.00 | (14, 8) | $\lfloor 14.2857 + 0.5 \rfloor = 14$ |
| 7 | 15.0000 | 9.00 | (15, 9) | $\lfloor 15.0 + 0.5 \rfloor = 15$ |

**Step 5 — Verification (1 mark):**
True slope $m = 7/5 = 1.4$. Check between $(10, 2)$ and $(15, 9)$: $\dfrac{9-2}{15-10} = \dfrac{7}{5} = 1.4$. ✓

**Final pixel set:** $\{(10,2),(11,3),(11,4),(12,5),(13,6),(14,7),(14,8),(15,9)\}$.

---

#### Model Solution — Sub-part (b) [7 Marks]

**Why max is used (3 marks):**
- The number of steps must be large enough so that **every pixel along the longer (major) axis is covered**, with no gaps.
- If we chose $\min$, the algorithm would step too slowly along the major axis, leaving disconnected pixels in between.
- The line should be sampled at the rate of the **denser** dimension so that adjacent plotted pixels are guaranteed to be 4-connected or 8-connected.

**What happens if min is used (2 marks):**
- For $\lvert \Delta x \rvert = 1$, $\lvert \Delta y \rvert = 7$: if we use $\min = 1$, then $X_{\text{inc}} = 1/1 = 1$ and $Y_{\text{inc}} = 7/1 = 7$. The algorithm would plot only **two pixels** — the two endpoints. The line would be invisible in between.
- With the correct $\max = 7$, we get $X_{\text{inc}} = 1/7 \approx 0.143$ and $Y_{\text{inc}} = 1$, producing **8 properly connected pixels** along the steep line.

**Conclusion (2 marks):**
- Using $\max$ ensures that the algorithm produces a connected, visually continuous line for all slopes, including near-horizontal and near-vertical lines.
- This is the fundamental reason DDA produces visually correct rasterisations regardless of line orientation.

---
