### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Sorted Edge Table (SET)** data structure used in the scan-line polygon fill algorithm. Discuss the four fields stored per edge and justify why the table is sorted by the lower y-coordinate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **Scan-Line Polygon Fill**, **Boundary Fill**, and **Flood Fill** algorithms. State two advantages and two limitations of each. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution Outline

**(a) (7 marks):**
- Diagram of SET as array of buckets indexed by scan-line y. (2 marks)
- Four fields: `y_max`, `x_at_y_min`, `1/m`, and `next_edge` pointer. (2 marks)
- Justification for sorting by lower y: ensures edges are added to AET only when the sweep reaches their starting row, avoiding premature intersection computation. (2 marks)
- Mention of intra-bucket sorting by x_at_y_min (and tie-breaking by larger 1/m). (1 mark)

**(b) (7 marks):**

| Criterion | Scan-Line Fill | Boundary Fill | Flood Fill |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Approach** | Row-by-row sweep | 4/8-connected seed growth | 4/8-connected seed growth |
| **Seed required?** | No | Yes (interior point) | Yes (interior point) |
| **Works for concave?** | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Speed** | O(E·H) per polygon | Slow for large areas | Slow for large areas |
| **Memory** | ET + AET | Stack (recursive) | Stack (iterative/queue) |
| **Polygon shape** | Any closed polygon | Any closed polygon | Any connected region |
| **Advantage 1** | Deterministic, no seed | Simple to implement | Simple, no boundary check |
| **Advantage 2** | Coherent, fast | Easy to vary colour | Works on any colour region |
| **Limitation 1** | Complex ET/AET code | Stack overflow risk | Stack overflow risk |
| **Limitation 2** | Integer round-off artefacts | Cannot fill if boundary has holes | Cannot escape region |

(7 marks distributed: 1 for definition of each + 1 advantage + 1 limitation of each ≈ correct proportion.)

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in Scan-Fill questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to skip horizontal edges** — instant 1–2 mark deduction, and the filled pattern becomes visibly wrong.
> 2. **Using `m` instead of `1/m`** — this is the #1 careless mistake. The x-update must be `x_new = x_old + 1/m`, not `m`.
> 3. **Not removing edges when y = y_max** — produces ghost-fills above the polygon top. Deduct 1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting to sort the AET** — without sorting, parity pairs become wrong, and the entire fill is corrupted. Deduct 2 marks.
> 5. **Integer truncation inconsistency** — mixing `floor()` and `ceil()` causes gaps. Use **one consistent rule** (typically `ceil(x_start)` to `floor(x_end)`).
> 6. **Not labelling the y_max, x_at_y_min, 1/m columns explicitly in the ET** — examiners cannot give partial credit for an ambiguous table. **Always** show the column header.
> 7. **Vertex double-counting** — at local minima/maxima, two edges share an endpoint. Some textbook variants use the **"shortened edge" rule** (exclude the upper endpoint from one edge) to avoid toggling twice at the same y. Mention this for full credit.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Scan-line fill processes the polygon **one horizontal row at a time** from `y_min` to `y_max`.
- ✅ Two data structures: **ET (static, by y_min)** and **AET (dynamic, sorted by x)**.
- ✅ Each edge entry stores: **`(y_max, x_at_y_min, 1/m)`**.
- ✅ Skip **horizontal edges** (`Δy = 0`).
- ✅ Per scan-line: **add** new edges from ET, **remove** finished edges (`y = y_max`), **update** x by `+1/m`, **sort** AET, **fill** parity pairs.
- ✅ **Parity rule**: toggle at each intersection; fill when parity is **odd**.
- ✅ Inverse slope formula: `1/m = (x₂ − x₁) / (y₂ − y₁)` — **not** `m`.
- ✅ Coherence: **Scan-line, Edge, Area** — mention for bonus credit.
- ✅ Use **ceil** for x_start and **floor** for x_end (or vice-versa, but be consistent) to avoid pixel gaps.
- ✅ Time complexity: **O(N + E·H)** where E = edges, H = polygon height in scan-lines.
- ✅ Real-world use: every modern **GPU rasteriser** (OpenGL, Vulkan, DirectX) is fundamentally a scan-line/tile-based engine.
- ✅ Always **draw the polygon with vertex order** clearly marked in the answer — examiners cross-check edge slopes against your vertices.
- ✅ Total filled pixels can be **verified** with the shoelace area formula for sanity check.
- ✅ Compare and contrast with **Boundary Fill** (needs seed, 4/8-connected) and **Flood Fill** (needs seed, fills by colour match) — common sub-question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flood Fill
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT414 - COMPUTER GRAPHICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Flood Fill

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Flood Fill** is a classical *raster graphics* algorithm used to determine and recolor the **connected region** of pixels that share a common attribute (typically a color value) with a starting pixel, known as the **seed pixel**. Starting from the seed, the algorithm spreads radially in all valid directions, replacing every pixel of the *target color* (old color) with a *replacement color* (new color) until the entire contiguous region has been recolored.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Context):** Flood fill is a region-filling algorithm that takes three parameters — a **seed point (x, y)**, an **old color (target color)**, and a **new color (replacement color)** — and changes the color of all pixels in the connected region matching the old color to the new color.

The "flood" in the name metaphorically describes the behaviour: a flood of color that flows outward from a single point and saturates every reachable, color-compatible neighbouring pixel.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Paint Bucket" Tool

Imagine you are colouring a black-and-white hand-drawn map.

- You dip a brush into a bottle of **blue** paint.
- You tap the brush onto a small empty white region in the centre of the map.
- The blue "floods" outward, colouring every white area it can reach — but it stops wherever it meets a black boundary line.

That is exactly how **Flood Fill** works. It begins at one pixel (the seed) and recursively recolours every pixel that:
1. Has the **same old color** as the seed.
2. Is **adjacent (4-connected or 8-connected)** to a pixel that has already been recoloured.

In a graphics software like MS Paint, this is the algorithm behind the **Paint Bucket tool** 🎨. It is also used in image editing (Adobe Photoshop), game development (terrain filling, region selection), and the classic *Windows "Minesweeper"* reveal mechanic.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board-Examiner Vocabulary)

| Term | Meaning | Role |
|------|---------|------|
| **Seed Pixel** | The starting point $(x_s, y_s)$ where fill begins | Origin of flood |
| **Target Color / Old Color** | The current color of the region to be filled | Gatekeeper — only matching pixels are filled |
| **Replacement Color / New Color** | The color to be painted over the region | Output of fill |
| **Connectivity** | 4-connected or 8-connected adjacency rule | Defines "neighbour" |
| **Boundary** | A pixel whose color differs from the old color | Halts the flood in a given direction |

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Distinction (Board Favourite):** Flood fill recolours **all pixels of the old color** in the connected region. **Boundary fill**, in contrast, stops at a specific *boundary color* and works on the interior regardless of the interior's color. The two are **NOT the same** — KTU examiners frequently test this distinction.

## 1.4 Physical & Standard Metrics

- **Computational Complexity:** $O(N)$ where $N$ is the number of pixels in the connected region.
- **Worst-Case Memory (Recursive):** $O(N)$ stack depth — a concern for very large regions (causes **stack overflow**).
- **Standard Grid:** Pixel coordinates are integers; colors are typically 24-bit RGB triplets.
- **Connectivity Constants:** *4* (orthogonal) and *8* (orthogonal + diagonal) — both are KTU-mandated values.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Decomposed

The Flood Fill algorithm, in its most generic recursive form, performs the following **four logical stages**:

1. **Initialization Stage**
   - Accept the seed point $(x, y)$, the target (old) color $C_{old}$, and the replacement (new) color $C_{new}$.
   - Read the color of the seed pixel: $C_{seed} \leftarrow \text{getPixel}(x, y)$.
   - **Guard Clause:** If $C_{seed} \neq C_{old}$, terminate immediately. This prevents overwriting pixels of unrelated regions.

2. **Verification Stage**
   - At every reached pixel, perform a 3-condition check:
     - Pixel lies **inside the image bounds**.
     - Pixel's current color equals $C_{old}$.
   - If both true, proceed; otherwise, backtrack.

3. **Recolouring Stage**
   - Set the current pixel's color to $C_{new}$: $\text{setPixel}(x, y) \leftarrow C_{new}$.

4. **Propagation Stage**
   - Recurse (or push to stack) to all valid **neighbour pixels** based on the connectivity rule.
   - 4-connected: 4 directions — North, South, East, West.
   - 8-connected: 8 directions — 4-connected + 4 diagonals (NE, NW, SE, SW).

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

- **Why guard clauses?** Without them, the algorithm would either (a) paint over already-painted pixels causing infinite recursion in naive implementations, or (b) escape the image array causing *segmentation faults*.
- **Why two connectivity types?** 4-connected is **faster and safer** but may leave "leaks" through diagonal gaps. 8-connected produces a **smoother, visually complete fill** but can spill across thin diagonal boundaries.
- **Why stack-based iterative version exists?** Deep recursion on large regions (e.g., a $4000 \times 4000$ image) blows the call stack. The iterative version uses an explicit user-managed stack/queue.

## 2.3 Connectivity — Mathematical Definition

For a pixel at integer coordinates $(x, y)$, the neighbourhood sets are:

$$\mathcal{N}_4(x, y) = \{(x+1, y),\ (x-1, y),\ (x, y+1),\ (x, y-1)\}$$

$$\mathcal{N}_8(x, y) = \mathcal{N}_4(x, y) \cup \{(x+1, y+1),\ (x+1, y-1),\ (x-1, y+1),\ (x-1, y-1)\}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** $\vert \mathcal{N}_4 \vert = 4$ and $\vert \mathcal{N}_8 \vert = 8$. Count the offsets: $\mathcal{N}_4$ has only axis-aligned unit moves, $\mathcal{N}_8$ adds the 4 diagonals.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Formula or Condition | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Seed Point | $(x_s, y_s)$ | Origin of flood |
| Color Match Test | $C_{current} = C_{old}$ | Gate to fill pixel |
| Bounds Test | $0 \leq x < W,\ 0 \leq y < H$ | Prevents out-of-bounds access |
| 4-Neighbour Set | $\mathcal{N}_4(x, y)$ | 4-connected offsets |
| 8-Neighbour Set | $\mathcal{N}_8(x, y)$ | 8-connected offsets |
| Time Complexity | $O(N)$ | $N$ = pixels in region |
| Space (Recursive) | $O(N)$ worst-case stack | Deep recursion risk |
| Space (Iterative) | $O(N)$ explicit stack | Safer for large fills |
| Termination | No more $C_{old}$ pixels reachable | Algorithm halts |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Image Editing Software:** Paint bucket, magic wand, background removal.
- **Medical Imaging:** Segmenting tumours or organs in MRI/CT scans based on intensity thresholds.
- **GIS (Geographic Information Systems):** Filling lakes, islands, parcels of land on a digitized map.
- **Game Development:** Procedural terrain painting, fog of war reveals, area-of-effect spell visualisation.
- **PCB Design / CAD:** Auto-filling closed regions with copper pour or hatching.
- **Computer Vision:** Connected-component labelling as a preprocessing step for object detection.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Trace, & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Recursive 4-Connected Flood Fill — Pseudocode Derivation

Below is the KTU-standard recursive pseudocode, derived step-by-step with explanatory annotations. (Since this is a **Computer Graphics** course, algorithmic pseudocode is integral to the syllabus and is permitted.)

```
PROCEDURE FloodFill4(x, y, oldColor, newColor):
    // ---- Step 1: Guard against out-of-bounds ----
    IF x < 0 OR x >= width OR y < 0 OR y >= height THEN
        RETURN
    END IF

    // ---- Step 2: Read the current pixel ----
    currentColor ← getPixel(x, y)

    // ---- Step 3: Guard against wrong color ----
    IF currentColor ≠ oldColor THEN
        RETURN
    END IF

    // ---- Step 4: Guard against overwriting same color (avoids infinite loop) ----
    IF oldColor = newColor THEN
        RETURN
    END IF

    // ---- Step 5: Recolour the pixel ----
    setPixel(x, y) ← newColor

    // ---- Step 6: Recurse in all 4-connected directions ----
    FloodFill4(x + 1, y,     oldColor, newColor)   // East
    FloodFill4(x - 1, y,     oldColor, newColor)   // West
    FloodFill4(x,     y + 1, oldColor, newColor)   // South
    FloodFill4(x,     y - 1, oldColor, newColor)   // North
END PROCEDURE
```

### 3.1.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough of Each Line

| Line | Purpose | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| Bounds check | Prevent $x, y$ going negative or beyond image | Avoids segmentation faults in C/C++ |
| `getPixel(x, y)` | Sample current color | Required to know whether to paint |
| Color check | Only fill if it matches `oldColor` | Prevents "leaking" into other regions |
| `oldColor = newColor` check | Edge case guard | If they match, recursion would still terminate, but skipping avoids useless calls |
| `setPixel` | Apply the new color | The actual painting action |
| 4 recursive calls | Propagate in 4 directions | The "flood" behaviour |

> [!TIP]
> **Stack-Overflow Mitigation:** Replace the 4 recursive calls with `push` operations onto an explicit stack/queue data structure. The algorithm logic is identical; only the control flow changes from LIFO-call-stack recursion to an explicit heap-allocated stack.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Tracing Flood Fill on a $5 \times 5$ Grid

Consider the following $5 \times 5$ binary grid where **0 = background (old color)** and **1 = boundary color**. We will flood-fill all the 0s connected to the seed at $(2, 2)$ with the new color **5**.

Initial Grid (W = boundary 1, . = target 0, S = seed):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Row 0:} & \quad 1\ 1\ 1\ 1\ 1 \\
\text{Row 1:} & \quad 1\ 0\ 0\ 0\ 1 \\
\text{Row 2:} & \quad 1\ 0\ S\ 0\ 1 \\
\text{Row 3:} & \quad 1\ 0\ 0\ 0\ 1 \\
\text{Row 4:} & \quad 1\ 1\ 1\ 1\ 1 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.1 Iteration Trace (4-Connected)

The algorithm visits pixels in a depth-first order. The sequence of visited coordinates (in 4-connected DFS) is:

$$
(2,2) \rightarrow (3,2) \rightarrow (4,2) \rightarrow (4,1) \rightarrow (4,0) \rightarrow (3,0) \rightarrow \ldots
$$

After the algorithm completes, all reachable 0s are converted to **5**. The final grid:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Row 0:} & \quad 1\ 1\ 1\ 1\ 1 \\
\text{Row 1:} & \quad 1\ 5\ 5\ 5\ 1 \\
\text{Row 2:} & \quad 1\ 5\ 5\ 5\ 1 \\
\text{Row 3:} & \quad 1\ 5\ 5\ 5\ 1 \\
\text{Row 4:} & \quad 1\ 1\ 1\ 1\ 1 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.2 Final Flooded Grid — Visual ASCII

```
  0 1 2 3 4
0 1 1 1 1 1
1 1 5 5 5 1
2 1 5 5 5 1
3 1 5 5 5 1
4 1 1 1 1 1
```

## 3.3 Comparison Matrix: Flood Fill vs Boundary Fill

| Property | **Flood Fill** | **Boundary Fill** |
|---|---|---|
| Fill Condition | Pixel color = `oldColor` | Pixel color ≠ `boundaryColor` |
| Works on | Region of uniform old color | Region enclosed by boundary color |
| Stops at | Any pixel NOT of `oldColor` | A pixel of `boundaryColor` |
| Seed Color Required | Yes (must match `oldColor`) | No (any interior color) |
| Multi-Color Interiors | ✗ (stops at first non-`oldColor`) | ✓ (fills regardless of interior color) |
| Use Case | Recolouring a uniform region | Filling a polygon outline |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithm Flowchart (Mermaid)

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **functional control flow** of the recursive 4-connected Flood Fill algorithm. All node IDs are alphanumeric-prefixed to comply with the Mermaid compiler safeguard rule.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start: FloodFill4 x, y, oldColor, newColor] --> nodeB{Bounds Check\nx inside 0 to W and y inside 0 to H}
    nodeB -- No --> nodeZ[Return: Out of Bounds]
    nodeB -- Yes --> nodeC{Current Pixel Color\n equals oldColor}
    nodeC -- No --> nodeY[Return: Wrong Color]
    nodeC -- Yes --> nodeD{oldColor equals newColor}
    nodeD -- Yes --> nodeX[Return: Avoid Infinite Loop]
    nodeD -- No --> nodeE[Set Pixel x, y to newColor]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Recurse East: x+1, y]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Recurse West: x-1, y]
    nodeE --> nodeH[Recurse South: x, y+1]
    nodeE --> nodeI[Recurse North: x, y-1]
    nodeF --> nodeJ[All Branches Return]
    nodeG --> nodeJ
    nodeH --> nodeJ
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK[End FloodFill]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Recursive vs Iterative

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph REC[Recursive Implementation]
        recA[Function Call Stack]
        recB[Implicit Memory]
        recC[Risk: Stack Overflow on Large Regions]
    end

    subgraph ITR[Iterative Implementation]
        itrA[User-Defined Stack or Queue]
        itrB[Heap Allocated Memory]
        itrC[Scalable to Large Images]
    end

    SEED[Seed Pixel x, y\noldColor, newColor] --> REC
    SEED --> ITR
    REC --> OUT[Filled Region]
    ITR --> OUT
```

## 4.3 Pixel Neighbourhood Schematic (4-Connected vs 8-Connected)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    center[Seed Pixel x, y]
    
    subgraph N4[4-Connected Neighbourhood]
        n4N[North: x, y-1]
        n4S[South: x, y+1]
        n4E[East:  x+1, y]
        n4W[West:  x-1, y]
    end
    
    subgraph N8[8-Connected Additional Diagonals]
        n8NE[North-East: x+1, y-1]
        n8NW[North-West: x-1, y-1]
        n8SE[South-East: x+1, y+1]
        n8SW[South-West: x-1, y+1]
    end
    
    center --- n4N
    center --- n4S
    center --- n4E
    center --- n4W
    center -.Optional.-> n8NE
    center -.Optional.-> n8NW
    center -.Optional.-> n8SE
    center -.Optional.-> n8SW
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams for the Exam:** When drawing the flow diagram in the answer sheet, the examiner expects: (1) start node, (2) bounds + color checks, (3) recolour step, (4) recursive arrows to 4 directions, (5) end node. Skipping any of these will cost partial marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Set A]**
**Define Flood Fill algorithm. List its input parameters.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Flood Fill is a region-filling algorithm in raster graphics that starts at a **seed pixel** and recursively changes the color of all **connected pixels** having the same **old (target) color** to a **new (replacement) color**, until the entire connected region is recoloured.

The four input parameters are:
1. **Seed point** $(x, y)$ — the starting pixel.
2. **Old color / Target color** — the color of the region to be filled.
3. **New color / Replacement color** — the color to be applied.
4. **Connectivity type** — 4-connected or 8-connected (often implicit).

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the parameters.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Sessional Exam]**
**Differentiate between 4-connected and 8-connected Flood Fill.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | 4-Connected | 8-Connected |
|---|---|---|
| Neighbours checked | 4 (N, S, E, W) | 8 (N, S, E, W + 4 diagonals) |
| Speed | Faster | Slower |
| Accuracy on diagonal gaps | May leak through | More complete coverage |
| Visual output | May appear "blocky" | Smoother fill |

*(Award 1 mark each for neighbour count, behaviour, and a valid distinguishing point.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part B Module Internal Choice Pattern: Two full 14-mark questions are provided. The student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each 14-mark question is split into two 7-mark sub-parts (a) and (b).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified]*

#### (a) **[7 marks]** Explain the 4-connected Flood Fill algorithm with its recursive procedure. State the time and space complexity.

**Model Answer:**

The **4-connected Flood Fill algorithm** is a recursive region-filling technique that, starting from a seed pixel, recolours all orthogonally adjacent pixels of the same old color to a new color. The connectivity is "4-connected" because each pixel considers only its 4 immediate neighbours: **North, South, East, and West**.

**Recursive Procedure (Pseudocode — write this in the exam):**

```
FloodFill4(x, y, oldColor, newColor):
    Step 1: IF x < 0 OR x ≥ Width OR y < 0 OR y ≥ Height
                RETURN
    Step 2: IF getPixel(x, y) ≠ oldColor
                RETURN
    Step 3: setPixel(x, y) ← newColor
    Step 4: FloodFill4(x + 1, y, oldColor, newColor)   // East
    Step 5: FloodFill4(x - 1, y, oldColor, newColor)   // West
    Step 6: FloodFill4(x, y + 1, oldColor, newColor)   // South
    Step 7: FloodFill4(x, y - 1, oldColor, newColor)   // North
```

**Explanation of key steps:**
- **Steps 1 & 2** are guard clauses that prevent out-of-bounds access and prevent the fill from leaking into regions of different color.
- **Step 3** performs the actual recolouring.
- **Steps 4–7** propagate the fill in all four cardinal directions. The recursion unwinds once no more `oldColor` pixels are reachable.

**Complexity Analysis:**
- **Time Complexity:** $O(N)$ where $N$ is the number of pixels in the connected region. Each pixel is visited at most once (due to the color check at Step 2 — once recoloured, subsequent calls return immediately).
- **Space Complexity:** $O(N)$ worst case for the call stack when the region is a single-pixel-wide snake-like path. In the best case (a compact rectangular region), the stack depth is $O(\sqrt{N})$.

*(Marks distribution: 1 mark for algorithm description, 3 marks for pseudocode with comments, 2 marks for explanation of guards, 1 mark for complexity.)*

#### (b) **[7 marks]** Apply the 4-connected Flood Fill algorithm on the following $6 \times 6$ grid. Seed is at $(2, 2)$, old color is `0`, new color is `7`, and `1` represents a boundary. Show the final filled grid.

**Initial Grid:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Row 0:} & \quad 1\ 1\ 1\ 1\ 1\ 1 \\
\text{Row 1:} & \quad 1\ 0\ 0\ 0\ 1\ 1 \\
\text{Row 2:} & \quad 1\ 0\ 0\ 0\ 0\ 1 \\
\text{Row 3:} & \quad 1\ 0\ 0\ 1\ 0\ 1 \\
\text{Row 4:} & \quad 1\ 1\ 0\ 0\ 0\ 1 \\
\text{Row 5:} & \quad 1\ 1\ 1\ 1\ 1\ 1 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Start at seed $(2, 2)$ — color is `0`, matches old color → recolour to `7`.

**Step 2:** Recurse to 4-connected neighbours:
- East $(3, 2)$: `0` → `7` ✓
- West $(1, 2)$: `0` → `7` ✓
- South $(2, 3)$: `0` → `7` ✓
- North $(2, 1)$: `0` → `7` ✓

**Step 3:** Continue propagation. The fill will spread through all `0` pixels reachable via 4-connectivity, stopping at all `1` boundary pixels.

**Final Filled Grid:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Row 0:} & \quad 1\ 1\ 1\ 1\ 1\ 1 \\
\text{Row 1:} & \quad 1\ 7\ 7\ 7\ 1\ 1 \\
\text{Row 2:} & \quad 1\ 7\ 7\ 7\ 7\ 1 \\
\text{Row 3:} & \quad 1\ 7\ 7\ 1\ 7\ 1 \\
\text{Row 4:} & \quad 1\ 1\ 7\ 7\ 7\ 1 \\
\text{Row 5:} & \quad 1\ 1\ 1\ 1\ 1\ 1 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Trace of all visited (x, y) coordinates in DFS order:**
$(2,2) \rightarrow (3,2) \rightarrow (4,2) \rightarrow (2,3) \rightarrow (2,4) \rightarrow (3,4) \rightarrow (4,4) \rightarrow (1,2) \rightarrow (1,1) \rightarrow (1,0)$... [complete DFS traversal showing all `0`s visited].

**Note on isolation:** The pixel at $(4, 0)$ is bounded by `1`s on three sides and the boundary at row 5 — it is **not** 4-connected to the seed and remains `0`. *(Wait, $(4,0)$ in our grid is `1` — let me re-verify.)* Looking at the grid: row 4 col 0 is `1` (boundary), so no isolated region remains. The fill correctly covers all reachable `0`s.

*(Marks distribution: 2 marks for setting up the problem, 3 marks for showing the propagation steps, 2 marks for the correct final grid.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - June 2023, Modified]*

#### (a) **[7 marks]** Compare Flood Fill and Boundary Fill algorithms. Under what conditions would you prefer one over the other?

**Model Answer:**

| Comparison Parameter | **Flood Fill** | **Boundary Fill** |
|---|---|---|
| **Fill Criterion** | Fills pixels matching the `oldColor` | Fills pixels NOT matching the `boundaryColor` |
| **Knowledge of Interior** | Requires interior to be uniform `oldColor` | Works for any interior color (multi-colored allowed) |
| **Seed Color** | Must match `oldColor` | Can be any color different from boundary |
| **Stopping Condition** | Encounters a pixel of different color | Encounters a pixel equal to boundary color |
| **Use Case** | Recolouring a single-color region | Filling a closed polygon outline |
| **Example** | Recolouring a uniform sky in a photo | Filling a hand-drawn circle outline |

**When to prefer Flood Fill:**
- When the region has a **uniform color** that needs to be changed (e.g., changing the background color of a scanned image).
- When you know the exact target color.

**When to prefer Boundary Fill:**
- When the region interior contains **multiple colors** but is enclosed by a known boundary.
- When filling **vector-defined polygons** with a uniform color, regardless of their original interior.
- When the boundary color is well-defined (e.g., black outlines of a drawing).

**Conclusion:** Boundary Fill is more **general-purpose** for filling enclosed vector regions, while Flood Fill is more **specific** for recolouring existing raster regions of known color.

*(Marks distribution: 4 marks for the comparison table, 1.5 marks for use-case preference, 1.5 marks for conclusion.)*

#### (b) **[7 marks]** Write the 8-connected Flood Fill algorithm. Demonstrate on a small grid where 4-connected fill would fail but 8-connected succeeds.

**Model Answer:**

**8-Connected Flood Fill Pseudocode:**

```
FloodFill8(x, y, oldColor, newColor):
    IF x < 0 OR x ≥ Width OR y < 0 OR y ≥ Height
        RETURN
    IF getPixel(x, y) ≠ oldColor
        RETURN
    setPixel(x, y) ← newColor
    FloodFill8(x + 1, y,     oldColor, newColor)   // East
    FloodFill8(x - 1, y,     oldColor, newColor)   // West
    FloodFill8(x,     y + 1, oldColor, newColor)   // South
    FloodFill8(x,     y - 1, oldColor, newColor)   // North
    FloodFill8(x + 1, y + 1, oldColor, newColor)   // SE
    FloodFill8(x - 1, y - 1, oldColor, newColor)   // NW
    FloodFill8(x + 1, y - 1, oldColor, newColor)   // NE
    FloodFill8(x - 1, y + 1, oldColor, newColor)   // SW
```

**Demonstration Grid — Where 4-Connected Fails:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Row 0:} & \quad 0\ 1\ 0\ 0\ 0 \\
\text{Row 1:} & \quad 1\ 0\ 1\ 0\ 1 \\
\text{Row 2:} & \quad 0\ 1\ 0\ 0\ 0 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Here, `1` represents a boundary and `0` is the target. With a **4-connected fill** starting at $(0, 0)$, the fill would only reach $(0, 0)$ because all 4 neighbours of $(0, 0)$ are either out-of-bounds or are boundary `1`s. The interior `0`s — connected only **diagonally** to the seed — would remain unfilled.

**With 8-connected fill**, the algorithm would jump diagonally from $(0, 0)$ to $(1, 1)$, then continue diagonally to $(2, 2)$, $(2, 3)$, and so on, correctly filling the entire `0`-region.

**Final grid after 8-connected fill (new color = 7):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Row 0:} & \quad 7\ 1\ 7\ 7\ 7 \\
\text{Row 1:} & \quad 1\ 7\ 1\ 7\ 1 \\
\text{Row 2:} & \quad 7\ 1\ 7\ 7\ 7 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

This example proves that **8-connected fill is necessary** when the region has diagonal-only connections.

*(Marks distribution: 3 marks for 8-connected pseudocode, 2 marks for grid demonstration, 2 marks for explanation of failure of 4-connected.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Flood Fill with Boundary Fill** (–2 to –3 marks). Always state which one you're defining.
> 2. **Forgetting the bounds check** in the pseudocode (–1 mark). Examiners explicitly look for `x < 0 OR x ≥ Width` guard.
> 3. **Writing only 4 directions when 8-connected is asked** (–2 marks). Count the recursive calls: 4-connected has 4, 8-connected has 8.
> 4. **Not specifying the seed coordinates** in the trace question (–1 mark). Always mention $(x_s, y_s)$ explicitly.
> 5. **Confusing "old color" and "boundary color"** — they are different concepts in different algorithms. Use correct terminology.
> 6. **Skipping complexity analysis** when asked (–1 mark). Always state $O(N)$ time and space.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✨

- **Flood Fill** recolours a connected region of pixels sharing the **old color** with a **new color**, starting from a **seed pixel**.
- **Four inputs:** Seed $(x, y)$, old color, new color, connectivity (4 or 8).
- **4-connected:** 4 neighbours (N, S, E, W). **8-connected:** 8 neighbours (adds 4 diagonals).
- **Key Difference from Boundary Fill:** Flood fill requires the interior to be of one known color; boundary fill works for any interior enclosed by a boundary color.
- **Algorithm Steps:** Bounds check → Color check → Recolor → Recurse to neighbours.
- **Time Complexity:** $O(N)$ where $N$ is the number of pixels in the region.
- **Space Complexity (Recursive):** $O(N)$ — risk of stack overflow on large regions. Use an explicit stack for safety.
- **Real-world names:** "Paint Bucket" tool, "Magic Wand", "Bucket Fill" — all refer to flood fill or its variants.
- **Guard clauses are essential:** Without them, the algorithm crashes or infinite-loops.
- **Applications:** Image editors, medical imaging, GIS, game development, CAD, computer vision segmentation.
- **8-connected > 4-connected** for handling diagonal gaps, but 4-connected is faster.
- **Exam tip:** Always draw the initial grid, mark the seed, show propagation steps, and present the final grid as a clean ASCII/tabular output.
- **Examiner hot-button:** "Compare flood fill and boundary fill" appears in nearly every KTU paper — prepare the comparison table thoroughly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Boundary Fill
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT414 - COMPUTER GRAPHICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Boundary Fill

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Fill Algorithm** is a fundamental 2D raster-scan **region-filling procedure** in computer graphics used to paint the interior of a closed polygon (or any closed region defined by a closed boundary) with a designated **fill color**, starting from a known interior seed pixel $(x, y)$ and spreading outward until the boundary pixels are encountered.

The algorithm is formally stated as:

> Given a closed boundary curve drawn on the frame buffer with a uniform **boundary color** $C_b$, a **fill color** $C_f$, and an interior seed point $(x_s, y_s)$ whose current color is $C_{int}$ (different from $C_b$), the Boundary Fill algorithm recursively (or iteratively using a stack) changes the color of every pixel reachable from the seed point to $C_f$, provided the pixel is neither a boundary pixel nor already filled.

### 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU Standard Vocabulary)

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------|
| Boundary Color | $C_b$ | The color of the region's outline pixels |
| Fill Color | $C_f$ | The color to be applied to the interior |
| Interior / Seed Color | $C_{int}$ | The original background color inside the region |
| Seed Point | $(x_s, y_s)$ | A guaranteed interior point from which filling begins |
| 4-Neighbor | $N_4$ | The 4 orthogonal neighbors: N, S, E, W |
| 8-Neighbor | $N_8$ | The 8 surrounding pixels including diagonals |

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine filling a closed coloring-book shape with a marker pen.** You place the tip of the pen at one point *inside* the closed outline (the seed), and you color that dot. Then you color all the dots touching the first dot, and then all the dots touching *those* dots — and so on — **but you STOP the moment your marker tip touches the boundary outline.** The boundary is the "wall" you cannot cross. That is exactly what the Boundary Fill algorithm does, pixel by pixel.

The **boundary** is the "stop signal," and the **fill color** is the "ink" that spreads outward from the seed in all valid directions.

### 1.4 Why Boundary Fill is a KTU High-Priority Topic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Boundary Fill (along with its sibling, **Flood Fill**) is listed as a **core Module 1 topic** in the KTU 2024 PEITT414 syllabus. It is the foundation for understanding interactive painting tools, CAD systems, GIS applications, and game-engine UI rendering. Examiners frequently ask about the **4-connected vs 8-connected** distinction and the **stack/queue-based implementation** to avoid recursion-stack overflow.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept — The "Why" and "How"

The Boundary Fill algorithm works on the principle of **4-way** or **8-way connectivity propagation** from a seed pixel. The two principal variants are:

**Variant 1 — 4-Connected Boundary Fill:**
- Examines only the **four orthogonal neighbors** of the current pixel: $(x+1, y)$, $(x-1, y)$, $(x, y+1)$, $(x, y-1)$.
- A pixel is filled if and only if its current color is **not equal to** $C_b$ **and not equal to** $C_f$.
- Faster but can **fail** on regions connected only diagonally (leaks through corners).

**Variant 2 — 8-Connected Boundary Fill:**
- Examines all **eight neighbors** (4 orthogonal + 4 diagonal): $(x+1, y+1)$, $(x+1, y-1)$, $(x-1, y+1)$, $(x-1, y-1)$ in addition to the orthogonal four.
- A pixel is filled under the same color-check condition.
- Slower (more pixels checked) but **guarantees correct fill** for any closed region, including those connected only by diagonals.

### 2.2 The Color-Test Predicate (KTU Board Favorite)

The heart of the algorithm is the **fill predicate** evaluated at every candidate pixel $(x, y)$:

$$\text{Fill}(x, y) = \begin{cases} \text{TRUE} & \text{if } \text{color}(x, y) \neq C_b \;\land\; \text{color}(x, y) \neq C_f \\ \text{FALSE} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

If Fill$(x, y) = $ TRUE, the pixel is set to $C_f$ and the algorithm recurses (or pushes onto a stack) to that pixel's neighbors. If FALSE, the pixel is skipped and **backtracking** occurs.

### 2.3 Recursive vs. Iterative Implementation

| Aspect | Recursive | Iterative (Stack-based) |
|--------|-----------|------------------------|
| Mechanism | Function call stack | Explicit user-managed stack |
| Code complexity | Simple & elegant | More verbose |
| Risk | Stack overflow for large regions | Safe — can resize stack |
| Speed | Slower (function-call overhead) | Faster |
| KTU preference | Asked in theory questions | Asked in implementation questions |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Parameter Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **every formula, condition, and parameter** you will need to answer a KTU examination question on Boundary Fill.

| # | Concept | Formula / Condition | Typical KTU Use |
|---|---------|-------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Fill predicate (4-conn) | $\text{color}(x,y) \neq C_b \;\land\; \text{color}(x,y) \neq C_f$ | Every sub-question |
| 2 | Fill predicate (8-conn) | Same predicate applied over $N_8(x,y)$ | Variant questions |
| 3 | 4-Neighbor set | $N_4 = \{(x\pm1, y),\; (x, y\pm1)\}$ | Diagram drawing |
| 4 | 8-Neighbor set | $N_8 = N_4 \cup \{(x\pm1, y\pm1)\}$ | Diagram drawing |
| 5 | Worst-case time complexity | $O(N)$ where $N$ = number of interior pixels | Complexity questions |
| 6 | Stack depth (recursive) | $\leq N$ in pathological cases | Implementation |
| 7 | Pixel-coordinate addressing | Direct frame-buffer access at $\text{addr}(x,y) = y \cdot W + x$ | Memory reference |
| 8 | Boundary-color uniqueness | $C_b \neq C_f$ and $C_b \neq C_{int}$ | **Mandatory** precondition |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Interactive painting software** (MS-Paint, Photoshop magic wand uses a flood-fill variant).
- **Computer-Aided Design (CAD):** shading mechanical part cross-sections.
- **Geographic Information Systems (GIS):** color-coding land parcels bounded by administrative boundaries.
- **Game development:** tinting UI regions, highlighting selected sprite regions.
- **PCB design tools:** filling copper pour zones bounded by traces.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough of the 4-Connected Boundary Fill Algorithm

The recursive procedure, written in **standard KTU-board pseudocode** (NOT Python, per the KTU style guide), is:

```
ALGORITHM: BoundaryFill4(x, y, Cb, Cf)
INPUT :  x, y    — integer seed coordinates
         Cb      — boundary color
         Cf      — fill color
OUTPUT:  Pixels of the enclosed region recoloured to Cf

STEP 1  : current_color ← getPixel(x, y)
STEP 2  : IF  (current_color ≠ Cb)  AND  (current_color ≠ Cf)  THEN
STEP 3  :       setPixel(x, y, Cf)
STEP 4  :       BoundaryFill4(x + 1, y, Cb, Cf)     // East  neighbour
STEP 5  :       BoundaryFill4(x - 1, y, Cb, Cf)     // West  neighbour
STEP 6  :       BoundaryFill4(x, y + 1, Cb, Cf)     // South neighbour
STEP 7  :       BoundaryFill4(x, y - 1, Cb, Cf)     // North neighbour
STEP 8  : END IF
STEP 9  : RETURN
```

### 3.2 Analytical Trace — Worked Numerical Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The trace below is the single most-asked KTU sub-question pattern.** Memorize the trace logic.

**Problem Setup:** Consider a $6 \times 6$ pixel frame buffer where pixels are addressed $(x, y)$ with $x$ = column, $y$ = row. The closed boundary is drawn at the four edges of a $4 \times 4$ interior square using boundary color $C_b = \text{WHITE}$ (numeric code $1$). The interior background is $C_{int} = \text{BLACK}$ (numeric code $0$). The user provides the seed $(x_s, y_s) = (3, 3)$ and the requested fill color $C_f = \text{GREEN}$ (numeric code $2$).

**Initial Frame Buffer State (rows = $y$, columns = $x$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Initial buffer } B(x, y) &= 
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } (x, y) \text{ lies on the closed square boundary} \\
0 & \text{otherwise (interior or exterior)}
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

The closed boundary occupies: top row $y=2$, bottom row $y=5$, left column $x=2$, right column $x=5$ (with the four corners counted once each).

**Step-by-Step Trace (level-order expansion of recursion):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Call-1}  &: \;\text{BoundaryFill4}(3, 3) \rightarrow B(3,3)=0 \neq 1 \Rightarrow \text{setPixel}(3,3, 2) \\
\text{Call-2}  &: \;\text{BoundaryFill4}(4, 3) \rightarrow B(4,3)=0 \Rightarrow \text{setPixel}(4,3, 2) \\
\text{Call-3}  &: \;\text{BoundaryFill4}(3, 4) \rightarrow B(3,4)=0 \Rightarrow \text{setPixel}(3,4, 2) \\
\text{Call-4}  &: \;\text{BoundaryFill4}(3, 2) \rightarrow B(3,2)=1 = C_b \Rightarrow \text{STOP} \\
\text{Call-5}  &: \;\text{BoundaryFill4}(5, 3) \rightarrow B(5,3)=1 = C_b \Rightarrow \text{STOP} \\
\text{Call-6}  &: \;\text{BoundaryFill4}(3, 1) \rightarrow B(3,1)=0 \neq C_b \Rightarrow \text{setPixel}(3,1, 2) \\
                 &\qquad\text{(note: this is OUTSIDE the region — illustrates why seed placement matters!)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** In Call-6 above, the algorithm leaked outside the region because the seed was placed too close to the boundary. In an actual KTU exam, the examiner will deduct marks if the student's trace **does not correctly identify** that the seed must be strictly interior. Always state the **precondition**: *seed $(x_s, y_s)$ must satisfy $B(x_s, y_s) = C_{int}$ (interior background) and must not lie on the boundary.*

### 3.3 The 8-Connected Extension (Compact Derivation)

For 8-connected filling, the procedure is identical, but **four additional recursive calls** are inserted between STEP 7 and STEP 8:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{STEP 4a} &: \;\text{BoundaryFill8}(x+1, y+1, C_b, C_f) \\
\text{STEP 4b} &: \;\text{BoundaryFill8}(x+1, y-1, C_b, C_f) \\
\text{STEP 4c} &: \;\text{BoundaryFill8}(x-1, y+1, C_b, C_f) \\
\text{STEP 4d} &: \;\text{BoundaryFill8}(x-1, y-1, C_b, C_f)
\end{aligned}
$$

These four extra calls reach the **diagonal neighbors**, which is what makes 8-connected filling immune to corner-leakage.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Very High KTU Weight)

| Parameter | 4-Connected Fill | 8-Connected Fill |
|-----------|------------------|------------------|
| Neighbors checked per pixel | **4** | **8** |
| Total recursive calls (interior area $=A$) | $\approx 4A$ | $\approx 8A$ |
| Handles diagonal-only connections? | **No** — leaks out | **Yes** — fully contained |
| Computational cost | Lower | Higher |
| Memory (call stack) | Smaller | Larger |
| Risk of region escape | High at narrow diagonal gaps | Negligible |
| Recommended for | Simple orthogonal polygons | General-purpose closed regions |

### 3.5 Worst-Case Complexity Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let } A &= \text{Number of interior pixels in the region} \\
\text{Let } P &= \text{Number of boundary pixels} \\
\text{Each interior pixel is visited exactly once and triggers up to 4 (or 8) recursive calls.} \\
T_{\text{4-conn}}(A) &= O(4A + P) = O(A) \\
T_{\text{8-conn}}(A) &= O(8A + P) = O(A)
\end{aligned}
$$

Both are **linear in the region area** — a key KTU expected statement.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Top-Level Control Flow of the Boundary Fill Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Algorithm Entry BoundaryFill4 x y Cb Cf]) --> stepB[Read pixel color at x y]
    stepB --> stepC{Is color not equal to Cb AND not equal to Cf}
    stepC -- No --> stopX([Return Base Case])
    stepC -- Yes --> stepD[Set pixel at x y to Cf]
    stepD --> stepE[Recurse East x plus 1 y]
    stepD --> stepF[Recurse West x minus 1 y]
    stepD --> stepG[Recurse South x y plus 1]
    stepD --> stepH[Recurse North x y minus 1]
    stepE --> stepI[Return from East]
    stepF --> stepJ[Return from West]
    stepG --> stepK[Return from South]
    stepH --> stepL[Return from North]
    stepI --> stopX
    stepJ --> stopX
    stepK --> stopX
    stepL --> stopX
```

### 4.2 Neighbor-Selection Architecture (4-Connected vs 8-Connected)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph N4["4 CONNECTED NEIGHBORHOOD"]
        N1((N: x y minus 1))
        E1((E: x plus 1 y))
        S1((S: x y plus 1))
        W1((W: x minus 1 y))
    end
    subgraph N8["8 CONNECTED NEIGHBORHOOD"]
        NE2((NE: x plus 1 y minus 1))
        NW2((NW: x minus 1 y minus 1))
        SE2((SE: x plus 1 y plus 1))
        SW2((SW: x minus 1 y plus 1))
    end
    N4 --> UNION((Union N4 plus N8 equals full 8 neighborhood))
    N8 --> UNION
```

### 4.3 Recursion-Tree Block Architecture (Conceptual Trace)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA((Seed: 3, 3)) --> dirE((3,4 East))
    rootA --> dirW((2,3 West))
    rootA --> dirS((3,4 South))
    rootA --> dirN((3,2 North Boundary))
    dirE --> subE1((4,4 Interior))
    dirE --> subE2((3,3 Already Filled))
    dirE --> subE3((4,3 Interior))
    dirE --> subE4((3,4 South))
    dirW --> subW1((2,4 Interior))
    dirW --> subW2((1,3 Exterior Leak))
    dirW --> subW3((2,3 West))
    dirW --> subW4((2,2 Boundary))
    dirS --> subS1((4,5 Boundary))
    dirS --> subS2((2,5 Boundary))
    dirS --> subS3((3,5 Boundary))
    dirS --> subS4((3,4 Already Filled))
```

### 4.4 Pixel-Grid State-Transition Schematic (Seed Spread)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0((Stage 0: Initial buffer)) --> A1((Stage 1: Seed painted Cf))
    A1 --> A2((Stage 2: 4 neighbours of seed painted Cf))
    A2 --> A3((Stage 3: Outer ring painted Cf))
    A3 --> A4((Stage 4: Fill terminates at boundary Cb))
```

### 4.5 Memory Architecture — Frame-Buffer Pixel Addressing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FRAME["LINEAR FRAME BUFFER MEMORY"]
        ADDR0[Offset 0: pixel 0,0]
        ADDR1[Offset 1: pixel 1,0]
        ADDR2[Offset 2: pixel 2,0]
        ADDR3[Offset W: pixel 0,1]
        ADDR4[Offset 2W plus 3: pixel 3,3 seed]
    end
    ADDR0 --> ADDR1 --> ADDR2 --> ADDR3 --> ADDR4
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**  
> **Differentiate between Boundary Fill Algorithm and Flood Fill Algorithm. List any two distinguishing factors.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Boundary Fill Algorithm** starts filling from a seed point located **inside a closed region defined by a specified boundary color** $C_b$. It stops when it encounters any pixel whose color equals $C_b$. In contrast, the **Flood Fill Algorithm** starts from a seed point and replaces **all connected pixels that share the seed's original color** $C_{old}$ with a new color $C_f$, regardless of any boundary.

**Two distinguishing factors:**

1. **Stopping criterion:** Boundary Fill stops on encountering a uniform boundary color $C_b$. Flood Fill stops when the original color $C_{old}$ is no longer matched.
2. **Prerequisite:** Boundary Fill requires a **pre-defined closed boundary** to exist. Flood Fill requires only a **uniform interior color** — no boundary is necessary.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**  
> **Explain the concept of 4-connected and 8-connected regions with the help of a pixel-neighborhood diagram.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **4-connected region** treats only the four **orthogonal neighbors** of any pixel as connected to it — namely the North, South, East, and West neighbors. An **8-connected region** adds the four **diagonal neighbors** (NE, NW, SE, SW) to the connected set.

**Pixel Neighborhood Diagram (textual):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{4-connected neighbors of } (x, y) &= (x+1, y),\; (x-1, y),\; (x, y+1),\; (x, y-1) \\
\text{8-connected neighbors of } (x, y) &= \text{4-connected} \cup (x+1, y+1),\; (x+1, y-1),\; (x-1, y+1),\; (x-1, y-1)
\end{aligned}
$$

The choice affects fill correctness: **4-connected** filling can leak through diagonal gaps; **8-connected** filling is leak-proof.

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **Q3(A). [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]** **(14 Marks)**  
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the Boundary Fill Algorithm in detail. Provide the recursive procedure and the fill-predicate condition.  
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A $5 \times 5$ closed region has its boundary drawn in color code $1$ (white) and interior in color code $0$ (black). The seed pixel is at $(2, 2)$ and the requested fill color is $2$ (green). Trace the 4-connected Boundary Fill and list the order in which the first 6 interior pixels are coloured.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks]** — Full description as in §1.1, §2.1, §2.2 above. Key points to be written:

- Definition with $C_b$, $C_f$, $C_{int}$ notation. **(1 mark)**
- Explanation of seed-pixel requirement. **(1 mark)**
- Fill-predicate condition: $\text{color}(x,y) \neq C_b \;\land\; \text{color}(x,y) \neq C_f$. **(2 marks)**
- Recursive procedure (4-connected form) with all four neighbour calls. **(2 marks)**
- Mention of stack-overflow risk and iterative alternative. **(1 mark)**

**(b) [7 marks] — Trace:**

The boundary encloses a $3 \times 3$ interior (rows $y \in \{1, 2, 3\}$, columns $x \in \{1, 2, 3\}$). Seed at $(2, 2)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Order of colouring (4-connected expansion from seed):} \\
1. \;& (2, 2) \;\; \text{— seed pixel itself} \\
2. \;& (3, 2) \;\; \text{— first East-call return} \\
3. \;& (2, 3) \;\; \text{— first South-call return} \\
4. \;& (1, 2) \;\; \text{— first West-call return} \\
5. \;& (2, 1) \;\; \text{— first North-call return} \\
6. \;& (3, 3) \;\; \text{— second-level East-then-South call}
\end{aligned}
$$

Order: $(2,2) \rightarrow (3,2) \rightarrow (2,3) \rightarrow (1,2) \rightarrow (2,1) \rightarrow (3,3)$. **(7 marks — 1 mark for each correctly identified pixel, 1 mark for the order justification.)**

---

> **Q3(B). [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]** **(14 Marks)** *(Alternative to Q3(A))*  
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Compare 4-connected and 8-connected Boundary Fill algorithms. Which one is preferred for general closed regions, and why?  
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Convert the recursive Boundary Fill into an **iterative stack-based** algorithm. State the steps and explain how it avoids the limitations of recursion.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks]** — Full comparison matrix as in §3.4 above. Key writing points:

- 4 vs 8 neighbours. **(1 mark)**
- Diagonal-leakage failure of 4-connected (with the 2-pixel diagonal-gap example). **(2 marks)**
- 8-connected fills all diagonally-connected pixels correctly. **(1 mark)**
- Cost difference: 4 calls vs 8 calls per pixel. **(1 mark)**
- Preference: **8-connected is preferred for general closed regions** because it is leak-proof. **(1 mark)**
- 4-connected may still be chosen for orthogonal-only polygons for efficiency. **(1 mark)**

**(b) [7 marks] — Iterative stack-based version:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{STEP 1} &: \text{Create an empty stack } S. \\
\text{STEP 2} &: \text{Push seed } (x_s, y_s) \text{ onto } S. \\
\text{STEP 3} &: \text{WHILE } S \text{ is not empty, DO} \\
\text{STEP 4} &: \qquad (x, y) \leftarrow \text{POP}(S) \\
\text{STEP 5} &: \qquad \text{IF } \text{color}(x, y) \neq C_b \;\land\; \text{color}(x, y) \neq C_f \;\text{THEN} \\
\text{STEP 6} &: \qquad\qquad \text{setPixel}(x, y, C_f) \\
\text{STEP 7} &: \qquad\qquad \text{PUSH}(x+1, y),\; (x-1, y),\; (x, y+1),\; (x, y-1) \text{ onto } S \\
\text{STEP 8} &: \qquad \text{END IF} \\
\text{STEP 9} &: \text{END WHILE} \\
\text{STEP 10} &: \text{RETURN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Advantages over recursion (3 marks distribution):**

- No risk of **stack overflow** because $S$ is heap-allocated and resizable. **(1 mark)**
- Avoids function-call overhead — faster execution. **(1 mark)**
- Easier to **debug** and to add **scanline optimizations**. **(1 mark)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing Boundary Fill with Flood Fill** — the algorithms are similar but the *stopping condition* is different. Examiners routinely award zero if the student writes the flood-fill predicate by mistake.
> 2. **Forgetting to declare that $C_b$, $C_f$, and $C_{int}$ must all be mutually distinct.** Always state this precondition.
> 3. **Drawing the 8-neighbor set incorrectly** — the four diagonal coordinates are $(x\pm1, y\pm1)$, NOT $(x\pm2, y)$ or similar. A single wrong sign costs full marks on the diagram.
> 4. **Omitting the iterative alternative** — in 14-mark questions, mentioning recursion alone is usually 2–3 marks short. Always show the stack-based variant.
> 5. **Not mentioning time complexity** $O(A)$ explicitly. KTU board examiners expect the Big-O statement in any algorithm question worth $\geq 7$ marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Boundary Fill** is a **region-filling algorithm** that paints the interior of a closed shape starting from an interior **seed pixel**.
- The **fill predicate** is: $\text{color}(x, y) \neq C_b \;\land\; \text{color}(x, y) \neq C_f$.
- **4-connected** = 4 orthogonal neighbours; **8-connected** = 4 orthogonal + 4 diagonal.
- **8-connected is preferred for general regions** because it is leak-proof across diagonal gaps.
- The algorithm can be **recursive** (function-call stack) or **iterative** (explicit user stack).
- **Time complexity** is **$O(A)$** where $A$ is the number of interior pixels.
- **Preconditions (mandatory):** $C_b \neq C_f$, $C_b \neq C_{int}$, $C_f \neq C_{int}$, and the seed must be a valid interior pixel.
- The **iterative stack-based version** avoids recursion-stack overflow and is the production-grade implementation.
- Boundary Fill is **distinct from Flood Fill**: Boundary Fill stops at a *fixed boundary color*; Flood Fill stops when the *original seed color* changes.
- The **frame-buffer address** of pixel $(x, y)$ in a $W$-column buffer is $\text{offset} = y \cdot W + x$ — useful for KTU numerical questions.
- Boundary Fill is the conceptual foundation for **scanline fill**, **painting tools**, and **CAD/UI rendering systems**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inside-Outside Tests
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT414 - COMPUTER GRAPHICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Inside-Outside Tests

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Inside-Outside Test** in computer graphics is a fundamental geometric decision procedure used to determine whether a given point lies *inside*, *outside*, or *on the boundary* of a defined region, typically a polygon. It is a critical primitive operation that underpins several higher-level graphics algorithms such as polygon clipping (Sutherland-Hodgman), hidden surface removal, ray tracing, and region filling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):** Inside-outside tests are the class of algorithms used to classify a point's spatial relationship with respect to a polygon boundary. The two standard approaches taught under KTU Module 1 are:
> 1. **The Even-Odd (Parity) Rule** — also called the *ray casting* or *Jordan curve* test.
> 2. **The Winding Number Method** — based on the algebraic sum of signed angular contributions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing inside a closed park fenced by a polygonal boundary (a multi-sided fence). You want to know whether you are **inside the park** or **outside it**. The Inside-Outside Test is your "decision-maker."

- **The Ray-Casting Analogy**: Shoot a horizontal laser beam from your position toward infinity. Count how many times it crosses the fence. If the count is **odd** → you are *inside*. If it is **even** (including zero) → you are *outside*. It is like firing a ray of light; every time it hits a wall, it "bounces" between inside and outside zones.
- **The Winding Analogy**: Walk around the park fence in a closed loop. Notice how many times you turn *clockwise* versus *counter-clockwise* relative to your starting point. If they cancel out perfectly, you are *outside*. If they don't, you are *inside*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metric:** For the Even-Odd rule, the result is always a **non-negative integer (parity bit)**. For the Winding Number method, the result is a **signed integer** representing net topological winding.

### 1.3 Why Inside-Outside Tests Matter in KTU Syllabus

In KTU Module 1, the Sutherland-Hodgman polygon clipping algorithm is taught with **inside-outside tests at its core**. For every edge of the clipping window, the algorithm must repeatedly ask: *Is this vertex inside the window edge's half-plane or outside?* This is precisely the inside-outside test applied to a half-plane.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Even-Odd Rule (Ray Casting / Jordan Curve Test)

**Operational Principle:** Cast a ray (typically horizontal, pointing in the +X direction) from the test point $P(x_p, y_p)$ and count the number of times it intersects the polygon edges.

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

1. **Set up a test ray** — most commonly a horizontal ray going rightward (positive X direction) from point $P$.
2. **Test each polygon edge** — for each edge, check whether the ray crosses it.
3. **Count intersections** — tally only those intersections that occur strictly to the right of $P$ (i.e., with $x_{intersection} > x_p$).
4. **Apply parity rule**:
   - **Odd count** → Point is **INSIDE** the polygon.
   - **Even count (including 0)** → Point is **OUTSIDE** the polygon.
   - **On boundary** → Classify as **ON** the polygon (special case).

**Edge Crossing Test (Slab Method):**
An edge from $V_1(x_1, y_1)$ to $V_2(x_2, y_2)$ is crossed by a horizontal ray at $y = y_p$ if and only if the y-coordinates of the two endpoints *straddle* the ray's y-coordinate:

$$(y_1 - y_p) \cdot (y_2 - y_p) < 0$$

If the equality holds ($= 0$), the ray passes through a vertex — a special case requiring careful handling (otherwise double-counting occurs).

### 2.2 The Winding Number Method

**Operational Principle:** Compute the *algebraic* (signed) number of times the polygon winds around the test point. This is more robust for self-intersecting polygons and complex shapes.

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

1. For each edge $E_i$ from $V_i(x_i, y_i)$ to $V_{i+1}(x_{i+1}, y_{i+1})$, compute its angular contribution to the winding around $P(x_p, y_p)$.
2. Sum all signed angular contributions.
3. **If the sum is non-zero (typically $\pm 1, \pm 2, ...$)** → Point is **INSIDE**.
4. **If the sum is exactly zero** → Point is **OUTSIDE**.

### 2.3 Edge-Specific Inside-Outside Test (For Half-Planes / Clipping)

In Sutherland-Hodgman, the test is simplified because the "polygon" is a **half-plane** bounded by a clipping edge. The signed distance from a point to the clipping edge determines the inside/outside status.

For a clipping edge with endpoints $(x_1, y_1)$ and $(x_2, y_2)$:

$$F(x, y) = (y_2 - y_1) \cdot (x - x_1) - (x_2 - x_1) \cdot (y - y_1)$$

- If $F(x, y) \geq 0$ → Point is **INSIDE** the half-plane.
- If $F(x, y) < 0$ → Point is **OUTSIDE** the half-plane.

> [!NOTE]
> This is the same as the cross-product $\vec{E} \times \vec{EP}$ where $\vec{E} = (x_2-x_1, y_2-y_1)$ and $\vec{EP} = (x-x_1, y-y_1)$.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Condition / Sign | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Edge crosses ray (Even-Odd) | $(y_1 - y_p)(y_2 - y_p) < 0$ | Straddle condition | Ray casting test |
| Inside test (Even-Odd) | $\text{intersection count} \pmod 2 = 1$ | Odd $\Rightarrow$ INSIDE | Point-in-polygon |
| Outside test (Even-Odd) | $\text{intersection count} \pmod 2 = 0$ | Even $\Rightarrow$ OUTSIDE | Point-in-polygon |
| Half-plane test (clipping) | $F(x,y) = \Delta y \cdot (x - x_1) - \Delta x \cdot (y - y_1)$ | $F \geq 0 \Rightarrow$ INSIDE | Sutherland-Hodgman |
| Winding number (vector form) | $w = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{sign}(\vec{PV_i} \times \vec{PV_{i+1}})$ | $w \neq 0 \Rightarrow$ INSIDE | Robust self-intersect test |
| Boundary case | $F(x,y) = 0$ | ON the edge | Treat as inside (KTU convention) |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **GIS & Cartography**: Determining if a GPS coordinate lies within a district/country boundary.
- **CAD/CAM**: Validating whether a point lies inside a tool-path polygon.
- **Game Development**: Hit-box detection, click-to-select inside 3D meshes projected to 2D.
- **Computer-Aided Surgery**: Verifying instrument position is inside the patient's safe region.
- **Computational Geometry**: Foundation for Voronoi diagrams, Delaunay triangulation, and convex hull algorithms.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Half-Plane Inside-Outside Test Equation

We need to determine on which side of the directed line from $V_1(x_1, y_1)$ to $V_2(x_2, y_2)$ the point $P(x, y)$ lies.

**Step 1:** Define the direction vector of the edge.
$$\vec{E} = (x_2 - x_1,\ y_2 - y_1) = (\Delta x,\ \Delta y)$$

**Step 2:** Define the vector from the start of the edge to the test point.
$$\vec{EP} = (x - x_1,\ y - y_1)$$

**Step 3:** Compute the 2D cross product (signed area / z-component of the 3D cross product).
$$F(x, y) = \vec{E} \times \vec{EP} = E_x \cdot EP_y - E_y \cdot EP_x$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the components.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F(x, y) &= (x_2 - x_1)(y - y_1) - (y_2 - y_1)(x - x_1)
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Note: This is the standard form. The KTU-syllabus form shown in the cheat sheet rearranges signs to $F = \Delta y (x-x_1) - \Delta x (y-y_1)$, which simply inverts the convention for "inside." Both are valid; examiners typically accept either as long as the convention is consistent.*

**Step 5:** Interpret the sign.
- $F(x, y) > 0$ → Point is to the **left** of the directed edge.
- $F(x, y) < 0$ → Point is to the **right** of the directed edge.
- $F(x, y) = 0$ → Point is **collinear** with the edge.

**Step 6:** Apply the KTU clipping convention. For Sutherland-Hodgman, traverse clipping-window edges in a consistent (say counter-clockwise) order. The "inside" half-plane is consistently the *left* side, giving:

$$\text{INSIDE} \iff F(x, y) \geq 0$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Even-Odd Rule

**Problem:** Determine whether the point $P(5, 5)$ lies inside the polygon with vertices (in order): $A(2, 1)$, $B(8, 1)$, $C(8, 8)$, $D(2, 8)$. This is an axis-aligned square.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Cast a horizontal ray from $P(5, 5)$ going to the right ($+X$ direction). The ray equation: $y = 5$, $x > 5$.

**Step 2:** Test each edge:

| Edge | $y_1$ | $y_2$ | Straddle $y_p=5$? | Intersection $x$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $A(2,1) \to B(8,1)$ | 1 | 1 | No (both below) | — |
| $B(8,1) \to C(8,8)$ | 1 | 8 | Yes ($1<5<8$) | $x = 8$ |
| $C(8,8) \to D(2,8)$ | 8 | 8 | No (both above) | — |
| $D(2,8) \to A(2,1)$ | 8 | 1 | Yes ($1<5<8$) | $x = 2$ |

**Step 3:** Apply the $x_{intersection} > x_p = 5$ filter:
- $B \to C$: intersection at $x = 8 > 5$ ✓ **Count it.**
- $D \to A$: intersection at $x = 2 < 5$ ✗ **Ignore it.**

**Step 4:** Total intersections counted = **1 (odd)**.

**Step 5:** Apply the parity rule.
$$\text{Intersection count} = 1 \pmod 2 = 1 \equiv \text{ODD} \implies P \text{ is INSIDE the square.}$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Half-Plane Test

**Problem:** A clipping edge runs from $V_1(0, 0)$ to $V_2(10, 0)$ (the bottom edge of a clipping rectangle). Determine the status of point $P(5, -1)$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute $\Delta x$ and $\Delta y$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta x &= x_2 - x_1 = 10 - 0 = 10 \\
\Delta y &= y_2 - y_1 = 0 - 0 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the half-plane test function.
$$F(x, y) = (y_2 - y_1)(x - x_1) - (x_2 - x_1)(y - y_1)$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values.
$$F(5, -1) = (0)(5 - 0) - (10)(-1 - 0) = 0 - (10)(-1) = 10$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the sign.

$$F(5, -1) = 10 > 0$$

**Step 5:** Apply the inside/outside rule for the bottom edge traversed left-to-right (the "inside" of the rectangle is *above* the bottom edge, which is to the left of the directed edge $V_1 \to V_2$).

$$F > 0 \implies P(5,-1) \text{ is to the LEFT of the directed edge } \implies P \text{ is OUTSIDE the clipping rectangle (below the bottom edge).}$$

### 3.4 Handling the Vertex-Special-Case (VT and VU Problem)

When the test ray passes exactly through a polygon vertex, naive counting yields a wrong parity. Two standard fixes:

1. **Slab midpoint test:** Instead of checking $y = y_p$ exactly, use $y = y_p - \epsilon$ for some tiny $\epsilon$ (e.g., $\epsilon = 10^{-6}$).
2. **Counting rule (Heckbert):** Count a vertex intersection only if the edge endpoint has the smaller $y$-coordinate (or smaller $x$ on horizontal edges, to break ties).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU-syllabus approach is the **slab method**: count only edges where the *interior* of the edge (excluding the upper endpoint) crosses the ray. The standard practice is to treat horizontal edges as not crossing to avoid double-counting.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Even-Odd Rule Operational Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Test point P and Polygon vertices] --> B[Set up horizontal ray from P along +X direction]
    B --> C[Initialize counter N = 0]
    C --> D[Loop: For each polygon edge from Vi to Vi+1]
    D --> E{Does the edge straddle y = yp?}
    E -->|No| D
    E -->|Yes| F[Compute intersection x-coordinate]
    F --> G{Is x_intersection greater than xp?}
    G -->|No| D
    G -->|Yes| H[Increment N = N + 1]
    H --> D
    D -->|All edges processed| I{N mod 2 == 1?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Result: P is INSIDE polygon]
    I -->|No| K[Result: P is OUTSIDE polygon]
    J --> L[End]
    K --> L
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Inside-Outside Test in Sutherland-Hodgman Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Polygon Vertex S] --> B[Compute F(S) using half-plane test]
    B --> C{F(S) greater than or equal 0?}
    C -->|Yes| D[S is INSIDE clipping half-plane]
    C -->|No| E[S is OUTSIDE clipping half-plane]
    D --> F[Save S to output list]
    E --> G[Compute intersection I with clipping edge]
    G --> H[Save I to output list]
    F --> I[Next vertex]
    H --> I
    I --> A
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Winding Number Method Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Test point P, Polygon edges] --> B[Initialize winding number W = 0]
    B --> C[For each edge from Vi to Vi+1]
    C --> D[Compute cross product CPi = PVi x PVi+1 z-component]
    D --> E[Compute sign of CPi]
    E --> F[Add signed contribution to W]
    F --> C
    C -->|All edges done| G{Is W equal to 0?}
    G -->|Yes| H[P is OUTSIDE polygon]
    G -->|No| I[P is INSIDE polygon]
    H --> J[End]
    I --> J
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Visual Sketch of the Even-Odd Rule

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Point P at 5,5] --> R[Horizontal ray to +X]
    R --> E1[Edge BC at x=8]
    R --> E2[Edge DA at x=2, but x less than xp, so ignored]
    E1 --> C[Count = 1]
    C --> D[Odd count equals INSIDE]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The above flowcharts model the decision logic of the inside-outside test. The actual physical ray casting (left to right) is best illustrated by hand-drawn sketches in the KTU answer sheet, but the block-level architecture flow above captures the operational sequence accepted by the examiner.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Define the Even-Odd rule for inside-outside testing of a point with respect to a polygon.**

**Model Answer:**
The Even-Odd rule (also called the ray-casting or Jordan curve test) is a method to determine whether a point lies inside or outside a polygon. A horizontal ray is cast from the test point in a fixed direction (e.g., $+X$). The number of times the ray intersects the polygon edges is counted. If the count is **odd**, the point is **inside** the polygon; if the count is **even** (including zero), the point is **outside**. The test relies on the topological fact that crossing a polygon boundary toggles the inside/outside state. *(3 marks)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Differentiate between the Even-Odd rule and the Winding Number method for inside-outside tests.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Even-Odd Rule | Winding Number Method |
|---|---|---|
| Output | Parity (odd/even) | Signed integer (can be 0) |
| Robustness | Fails for self-intersecting polygons | Robust for self-intersecting polygons |
| Computation | Ray-casting intersection count | Algebraic sum of signed angular contributions |
| Complexity | Lower (O(n)) | Higher (uses trigonometry) |
| KTU Usage | Most common, taught first | Mentioned as advanced alternative |

*(3 marks)*

---

### Part B: 14-Mark Module-Internal Choice Questions

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain the inside-outside test used in the Sutherland-Hodgman polygon clipping algorithm. Derive the half-plane test function $F(x, y)$ and explain its geometric significance. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** For the polygon with vertices $P_1(1, 1)$, $P_2(6, 1)$, $P_3(6, 5)$, $P_4(4, 5)$, $P_5(4, 3)$, $P_6(1, 3)$ (in that order), determine whether the point $Q(3, 4)$ lies inside or outside the polygon using the Even-Odd rule. Show all steps. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for (a):**

The Sutherland-Hodgman algorithm clips a polygon against a convex clipping window one edge at a time. For each clipping edge, the algorithm must classify every input vertex as either *inside* or *outside* the half-plane defined by that edge. This classification is the inside-outside test.

**Derivation of $F(x, y)$:**

Let the clipping edge be directed from $V_1(x_1, y_1)$ to $V_2(x_2, y_2)$. The half-plane to the *left* of this directed edge is the "inside" of the clipping region (when vertices are traversed counter-clockwise).

Define the edge vector:
$$\vec{E} = (x_2 - x_1,\ y_2 - y_1)$$

Define the point-to-edge vector:
$$\vec{P} - \vec{V_1} = (x - x_1,\ y - y_1)$$

The 2D cross product (z-component) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F(x, y) &= \vec{E} \times (\vec{P} - \vec{V_1}) \\
&= (x_2 - x_1)(y - y_1) - (y_2 - y_1)(x - x_1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Geometric Significance:**
- $F(x, y) > 0$ → Point lies to the **left** of the directed edge → **INSIDE**.
- $F(x, y) < 0$ → Point lies to the **right** of the directed edge → **OUTSIDE**.
- $F(x, y) = 0$ → Point lies **on the edge** (boundary case, KTU convention: treat as inside).

**Model Solution for (b):**

The polygon is a notched rectangle (concave) with vertices in order:
$P_1(1,1)$, $P_2(6,1)$, $P_3(6,5)$, $P_4(4,5)$, $P_5(4,3)$, $P_6(1,3)$.

**Step 1:** Cast a horizontal ray from $Q(3, 4)$ along $+X$ (i.e., $y = 4$, $x > 3$).

**Step 2:** Test each edge for the straddle condition $(y_i - 4)(y_{i+1} - 4) < 0$:

| Edge | $y_i$ | $y_{i+1}$ | Straddle $y=4$? | Intersection $x$ | $x > 3$? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $P_1P_2$ | 1 | 1 | No (both below) | — | — |
| $P_2P_3$ | 1 | 5 | **Yes** | $x = 6$ | ✓ Count |
| $P_3P_4$ | 5 | 5 | No (both above) | — | — |
| $P_4P_5$ | 5 | 3 | **Yes** | $x = 4$ | ✓ Count |
| $P_5P_6$ | 3 | 3 | No (both below) | — | — |
| $P_6P_1$ | 3 | 1 | No (both below) | — | — |

**Step 3:** Total intersections with $x > 3$ is **2** (even).

**Step 4:** Apply the Even-Odd rule. Even count (2) → $Q(3, 4)$ is **OUTSIDE the polygon**.

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the Winding Number method for inside-outside testing. How is it more robust than the Even-Odd rule for self-intersecting polygons? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A clipping edge runs from $V_1(0, 0)$ to $V_2(0, 10)$ (left edge of a rectangular clipping window). Using the half-plane test, classify each of the following points as inside (I) or outside (O) the half-plane: $A(5, 5)$, $B(-2, 4)$, $C(0, 5)$, $D(-1, -1)$. Justify each classification with a calculation. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for (a):**

The Winding Number method computes the **algebraic (signed) number of times** a polygon winds around the test point $P$. For each edge of the polygon, we compute its angular sweep around $P$ using the atan2 function:

$$\theta_i = \text{atan2}(y_{i+1} - y_p,\ x_{i+1} - x_p) - \text{atan2}(y_i - y_p,\ x_i - x_p)$$

The total winding number is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \theta_i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Classification:**
- $W = 0$ → Point is **OUTSIDE**.
- $W \neq 0$ (typically $W = \pm 1, \pm 2, ...$) → Point is **INSIDE** (the magnitude tells how many times the polygon winds around).

**Robustness for self-intersecting polygons:**
For a self-intersecting (complex) polygon, the Even-Odd rule gives ambiguous results because the simple parity logic fails. The Winding Number method correctly classifies points because it accounts for the *signed* (directional) contributions, ensuring that a clockwise edge and a counter-clockwise edge cancel each other in the proper topological sense. For example, a figure-8 polygon will have $W = 0$ in the "outside" loops and $W = \pm 2$ in regions doubly wound. The Even-Odd rule cannot distinguish these cases.

**Model Solution for (b):**

The clipping edge is $V_1(0, 0) \to V_2(0, 10)$. We apply:

$$F(x, y) = (x_2 - x_1)(y - y_1) - (y_2 - y_1)(x - x_1)$$

With $x_1 = 0$, $y_1 = 0$, $x_2 = 0$, $y_2 = 10$, this simplifies to:

$$F(x, y) = (0)(y - 0) - (10)(x - 0) = -10x$$

So the sign of $F$ is determined by $-x$.

| Point | Calculation | $F$ value | $F \geq 0$? | Classification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $A(5, 5)$ | $F = -10 \cdot 5 = -50$ | $-50$ | No | **OUTSIDE (O)** |
| $B(-2, 4)$ | $F = -10 \cdot (-2) = 20$ | $20$ | Yes | **INSIDE (I)** |
| $C(0, 5)$ | $F = -10 \cdot 0 = 0$ | $0$ | Yes (boundary) | **INSIDE (I)** (on edge) |
| $D(-1, -1)$ | $F = -10 \cdot (-1) = 10$ | $10$ | Yes | **INSIDE (I)** |

**Geometric Verification:** The directed edge goes upward along the Y-axis. The "inside" of the clipping window (the rectangle) is to the **right** of this edge (i.e., in the +X direction). Points $B$, $C$, $D$ all lie on or to the right (left of the edge in the conventional sense — note the sign convention here). Point $A(5,5)$ lies in the +X region, but our test says OUTSIDE because the cross-product convention treats the +X side as outside when the edge is directed upward. This confirms the need for a *consistent* traversal convention (CCW vs CW) of the clipping window.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (commonly losing marks):**
> 1. **Sign convention confusion:** Many students mix up left/right conventions. Always state the convention explicitly at the start of the answer.
> 2. **Ignoring the vertex case:** When the ray passes exactly through a vertex, students often count it twice, leading to wrong parity. Always state that vertices on the ray are handled by the "upper-endpoint" rule or slab method.
> 3. **Forgetting the half-plane inequality direction:** For Sutherland-Hodgman, the test is $F \geq 0$ for inside (left side). Reversing this gives wrong clipping output.
> 4. **Not showing straddle-condition algebra:** Examiners want to see $(y_i - y_p)(y_{i+1} - y_p) < 0$ written out, not just the final count.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Inside-Outside Test = Geomentry Decision Procedure**: Determines if a point is inside, outside, or on a polygon's boundary.
- **Two Primary Methods**: (1) Even-Odd rule (ray casting, parity test) and (2) Winding Number method (signed sum).
- **Even-Odd Rule**: Horizontal ray from test point, count rightward edge intersections, **odd = inside**, **even = outside**.
- **Edge Straddle Condition**: $(y_i - y_p)(y_{i+1} - y_p) < 0$ — fundamental algebraic test for the Even-Odd rule.
- **Half-Plane Test Function**: $F(x, y) = (x_2 - x_1)(y - y_1) - (y_2 - y_1)(x - x_1)$; $F \geq 0$ means **inside** the left half-plane.
- **Sutherland-Hodgman Linkage**: Inside-outside test is the *core decision primitive* of this clipping algorithm; classify each vertex of input polygon against each clipping edge.
- **Vertex Special Case**: Use the slab method or upper-endpoint rule to avoid double-counting intersections at vertices.
- **Concave Polygons**: The Even-Old rule works correctly for concave polygons (e.g., the notched rectangle in Q1(b) above).
- **Winding Number Robustness**: For self-intersecting polygons, the Winding Number method is preferred over Even-Odd.
- **Cross Product Geometry**: $F(x, y)$ is the 2D cross product of the edge vector and the point-vector, giving the signed area.
- **KTU Exam Tip**: Always show the straddle test table and the parity calculation in tabular form for full marks.
- **Time Complexity**: Both Even-Old rule and Winding Number method run in $O(n)$ time for an $n$-vertex polygon.
- **Boundary Convention**: $F(x, y) = 0$ (point on edge) is conventionally treated as **inside** to avoid clipping boundary artifacts.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Two-Dimensional Transformations - Translation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT414 - COMPUTER GRAPHICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Two-Dimensional Transformations - Translation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — Two-Dimensional Transformations: Translation

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In computer graphics, a **Two-Dimensional Translation** is a rigid-body geometric transformation that repositions every point of an object or graphical primitive from one location in the 2D Cartesian plane to another location, by adding a constant displacement vector $(t_x, t_y)$ to the coordinates of every point. Translation is a **non-degenerate, shape-preserving, distance-preserving (isometric) transformation** that alters only the **position**, never the **size**, **orientation**, or **shape** of the object.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Box**
> Translation is a transformation that moves an object from one coordinate location to another in a 2D plane by applying a constant translation vector $\mathbf{t} = (t_x, t_y)$ to all points of the object. It is the simplest of all 2D affine transformations and the foundational building block for composing more complex transformations like rotation and scaling.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sliding Glass" Intuition

Imagine you have a **transparent glass sheet** placed on top of a printed photograph lying on a flat table. The photograph depicts a house. Now imagine pushing the entire glass sheet exactly **5 cm to the right** and **3 cm upward** while keeping it perfectly parallel to the table (no rotation, no tilt, no zooming). The picture of the house, viewed through the glass, has visibly **moved** to a new position. However:

- The size of the house has **not changed**.
- The orientation (upright) has **not changed**.
- The shape (proportions) have **not changed**.
- Only the **location** has shifted.

This is precisely what a 2D translation does in computer graphics — it is the digital equivalent of "sliding" an object to a new position without distorting it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU 2024 Scheme examinations, translation is consistently tested alongside the **homogeneous coordinate representation** (3×3 matrix form). Always represent your translation matrix in the 3×3 homogeneous form during derivations and numerical problems, even though the underlying 2D point is 2-dimensional.

### 1.3 The Translation Vector — Key Metric

The translation is fully characterized by a single mathematical construct called the **Translation Vector**:

$$\mathbf{t} = (t_x, t_y)$$

where:
- $t_x$ = displacement along the **X-axis** (positive = right, negative = left)
- $t_y$ = displacement along the **Y-axis** (positive = up, negative = down)

**Standard Units (KTU Convention):** In the 2024 Scheme, translation distances are treated as **dimensionless real numbers** in the 2D world coordinate system. In applied graphics (CAD, game development), these values represent **screen units, pixels, or meters**, depending on the application context.
