> **Shearing** is a non-rigid (distortion-producing) transformation that displaces each point of an object by an amount **proportional to its signed distance from a reference axis** (called the *shear axis*). The shape is distorted — area is preserved in the mathematical sense, but the object "leans" or "slants" sideways. It is parameterised by a dimensionless **shear factor** $Sh_x$ (for x-shear) or $Sh_y$ (for y-shear).

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogies

### Reflection — "The Looking Glass Analogy"
Imagine placing a triangular cardboard cut-out in front of a **mirror** lying along a straight line drawn on the floor. The image you see behind the mirror is the *reflection*. If the mirror lies along the x-axis (the floor line), the triangle is flipped vertically — what was on top is now on the bottom, but the left-right arrangement is preserved. Every point $P(x, y)$ has its image $P'(x, -y)$ — the x-coordinate is untouched, the y-coordinate is "negated" with respect to the mirror.

### Shearing — "The Stack of Cards Analogy"
Imagine a row of rectangular cards placed on a table. If you press down on the **top edge** of the rightmost card and slide the **bottom edge** to the right, each card tilts progressively. The cards remain the same height (y-coordinates unchanged for y-shear) but their x-coordinates are **shifted by an amount proportional to their height**. This horizontal sliding is x-shear. The shear factor controls how aggressively the cards lean.

## 1.4 Standard Reflections — Quick Reference

| Axis / Line of Reflection | Transformation Mapping | KTU Notation |
|---|---|---|
| x-axis (i.e. $y = 0$) | $(x, y) \rightarrow (x, -y)$ | Most frequently asked |
| y-axis (i.e. $x = 0$) | $(x, y) \rightarrow (-x, y)$ | Frequently asked |
| Origin $(0, 0)$ | $(x, y) \rightarrow (-x, -y)$ | Less frequent |
| Line $y = x$ | $(x, y) \rightarrow (y, x)$ | Very frequently asked |
| Line $y = -x$ | $(x, y) \rightarrow (-y, -x)$ | Frequently asked |

> [!NOTE]
> **Determinant Rule (must remember for KTU):** A $2 \times 2$ transformation matrix with determinant $+1$ represents a *rotation* (orientation-preserving), while a determinant of $-1$ represents a *reflection* (orientation-reversing). Examiners love to test this.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Reflection — Step-by-Step Logic

A reflection about a coordinate axis is mathematically the **negation of one coordinate** while leaving the other untouched. The general principle:

1. **Identify the axis of reflection** (x-axis, y-axis, line $y=x$, line $y=-x$, or origin).
2. **For each vertex** of the object, apply the sign-change rule corresponding to the chosen axis.
3. **Construct the homogeneous $3 \times 3$ matrix** by embedding the $2 \times 2$ core inside a $3 \times 3$ identity (third row always `[0 0 1]` for pure reflections).
4. **Multiply the matrix by the homogeneous coordinate vector** $\begin{bmatrix} x & y & 1 \end{bmatrix}^T$ of every vertex.
5. **Re-draw the polygon** by connecting the transformed vertices in the **same order** as the original (unless you intend a manual reversal).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the determinant become −1?** Each reflection matrix flips the sign of one row (or column), which is equivalent to multiplying one row of an identity matrix by −1. Multiplication of a row by −1 changes the sign of the determinant without changing its magnitude, giving $\det = -1$.

## 2.2 Shearing — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Identify the shear axis** — the axis along which coordinates are *unchanged*.
   * For **x-shear**, the shear axis is the **x-axis** (y-coordinates preserved).
   * For **y-shear**, the shear axis is the **y-axis** (x-coordinates preserved).
2. **Select the shear factor** ($Sh_x$ or $Sh_y$).
   * Positive $Sh_x$ → object leans **right** as y increases.
   * Negative $Sh_x$ → object leans **left** as y increases.
3. **Apply the rule**:
   * x-shear: $x' = x + Sh_x \cdot y$, \quad $y' = y$
   * y-shear: $x' = x$, \quad $y' = y + Sh_y \cdot x$
4. **Build the matrix** and multiply by each vertex.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility of Shearing:** In CAD, shearing is used to model **skew correction in scanned documents**, **italic text rendering**, **oblique projections in engineering drawings**, and **simulating wind force on a structure** (where horizontal layers slide proportionally to their height).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Transformation | Homogeneous Matrix $M$ | Mapping Rule | Determinant | Shear Factor / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reflection about x-axis | $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $(x, y) \rightarrow (x, -y)$ | $-1$ | y-component sign-flipped |
| 2 | Reflection about y-axis | $\begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $(x, y) \rightarrow (-x, y)$ | $-1$ | x-component sign-flipped |
| 3 | Reflection about origin | $\begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $(x, y) \rightarrow (-x, -y)$ | $+1$ | Composition of (1) then (2) |
| 4 | Reflection about line $y = x$ | $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $(x, y) \rightarrow (y, x)$ | $-1$ | Swap coordinates |
| 5 | Reflection about line $y = -x$ | $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $(x, y) \rightarrow (-y, -x)$ | $-1$ | Swap + negate |
| 6 | x-shear (x-direction) | $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & Sh_x & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $(x, y) \rightarrow (x + Sh_x \cdot y, \; y)$ | $1$ | Shear axis = x-axis |
| 7 | y-shear (y-direction) | $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ Sh_y & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $(x, y) \rightarrow (x, \; y + Sh_y \cdot x)$ | $1$ | Shear axis = y-axis |
| 8 | Combined x and y shear | $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & Sh_x & 0 \\ Sh_y & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $(x, y) \rightarrow (x + Sh_x \cdot y, \; y + Sh_y \cdot x)$ | $1 - Sh_x \cdot Sh_y$ | Valid only when $Sh_x \cdot Sh_y \neq 1$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Heuristic:** For Part-A questions (3 marks), write the matrix and the mapping rule. For Part-B (7-mark sub-parts), also show the multiplication with at least one sample point to demonstrate working.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Reflection about the Line $y = x$ [KTU Standard]

**Problem:** Derive the transformation matrix for reflection about the line $y = x$ and apply it to a triangle with vertices $A(2, 1)$, $B(4, 3)$, $C(5, 1)$.

### Step 1 — Geometric reasoning
The line $y = x$ makes an angle of $45°$ with the x-axis. Reflection about this line effectively **swaps the roles of x and y** — any point above the line maps to a point equidistant below it (and vice versa), but the perpendicular distance is preserved.

### Step 2 — Construct the mapping rule
For any point $P(x, y)$, the reflected point $P'(x', y')$ on the other side of $y = x$ satisfies:

$$
x' = y \qquad \text{and} \qquad y' = x
$$

### Step 3 — Express the mapping as a matrix
In homogeneous form, the system $x' = y$ and $y' = x$ becomes:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 4 — Verify with a sample point
Let $P(3, 5)$. Then $P'(5, 3)$. Plugging into the matrix:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 3 \\ 5 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \cdot 3 + 1 \cdot 5 + 0 \cdot 1 \\ 1 \cdot 3 + 0 \cdot 5 + 0 \cdot 1 \\ 0 \cdot 3 + 0 \cdot 5 + 1 \cdot 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 3 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> The determinant of this matrix is $(0 \cdot 0 - 1 \cdot 1) = -1$, confirming it is a reflection.

### Step 5 — Apply to the triangle vertices
Using the matrix $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$:

$$
A(2, 1) \;\rightarrow\; A'(1, 2)
$$

$$
B(4, 3) \;\rightarrow\; B'(3, 4)
$$

$$
C(5, 1) \;\rightarrow\; C'(1, 5)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The original triangle $A(2,1), B(4,3), C(5,1)$ lies mostly **below** the line $y = x$ (because $y < x$ for most vertices). The reflected triangle $A'(1,2), B'(3,4), C'(1,5)$ lies mostly **above** the line. This is the visual hallmark of a correct reflection.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Reflection about the x-axis and y-axis (Combined Origin Reflection)

**Problem:** A square has vertices $P_1(1, 1)$, $P_2(4, 1)$, $P_3(4, 4)$, $P_4(1, 4)$. Apply reflection about the x-axis, followed by reflection about the y-axis.

### Step 1 — Reflection about x-axis matrix

$$
M_x = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Applied to $P_1$:

$$
M_x \cdot P_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

All four intermediate vertices after x-axis reflection:

$$
P_1(1,1) \to P_1'(1,-1), \quad P_2(4,1) \to P_2'(4,-1), \quad P_3(4,4) \to P_3'(4,-4), \quad P_4(1,4) \to P_4'(1,-4)
$$

### Step 2 — Reflection about y-axis matrix

$$
M_y = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Applied to $P_1'$:

$$
M_y \cdot P_1' = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 \\ -1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

All four final vertices:

$$
P_1''(-1,-1), \quad P_2''(-4,-1), \quad P_3''(-4,-4), \quad P_4''(-1,-4)
$$

### Step 3 — Composite matrix (single-shot)
Since matrix multiplication is associative, $M_y \cdot M_x$ gives a single matrix:

$$
M_{yx} = M_y \cdot M_x = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

This is exactly the **reflection-about-origin** matrix. The order of the two reflections does not matter here because both axes commute (this is a special property of axis-aligned reflections).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** When a question says "apply reflection about y-axis followed by x-axis", students often multiply in the wrong order. The correct rule is: **the rightmost matrix is applied first** (closest to the point vector). So $P_{final} = M_x \cdot M_y \cdot P$ means $M_y$ is applied first.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — x-Shear Application

**Problem:** Apply an x-shear with $Sh_x = 2$ to a unit square with vertices $(0, 0)$, $(1, 0)$, $(1, 1)$, $(0, 1)$. Sketch the result.

### Step 1 — Write the x-shear matrix

$$
M_{Sh_x} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 2 — Mapping rule

$$
x' = x + 2y, \quad y' = y
$$

### Step 3 — Apply to each vertex
For $(0, 0)$:

$$
x' = 0 + 2(0) = 0, \quad y' = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad (0, 0)
$$

For $(1, 0)$:

$$
x' = 1 + 2(0) = 1, \quad y' = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad (1, 0)
$$

For $(1, 1)$:

$$
x' = 1 + 2(1) = 3, \quad y' = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad (3, 1)
$$

For $(0, 1)$:

$$
x' = 0 + 2(1) = 2, \quad y' = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad (2, 1)
$$

### Step 4 — Visualise
The bottom edge (y = 0) is **unmoved** (it lies on the shear axis), and the top edge has slid to the right by $Sh_x \cdot 1 = 2$ units. The square becomes a **parallelogram** with base on the x-axis.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — y-Shear Application

**Problem:** Apply a y-shear with $Sh_y = 3$ to a triangle with vertices $A(1, 1)$, $B(2, 3)$, $C(4, 2)$. Also find the area of the original and sheared triangle.

### Step 1 — y-shear matrix

$$
M_{Sh_y} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 3 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply to vertices
Using $x' = x$, $y' = 3x + y$:

$$
A(1, 1) \to A'(1, 3 \cdot 1 + 1) = A'(1, 4)
$$

$$
B(2, 3) \to B'(2, 3 \cdot 2 + 3) = B'(2, 9)
$$

$$
C(4, 2) \to C'(4, 3 \cdot 4 + 2) = C'(4, 14)
$$

### Step 3 — Area check (Shoelace formula)
Original area:

$$
A_{orig} = \tfrac{1}{2} \vert x_1(y_2 - y_3) + x_2(y_3 - y_1) + x_3(y_1 - y_2) \vert
$$

$$
A_{orig} = \tfrac{1}{2} \vert 1(3-2) + 2(2-1) + 4(1-3) \vert = \tfrac{1}{2} \vert 1 + 2 - 8 \vert = \tfrac{5}{2}
$$

Sheared area:

$$
A_{shear} = \tfrac{1}{2} \vert 1(9-14) + 2(14-4) + 4(4-9) \vert = \tfrac{1}{2} \vert -5 + 20 - 20 \vert = \tfrac{5}{2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the area preserved under shear?** A shear has determinant $1$ (look at the matrix — it is triangular with ones on the diagonal), so it is an **area-preserving (equiareal)** transformation. This is a frequently-asked concept in KTU viva and Part-A questions.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Reflection about the Line $y = -x$ (Detailed Derivation)

**Problem:** Derive the transformation matrix for reflection about $y = -x$ and apply it to the point $P(3, -7)$.

### Step 1 — Geometric reasoning
The line $y = -x$ is a diagonal in the **second and fourth quadrants**. Reflection about this line **swaps** the x and y coordinates **and negates both**.

### Step 2 — Mapping rule

$$
x' = -y \quad \text{and} \quad y' = -x
$$

### Step 3 — Matrix

$$
M_{y=-x} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply to $P(3, -7)$

$$
M_{y=-x} \cdot P = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 3 \\ -7 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \cdot 3 + (-1)(-7) + 0 \\ (-1)(3) + 0(-7) + 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 7 \\ -3 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

So $P'(7, -3)$. Check: original $P$ lies in the fourth quadrant (x > 0, y < 0); the line $y = -x$ in the fourth quadrant passes through $(a, -a)$ for $a > 0$. The reflection $(7, -3)$ is also in the fourth quadrant, but on the opposite side of the line — which is geometrically correct.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification of determinant:** $\det(M_{y=-x}) = (0)(0) - (-1)(-1) = -1$. Confirmed: it is a proper reflection.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Transformation Process Flow

The following block diagram depicts the complete workflow for applying reflection or shearing transformations to a 2D object in the KTU-recommended procedure.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Object Vertices] --> B[Convert to Homogeneous Coordinates x y 1]
    B --> C{Choose Transformation}
    C --> D1[Reflection Matrix]
    C --> D2[Shear Matrix]
    C --> D3[Composite Matrix]
    D1 --> E1[Select Axis X Y Origin Line]
    D2 --> E2[Select Shear Type X-shear Y-shear Combined]
    D3 --> E3[Multiply Matrices in Order]
    E1 --> F[Build 3x3 Homogeneous Matrix]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G[Multiply Matrix with Each Vertex Column Vector]
    G --> H[Extract New x and y Coordinates]
    H --> I[Reconnect Vertices in Order]
    I --> J[Render Transformed Object]
    J --> K[Verify with Determinant Check]
```

## 4.2 Reflection Transformation Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Reflection Transformations] --> RA[About x-axis y=0]
    R --> RB[About y-axis x=0]
    R --> RC[About Origin]
    R --> RD[About Line y=x]
    R --> RE[About Line y=-x]
    R --> RF[About Arbitrary Line y=mx+c]

    RA --> RA1[Matrix negates y component]
    RB --> RB1[Matrix negates x component]
    RC --> RC1[Composition of x-axis and y-axis reflections]
    RD --> RD1[Matrix swaps x and y]
    RE --> RE1[Matrix swaps and negates both]
    RF --> RF1[Requires rotation to align with y=x then reflection]
```

## 4.3 Shear Transformation Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Shear Transformations] --> SX[X-Shear]
    S --> SY[Y-Shear]
    S --> SC[Combined X-Y Shear]

    SX --> SX1[Shear axis is x-axis]
    SX --> SX2[Formula x prime equals x plus Shx times y]
    SX --> SX3[Matrix has Shx in position 0 1]
    SX --> SX4[Effect Object leans right as y increases]

    SY --> SY1[Shear axis is y-axis]
    SY --> SY2[Formula y prime equals y plus Shy times x]
    SY --> SY3[Matrix has Shy in position 1 0]
    SY --> SY4[Effect Object leans upward as x increases]

    SC --> SC1[Applies both shears simultaneously]
    SC --> SC2[General 3x3 matrix with both Shx and Shy]
```

## 4.4 Reflection about $y = x$ — Visual Block Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Point P at x equals 3, y equals 5] --> M1[Reflection Matrix M equals 0 1 0, 1 0 0, 0 0 1]
    M1 --> P2[Reflected Point P prime at x equals 5, y equals 3]
    P2 --> V[Verify On opposite side of y equals x line at equal perpendicular distance]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each box represents a *processing step* (matrix construction, vertex multiplication, verification). The arrows indicate data flow. This is the exact pattern KTU examiners expect students to follow when answering "apply a transformation to" type questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define reflection transformation. Write the homogeneous matrix for reflection about the line $y = x$ and apply it to a point $(4, 7)$.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Reflection** is a transformation that produces a mirror image of a 2D object with respect to a reflection axis, reversing the handedness of the figure while preserving its size and shape. The matrix is orthogonal with determinant $-1$.

The homogeneous matrix for reflection about $y = x$:

$$
M_{y=x} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Applied to point $(4, 7)$:

$$
M_{y=x} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 7 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \cdot 4 + 1 \cdot 7 \\ 1 \cdot 4 + 0 \cdot 7 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 7 \\ 4 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

The reflected point is $(7, 4)$.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between x-shear and y-shear transformations. State the matrix for each.**

**Model Answer:**

> **X-shear** distorts an object horizontally. The y-coordinates of all points remain unchanged, but the x-coordinates are shifted by an amount proportional to the y-coordinate. The shear axis is the x-axis.
>
> **Y-shear** distorts an object vertically. The x-coordinates remain unchanged, but the y-coordinates are shifted by an amount proportional to the x-coordinate. The shear axis is the y-axis.

X-shear matrix (shear factor $Sh_x$):

$$
M_{Sh_x} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & Sh_x & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Y-shear matrix (shear factor $Sh_y$):

$$
M_{Sh_y} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ Sh_y & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

X-shear: $(x, y) \rightarrow (x + Sh_x \cdot y, \; y)$. \quad Y-shear: $(x, y) \rightarrow (x, \; y + Sh_y \cdot x)$.

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]`

**(a)** Derive the transformation matrix for reflection about the line $y = -x$ and apply it to a triangle with vertices $A(1, 2)$, $B(3, 4)$, $C(5, 1)$. Find the new coordinates and verify the determinant. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A square has vertices $P_1(0, 0)$, $P_2(4, 0)$, $P_3(4, 4)$, $P_4(0, 4)$. Apply an x-shear with $Sh_x = 1$ followed by a y-shear with $Sh_y = 0.5$. Find the composite matrix and the final coordinates. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (a)

**Derivation of reflection matrix about $y = -x$:**

For any point $P(x, y)$, reflection about $y = -x$ maps it to $P'(x', y')$ such that:

$$
x' = -y \quad \text{and} \quad y' = -x
$$

The homogeneous matrix is therefore:

$$
M_{y=-x} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Determinant check:**

$$
\det(M_{y=-x}) = (0)(0) - (-1)(-1) = -1
$$

A determinant of $-1$ confirms that this is indeed a reflection (orientation-reversing).

**Apply to the triangle vertices:**

For $A(1, 2)$:

$$
M_{y=-x} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -2 \\ -1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \Rightarrow A'(-2, -1)
$$

For $B(3, 4)$:

$$
M_{y=-x} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 3 \\ 4 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -4 \\ -3 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \Rightarrow B'(-4, -3)
$$

For $C(5, 1)$:

$$
M_{y=-x} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 \\ -5 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \Rightarrow C'(-1, -5)
$$

**Result:** $A'(-2, -1)$, $B'(-4, -3)$, $C'(-1, -5)$.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (b)

**Step 1 — Construct the individual matrices**

X-shear matrix with $Sh_x = 1$:

$$
M_{Sh_x} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Y-shear matrix with $Sh_y = 0.5$:

$$
M_{Sh_y} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0.5 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the composite matrix**

The problem says "x-shear **followed by** y-shear". In matrix notation, the first-applied transformation goes on the **right**, so:

$$
M_{comp} = M_{Sh_y} \cdot M_{Sh_x}
$$

$$
M_{comp} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0.5 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Row 1 of product:

$$
(1 \cdot 1 + 0 \cdot 0 + 0 \cdot 0, \quad 1 \cdot 1 + 0 \cdot 1 + 0 \cdot 0, \quad 1 \cdot 0 + 0 \cdot 0 + 0 \cdot 1) = (1, 1, 0)
$$

Row 2 of product:

$$
(0.5 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 0 + 0 \cdot 0, \quad 0.5 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 1 + 0 \cdot 0, \quad 0.5 \cdot 0 + 1 \cdot 0 + 0 \cdot 1) = (0.5, 1.5, 0)
$$

Row 3 of product:

$$
(0, 0, 1)
$$

Composite matrix:

$$
M_{comp} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 0 \\ 0.5 & 1.5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply to each vertex**

For $P_1(0, 0)$:

$$
M_{comp} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \Rightarrow P_1'(0, 0)
$$

For $P_2(4, 0)$:

$$
M_{comp} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \cdot 4 + 1 \cdot 0 \\ 0.5 \cdot 4 + 1.5 \cdot 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \Rightarrow P_2'(4, 2)
$$

For $P_3(4, 4)$:

$$
M_{comp} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 4 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \cdot 4 + 1 \cdot 4 \\ 0.5 \cdot 4 + 1.5 \cdot 4 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 8 \\ 8 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \Rightarrow P_3'(8, 8)
$$

For $P_4(0, 4)$:

$$
M_{comp} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 4 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \cdot 0 + 1 \cdot 4 \\ 0.5 \cdot 0 + 1.5 \cdot 4 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 6 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \Rightarrow P_4'(4, 6)
$$

**Final transformed vertices:** $P_1'(0, 0)$, $P_2'(4, 2)$, $P_3'(8, 8)$, $P_4'(4, 6)$.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]`

**(a)** Derive the transformation matrix for reflection about an arbitrary line making an angle $\theta$ with the x-axis. Show that it can be obtained as a composition of a rotation, a reflection about the x-axis, and an inverse rotation. Apply it to find the reflection of $P(4, 3)$ about a line passing through the origin at $60°$ to the x-axis. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain combined x and y shearing. Apply combined shear with $Sh_x = 0.5$ and $Sh_y = 0.25$ to a triangle with vertices $A(2, 1)$, $B(6, 1)$, $C(4, 5)$. Compute the new coordinates and verify the area is preserved. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B (a)

**Derivation of general reflection matrix:**

Let the reflection axis make angle $\theta$ with the x-axis. The standard 3-step composition is:

1. **Rotate by $-\theta$** to bring the line onto the x-axis.
2. **Reflect about the x-axis** (sign-flip y).
3. **Rotate back by $+\theta$** to restore the original orientation.

The three matrices are:

$$
R(-\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta & 0 \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad M_x = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

The composite matrix is $M = R(\theta) \cdot M_x \cdot R(-\theta)$. The middle step:

$$
M_x \cdot R(-\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & -\cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Now multiply $R(\theta)$ on the left:

$$
M = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & -\cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Row 1 of product:

$$
(\cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta, \quad 2\sin\theta\cos\theta, \quad 0) = (\cos 2\theta, \quad \sin 2\theta, \quad 0)
$$

Row 2 of product:

$$
(\sin\theta\cos\theta - \cos\theta\sin\theta, \quad \sin^2\theta - \cos^2\theta, \quad 0) = (\sin\theta\cos\theta - \cos\theta\sin\theta, \quad -\cos 2\theta, \quad 0)
$$

Wait — let me re-compute Row 2 carefully. Row 2 of $R(\theta)$ is $(\sin\theta, \cos\theta, 0)$. Multiplying with the column vectors of $M_x \cdot R(-\theta)$:

Column 1: $\sin\theta \cdot \cos\theta + \cos\theta \cdot \sin\theta = 2\sin\theta\cos\theta = \sin 2\theta$.
Column 2: $\sin\theta \cdot \sin\theta + \cos\theta \cdot (-\cos\theta) = \sin^2\theta - \cos^2\theta = -\cos 2\theta$.
Column 3: $0$.

So Row 2 is $(\sin 2\theta, -\cos 2\theta, 0)$.

The final reflection matrix:

$$
M = \begin{bmatrix} \cos 2\theta & \sin 2\theta & 0 \\ \sin 2\theta & -\cos 2\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Apply to $P(4, 3)$ with $\theta = 60°$:**

$\cos(120°) = -0.5$ and $\sin(120°) = \sqrt{3}/2 \approx 0.866$.

$$
M = \begin{bmatrix} -0.5 & 0.866 & 0 \\ 0.866 & 0.5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Applied to $P(4, 3)$:

$$
M \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 3 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.5 \cdot 4 + 0.866 \cdot 3 \\ 0.866 \cdot 4 + 0.5 \cdot 3 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -2 + 2.598 \\ 3.464 + 1.5 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.598 \\ 4.964 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

So $P' \approx (0.60, 4.96)$.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B (b)

**Combined shear matrix:**

$$
M_{Sh_{xy}} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & Sh_x & 0 \\ Sh_y & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.5 & 0 \\ 0.25 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Mapping rule:**

$$
x' = x + 0.5y, \quad y' = 0.25x + y
$$

**Apply to vertices:**

For $A(2, 1)$:

$$
x' = 2 + 0.5(1) = 2.5, \quad y' = 0.25(2) + 1 = 1.5 \Rightarrow A'(2.5, 1.5)
$$

For $B(6, 1)$:

$$
x' = 6 + 0.5(1) = 6.5, \quad y' = 0.25(6) + 1 = 2.5 \Rightarrow B'(6.5, 2.5)
$$

For $C(4, 5)$:

$$
x' = 4 + 0.5(5) = 6.5, \quad y' = 0.25(4) + 5 = 6 \Rightarrow C'(6.5, 6)
$$

**Area verification using Shoelace formula:**

Original area:

$$
A_{orig} = \tfrac{1}{2} \vert x_A(y_B - y_C) + x_B(y_C - y_A) + x_C(y_A - y_B) \vert
$$

$$
A_{orig} = \tfrac{1}{2} \vert 2(1 - 5) + 6(5 - 1) + 4(1 - 1) \vert = \tfrac{1}{2} \vert -8 + 24 + 0 \vert = 8
$$

Sheared area:

$$
A_{shear} = \tfrac{1}{2} \vert 2.5(2.5 - 6) + 6.5(6 - 1.5) + 6.5(1.5 - 2.5) \vert
$$

$$
A_{shear} = \tfrac{1}{2} \vert 2.5(-3.5) + 6.5(4.5) + 6.5(-1) \vert = \tfrac{1}{2} \vert -8.75 + 29.25 - 6.5 \vert = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 14 = 7
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A naive student might expect the sheared area to equal the original (since $\det(M_{Sh_{xy}}) = 1 - Sh_x \cdot Sh_y = 1 - (0.5)(0.25) = 0.875 \neq 1$). The **combined** shear matrix is **not** area-preserving in general — only the **pure** x-shear and pure y-shear are individually area-preserving. Here, the area is scaled by $\det = 0.875$, giving $8 \times 0.875 = 7$. ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Points:**
> 1. **Matrix multiplication order confusion** (loses 2–3 marks): Students write $M_x \cdot M_y$ when the question says "y-reflection first". Remember — rightmost matrix is applied first.
> 2. **Forgetting homogeneous coordinate**: The 3×3 matrix must always be used, not the 2×2. A 2×2 matrix cannot handle translation later.
> 3. **Confusing reflection-about-line-$y=x$ with rotation-by-90°**: The matrix $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$ is a 90° rotation; the matrix $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$ is reflection about $y = x$. A 90° rotation has determinant $+1$; the reflection has determinant $-1$.
> 4. **Sign errors in $y = -x$ reflection**: The matrix is $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 \\ -1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$, not $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$ (which is reflection about $y = x$).
> 5. **Not verifying the determinant**: Always compute $\det$ as a sanity check — KTU examiners award 1 mark for this if explicitly shown.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- **Reflection matrices** are orthogonal with **$\det = -1$**; **rotation matrices** are orthogonal with **$\det = +1$**.
- **Reflection about x-axis**: negate y. Matrix: $\text{diag}(1, -1, 1)$.
- **Reflection about y-axis**: negate x. Matrix: $\text{diag}(-1, 1, 1)$.
- **Reflection about origin**: negate both. Matrix: $\text{diag}(-1, -1, 1)$.
- **Reflection about $y = x$**: swap coordinates. Matrix: $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$.
- **Reflection about $y = -x$**: swap and negate. Matrix: $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$.
- **X-shear** preserves the x-axis (y unchanged); **Y-shear** preserves the y-axis (x unchanged).
- **X-shear matrix** has $Sh_x$ in position $(0, 1)$ — i.e., first row, second column.
- **Y-shear matrix** has $Sh_y$ in position $(1, 0)$ — i.e., second row, first column.
- **Pure x-shear and y-shear are area-preserving** ($\det = 1$); **combined shear** is area-preserving only when $Sh_x \cdot Sh_y = 0$.
- **General reflection about a line at angle $\theta$**: $M = \begin{bmatrix} \cos 2\theta & \sin 2\theta & 0 \\ \sin 2\theta & -\cos 2\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$.
- **Composite transformation** rule: $M_{\text{composite}} = M_{\text{last applied}} \cdot M_{\text{first applied}} \cdot P$ (rightmost = first).
- **Handedness flip**: Reflection changes orientation (clockwise ↔ counter-clockwise); shear does not.
- **Engineering applications**: Reflection for mirror imaging, 3D face symmetry, water reflection effects. Shearing for italic fonts, oblique projection, wind load simulation, document deskew.
- **Always include the third row** `[0 0 1]` in the homogeneous matrix — this is the universal KTU requirement.
- **Verify each result** with at least one numerical substitution; KTU examiners appreciate visible working.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Composite Transformations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT414 - COMPUTER GRAPHICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Composite Transformations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Composite Transformation** is a sequence of two or more elementary geometric transformations (Translation, Rotation, Scaling, Reflection, or Shearing) that are combined into a single equivalent transformation by multiplying their individual homogeneous coordinate matrices together. The result is a single transformation matrix that, when applied to a geometric object, produces the same final position and orientation as applying each transformation sequentially.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Definition:** A composite transformation is formed by the matrix product of individual transformation matrices, with each subsequent transformation multiplying on the **left-hand side** of the previous one (in column-vector convention). The combined matrix is called the **composite transformation matrix**.

Mathematically, for a sequence of $n$ transformations $T_1, T_2, T_3, \dots, T_n$ applied to a point $P$ in column-vector form, the resulting composite is expressed as:

$$P' = T_n \cdot T_{n-1} \cdots T_2 \cdot T_1 \cdot P$$

Each $T_i$ is a $3 \times 3$ homogeneous coordinate matrix in 2D graphics (or $4 \times 4$ in 3D).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are giving **directions to a tourist** in a city:
- **Step 1:** Walk 2 blocks North (Translation)
- **Step 2:** Turn 90° clockwise (Rotation)
- **Step 3:** Walk 3 blocks (Translation)

If the tourist follows these three steps, the **net effect** is that the tourist has moved from the origin to some final location at some final orientation. A *composite transformation* is like **collapsing these three instructions into a single equivalent instruction** — *"Walk diagonally to the coffee shop on 5th Avenue, facing east."* That single instruction is the composite of the three individual movements.

In graphics terms, instead of sending a 2D object through three separate transformation stages (which costs $3 \times 9 = 27$ multiply-add operations per vertex for $3 \times 3$ matrices), we **pre-compute** the combined $3 \times 3$ matrix once (cost: $9$ multiply-add operations per vertex). This is the **engineering motivation** for composite transformations — efficiency in real-time rendering.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why study composites?** Most "real" graphical effects (rotating a wheel around its center, scaling a shape from its midpoint, animating a sprite) cannot be expressed by a *single* elementary transformation. They always require **combinations** of translations, rotations, and scalings.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Glossary)

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Homogeneous Coordinates** | A $(n+1)$-dimensional representation of an $n$-dimensional point that enables all affine transformations (including translation) to be expressed as a single matrix multiplication. |
| **Pivot Point / Fixed Point** | The point about which a rotation or scaling is performed without translating the object. |
| **Pre-Multiplication** | A new transformation $T_{new}$ is applied to the current composite by multiplying on the **left**: $T_{composite} = T_{new} \cdot T_{old}$. |
| **Post-Multiplication** | A new transformation $T_{new}$ is applied by multiplying on the **right**: $T_{composite} = T_{old} \cdot T_{new}$. |
| **Left-to-Right Convention** | The leftmost matrix in the product is applied **last** to the geometric object. |

---

## 1.4 Real-World & Engineering Use Cases

- **CAD/CAM Software:** Rotating a gear tooth about the gear's center while scaling a drill bit around a fixed pivot.
- **Animation Pipelines:** Rigging character bones — each bone transformation is a composite of translation, rotation, and scaling relative to a parent bone.
- **Robotics:** End-effector positioning requires composing joint rotations and translations (forward kinematics).
- **Game Engines:** Every rendered sprite carries a single $4 \times 4$ **Model-View-Projection (MVP) matrix**, which is itself a composite of many sub-transformations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Composites Exist

The motivation arises from three fundamental truths in computer graphics:

1. **Translation is not a linear operation** in standard Cartesian 2D/3D coordinates. To express translation as a matrix multiplication, we **must** embed our points in a higher-dimensional space → **homogeneous coordinates**.
2. **Rotations and scalings are conventionally performed about the origin**, not about an arbitrary user-chosen pivot point (e.g., the center of a wheel).
3. **Real graphics applications always need pivot-based operations**, so we must build them by *composing* a translate-to-origin, an elementary transform, and a translate-back. This is the canonical composite.

## 2.2 Order of Operations — The Critical Rule

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Matrix multiplication is NOT commutative.** This is the single most common source of errors in composite transformation problems.
> $$T_2 \cdot T_1 \neq T_1 \cdot T_2 \quad \text{(in general)}$$

The order of matrix multiplication (in column-vector convention) corresponds to the order in which the transformations are applied to the geometry. The **leftmost matrix is applied LAST** to the object.

For a point $P$, the composite $C = T_3 \cdot T_2 \cdot T_1$ produces:
$$P' = T_3 \cdot (T_2 \cdot (T_1 \cdot P))$$

So $T_1$ acts first, then $T_2$, then $T_3$.

## 2.3 Three Canonical Composite Transformations (KTU High-Yield)

### (A) Rotation About an Arbitrary Pivot Point $(x_f, y_f)$

To rotate an object by angle $\theta$ about an arbitrary fixed point $(x_f, y_f)$:

$$
T(x_f, y_f) \cdot R(\theta) \cdot T(-x_f, -y_f)
$$

**Step-wise logic:**
1. $T(-x_f, -y_f)$ — Translate the object so the pivot coincides with the origin.
2. $R(\theta)$ — Rotate about the origin (elementary operation).
3. $T(x_f, y_f)$ — Translate the object back to its original pivot location.

### (B) Scaling About an Arbitrary Fixed Point $(x_f, y_f)$

To scale an object by factors $(s_x, s_y)$ with respect to fixed point $(x_f, y_f)$:

$$
T(x_f, y_f) \cdot S(s_x, s_y) \cdot T(-x_f, -y_f)
$$

### (C) General Directional Scaling

To scale an object in a direction specified by angle $\theta$:

$$
R(-\theta) \cdot S(s_x, s_y) \cdot R(\theta)
$$

**Step-wise logic:**
1. $R(\theta)$ — Rotate the object so the scaling direction aligns with the x-axis.
2. $S(s_x, s_y)$ — Perform the scaling along the x-axis.
3. $R(-\theta)$ — Rotate back to original orientation.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All elementary transformation matrices below are $3 \times 3$ homogeneous matrices for 2D graphics. For 3D, extend to $4 \times 4$.

| # | Transformation | Homogeneous Matrix $(3 \times 3)$ | KTU Notes |
|---|----------------|----------------------------------|-----------|
| 1 | **Translation** $T(t_x, t_y)$ | $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & t_x \\ 0 & 1 & t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ | Moves a point by vector $(t_x, t_y)$ |
| 2 | **Rotation** $R(\theta)$ about origin | $\begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ | Positive $\theta$ = counter-clockwise |
| 3 | **Scaling** $S(s_x, s_y)$ about origin | $\begin{pmatrix} s_x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & s_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ | $s_x, s_y > 1$ enlarges; between 0 and 1 shrinks |
| 4 | **Reflection about x-axis** | $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ | Mirror across x-axis |
| 5 | **Reflection about y-axis** | $\begin{pmatrix} -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ | Mirror across y-axis |
| 6 | **Reflection about line $y = x$** | $\begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ | Swap coordinates |
| 7 | **Shear along x-axis** $Sh_x$ | $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & sh_x & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ | $x' = x + sh_x \cdot y$ |
| 8 | **Shear along y-axis** $Sh_y$ | $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ sh_y & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ | $y' = y + sh_y \cdot x$ |
| 9 | **Composite: Rotate about pivot $(x_f, y_f)$ by $\theta$** | $T(x_f, y_f) \cdot R(\theta) \cdot T(-x_f, -y_f)$ | Step: translate-to-origin → rotate → translate-back |
| 10 | **Composite: Scale about fixed point $(x_f, y_f)$** | $T(x_f, y_f) \cdot S(s_x, s_y) \cdot T(-x_f, -y_f)$ | Same sandwich pattern as rotation |
| 11 | **Composite: General 2D Rigid (Rotation + Translation)** | $\begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & t_x \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ | Often used in robotics |
| 12 | **Identity Matrix** $I$ | $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ | Neutral element of composition |

## 2.5 Properties of Composite Transformations

- **Associativity:** $(T_3 \cdot T_2) \cdot T_1 = T_3 \cdot (T_2 \cdot T_1)$ — order of *evaluation* may be grouped, but the left-to-right application order is preserved.
- **Non-Commutativity:** $T_2 \cdot T_1 \neq T_1 \cdot T_2$ for translations, rotations, and scalings involving different parameters.
- **Identity:** $I \cdot T = T \cdot I = T$.
- **Inverse:** Every invertible transformation $T$ has an inverse $T^{-1}$ such that $T \cdot T^{-1} = I$.
- **Determinant property:** A composite of pure rotations has determinant $= +1$ (preserves orientation). A composite involving an odd number of reflections has determinant $= -1$ (reverses orientation).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Composite Matrix for Rotation About an Arbitrary Pivot

**Goal:** Derive the explicit $3 \times 3$ composite matrix for rotating any point $(x, y)$ by angle $\theta$ about the pivot $(x_f, y_f)$.

### Step 1 — Write the three elementary matrices.

Translation to bring pivot to origin:
$$T(-x_f, -y_f) = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & -x_f \\ 0 & 1 & -y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

Rotation by $\theta$ about the origin:
$$R(\theta) = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

Translation back to original pivot:
$$T(x_f, y_f) = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & x_f \\ 0 & 1 & y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

### Step 2 — Compute $R(\theta) \cdot T(-x_f, -y_f)$ (intermediate product).

We multiply $R(\theta)$ on the left of $T(-x_f, -y_f)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R(\theta) \cdot T(-x_f, -y_f) &= \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & -x_f \\ 0 & 1 & -y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \\
&= \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta \cdot 1 + (-\sin\theta) \cdot 0 + 0 \cdot 0 & \cos\theta \cdot 0 + (-\sin\theta) \cdot 1 + 0 \cdot 0 & \cos\theta \cdot (-x_f) + (-\sin\theta) \cdot (-y_f) + 0 \cdot 1 \\ \sin\theta \cdot 1 + \cos\theta \cdot 0 + 0 \cdot 0 & \sin\theta \cdot 0 + \cos\theta \cdot 1 + 0 \cdot 0 & \sin\theta \cdot (-x_f) + \cos\theta \cdot (-y_f) + 0 \cdot 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
R(\theta) \cdot T(-x_f, -y_f) = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & -x_f \cos\theta + y_f \sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & -x_f \sin\theta - y_f \cos\theta \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the full composite: $T(x_f, y_f) \cdot [R(\theta) \cdot T(-x_f, -y_f)]$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & x_f \\ 0 & 1 & y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & -x_f \cos\theta + y_f \sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & -x_f \sin\theta - y_f \cos\theta \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \\
&= \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & -x_f \cos\theta + y_f \sin\theta + x_f \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & -x_f \sin\theta - y_f \cos\theta + y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Simplify the translation column.

The $(1,3)$ entry: $x_f - x_f \cos\theta + y_f \sin\theta = x_f(1 - \cos\theta) + y_f \sin\theta$.

The $(2,3)$ entry: $y_f - x_f \sin\theta - y_f \cos\theta = y_f(1 - \cos\theta) - x_f \sin\theta$.

### Final Result (the KTU Exam Formula):

$$
\boxed{
C_{pivot\text{-}rot} = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & x_f(1 - \cos\theta) + y_f \sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & y_f(1 - \cos\theta) - x_f \sin\theta \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Composite Matrix for Scaling About a Fixed Point

**Goal:** Derive the explicit matrix for scaling an object by $(s_x, s_y)$ about fixed point $(x_f, y_f)$.

### Step 1 — Form the sandwich product $T(x_f, y_f) \cdot S(s_x, s_y) \cdot T(-x_f, -y_f)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(s_x, s_y) \cdot T(-x_f, -y_f) &= \begin{pmatrix} s_x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & s_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & -x_f \\ 0 & 1 & -y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \\
&= \begin{pmatrix} s_x & 0 & -s_x x_f \\ 0 & s_y & -s_y y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Multiply by the front translation.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & x_f \\ 0 & 1 & y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} s_x & 0 & -s_x x_f \\ 0 & s_y & -s_y y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \\
&= \begin{pmatrix} s_x & 0 & x_f(1 - s_x) \\ 0 & s_y & y_f(1 - s_y) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Result:

$$
\boxed{
C_{pivot\text{-}scale} = \begin{pmatrix} s_x & 0 & x_f(1 - s_x) \\ 0 & s_y & y_f(1 - s_y) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
}
$$

This is **directly testable** in KTU exams. Notice the structural symmetry with the rotation case.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — KTU-Style Numerical Problem

**Problem (KTU University Exam - May 2024 style):**
A triangle has vertices $A(2, 1)$, $B(5, 1)$, $C(3, 4)$. Apply the following composite transformation:
1. Rotate by $90°$ counter-clockwise about the pivot point $P(2, 1)$.
2. Then scale the result by $2$ in the $x$-direction and $3$ in the $y$-direction about the same pivot $P(2, 1)$.

Find the final coordinates of the triangle.

### Solution

**Step A — Form the rotation composite matrix using the formula from Section 3.1.**

For $\theta = 90°$, we have $\cos 90° = 0$ and $\sin 90° = 1$.

Pivot: $x_f = 2$, $y_f = 1$.

Plugging into $C_{pivot\text{-}rot}$:

$$
R_{pivot} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & -1 & 2(1-0) + 1 \cdot 1 \\ 1 & 0 & 1(1-0) - 2 \cdot 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & -1 & 3 \\ 1 & 0 & -1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step B — Form the scaling composite matrix using the formula from Section 3.2.**

For $s_x = 2$, $s_y = 3$, with same pivot $(2, 1)$:

$$
S_{pivot} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 & 2(1-2) \\ 0 & 3 & 1(1-3) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 & -2 \\ 0 & 3 & -2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step C — Apply the rotation to vertex $A(2, 1)$.**

In homogeneous form, $A_h = \begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$.

$$
R_{pivot} \cdot A_h = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & -1 & 3 \\ 1 & 0 & -1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 - 1 + 3 \\ 2 + 0 - 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Sanity check:** Vertex $A$ is the pivot point itself, so rotation about $A$ should leave it unchanged. ✓

**Step D — Apply the scaling (in the second step) to the rotated $A$.**

$$
S_{pivot} \cdot \begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 & -2 \\ 0 & 3 & -2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 - 2 \\ 3 - 2 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Sanity check:** The pivot point also remains invariant under scaling about itself. ✓

**Step E — Apply rotation to vertex $B(5, 1)$.**

$$
R_{pivot} \cdot \begin{pmatrix} 5 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \cdot 5 - 1 \cdot 1 + 3 \\ 1 \cdot 5 + 0 \cdot 1 - 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 4 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

So $B$ moves to $(2, 4)$ after rotation.

**Step F — Apply scaling to the rotated $B$ at $(2, 4)$.**

$$
S_{pivot} \cdot \begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 4 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 \cdot 2 + 0 - 2 \\ 0 + 3 \cdot 4 - 2 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 10 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step G — Apply rotation to vertex $C(3, 4)$.**

$$
R_{pivot} \cdot \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 4 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 - 4 + 3 \\ 3 + 0 - 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} -1 \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

So $C$ moves to $(-1, 2)$ after rotation.

**Step H — Apply scaling to the rotated $C$ at $(-1, 2)$.**

$$
S_{pivot} \cdot \begin{pmatrix} -1 \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 \cdot (-1) - 2 \\ 3 \cdot 2 - 2 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} -4 \\ 4 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

### Final Answer

$$
\boxed{A'(2, 1), \quad B'(2, 10), \quad C'(-4, 4)}
$$

**Geometric verification:** Vertex $A$ (the pivot) is unchanged. Vertices $B$ and $C$ have been rotated and then stretched away from the pivot. The result is consistent.

---

## 3.4 Order-Dependence Counter-Example (KTU High-Yield Pitfall)

Consider the same point $P(5, 1)$, pivot $(2, 1)$, rotation $90°$ and scaling $(2, 3)$.

**Order 1: Rotate first, then Scale (as above):**
After rotation, $B$ was at $(2, 4)$. After scaling about $(2, 1)$:
$$(2, 4) \to (2, 10)$$

**Order 2: Scale first, then Rotate:**

Apply scaling about $(2, 1)$ to $(5, 1)$:
$$
\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 & -2 \\ 0 & 3 & -2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 5 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 8 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

Then apply rotation about $(2, 1)$ to $(8, 1)$:
$$
\begin{pmatrix} 0 & -1 & 3 \\ 1 & 0 & -1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 8 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 7 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Result:** Order 1 gives $(2, 10)$, Order 2 gives $(2, 7)$. **They are different!** This proves non-commutativity and is a classic KTU problem.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Composite Transformation Pipeline

The diagram below illustrates the data-flow architecture of a composite transformation pipeline, mapping how a geometric object traverses the sequence of elementary transforms in the graphics rendering engine.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Object P at x y] --> B[Step 1 T1 Matrix]
    B --> C[Step 2 T2 Matrix]
    C --> D[Step 3 T3 Matrix]
    D --> E[Output Object P final]
    F[Homogeneous Embedding] --> B
    G[Pivot Point xf yf] --> H[Translate to Origin T neg pivot]
    H --> I[Elementary Transform R or S]
    I --> J[Translate Back T pivot]
    J --> K[Composite Matrix C stored once]
    K --> L[Reused for All Vertices]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Order of Operations Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OrderA[Order A: Rotate then Scale]
        A1[Original Triangle] --> A2[Rotate 90 deg]
        A2 --> A3[Scale 2x 3x]
        A3 --> A4[Final A]
    end
    subgraph OrderB[Order B: Scale then Rotate]
        B1[Original Triangle] --> B2[Scale 2x 3x]
        B2 --> B3[Rotate 90 deg]
        B3 --> B4[Final B]
    end
    A4 -. Different result .-> A4note((Final A ≠ Final B))
    B4 -. Different result .-> B4note((Proves non-commutativity))
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Conceptual Map — Transformation Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Composite Transformation] --> R1[Translation T]
    R --> R2[Rotation R theta]
    R --> R3[Scaling S sx sy]
    R --> R4[Reflection]
    R --> R5[Shearing]
    R1 --> C1[Pivot-Based R about xf yf]
    R2 --> C1
    R3 --> C2[Pivot-Based S about xf yf]
    R4 --> C3[Reflection Composite]
    R5 --> C4[Shear Composite]
    C1 --> F[3x3 Homogeneous Matrix]
    C2 --> F
    C3 --> F
    C4 --> F
    F --> APP[Applied to Vertices x y 1]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Architecture — Sandwich-Pattern Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[Final Composite C] --> M1[T forward xf yf]
    M1 --> M2[Elementary R or S at Origin]
    M2 --> M3[T backward neg xf neg yf]
    M3 --> M4[Pre-multiplied Sequence]
    M4 -. Identity proof .-> M5[I Identity Matrix]
```
