Similarly, 3D translation uses a $4 \times 4$ homogeneous matrix.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Translation is classified as a *non-linear* transformation in terms of origin movement but becomes *linear* when expressed in homogeneous coordinates. It is one of the four fundamental transformations (Translation, Rotation, Scaling, Shearing) covered in Module 3.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you have a **photograph of a book lying on a table**. If you slide the entire book 10 cm to the right and 5 cm forward (towards you), every single point on the book — its corners, its pages, even the tiny text on the cover — moves by exactly the same amount in the same direction. No point gets left behind, no point moves differently, and the book's shape, size, and orientation remain absolutely unchanged. That pure, uniform "sliding" motion is precisely what we call **Translation** in computer graphics.

A more technical analogy: think of translation as the action of a **CNC machine's plotter head** receiving a command like "G-code G00 X+10 Y+5" — the head moves by an absolute offset $(10, 5)$ from its current location without any rotation or scaling of its drawing tool.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Translation is the simplest transformation because it never alters the geometry, size, or orientation of an object — only its position in space. The **Euclidean distance** between any two points of the object is preserved exactly ($\sqrt{(t_x)^2 + (t_y)^2}$ displacement is uniform).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

Translation operates through a strictly deterministic pipeline. The reasoning behind each step is explained below:

- **Step 1 — Identify the Translation Vector:** The user/system specifies a displacement vector $\mathbf{t} = (t_x, t_y)$ in 2D (or $(t_x, t_y, t_z)$ in 3D). This vector represents *how far* and *in what direction* the object must move.
- **Step 2 — Vector Addition per Vertex:** For every vertex $P_i = (x_i, y_i)$ of the object, the translation vector is added component-wise: $P_i' = P_i + \mathbf{t}$. For a polyline with $n$ vertices, $n$ additions are performed.
- **Step 3 — Reconnection of Edges:** The translated vertices $P_i'$ are then reconnected using the same edge topology as the original object, producing the translated image.
- **Step 4 — Homogeneous Representation (for pipeline compatibility):** Because standard $2 \times 2$ matrix multiplication cannot express translation (it always anchors at the origin), we embed the point into a 3D space (homogeneous coordinates) using a $3 \times 3$ matrix, allowing the entire transform to be a single multiply.
- **Step 5 — Inverse Operation (Untranslation):** Translating back to the original location is achieved by negating the vector: $\mathbf{t}^{-1} = (-t_x, -t_y)$. The inverse matrix is the transpose (for orthogonal) but for translation, it is the negated translation matrix in the same form.

### 2.2 Why Homogeneous Coordinates Are Mandatory

In affine transformation theory, **linear transformations** (rotation, scaling, shearing) are expressible as $2 \times 2$ matrices, but translation is *affine*, not *linear*. To unify all four transformations under a single matrix-multiplication framework, computer graphics uses homogeneous coordinates by appending a constant **1** to every point:

$$P_{homog} = (x, y, 1)^T$$

This allows the entire transformation pipeline — translate, rotate, scale, shear, project — to be chained as a sequence of $3 \times 3$ (2D) or $4 \times 4$ (3D) matrix multiplications, which is exactly how GPUs and rendering engines operate.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Translation is used in:
- **CAD/CAM systems** for moving components on a design grid.
- **Animation pipelines** to reposition characters and props between keyframes.
- **Robotics** for end-effector path planning.
- **Geospatial Information Systems (GIS)** to overlay satellite tiles.
- **Game engines** (Unity, Unreal) to update the world position of every object per frame.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Absolute Exam-Booster Table** — Memorize the matrix structures; KTU frequently tests the homogeneous matrix form.

| Transformation | Dimension | Homogeneous Matrix | Equations | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2D Translation | $3 \times 3$ | $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & t_x \\ 0 & 1 & t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $x' = x + t_x$ , $y' = y + t_y$ | Origin remains at $(0,0)$ |
| 3D Translation | $4 \times 4$ | $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 & t_x \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & t_z \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $x' = x + t_x$ , $y' = y + t_y$ , $z' = z + t_z$ | Right-handed coord. system |
| 2D Inverse Translation | $3 \times 3$ | $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -t_x \\ 0 & 1 & -t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $x' = x - t_x$ , $y' = y - t_y$ | Used for un-translation |
| 3D Inverse Translation | $4 \times 4$ | $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 & -t_x \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & -t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & -t_z \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $x' = x - t_x$ , $y' = y - t_y$ , $z' = z - t_z$ | Negated vector |
| Composite 2D Translation | $3 \times 3$ | $T(t_{x1},t_{y1}) \cdot T(t_{x2},t_{y2}) = T(t_{x1}+t_{x2}, t_{y1}+t_{y2})$ | $T_{total} = T_1 \cdot T_2$ | Addition of vectors |
| Composite 3D Translation | $4 \times 4$ | $T(t_{x1},t_{y1},t_{z1}) \cdot T(t_{x2},t_{y2},t_{z2}) = T(t_{x1}+t_{x2}, t_{y1}+t_{y2}, t_{z1}+t_{z2})$ | $T_{total} = T_1 \cdot T_2$ | Component-wise sum |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pitfall:** The order of multiplication in composite transformations is **NOT** commutative in general. However, for two pure translations, $T_1 \cdot T_2 = T_2 \cdot T_1$ because translation matrices form an **abelian subgroup** of the affine group. This is a frequently asked KTU conceptual question.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the 2D Translation Matrix in Homogeneous Form

We start with the direct vector-addition form:

$$\begin{aligned}
x' &= x + t_x \\
y' &= y + t_y
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 1 — Express as a linear system.** We rewrite the equations in matrix-friendly form by introducing a third dummy variable $w$ that we will set to 1.

$$\begin{aligned}
x' &= 1 \cdot x + 0 \cdot y + t_x \cdot 1 \\
y' &= 0 \cdot x + 1 \cdot y + t_y \cdot 1 \\
1  &= 0 \cdot x + 0 \cdot y + 1 \cdot 1
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Encapsulate in matrix form.** The above three equations can be written compactly as a single matrix multiplication.

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} &=
\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & t_x \\ 0 & 1 & t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Notation shorthand.** The translation matrix is denoted $T(t_x, t_y)$. The transformation is called a *rigid translation* because the bottom row is always $(0\ 0\ 1)$ for 2D and $(0\ 0\ 0\ 1)$ for 3D, preserving the homogeneous coordinate.

### 3.2 Derivation of Composite 2D Translation

When two translations $T_1(t_{x1}, t_{y1})$ and $T_2(t_{x2}, t_{y2})$ are applied sequentially, the resulting composite matrix is their product.

**Step 1 — Write the two individual matrices.**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & t_{x1} \\ 0 & 1 & t_{y1} \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
T_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & t_{x2} \\ 0 & 1 & t_{y2} \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Multiply the matrices using row-by-column rule.**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{total} = T_1 \cdot T_2 &=
\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & t_{x1} \\ 0 & 1 & t_{y1} \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & t_{x2} \\ 0 & 1 & t_{y2} \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute each entry of the product.**

- Row 1, Col 1: $(1)(1) + (0)(0) + (t_{x1})(0) = 1$
- Row 1, Col 2: $(1)(0) + (0)(1) + (t_{x1})(0) = 0$
- Row 1, Col 3: $(1)(t_{x2}) + (0)(t_{y2}) + (t_{x1})(1) = t_{x1} + t_{x2}$
- Row 2, Col 1: $(0)(1) + (1)(0) + (t_{y1})(0) = 0$
- Row 2, Col 2: $(0)(0) + (1)(1) + (t_{y1})(0) = 1$
- Row 2, Col 3: $(0)(t_{x2}) + (1)(t_{y2}) + (t_{y1})(1) = t_{y1} + t_{y2}$
- Row 3: $(0, 0, 1)$ — unchanged.

**Step 4 — Assemble the result.**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{total} =
\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & t_{x1} + t_{x2} \\ 0 & 1 & t_{y1} + t_{y2} \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
= T(t_{x1} + t_{x2},\ t_{y1} + t_{y2})
\end{aligned}$$

This proves that **sequential translations are equivalent to a single translation whose vector is the sum of the individual vectors**. The same derivation extends directly to 3D, giving $T_{total} = T(t_{x1}+t_{x2}, t_{y1}+t_{y2}, t_{z1}+t_{z2})$.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Inverse Translation Matrix

The inverse of a transformation $T$ is the matrix $T^{-1}$ such that $T \cdot T^{-1} = I$ (the identity matrix). For translation, this means moving back by the negative of the original vector.

**Step 1 — Propose the inverse candidate.** We guess $T^{-1} = T(-t_x, -t_y)$.

$$\begin{aligned}
T^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -t_x \\ 0 & 1 & -t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Verify by multiplication with $T$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
T \cdot T^{-1} &=
\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & t_x \\ 0 & 1 & t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -t_x \\ 0 & 1 & -t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the product.**

- Row 1, Col 3: $(1)(-t_x) + (0)(-t_y) + (t_x)(1) = -t_x + t_x = 0$
- Row 2, Col 3: $(0)(-t_x) + (1)(-t_y) + (t_y)(1) = -t_y + t_y = 0$
- All other diagonal entries are 1, off-diagonal entries are 0.

$$\begin{aligned}
T \cdot T^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = I
\end{aligned}$$

Hence $T^{-1} = T(-t_x, -t_y)$ is correct. This is also why the **inverse translation** is computed by simply negating both translation components.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (2D)

**Problem:** A triangle has vertices $A(2, 3)$, $B(5, 7)$, $C(8, 4)$. Translate it by $\mathbf{t} = (3, -2)$. Find the new coordinates and the composite translation matrix.

**Step 1 — Set up the translation matrix.**

$$\begin{aligned}
T(3, -2) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 3 \\ 0 & 1 & -2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Apply to vertex A.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} x_A' \\ y_A' \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} &=
\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 3 \\ 0 & 1 & -2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} 2 + 3 \\ 3 - 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Apply to vertex B.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} x_B' \\ y_B' \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} &=
\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 3 \\ 0 & 1 & -2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 7 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} 5 + 3 \\ 7 - 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} 8 \\ 5 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Apply to vertex C.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} x_C' \\ y_C' \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} &=
\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 3 \\ 0 & 1 & -2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} 8 \\ 4 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} 8 + 3 \\ 4 - 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} 11 \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Result:** The translated triangle has vertices $A'(5, 1)$, $B'(8, 5)$, $C'(11, 2)$. Notice that the side lengths of the triangle are preserved — only its position in the plane has shifted by the vector $(3, -2)$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of 2D Translation

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the full operational pipeline of a 2D translation as executed inside a graphics system. It shows how a user-defined translation vector, the original geometric object, and the homogeneous transformation interact to produce the translated image.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Input Translation Vector tx ty] --> C[Construct Homogeneous Translation Matrix T tx ty]
    B[Original Object Vertices x y] --> D[Append Homogeneous Coordinate w equals 1]
    D --> E[Form Column Vector x y 1]
    C --> F[Perform Matrix Multiplication T dot P]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Extract Translated Coordinates x prime y prime from result]
    G --> H[Reconnect Edges Using Original Topology]
    H --> I[Render Translated Object on Display]
    J[Inverse Translation Vector minus tx minus ty] -.-> C
    K[Composite Translation T1 then T2] -.-> C
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of 3D Translation

For 3D translation, the pipeline is analogous but operates on four-component homogeneous coordinates $(x, y, z, 1)$. The block diagram below shows the augmented data flow including the z-axis displacement and the projection-ready output.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Original 3D Point P x y z] --> Q1[Convert to Homogeneous Form x y z 1]
    Q1 --> R1[Multiply by 4x4 Translation Matrix T]
    T[Translation Matrix with tx ty tz components] --> R1
    R1 --> S1[Extract New Point P prime x prime y prime z prime]
    S1 --> U1[Pass to Next Stage Rotation Scaling or Projection]
    P2[3D Object with n Vertices] --> V1[Apply T to Every Vertex in Loop]
    V1 --> W1[Reassemble Polygon Mesh or Triangle List]
    W1 --> X1[Output Translated 3D Object]
```

### 4.3 Translation-Only Transformation Pipeline (Conceptual Hierarchy)

This block diagram shows where translation sits in the broader KTU Module 3 transformation hierarchy and how it interfaces with the other three core transformations (rotation, scaling, shearing) through the unifying homogeneous matrix framework.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0[Geometric Transformation Module 3 KTU] --> L1[Affine Transformations]
    L1 --> L2[Translation tx ty tz]
    L1 --> L3[Rotation theta]
    L1 --> L4[Scaling sx sy sz]
    L1 --> L5[Shearing shx shy]
    L2 --> M1[Uses Homogeneous Coordinates 3x3 for 2D and 4x4 for 3D]
    L3 --> M1
    L4 --> M1
    L5 --> M1
    M1 --> N1[Composite Matrix CT equals T times R times S]
    N1 --> O1[Applied as CT times P to Every Vertex]
    O1 --> P1[Final Transformed Object on Screen]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Tip:** In the Mermaid diagrams, every node ID follows the alphanumeric rule (e.g., `L0`, `M1`, `P2`). All labels are kept as plain uppercase text without any markdown formatting, in accordance with the Mermaid compilation safeguard protocol.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** Define translation in computer graphics. Write the 2D translation matrix using homogeneous coordinates. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Translation is a geometric transformation that repositions every point of an object by the same displacement vector $\mathbf{t} = (t_x, t_y)$ in 2D (or $(t_x, t_y, t_z)$ in 3D), without altering its shape, size, or orientation. The new coordinates of a point $P(x, y)$ are obtained as $x' = x + t_x$ and $y' = y + t_y$. To express translation as a matrix multiplication, homogeneous coordinates are used, which append a third coordinate $w = 1$ to the point.

The 2D translation matrix in homogeneous form is:

$$\begin{aligned}
T(t_x, t_y) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & t_x \\ 0 & 1 & t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

Such that $\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = T \cdot \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$.

**Key Concept Tested:** Homogeneous coordinate form and the rigid-body nature of translation.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** What is the inverse of a 2D translation? Under what condition is it useful? **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The inverse of a 2D translation $T(t_x, t_y)$ is another translation $T^{-1}(-t_x, -t_y)$ that moves the object back to its original position by negating the translation vector components.

$$\begin{aligned}
T^{-1}(t_x, t_y) = T(-t_x, -t_y) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -t_x \\ 0 & 1 & -t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Verification:** $T \cdot T^{-1} = I$ (the $3 \times 3$ identity matrix).

**Usefulness:** The inverse translation is widely applied in:

- **Animation systems** to return an animated object to its initial keyframe.
- **CAD systems** when a user issues an "undo" command.
- **Computer vision** when a previously registered image is being de-warped.
- **Hierarchical scene graphs** where child objects must be transformed into the local frame of their parent.

**Key Concept Tested:** Inverse of an affine transformation and its geometric meaning.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Part B questions carry 14 marks and follow a standard split of **7 marks + 7 marks**. Students must attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B (internal choice). Below, both alternatives are provided for full preparation.

---

### **Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a) Explain 2D translation with a suitable diagram. Derive the homogeneous 2D translation matrix and prove that two successive translations are equivalent to a single translation whose vector is the sum of the two individual vectors. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Concept of 2D Translation.** Translation in 2D shifts an object from one location to another in the XY-plane by a constant displacement vector $\mathbf{t} = (t_x, t_y)$. For a point $P(x, y)$, the new location is $P'(x + t_x,\ y + t_y)$. All points of the object undergo the same displacement; hence the shape and size are preserved.

**Step 2 — Need for Homogeneous Coordinates.** A $2 \times 2$ matrix can represent only linear transformations (rotation, scaling) that keep the origin fixed. To include translation (an affine operation), an extra dimension is added by representing the 2D point $(x, y)$ as the homogeneous 3-vector $(x, y, 1)$.

**Step 3 — Derivation of the 2D Translation Matrix.** Starting from the scalar equations $x' = x + t_x$ and $y' = y + t_y$, we rewrite them as:

$$\begin{aligned}
x' &= 1 \cdot x + 0 \cdot y + t_x \cdot 1 \\
y' &= 0 \cdot x + 1 \cdot y + t_y \cdot 1 \\
1  &= 0 \cdot x + 0 \cdot y + 1 \cdot 1
\end{aligned}$$

This is equivalent to the matrix equation:

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} &=
\underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & t_x \\ 0 & 1 & t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}}_{T(t_x, t_y)}
\begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Proof of Composite Translation.** Let $T_1 = T(t_{x1}, t_{y1})$ and $T_2 = T(t_{x2}, t_{y2})$ be two successive translations. Their product is:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_1 \cdot T_2 &=
\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & t_{x1} \\ 0 & 1 & t_{y1} \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & t_{x2} \\ 0 & 1 & t_{y2} \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

Computing the (1,3) entry: $(1)(t_{x2}) + (0)(t_{y2}) + (t_{x1})(1) = t_{x1} + t_{x2}$.
Computing the (2,3) entry: $(0)(t_{x2}) + (1)(t_{y2}) + (t_{y1})(1) = t_{y1} + t_{y2}$.

Therefore:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_1 \cdot T_2 = T(t_{x1} + t_{x2},\ t_{y1} + t_{y2})
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** The composition of two translations is itself a translation with the additive vector $(t_{x1}+t_{x2}, t_{y1}+t_{y2})$. By induction, this extends to $n$ successive translations whose vectors simply sum up component-wise.

---

**(b) A square has vertices $A(1,1)$, $B(4,1)$, $C(4,4)$, $D(1,4)$. Apply a 2D translation of $\mathbf{t} = (5, 3)$ and find the coordinates of the translated square. Verify that the side length of the square is preserved. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Translation Matrix.** The homogeneous translation matrix is:

$$\begin{aligned}
T(5, 3) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 5 \\ 0 & 1 & 3 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Translate Vertex A(1, 1).**

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} x_A' \\ y_A' \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} &=
\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 5 \\ 0 & 1 & 3 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} 1 + 5 \\ 1 + 3 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} 6 \\ 4 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

So $A' = (6, 4)$.

**Step 3 — Translate Vertex B(4, 1).**

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} x_B' \\ y_B' \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} &=
\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 5 \\ 0 & 1 & 3 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} 4 + 5 \\ 1 + 3 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} 9 \\ 4 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

So $B' = (9, 4)$.

**Step 4 — Translate Vertex C(4, 4).**

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} x_C' \\ y_C' \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} &=
\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 5 \\ 0 & 1 & 3 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 4 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} 4 + 5 \\ 4 + 3 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} 9 \\ 7 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

So $C' = (9, 7)$.

**Step 5 — Translate Vertex D(1, 4).**

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} x_D' \\ y_D' \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} &=
\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 5 \\ 0 & 1 & 3 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 4 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} 1 + 5 \\ 4 + 3 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} 6 \\ 7 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

So $D' = (6, 7)$.

**Step 6 — Verify Side Length Preservation.**

Original side length (e.g., $AB$): $\sqrt{(4-1)^2 + (1-1)^2} = \sqrt{9} = 3$.
Translated side length (e.g., $A'B'$): $\sqrt{(9-6)^2 + (4-4)^2} = \sqrt{9} = 3$.

**Conclusion:** The translated square has vertices $A'(6, 4)$, $B'(9, 4)$, $C'(9, 7)$, $D'(6, 7)$, and its side length remains **3 units** — proving that translation is a **rigid transformation** that preserves distance.

---

### **Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a) Explain 3D translation in computer graphics. Derive the $4 \times 4$ homogeneous translation matrix and discuss its properties. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Concept of 3D Translation.** A 3D translation repositions every point of a 3D object by a constant displacement vector $\mathbf{t} = (t_x, t_y, t_z)$ along the X, Y, and Z axes. The new coordinates of a point $P(x, y, z)$ are:

$$\begin{aligned}
x' &= x + t_x \\
y' &= y + t_y \\
z' &= z + t_z
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Derivation of the $4 \times 4$ Homogeneous Matrix.** To express 3D translation as a matrix multiplication, the point is embedded in 4D homogeneous coordinates $(x, y, z, 1)$. The translation matrix is:

$$\begin{aligned}
T(t_x, t_y, t_z) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 & t_x \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & t_z \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Derivation from scalar equations:** The three scalar equations can be written as:

$$\begin{aligned}
x' &= 1 \cdot x + 0 \cdot y + 0 \cdot z + t_x \cdot 1 \\
y' &= 0 \cdot x + 1 \cdot y + 0 \cdot z + t_y \cdot 1 \\
z' &= 0 \cdot x + 0 \cdot y + 1 \cdot z + t_z \cdot 1 \\
1  &= 0 \cdot x + 0 \cdot y + 0 \cdot z + 1 \cdot 1
\end{aligned}$$

Reading off the coefficients of $x, y, z, 1$ gives the $4 \times 4$ matrix above.

**Step 3 — Properties of the 3D Translation Matrix.**

- **Identity when zero vector:** $T(0, 0, 0) = I_4$, the $4 \times 4$ identity.
- **Inverse exists:** $T^{-1} = T(-t_x, -t_y, -t_z)$ (verified by $T \cdot T^{-1} = I$).
- **Composition:** $T_1 \cdot T_2 = T(t_{x1}+t_{x2}, t_{y1}+t_{y2}, t_{z1}+t_{z2})$.
- **Commutativity:** $T_1 \cdot T_2 = T_2 \cdot T_1$ for pure translations only.
- **Rigid body:** Preserves distances, angles, and orientation.

**Step 4 — Application.** 3D translation is used to move models in a virtual world (e.g., a player walking in a game), to align medical imaging volumes, and to perform camera dolly movements in 3D animation.

---

**(b) A 3D object has a vertex at $P(2, 3, 5)$. Apply a translation of $\mathbf{t}_1 = (4, -2, 1)$ followed by a translation of $\mathbf{t}_2 = (-1, 3, 2)$. Compute the new position of the vertex using: (i) two separate matrix multiplications, and (ii) the composite translation matrix. Verify that both methods yield the same result. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Method (i) — Two Separate Matrix Multiplications:**

**Step 1 — First translation $\mathbf{t}_1 = (4, -2, 1)$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 & 4 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & -2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
P = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \\ 5 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P_1 = T_1 \cdot P =
\begin{bmatrix} 1 \cdot 2 + 0 + 0 + 4 \cdot 1 \\ 0 + 1 \cdot 3 + 0 + (-2) \cdot 1 \\ 0 + 0 + 1 \cdot 5 + 1 \cdot 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} 6 \\ 1 \\ 6 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

So $P_1 = (6, 1, 6)$.

**Step 2 — Second translation $\mathbf{t}_2 = (-1, 3, 2)$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 & -1 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & 3 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P_2 = T_2 \cdot P_1 =
\begin{bmatrix} 1 \cdot 6 + 0 + 0 + (-1) \cdot 1 \\ 0 + 1 \cdot 1 + 0 + 3 \cdot 1 \\ 0 + 0 + 1 \cdot 6 + 2 \cdot 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 4 \\ 8 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

So $P_2 = (5, 4, 8)$.

**Method (ii) — Composite Translation Matrix:**

**Step 1 — Compute the composite $T_{total} = T_1 \cdot T_2$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{total} = T_1 \cdot T_2 &=
\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 & 4 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & -2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 & -1 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & 3 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the (1,4), (2,4), (3,4) entries (all other entries form the identity block):**

- Row 1, Col 4: $(1)(-1) + (0)(3) + (0)(2) + (4)(1) = -1 + 4 = 3$
- Row 2, Col 4: $(0)(-1) + (1)(3) + (0)(2) + (-2)(1) = 3 - 2 = 1$
- Row 3, Col 4: $(0)(-1) + (0)(3) + (1)(2) + (1)(1) = 2 + 1 = 3$

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{total} = T(3, 1, 3) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 & 3 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 3 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Apply $T_{total}$ to $P$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_2 = T_{total} \cdot P =
\begin{bmatrix} 1 \cdot 2 + 3 \cdot 1 \\ 1 \cdot 3 + 1 \cdot 1 \\ 1 \cdot 5 + 3 \cdot 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 4 \\ 8 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Verification:** Both methods produce the identical result $P_2 = (5, 4, 8)$. This confirms the **composite translation theorem** for 3D.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Zones Identified by Senior KTU Evaluators:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Homogeneous Coordinate ($-1$ to $-2$ marks):** When students write the 2D translation as a $2 \times 2$ matrix $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & t_x \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$, they are marked down heavily. Always use the $3 \times 3$ homogeneous form for 2D and $4 \times 4$ for 3D.
> 2. **Matrix-Multiplication Order Confusion ($-2$ marks):** In composite transformations, the *last* transformation is applied to the *left* of the matrix equation: $P_{final} = T_n \cdot \ldots \cdot T_2 \cdot T_1 \cdot P$. Students often reverse this.
> 3. **Skipping the Composite Proof ($-3$ marks):** For 7-mark sub-questions asking "prove that successive translations are additive," students must show the matrix product and isolate the $(1,3)$ and $(2,3)$ entries explicitly. A mere verbal statement is insufficient.
> 4. **Ignoring the Inverse Verification ($-1$ mark):** When asked for $T^{-1}$, simply writing $T(-t_x, -t_y)$ without verifying $T \cdot T^{-1} = I$ loses a mark.
> 5. **Units and Coordinate Confusion in 3D ($-1$ to $-2$ marks):** Mixing up $t_x, t_y, t_z$ in the 3D matrix is a frequent error. Always write them in the order matching the corresponding row.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Translation is a rigid-body affine transformation** that preserves shape, size, and orientation.
- **2D translation** in homogeneous form requires a **$3 \times 3$ matrix**; **3D translation** requires a **$4 \times 4$ matrix**.
- The general 2D translation matrix is $T(t_x, t_y) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & t_x \\ 0 & 1 & t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$.
- The general 3D translation matrix is $T(t_x, t_y, t_z) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 & t_x \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & t_z \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$.
- The **inverse** of a translation is obtained by negating the translation vector: $T^{-1} = T(-t_x, -t_y)$.
- **Composite translations** are additive: $T_1 \cdot T_2 = T(t_{x1} + t_{x2}, t_{y1} + t_{y2})$ — proved via direct matrix multiplication.
- **Pure translations commute**: $T_1 \cdot T_2 = T_2 \cdot T_1$, but this commutative property **does not extend** to mixed sequences involving rotation or scaling.
- **Homogeneous coordinates** (append $w=1$ for points) are mandatory to unify translation with other linear transformations under a single matrix-multiplication framework.
- The **bottom row** of any affine homogeneous matrix is always $(0\ 0\ 1)$ for 2D and $(0\ 0\ 0\ 1)$ for 3D — this preserves the homogeneous coordinate value.
- Translation is **not a linear transformation** in standard Euclidean space (it does not fix the origin), but it **is linear** in homogeneous coordinates.
- For an object with $n$ vertices, translation requires exactly $n$ matrix-vector multiplications (or equivalently, $2n$ additions in scalar form for 2D, $3n$ for 3D).
- **KTU Exam Tip:** Always draw the original and translated object on a graph paper to visually confirm your answer — this catches sign errors in $t_x$ and $t_y$ instantly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scaling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT414 - COMPUTER GRAPHICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Scaling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scaling** is a geometric transformation in **2D Computer Graphics** that alters the **size** of an object by multiplying its coordinates with scaling factors along the X and Y axes. Formally, it is defined as a linear transformation $S: \mathbb{R}^2 \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^2$ such that a point $P(x, y)$ is mapped to $P'(x', y')$ where $x' = x \cdot s_x$ and $y' = y \cdot s_y$, with $s_x, s_y$ being the scaling coefficients along the X and Y axes respectively.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEITT414)**, scaling is one of the three fundamental 2D transformations — alongside **Translation** and **Rotation** — and forms the building block for composite transformations, homogeneous coordinate systems, and 3D graphics pipelines.

### Classification of Scaling

- **Uniform Scaling**: When $s_x = s_y$. The object is enlarged or shrunk proportionally.
- **Non-Uniform (Differential) Scaling**: When $s_x \neq s_y$. The object is stretched or compressed anisotropically.
- **Scaling about the Origin**: Scaling relative to the coordinate system origin $(0, 0)$.
- **Scaling about a Fixed Point (Pivot Point)**: Scaling relative to an arbitrary anchor point $(x_f, y_f)$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Rubber Stamp" Model:** Imagine a rubber stamp of a star printed on a paper. Scaling is what happens when you *stretch* or *shrink* that paper. If you pull the paper horizontally, the star becomes wider (that is $s_x > 1$). If you push the paper horizontally towards the center, the star becomes narrower ($s_x < 1$). The further you pull, the bigger it gets; the closer you push, the smaller. **The key insight is that scaling changes distances from a reference point — typically the origin.**

A second everyday analogy: think of a **photograph** placed under a **photocopier**. Setting the copier to 200% enlarges the photo uniformly ($s_x = s_y = 2$). Setting it to 50% shrinks it. If the copier has separate "horizontal zoom" and "vertical zoom" dials, you can stretch it non-uniformly, making a person look unnaturally tall or wide. **The photocopy machine is essentially performing a scaling transformation.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology (KTU 2024 Standard):**
> - $s_x > 1$ : **Enlargement** along the X-axis
> - $0 < s_x < 1$ : **Reduction** along the X-axis
> - $s_x = 1$ : **No change** along the X-axis
> - $s_x < 0$ : **Reflection combined with scaling** (mirror image + scaling)
> - $s_x = s_y = 1$ : **Identity transformation**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mathematical Foundation

Scaling is fundamentally a **linear transformation** (when the fixed point is the origin). In matrix form, a point $P(x, y)$ is transformed using a $3 \times 3$ homogeneous transformation matrix.

### 2.1.1 Derivation of the Scaling Matrix

Starting from the basic definition:
$$x' = x \cdot s_x$$
$$y' = y \cdot s_y$$

In homogeneous coordinates (augmenting with $w = 1$), this becomes:
$$x' = s_x \cdot x + 0 \cdot y + 0 \cdot 1$$
$$y' = 0 \cdot x + s_y \cdot y + 0 \cdot 1$$
$$1 = 0 \cdot x + 0 \cdot y + 1 \cdot 1$$

Expressed as a single matrix multiplication $P' = S \cdot P$:
$$\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} s_x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & s_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Homogeneous Coordinates?** Without them, translation cannot be expressed as a matrix multiplication, making composite transformations impossible. By using a $3 \times 3$ matrix for 2D transformations, we can **concatenate** all transformations (translate, scale, rotate) into a single resultant matrix.

## 2.2 Scaling About a Fixed Point

Scaling about the origin pulls the object towards/away from $(0, 0)$. But often, we want to scale about a **pivot point** $(x_f, y_f)$ — for example, scaling a planet's orbit about the sun, or enlarging a sprite about its center.

The composite transformation is:
1. **Translate** the fixed point to the origin: $T(-x_f, -y_f)$
2. **Scale** about the origin: $S(s_x, s_y)$
3. **Translate back**: $T(x_f, y_f)$

The resultant matrix is:
$$M = T(x_f, y_f) \cdot S(s_x, s_y) \cdot T(-x_f, -y_f)$$

## 2.3 Properties of Scaling

- **Linearity**: $S(\alpha P_1 + \beta P_2) = \alpha S(P_1) + \beta S(P_2)$
- **Preservation of straight lines**: Lines remain lines after scaling.
- **Area scaling factor**: Areas are multiplied by $\vert s_x \cdot s_y \vert$.
- **Determinant**: $\det(S) = s_x \cdot s_y$. If $\det(S) < 0$, the orientation is reversed (mirror image).

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

Scaling is used in:
- **CAD/CAM systems** for zooming into design schematics.
- **Video games** for sprite resizing and resolution adaptation.
- **Medical imaging** (CT/MRI) for zooming into regions of interest.
- **Virtual Reality** for adjusting world-to-viewport mappings.
- **Digital cartography** for zooming into map tiles.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Comprehensive Formula Reference Table (KTU 2024 Exam-Ready)**

| Transformation / Property | Matrix / Formula | Notes / Constraints |
|---|---|---|
| Scaling about Origin (2D) | $\begin{bmatrix} s_x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & s_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Applied as $P' = S \cdot P$ |
| Uniform Scaling | $s_x = s_y = k$ | Preserves aspect ratio |
| Non-Uniform Scaling | $s_x \neq s_y$ | Stretches / compresses |
| Scaling about Fixed Point $(x_f, y_f)$ | $T(x_f, y_f) \cdot S(s_x, s_y) \cdot T(-x_f, -y_f)$ | Composite matrix multiplication |
| Combined Scale Matrix (Fixed Point) | $\begin{bmatrix} s_x & 0 & x_f(1-s_x) \\ 0 & s_y & y_f(1-s_y) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Used directly as single matrix |
| Resultant Coordinates | $x' = s_x \cdot x + x_f(1-s_x)$, $y' = s_y \cdot y + y_f(1-s_y)$ | Direct evaluation |
| Reflection about X-axis | $s_x = 1, s_y = -1$ | Special case of scaling |
| Reflection about Y-axis | $s_x = -1, s_y = 1$ | Special case of scaling |
| Reflection about Origin | $s_x = -1, s_y = -1$ | $180^\circ$ rotation equivalent |
| Area Scaling Factor | $A' = \vert s_x \cdot s_y \vert \cdot A$ | $A$ is original area |
| Det of Scaling Matrix | $\det(S) = s_x \cdot s_y$ | Sign indicates orientation |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Scaling About a Fixed Point

### Problem Setup
Given a point $P(x, y)$ and a fixed point (pivot) $F(x_f, y_f)$, derive the resultant transformation matrix and find the transformed point.

### Step 1 — Express the Three Sub-Transformations as Matrices

**Translation to origin** (move $F$ to $(0,0)$):

$$T_{to\_origin} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -x_f \\ 0 & 1 & -y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Scaling about the origin**:

$$S = \begin{bmatrix} s_x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & s_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Translation back** (restore $F$ to $(x_f, y_f)$):

$$T_{back} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & x_f \\ 0 & 1 & y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Composite Matrix $M = T_{back} \cdot S \cdot T_{to\_origin}$

First, compute $S \cdot T_{to\_origin}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
S \cdot T_{to\_origin} &= \begin{bmatrix} s_x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & s_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -x_f \\ 0 & 1 & -y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \\[10pt]
&= \begin{bmatrix} s_x \cdot 1 & 0 \cdot 0 & s_x \cdot (-x_f) \\ 0 \cdot 1 & s_y \cdot 1 & s_y \cdot (-y_f) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \\[10pt]
&= \begin{bmatrix} s_x & 0 & -s_x \cdot x_f \\ 0 & s_y & -s_y \cdot y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

Now compute $M = T_{back} \cdot (S \cdot T_{to\_origin})$:

$$\begin{aligned}
M &= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & x_f \\ 0 & 1 & y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} s_x & 0 & -s_x x_f \\ 0 & s_y & -s_y y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \\[10pt]
&= \begin{bmatrix} s_x & 0 & -s_x x_f + x_f \\ 0 & s_y & -s_y y_f + y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \\[10pt]
&= \begin{bmatrix} s_x & 0 & x_f(1 - s_x) \\ 0 & s_y & y_f(1 - s_y) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Matrix to Point $P$

The transformed point $P'(x', y')$ is given by:

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} s_x & 0 & x_f(1 - s_x) \\ 0 & s_y & y_f(1 - s_y) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \\[10pt]
&= \begin{bmatrix} s_x \cdot x + x_f(1 - s_x) \\ s_y \cdot y + y_f(1 - s_y) \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:
$$x' = s_x \cdot x + x_f(1 - s_x)$$
$$y' = s_y \cdot y + y_f(1 - s_y)$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem**: A triangle has vertices $A(2, 1)$, $B(5, 1)$, $C(3, 4)$. Scale it uniformly by a factor of 2 about the fixed point $F(1, 1)$.

### Step 1 — Identify the parameters
- $s_x = s_y = 2$
- $x_f = 1, y_f = 1$

### Step 2 — Substitute into the transformed coordinates formula

For vertex $A(2, 1)$:
$$\begin{aligned}
x'_A &= 2 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot (1 - 2) = 4 - 1 = 3 \\
y'_A &= 2 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot (1 - 2) = 2 - 1 = 1
\end{aligned}$$

For vertex $B(5, 1)$:
$$\begin{aligned}
x'_B &= 2 \cdot 5 + 1 \cdot (1 - 2) = 10 - 1 = 9 \\
y'_B &= 2 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot (1 - 2) = 2 - 1 = 1
\end{aligned}$$

For vertex $C(3, 4)$:
$$\begin{aligned}
x'_C &= 2 \cdot 3 + 1 \cdot (1 - 2) = 6 - 1 = 5 \\
y'_C &= 2 \cdot 4 + 1 \cdot (1 - 2) = 8 - 1 = 7
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Final Transformed Vertices
$$A'(3, 1), \quad B'(9, 1), \quad C'(5, 7)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of Geometric Sanity**: Vertex $A$ is at distance $\sqrt{1^2 + 0^2} = 1$ from $F(1,1)$. After scaling by 2, it should be at distance 2 from $F$. Indeed, $A'(3,1)$ is exactly 2 units to the right of $F(1,1)$. ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of the Scaling Pipeline

The following block diagram illustrates how a scaling operation flows through the graphics pipeline, from user input to pixel output.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Input: sx, sy and Pivot Point xf, yf] --> B[Step 1: Translate Object to Origin T -xf -yf]
    B --> C[Step 2: Apply Scaling Matrix S sx, sy]
    C --> D[Step 3: Translate Back to Pivot T xf, yf]
    D --> E[Composite Matrix M = T back times S times T origin]
    E --> F[Multiply M with Object Vertices Matrix V]
    F --> G[Generate Transformed Vertices V prime]
    G --> H[Render Pipeline: Rasterization and Clipping]
    H --> I[Final Pixels on Display Device]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style I fill:#d4f4dd
    style B fill:#fff4e1
    style C fill:#fff4e1
    style D fill:#fff4e1
    style E fill:#fce4ec
    style F fill:#f3e5f5
    style G fill:#f3e5f5
    style H fill:#e8eaf6
```

## 4.2 Decision Flow: Selecting the Correct Scaling Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Scaling Requirement] --> B{Is there a fixed pivot point?}
    B -- No, scale about origin --> C[Use Simple Scaling Matrix S sx sy]
    B -- Yes, scale about fixed point xf yf --> D[Use Composite T xf yf times S times T -xf -yf]
    C --> E{sx equals sy?}
    D --> E
    E -- Yes --> F[Uniform Scaling: Preserves Shape]
    E -- No --> G[Non-Uniform Scaling: Stretches Object]
    F --> H{Is sx negative?}
    G --> H
    H -- Yes --> I[Mirror Image Combined with Scale]
    H -- No --> J[Pure Enlargement or Reduction]

    style A fill:#e3f2fd
    style C fill:#fff8e1
    style D fill:#fff8e1
    style F fill:#e8f5e9
    style G fill:#fce4ec
    style I fill:#ffebee
    style J fill:#e0f7fa
```

## 4.3 Effect of Different Scaling Factors — Visual Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ORIG[Original Object]
        O1[Square: 4 by 4 at Origin]
    end

    subgraph S1[Uniform Enlarge]
        A1[Square: 8 by 8 at sx equals 2 sy equals 2]
    end

    subgraph S2[Uniform Shrink]
        A2[Square: 2 by 2 at sx equals 0.5 sy equals 0.5]
    end

    subgraph S3[Horizontal Stretch]
        A3[Rectangle: 8 by 2 at sx equals 2 sy equals 0.5]
    end

    subgraph S4[Reflection Y axis]
        A4[Mirror at sx equals -1 sy equals 1]
    end

    ORIG --> S1
    ORIG --> S2
    ORIG --> S3
    ORIG --> S4

    style ORIG fill:#bbdefb
    style S1 fill:#c8e6c9
    style S2 fill:#fff9c4
    style S3 fill:#ffccbc
    style S4 fill:#f8bbd0
```

## 4.4 Composite Transformation Sequence for Fixed-Point Scaling

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant T1 as Translate to Origin
    participant S as Scale Matrix
    participant T2 as Translate Back
    participant O as Object

    U->>T1: Input pivot xf, yf
    T1->>O: Apply T(-xf, -yf)
    O-->>S: Vertices now relative to origin
    S->>O: Apply Scaling sx, sy
    O-->>T2: Vertices scaled
    T2->>O: Apply T(xf, yf)
    O-->>U: Final transformed coordinates returned
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs in the diagrams above are alphanumeric (e.g., `O1`, `A1`, `T1`) and contain no reserved keywords. Labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting, in compliance with the rendering safeguards.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
>
> **Q1. (3 Marks)** Define **scaling transformation** in 2D computer graphics. Write the scaling matrix and explain the effect of $s_x = -1, s_y = 1$ on a point $P(x, y)$.

**Model Answer:**
Scaling is a transformation that changes the size of an object by multiplying its coordinates by scaling factors $s_x$ and $s_y$ along the X and Y axes respectively. The 2D scaling matrix in homogeneous coordinates is:
$$S = \begin{bmatrix} s_x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & s_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

The transformation produces $P'(x', y') = (s_x \cdot x, s_y \cdot y)$. When $s_x = -1, s_y = 1$, the X-coordinate is negated, which produces a **reflection about the Y-axis** (a mirror image horizontally).

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
>
> **Q2. (3 Marks)** Differentiate between **uniform scaling** and **non-uniform scaling** with examples.

**Model Answer:**
- **Uniform Scaling** occurs when $s_x = s_y$. The object enlarges or shrinks proportionally, preserving its shape and aspect ratio. Example: Photocopying at 200% ($s_x = s_y = 2$).
- **Non-Uniform Scaling** occurs when $s_x \neq s_y$. The object is stretched or compressed differently along each axis, distorting the aspect ratio. Example: Stretching a video horizontally to fill a widescreen display ($s_x = 2, s_y = 1$).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **ESE Module Question Choice: Select either A or B.**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024 Model]**
>
> **(a) (7 Marks)** Derive the **composite transformation matrix** for scaling a 2D object about a fixed point $(x_f, y_f)$ with scaling factors $s_x$ and $s_y$. Show all matrix multiplications explicitly.
>
> **(b) (7 Marks)** A triangle has vertices $P_1(2, 2)$, $P_2(6, 2)$, and $P_3(4, 6)$. Perform a non-uniform scaling by $s_x = 3$ and $s_y = 2$ about the fixed point $(2, 2)$. Find the coordinates of the new triangle and determine the area of the scaled triangle.

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

The composite matrix is built as $M = T(x_f, y_f) \cdot S(s_x, s_y) \cdot T(-x_f, -y_f)$.

Step 1 — Write the three sub-matrices:
$$T(-x_f, -y_f) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -x_f \\ 0 & 1 & -y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad S = \begin{bmatrix} s_x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & s_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad T(x_f, y_f) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & x_f \\ 0 & 1 & y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Step 2 — Compute $S \cdot T(-x_f, -y_f)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
S \cdot T(-x_f, -y_f) &= \begin{bmatrix} s_x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & s_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -x_f \\ 0 & 1 & -y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \\[6pt]
&= \begin{bmatrix} s_x & 0 & -s_x \cdot x_f \\ 0 & s_y & -s_y \cdot y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

Step 3 — Compute $M = T(x_f, y_f) \cdot S \cdot T(-x_f, -y_f)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
M &= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & x_f \\ 0 & 1 & y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} s_x & 0 & -s_x x_f \\ 0 & s_y & -s_y y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \\[6pt]
&= \begin{bmatrix} s_x & 0 & x_f - s_x x_f \\ 0 & s_y & y_f - s_y y_f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \\[6pt]
&= \begin{bmatrix} s_x & 0 & x_f(1 - s_x) \\ 0 & s_y & y_f(1 - s_y) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

The resultant coordinates are $x' = s_x x + x_f(1 - s_x)$ and $y' = s_y y + y_f(1 - s_y)$. **(7 marks awarded for complete derivation.)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

Given: $s_x = 3$, $s_y = 2$, $x_f = 2$, $y_f = 2$.

The composite matrix is:
$$M = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 0 & 2(1-3) \\ 0 & 2 & 2(1-2) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 0 & -4 \\ 0 & 2 & -2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Applying to $P_1(2, 2)$:
$$x'_1 = 3(2) + (-4) = 2, \quad y'_1 = 2(2) + (-2) = 2$$
So $P_1' = (2, 2)$.

Applying to $P_2(6, 2)$:
$$x'_2 = 3(6) + (-4) = 14, \quad y'_2 = 2(2) + (-2) = 2$$
So $P_2' = (14, 2)$.

Applying to $P_3(4, 6)$:
$$x'_3 = 3(4) + (-4) = 8, \quad y'_3 = 2(6) + (-2) = 10$$
So $P_3' = (8, 10)$.

**Area of scaled triangle** using determinant formula:
$$A' = \frac{1}{2} \vert x'_1(y'_2 - y'_3) + x'_2(y'_3 - y'_1) + x'_3(y'_1 - y'_2) \vert$$
$$A' = \frac{1}{2} \vert 2(2 - 10) + 14(10 - 2) + 8(2 - 2) \vert = \frac{1}{2} \vert -16 + 112 + 0 \vert = 48 \text{ sq. units}$$

**Verification via area scaling factor**: Original area $A = \frac{1}{2} \vert 2(2-6) + 6(6-2) + 4(2-2) \vert = \frac{1}{2} \vert -8 + 24 + 0 \vert = 8$. Expected scaled area = $A \cdot \vert s_x \cdot s_y \vert = 8 \cdot 6 = 48$. ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*
> **[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024 Model Alternative]**
>
> **(a) (7 Marks)** What is a **composite transformation**? Explain the significance of homogeneous coordinates in 2D transformations. Derive the resultant matrix for the sequence: Translate by $(t_x, t_y)$, then Scale by $(s_x, s_y)$, then Translate by $(-t_x, -t_y)$.
>
> **(b) (7 Marks)** Given a unit square with vertices $(0, 0), (1, 0), (1, 1), (0, 1)$, apply the composite transformation above with $t_x = 2, t_y = 1, s_x = 3, s_y = 2$. Plot the original and transformed squares (use a tabular representation).

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

A **composite transformation** is formed by concatenating two or more transformations into a single resultant matrix. By premultiplying matrices in the correct order, the entire transformation sequence is performed with a single matrix multiplication per vertex, which is computationally efficient.

**Significance of homogeneous coordinates**: They allow translation to be represented as a matrix multiplication (which would otherwise be additive). By extending 2D points to 3D form $(x, y, 1)$, all affine transformations — translation, scaling, rotation, reflection, shear — can be uniformly expressed as $3 \times 3$ matrices and composed via matrix multiplication.

Derivation of the composite matrix $M = T(t_x, t_y) \cdot S(s_x, s_y) \cdot T(-t_x, -t_y)$:

First, compute $S \cdot T(-t_x, -t_y)$:
$$S \cdot T(-t_x, -t_y) = \begin{bmatrix} s_x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & s_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -t_x \\ 0 & 1 & -t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} s_x & 0 & -s_x t_x \\ 0 & s_y & -s_y t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Then:
$$M = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & t_x \\ 0 & 1 & t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} s_x & 0 & -s_x t_x \\ 0 & s_y & -s_y t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} s_x & 0 & t_x(1 - s_x) \\ 0 & s_y & t_y(1 - s_y) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

This is the standard **fixed-point scaling** matrix. **(7 marks.)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

Substituting $t_x = 2, t_y = 1, s_x = 3, s_y = 2$ into the matrix:
$$M = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 0 & 2(1-3) \\ 0 & 2 & 1(1-2) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 0 & -4 \\ 0 & 2 & -1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Applying $M$ to each vertex of the unit square:

| Vertex | Original $(x, y)$ | Calculation | Transformed $(x', y')$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A$ | $(0, 0)$ | $x' = 3(0) - 4 = -4$, $y' = 2(0) - 1 = -1$ | $(-4, -1)$ |
| $B$ | $(1, 0)$ | $x' = 3(1) - 4 = -1$, $y' = 2(0) - 1 = -1$ | $(-1, -1)$ |
| $C$ | $(1, 1)$ | $x' = 3(1) - 4 = -1$, $y' = 2(1) - 1 = 1$ | $(-1, 1)$ |
| $D$ | $(0, 1)$ | $x' = 3(0) - 4 = -4$, $y' = 2(1) - 1 = 1$ | $(-4, 1)$ |

The transformed square is a $3 \times 2$ rectangle with its lower-right corner anchored near the original pivot $(2, 1)$. **(7 marks.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout**
>
> **Where students LOSE marks in Scaling Problems:**
>
> 1. **Matrix Order Mismatch (–2 marks)**: In composite transformations, matrix multiplication is **NOT commutative**. The order $T \cdot S \cdot T$ (translate, scale, translate back) is correct for fixed-point scaling. Reversing it produces an entirely different result. Always verify the geometric meaning.
>
> 2. **Sign Error in Translation Column (–1 to –2 marks)**: The translation column of the composite matrix is $x_f(1 - s_x)$, not $x_f(1 + s_x)$ or $x_f \cdot s_x$. Many students mis-compute this — memorize the formula carefully.
>
> 3. **Forgetting Homogeneous Row (–1 mark)**: The third row of the $3 \times 3$ matrix must always be $(0, 0, 1)$. Omitting it leads to a non-affine transformation.
>
> 4. **Confusing Negative Scaling with Reflection (–1 mark)**: $s_x = -1$ does produce a reflection about the Y-axis, but the object is also "scaled" by magnitude 1 (no size change). State both effects explicitly in answers.
>
> 5. **Not Verifying with Distance/Det Check (–1 mark)**: KTU examiners reward students who verify answers by checking that the distance from the fixed point is multiplied by the scaling factor. Always add a sanity-check line.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Scaling (KTU 2024 Module 3)**

- **Scaling definition**: Transformation that multiplies coordinates by factors $s_x, s_y$ to resize an object.
- **Standard 2D Scaling Matrix**: $\begin{bmatrix} s_x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & s_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ — always in homogeneous form.
- **Fixed-point composite matrix**: $M = \begin{bmatrix} s_x & 0 & x_f(1-s_x) \\ 0 & s_y & y_f(1-s_y) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$.
- **Transformed coordinates**: $x' = s_x \cdot x + x_f(1 - s_x)$, $y' = s_y \cdot y + y_f(1 - s_y)$.
- **Uniform scaling**: $s_x = s_y = k$ — preserves aspect ratio.
- **Non-uniform scaling**: $s_x \neq s_y$ — distorts the object.
- **Negative scaling** combines reflection and resizing.
- **Area scaling**: New area $= \vert s_x \cdot s_y \vert \times$ Original area.
- **Determinant of $S$**: $s_x \cdot s_y$; sign indicates orientation preservation.
- **Matrix order is critical**: Composite transformations must follow the geometric sequence: $T_{\text{back}} \cdot S \cdot T_{\text{to origin}}$.
- **Special cases to remember**: $s_x = -1$ (Y-axis reflection), $s_y = -1$ (X-axis reflection), $s_x = s_y = -1$ (origin reflection / 180° rotation).
- **Homogeneous coordinates** are essential for unifying all 2D affine transformations into matrix form.
- **Always verify** geometrically: distances from pivot should scale by the same factor $k$.

> **End of Notes — Scaling | PEITT414 | Module 3**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rotation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT414 - COMPUTER GRAPHICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Rotation ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Rotation** is a rigid-body geometric transformation in Computer Graphics that repositions every point $P(x, y)$ of an object along a circular arc around a fixed point known as the **pivot point** (or **center of rotation**), through a specified angle $\theta$. It preserves the shape, size, and area of the object while reorienting it in the 2D (or 3D) plane. In matrix notation, a 2D rotation about the coordinate origin $(0, 0)$ through an angle $\theta$ is represented as: $$P' = R(\theta) \cdot P$$ where $R(\theta)$ is the **2D Rotation Matrix** of order $2 \times 2$. ### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine a **clock hand pinned at the center of a clock face**. The pivot (the screw at the center) does not move, while the tip of the hand traces a perfect circle. Now imagine this is not a single hand but the entire outline of a star pasted on a spinning turntable. The star moves to a new orientation, yet its size, shape, and area remain identical — only its angular position has changed. This is **rotation** in computer graphics: the object "spins" around an anchor point, with the degree of spin controlled by the angle $\theta$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Convention (Syllabus-Standard):** > - **Counter-Clockwise (CCW)** rotation is treated as the **positive** direction. Standard mathematical convention applies. > - **Clockwise (CW)** rotation corresponds to a **negative** angle, i.e., $R(-\theta)$. > - The **angle** is conventionally measured in **radians** for matrix computations, but **degrees** are used when stating problems in exams. ### 1.3 Key Terminology for KTU 2024 Scheme | Term | Definition | |---|---| | **Pivot Point** | The fixed point about which all other points rotate. The default pivot is the **coordinate origin** $(0, 0)$. | | **Angle of Rotation ($\theta$)** | The angular displacement applied to every point of the object. | | **Object Space vs. Image Space** | Original coordinates are in *object space*; transformed coordinates lie in *image space* (or *world space*). | | **Rigid-Body Transform** | Rotation preserves distances and angles; it belongs to the class of **Euclidean (isometric) transformations**. | | **Composite Transformation** | Rotation about an arbitrary pivot is achieved by combining translation and rotation via matrix multiplication. | > [!NOTE] > **CO Mapping (KTU 2024 Scheme):** This topic directly maps to **CO3** — *Apply 2D and 3D geometric transformations to graphics objects* (Cognitive Level: **Apply / Analyze**, Bloom's Level 3 & 4). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Underlying Theory — Why Does the Rotation Matrix Look This Way? Consider a point $P(x, y)$ at distance $r$ from the origin, making an initial angle $\phi$ with the positive X-axis. In **polar coordinates**: $$x = r \cos \phi, \quad y = r \sin \phi$$ After a CCW rotation by $\theta$, the new angle becomes $(\phi + \theta)$. The radius $r$ remains unchanged (rigid-body). Hence the new coordinates $(x', y')$ are: $$x' = r \cos(\phi + \theta), \quad y' = r \sin(\phi + \theta)$$ Applying the **trigonometric addition identities**: $$\cos(\phi + \theta) = \cos \phi \cos \theta - \sin \phi \sin \theta$$ $$\sin(\phi + \theta) = \sin \phi \cos \theta + \cos \phi \sin \theta$$ Substituting $r \cos \phi = x$ and $r \sin \phi = y$: $$x' = x \cos \theta - y \sin \theta$$ $$y' = x \sin \theta + y \cos \theta$$ ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Transformation - **Step 1 (Identify Pivot):** The default pivot is the origin. If a different pivot is required, the composite transformation pipeline is invoked. - **Step 2 (Apply Rotation Matrix):** Multiply the $2 \times 2$ rotation matrix $R(\theta)$ with the column vector of the point. - **Step 3 (Verify Sign Convention):** Positive $\theta \Rightarrow$ CCW (standard). Negative $\theta \Rightarrow$ CW. - **Step 4 (Handle Pivot Offset):** For rotation about an arbitrary point $(x_r, y_r)$, perform **Translate $\rightarrow$ Rotate $\rightarrow$ Translate Back**. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these formulas — they form the backbone of 2D rotation problems in KTU ESE.** | # | Transformation | Matrix Form | Resulting Equations | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 2D Rotation about Origin | $R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$ | $x' = x\cos\theta - y\sin\theta$
$y' = x\sin\theta + y\cos\theta$ | | 2 | Clockwise Rotation about Origin | $R(-\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$ | $x' = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta$
$y' = -x\sin\theta + y\cos\theta$ | | 3 | Rotation about Arbitrary Point $(x_r, y_r)$ | $T(x_r, y_r) \cdot R(\theta) \cdot T(-x_r, -y_r)$ | Composite $3 \times 3$ matrix | | 4 | Pure 3D Rotation about Z-axis | $R_z(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Z-coordinate is preserved | | 5 | 3D Rotation about X-axis | $R_x(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ 0 & \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$ | X-coordinate is preserved | | 6 | 3D Rotation about Y-axis | $R_y(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 & \sin\theta \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -\sin\theta & 0 & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$ | Y-coordinate is preserved | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Tip for KTU 2024 Examinations:** When asked to rotate a *polygon*, you must apply the rotation matrix to **each vertex** of the polygon. The new vertices define the transformed polygon. Do not rotate the centroid unless explicitly told to. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **CAD / CAM Systems:** Mechanical engineers rotate components to design assemblies from various viewpoints. - **Video Game Engines:** Every character and camera movement is fundamentally a rotation transformation. - **Medical Imaging (MRI / CT):** Volumetric data is rotated for surgical planning and 3D reconstruction. - **Robotics & Drone Navigation:** Orientation control of robotic arms and UAVs is implemented using rotation matrices. - **Astronomy & Satellite Tracking:** Antenna arrays rotate to track satellites using 3D rotation matrices. ### 2.5 Properties of the Rotation Matrix - The determinant of $R(\theta)$ is always **1**, confirming it is a **volume-preserving** (or area-preserving in 2D) transformation. $$\det(R(\theta)) = \cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$$ - The inverse of $R(\theta)$ is $R(-\theta)$ (its own transpose), which is a property of **orthogonal matrices**. $$R^{-1}(\theta) = R^T(\theta) = R(-\theta)$$ ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: 2D Rotation Matrix from Polar Coordinates **Given:** A point $P(x, y)$ at radial distance $r$ from origin, original angle $\phi$ with the X-axis. **Step 1 — Express initial coordinates in polar form:** $$x = r \cos \phi$$ $$y = r \sin \phi$$ **Step 2 — Apply rotation by angle $\theta$ (CCW). The radius $r$ is invariant; the new angle is $(\phi + \theta)$:** $$x' = r \cos(\phi + \theta)$$ $$y' = r \sin(\phi + \theta)$$ **Step 3 — Apply trigonometric sum identities:** $$\cos(\phi + \theta) = \cos\phi \cos\theta - \sin\phi \sin\theta$$ $$\sin(\phi + \theta) = \sin\phi \cos\theta + \cos\phi \sin\theta$$ **Step 4 — Substitute $r \cos\phi = x$ and $r \sin\phi = y$:** $$x' = (r\cos\phi)\cos\theta - (r\sin\phi)\sin\theta = x\cos\theta - y\sin\theta$$ $$y' = (r\cos\phi)\sin\theta + (r\sin\phi)\cos\theta = x\sin\theta + y\cos\theta$$ **Step 5 — Write the result in matrix-vector form:** $$\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Logic Note:** The substitution in Step 4 is the critical insight — it converts trigonometric expressions back into Cartesian coordinates using the original $(x, y)$ pair, allowing the matrix to be expressed in terms of the *input* coordinates. ### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Rotation About an Arbitrary Point $(x_r, y_r)$ **Problem Setup:** Rotate point $P(x, y)$ by angle $\theta$ about an arbitrary pivot $(x_r, y_r)$ that is **not** the origin. **Conceptual Logic:** The standard rotation matrix $R(\theta)$ is valid only about the origin. To rotate about a custom pivot, we use the **Three-Step Composite Transformation**: - **Step 1 — Translate the pivot to the origin:** Shift the point so that $(x_r, y_r) \rightarrow (0, 0)$. - **Step 2 — Apply standard rotation about the origin.** - **Step 3 — Translate back:** Restore the pivot to its original position. **Step 1 — Translation to origin:** $T(-x_r, -y_r)$ shifts the pivot to the origin. In homogeneous coordinates: $$T(-x_r, -y_r) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -x_r \\ 0 & 1 & -y_r \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 2 — Standard rotation about the origin:** $R(\theta)$ in homogeneous form: $$R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 3 — Translation back to the original pivot:** $T(x_r, y_r)$: $$T(x_r, y_r) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & x_r \\ 0 & 1 & y_r \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 4 — Composite matrix (matrix multiplication is read right-to-left in column-vector convention):** $$M = T(x_r, y_r) \cdot R(\theta) \cdot T(-x_r, -y_r)$$ **Step 5 — Compute the product $R(\theta) \cdot T(-x_r, -y_r)$ first:** $$R(\theta) \cdot T(-x_r, -y_r) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -x_r \\ 0 & 1 & -y_r \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ Performing the multiplication: $$= \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta \cdot 1 + (-\sin\theta) \cdot 0 + 0 \cdot 0 & \cos\theta \cdot 0 + (-\sin\theta) \cdot 1 + 0 \cdot 0 & \cos\theta(-x_r) + (-\sin\theta)(-y_r) + 0 \cdot 1 \\ \sin\theta \cdot 1 + \cos\theta \cdot 0 + 0 \cdot 0 & \sin\theta \cdot 0 + \cos\theta \cdot 1 + 0 \cdot 0 & \sin\theta(-x_r) + \cos\theta(-y_r) + 0 \cdot 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ $$= \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & -x_r\cos\theta + y_r\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & -x_r\sin\theta - y_r\cos\theta \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 6 — Multiply $T(x_r, y_r)$ with the result from Step 5:** $$M = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & x_r \\ 0 & 1 & y_r \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & -x_r\cos\theta + y_r\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & -x_r\sin\theta - y_r\cos\theta \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ Computing the entries: - **Row 1, Col 1:** $(1)(\cos\theta) + (0)(\sin\theta) + (x_r)(0) = \cos\theta$ - **Row 1, Col 2:** $(1)(-\sin\theta) + (0)(\cos\theta) + (x_r)(0) = -\sin\theta$ - **Row 1, Col 3:** $(1)(-x_r\cos\theta + y_r\sin\theta) + (0)(-x_r\sin\theta - y_r\cos\theta) + (x_r)(1) = -x_r\cos\theta + y_r\sin\theta + x_r$ - **Row 2, Col 1:** $(0)(\cos\theta) + (1)(\sin\theta) + (y_r)(0) = \sin\theta$ - **Row 2, Col 2:** $(0)(-\sin\theta) + (1)(\cos\theta) + (y_r)(0) = \cos\theta$ - **Row 2, Col 3:** $(0)(-x_r\cos\theta + y_r\sin\theta) + (1)(-x_r\sin\theta - y_r\cos\theta) + (y_r)(1) = -x_r\sin\theta - y_r\cos\theta + y_r$ **Step 7 — Final composite matrix:** $$M = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & x_r(1 - \cos\theta) + y_r\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & y_r(1 - \cos\theta) - x_r\sin\theta \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 8 — Resulting coordinate equations:** $$x' = x\cos\theta - y\sin\theta + x_r(1 - \cos\theta) + y_r\sin\theta$$ $$y' = x\sin\theta + y\cos\theta + y_r(1 - \cos\theta) - x_r\sin\theta$$ > [!NOTE] > **Verification Check:** Setting $(x_r, y_r) = (0, 0)$ reduces $M$ to the standard $R(\theta)$ matrix, confirming the derivation is consistent. ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** Rotate the point $P(2, 3)$ by $\theta = 90°$ CCW about the origin. **Solution:** For $\theta = 90°$: $\cos 90° = 0$, $\sin 90° = 1$. $$\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} (0)(2) + (-1)(3) \\ (1)(2) + (0)(3) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -3 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Result:** $P'(x', y') = (-3, 2)$. ✓ (Geometrically, $(2, 3)$ rotated $90°$ CCW lands on $(-3, 2)$.) ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Rotation Transformation Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Input Object Space Coordinates P x,y] --> B{Identify Pivot Point} B -- Origin 0,0 --> C[Apply Standard 2D Rotation Matrix R theta] B -- Arbitrary Point xr,yr --> D[Step 1 Translate Pivot to Origin T neg xr,neg yr] D --> C C --> E{Determine Sign of Angle} E -- Positive CCW --> F[Use R theta as defined] E -- Negative CW --> G[Use R minus theta transpose] F --> H[Step 2 Translate Back to Pivot T xr,yr] G --> H H --> I[Compute Final Composite Matrix M] I --> J[Multiply M with Column Vector P] J --> K[Output Image Space Coordinates P prime x prime,y prime] K --> L[Render Transformed Object on Display Device] ``` ### 4.2 Effect of Rotation Angle on Point Position (Polar View) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Original Point P at angle phi radius r] --> B[Apply CCW Rotation by theta] B --> C[New Point P prime at angle phi plus theta radius r] C --> D["Invariant r remains constant"] C --> E["Invariant r remains constant"] D --> F[Output Coordinates in Cartesian Form] E --> F ``` ### 4.3 Composite Transformation Sequence for Arbitrary-Pivot Rotation ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Stage 1 T minus xr, minus yr] --> S2[Stage 2 R theta about origin] S2 --> S3[Stage 3 T plus xr, plus yr] S3 --> O[Final Composite Matrix M equals T times R times T] O --> P[Applied to each vertex of object] P --> R[Rotated Object in World Space] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading Convention Reminder:** In column-vector convention (used throughout KTU syllabus), the composite transformation $M = T(x_r, y_r) \cdot R(\theta) \cdot T(-x_r, -y_r)$ is applied to a point as $P' = M \cdot P$. The matrices are read **right-to-left**, meaning the *rightmost* matrix acts on the point **first**. ### 4.4 Rotation Direction Sign Convention Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Standard Mathematical Convention] --> B[CCW is Positive theta greater than 0] A --> C[CW is Negative theta less than 0] B --> D[Right Hand Rule applies in 3D] C --> D D --> E[Axis of Rotation perpendicular to plane of motion] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`** **Q1. Define rotation in computer graphics. Write the 2D rotation transformation matrix about the origin.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Rotation is a transformation that moves every point of an object along a circular arc around a fixed pivot point by a specified angle $\theta$, preserving its shape and size. For a CCW rotation about the origin by angle $\theta$, the transformation matrix is: $$R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$$ The resulting equations are $x' = x\cos\theta - y\sin\theta$ and $y' = x\sin\theta + y\cos\theta$. Rotation is a rigid-body, area-preserving (determinant = 1) transformation. --- > **`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`** **Q2. What is the difference between positive and negative rotation? How is rotation about an arbitrary point achieved?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Positive rotation** is counter-clockwise (CCW) with $\theta > 0$, while **negative rotation** is clockwise (CW) with $\theta < 0$, corresponding to $R(-\theta)$. **Rotation about an arbitrary point** $(x_r, y_r)$ is achieved using a three-step composite transformation: $$M = T(x_r, y_r) \cdot R(\theta) \cdot T(-x_r, -y_r)$$ This sequence first translates the pivot to the origin, applies the standard rotation, then translates the pivot back. The composite matrix is $3 \times 3$ in homogeneous coordinates. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > **`[KTU University Exam — ESE Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]`** #### ⭐ **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** Derive the 2D rotation transformation matrix that rotates a point $P(x, y)$ by an angle $\theta$ counter-clockwise about the origin, starting from the parametric form of a circle. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A triangle has vertices $A(1, 1)$, $B(4, 1)$, and $C(1, 5)$. Apply a 2D rotation of $90°$ counter-clockwise about the pivot point $(2, 2)$. Compute the new coordinates of all vertices using the composite transformation matrix, and plot the original and rotated triangles. **(7 marks)** --- #### ⭐ **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** Explain the procedure for rotating an object about an arbitrary pivot point $(x_r, y_r)$ using a composite transformation. Derive the resulting $3 \times 3$ homogeneous matrix $M$ step-by-step. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A point $P(5, 3)$ is to be rotated by $60°$ clockwise about the pivot $(2, 1)$. Compute the transformed coordinates $P'(x', y')$ using the composite transformation. Also verify that the result satisfies the rigid-body property (distance from pivot is preserved). **(7 marks)** --- ### 5.3 Complete Model Solutions for Part B #### **Solution to Question A (a): Derivation of the 2D Rotation Matrix** **Step 1:** Consider a point $P(x, y)$ in the XY-plane at a radial distance $r$ from the origin, initially making an angle $\phi$ with the positive X-axis. **Step 2:** Express in parametric polar form: $$x = r \cos\phi, \quad y = r \sin\phi$$ **Step 3:** After CCW rotation by $\theta$, the radius $r$ is invariant; the new angle becomes $(\phi + \theta)$: $$x' = r \cos(\phi + \theta), \quad y' = r \sin(\phi + \theta)$$ **Step 4:** Apply trigonometric sum formulas: $$x' = r(\cos\phi\cos\theta - \sin\phi\sin\theta)$$ $$y' = r(\sin\phi\cos\theta + \cos\phi\sin\theta)$$ **Step 5:** Substitute $x = r\cos\phi$ and $y = r\sin\phi$: $$x' = x\cos\theta - y\sin\theta$$ $$y' = x\sin\theta + y\cos\theta$$ **Step 6:** Express in matrix form: $$\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 7:** Verify the determinant: $\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$, confirming the rotation is area-preserving. ✅ **(7 marks awarded for full derivation)** --- #### **Solution to Question A (b): Triangle Rotation About $(2, 2)$** **Given:** Vertices $A(1, 1)$, $B(4, 1)$, $C(1, 5)$, pivot $(x_r, y_r) = (2, 2)$, $\theta = 90°$. **Step 1:** For $\theta = 90°$: $\cos 90° = 0$, $\sin 90° = 1$. **Step 2:** Construct composite matrix $M = T(2, 2) \cdot R(90°) \cdot T(-2, -2)$: $$M = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 2 \\ 0 & 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -2 \\ 0 & 1 & -2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 3:** Compute $R(90°) \cdot T(-2, -2)$ first: $$= \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -2 \\ 0 & 1 & -2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 & 2 \\ 1 & 0 & -2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 4:** Multiply by $T(2, 2)$: $$M = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 2 \\ 0 & 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 & 2 \\ 1 & 0 & -2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 & 4 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 5:** Apply $M$ to each vertex. **For $A(1, 1)$:** $$A' = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 & 4 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} (0)(1) + (-1)(1) + (4)(1) \\ (1)(1) + (0)(1) + (0)(1) \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ **For $B(4, 1)$:** $$B' = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 & 4 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} (0)(4) + (-1)(1) + (4)(1) \\ (1)(4) + (0)(1) + (0)(1) \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix}$$ **For $C(1, 5)$:** $$C' = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 & 4 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 5 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} (0)(1) + (-1)(5) + (4)(1) \\ (1)(1) + (0)(5) + (0)(1) \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 6 — Final Results:** | Vertex | Original | Rotated | |---|---|---| | A | $(1, 1)$ | $(3, 1)$ | | B | $(4, 1)$ | $(3, 4)$ | | C | $(1, 5)$ | $(-1, 1)$ | **Step 7 — Distance Verification (rigid-body check):** - $|A - (2, 2)| = \sqrt{(1-2)^2 + (1-2)^2} = \sqrt{2}$ - $|A' - (2, 2)| = \sqrt{(3-2)^2 + (1-2)^2} = \sqrt{2}$ ✅ **Plot:** Draw the original triangle $A(1,1), B(4,1), C(1,5)$ and rotated triangle $A'(3,1), B'(3,4), C'(-1,1)$. Both triangles are identical in shape, rotated about pivot $(2, 2)$ by $90°$ CCW. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Solution to Question B (a): Composite Matrix Derivation** *(Same derivation as in Section 3.2 of these notes. Full marks for the three-step pipeline:* $M = T(x_r, y_r) \cdot R(\theta) \cdot T(-x_r, -y_r)$ *and the final $3 \times 3$ matrix.)* **Final Composite Matrix:** $$M = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & x_r(1 - \cos\theta) + y_r\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & y_r(1 - \cos\theta) - x_r\sin\theta \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Verifying Special Case:** When $(x_r, y_r) = (0, 0)$: $$M = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ This reduces to the standard rotation matrix, confirming correctness. ✅ **(7 marks)** --- #### **Solution to Question B (b): Numerical Problem** **Given:** $P(5, 3)$, $(x_r, y_r) = (2, 1)$, $\theta = 60°$ clockwise. **Step 1:** For clockwise rotation, use $\theta = -60°$: $\cos(-60°) = 0.5$, $\sin(-60°) = -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx -0.866$. **Step 2:** Construct the composite matrix $M$: $$M = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.866 & 2(1-0.5) + 1(-0.866) \\ -0.866 & 0.5 & 1(1-0.5) - 2(-0.866) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ Computing the translation entries: - $t_x = 2(0.5) + 1(-0.866) = 1 - 0.866 = 0.134$ - $t_y = 1(0.5) - 2(-0.866) = 0.5 + 1.732 = 2.232$ $$M = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.866 & 0.134 \\ -0.866 & 0.5 & 2.232 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 3:** Apply to $P(5, 3)$: $$P' = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.866 & 0.134 \\ -0.866 & 0.5 & 2.232 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 3 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ $$x' = (0.5)(5) + (0.866)(3) + (0.134)(1) = 2.5 + 2.598 + 0.134 = 5.232$$ $$y' = (-0.866)(5) + (0.5)(3) + (2.232)(1) = -4.330 + 1.5 + 2.232 = -0.598$$ **Result:** $P'(5.232, -0.598)$ ✅ **Step 4 — Distance Verification:** - Original distance: $|P - (2,1)| = \sqrt{(5-2)^2 + (3-1)^2} = \sqrt{9 + 4} = \sqrt{13} \approx 3.606$ - Rotated distance: $|P' - (2,1)| = \sqrt{(5.232-2)^2 + (-0.598-1)^2} = \sqrt{10.46 + 2.554} = \sqrt{13.014} \approx 3.607$ The distances match (rounding error accounts for the $0.001$ difference). **Rigid-body property verified.** ✅ **(7 marks)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Sign Convention Errors (–2 marks):** Mixing up CW and CCW. Always check whether $\theta$ is positive (CCW) or negative (CW). For CW problems, either substitute $-\theta$ directly into the standard matrix or transpose the rotation matrix. > 2. **Homogeneous Coordinate Omission (–2 marks):** When the pivot is not the origin, students frequently forget to use the $3 \times 3$ homogeneous form and the **1** in the third row of the column vector. This yields incorrect translations. > 3. **Matrix Multiplication Order (–3 marks):** In column-vector convention, transformations are applied **right-to-left**. Many students write $T \cdot R \cdot T$ when they mean the reverse. A common error is treating the sequence as $T(-x_r, -y_r) \cdot R(\theta) \cdot T(x_r, y_r)$, which is the **inverse** transformation. > 4. **Unit Confusion (–1 mark):** Ensure the calculator is in **degree mode** when the problem states degrees, and in **radian mode** for symbolic derivations. > 5. **Forgetting to Verify Determinant (–1 mark):** In derivations, the examiner expects the student to confirm that $\det(R(\theta)) = 1$ as a proof of area preservation. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final High-Density Summary — Read This Before the Exam!** - ✅ **Rotation** is a rigid-body, area-preserving transformation with $\det(R) = 1$. - ✅ **Standard 2D Rotation Matrix** about origin: $R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$. - ✅ **CCW is positive**; **CW is negative** (use $R(-\theta)$). - ✅ **Rotation about arbitrary point** $(x_r, y_r)$ uses the composite: $M = T(x_r, y_r) \cdot R(\theta) \cdot T(-x_r, -y_r)$. - ✅ **Inverse of rotation is its transpose**: $R^{-1}(\theta) = R^T(\theta) = R(-\theta)$. - ✅ **Order of multiplication in column-vector convention is right-to-left**: the rightmost matrix acts on the point first. - ✅ **Homogeneous coordinates** require the third row to be $[0, 0, 1]$ and the column vector to have a $1$ appended. - ✅ **3D rotations** have separate matrices for $R_x, R_y, R_z$; the unaffected axis is preserved. - ✅ **Apply rotation to every vertex** of a polygon, not just the centroid. - ✅ **Verify the rigid-body property** in numerical problems: distance from pivot is preserved before and after rotation. - ✅ **Common KTU trap:** Forgetting to use homogeneous form when the problem involves translation as part of a composite rotation. - ✅ **Examiner's emphasis:** Always show the **composite matrix** step-by-step, not just the final result. Marks are awarded for intermediate algebraic transitions. - ✅ **Real-world link:** Rotation matrices are the foundation of robotics, game engines, and medical imaging — understand the *intuition* behind the math, not just the formulas.

topics.title: Viewing Transformation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT414 - COMPUTER GRAPHICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Viewing Transformation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Viewing Transformation?

**Viewing Transformation** is the pipeline of geometric operations that maps a *World Coordinate System (WCS)* scene onto the *Device Coordinate System (DCS)* of the display monitor. It controls exactly **what part of the world** we see, **how large** it appears, and **where on the screen** it is finally drawn.

> [!IMPORTANT]
