5. Optimization: if $A_i = 1.0$ (full pixel) and $z_i$ is the closest, all farther nodes may be discarded (opaque cull). *(0.5 mark)*
6. Repeat for all polygons. *(0.5 mark)*

**Phase 2 — Accumulation (4 marks):**

7. Iterate over every pixel. *(0.5 mark)*
8. **Sort the fragment list at the pixel by depth ascending** (farthest to nearest). *(1 mark)*
9. For each fragment $i$, compute the mask weight:
   $$m_i = A_i \cdot \prod_{j=1}^{i-1}(1 - \alpha_j)$$
   This represents the *fraction of light that survives* through all transparent layers in front of fragment $i$, scaled by the geometric area it covers. *(1 mark)*
10. Accumulate the final pixel color using the **Carpenter compositing formula**:
   $$C_{out} = C_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{L} m_i \cdot (C_i - C_0)$$
   where $C_0$ is the background color, $C_i$ is the fragment color. *(1 mark)*
11. Write $C_{out}$ to the frame buffer. *(0.5 mark)*

**Transparency handling:** The product term $\prod (1 - \alpha_j)$ naturally attenuates the contribution of every fragment by the opacity of the layers in front of it. When all $\alpha_i = 1$ (opaque case), the formula degenerates to a pure Z-Buffer winner-take-all (the closest fragment dominates). When $\alpha_i < 1$, light bleeds through, producing correct transparent blending. *(covered in mask step)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast the **A-Buffer** and the **Z-Buffer** algorithms in terms of data structure, anti-aliasing capability, transparency handling, memory usage, and speed. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A pixel receives 3 surface fragments. Fragment 1 (back) has $A_1 = 1.0$, color $(255, 0, 0)$, $\alpha_1 = 1.0$. Fragment 2 (middle) has $A_2 = 0.6$, color $(0, 255, 0)$, $\alpha_2 = 0.4$. Fragment 3 (front) has $A_3 = 0.3$, color $(0, 0, 255)$, $\alpha_3 = 0.7$. The background is black $(0, 0, 0)$. Compute the final pixel color using the A-Buffer accumulation formula. *(7 marks)*

---

##### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks — 1 mark per comparison point + 0.5 mark justification each]

| Aspect | Z-Buffer | A-Buffer |
|---|---|---|
| **Data structure** | One depth $z$ per pixel (24–32 bits) | Linked list of fragments per pixel, each with $z$, $A$, RGB, $\alpha$ |
| **Anti-aliasing** | None inherently; requires supersampling for AA | **Native**, via fractional area $A_i$ |
| **Transparency** | Not handled; only nearest opaque surface wins | **Native**, via opacity $\alpha_i$ and mask product |
| **Memory** | Low — fixed per pixel | High — proportional to $L$ (overlaps per pixel) |
| **Speed** | Fastest — single-pass scan convert | Slower — two passes (collect, then resolve) |
| **Order-dependence** | Independent of polygon order | Independent (Phase 2 sorts by depth) |
| **Generality** | Special case of A-Buffer with $L = 1$ | **Superset** of Z-Buffer |

---

##### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks — full numerical walkthrough]

**Given:**
- $C_0 = (0, 0, 0)$
- Fragment 1: $A_1 = 1.0$, $C_1 = (255, 0, 0)$, $\alpha_1 = 1.0$
- Fragment 2: $A_2 = 0.6$, $C_2 = (0, 255, 0)$, $\alpha_2 = 0.4$
- Fragment 3: $A_3 = 0.3$, $C_3 = (0, 0, 255)$, $\alpha_3 = 0.7$

Fragments are already back-to-front.

**Step 1: Compute masks.** *(3 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_1 &= A_1 \cdot \prod_{j<1}(\ldots) = 1.0 \cdot 1 = 1.0 \\[4pt]
m_2 &= A_2 \cdot (1 - \alpha_1) = 0.6 \cdot (1 - 1.0) = 0.6 \cdot 0 = 0.0 \\[4pt]
m_3 &= A_3 \cdot (1 - \alpha_1) \cdot (1 - \alpha_2) = 0.3 \cdot 0 \cdot 0.6 = 0.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply Carpenter formula.** *(2 marks)*

$$
C_{out} = C_0 + m_1(C_1 - C_0) + m_2(C_2 - C_0) + m_3(C_3 - C_0)
$$

$$
C_{out} = (0,0,0) + 1.0 \cdot (255, 0, 0) + 0 + 0 = (255, 0, 0)
$$

**Step 3: Interpretation.** *(2 marks)*

The result is **pure red (255, 0, 0)**. The reason: Fragment 1 is fully opaque ($\alpha_1 = 1.0$) and covers the entire pixel ($A_1 = 1.0$). Any fragment behind an opaque full-coverage layer in the over-compositing sense is *invisible*. Fragments 2 and 3, although geometrically present in 60% and 30% of the pixel area respectively, are **fully occluded** by Fragment 1 from the compositing standpoint.

> **Note to examiner:** If the student misinterpreted the layering order (e.g., treated Fragment 1 as the *front* layer), the answer would differ and the student should lose 1–2 marks. The correct back-to-front convention must be enforced.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in A-Buffer Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing A-Buffer with Accumulation Buffer.** The Accumulation Buffer supersamples a framebuffer at sub-pixel offsets; the A-Buffer stores *lists of fragments*. They are *not* the same. (Loss: up to 3 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting the area term in the mask.** Writing $m_i = \prod(1-\alpha_j)$ *without* $A_i$ gives the over-compositing formula but **not** the A-Buffer formula. The A-Buffer ALWAYS multiplies by area coverage. (Loss: 1–2 marks)
> 3. **Single-pass assumption.** A-Buffer is a *two-pass* algorithm. Stating it works in one pass is a textbook error. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 4. **Sorting in the wrong direction.** Lists must be sorted *back-to-front* (farthest first) for over-compositing. Front-to-back is used for under-compositing (Z-Buffer style culling). (Loss: 1–2 marks)
> 5. **Storing RGB only.** A common error is to omit the **alpha / opacity** field. Without $\alpha$, transparency cannot be resolved. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 6. **Claiming A-Buffer is "just a Z-Buffer with color".** This misses the anti-aliasing and transparency contributions — these are what *make* it a superset. (Loss: 2 marks)

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — A-Buffer (Module 4)**

- ✅ **Full form:** Anti-aliased Area-averaged Buffer (Carpenter, 1984).
- ✅ **Inventor:** Loren Carpenter; deployed in Pixar's **REYES** architecture.
- ✅ **Superset of Z-Buffer:** degenerates to Z-Buffer when $L = 1$ and $\alpha = 1$.
- ✅ **Data structure:** Per-pixel **linked list of fragment nodes** — NOT a single depth.
- ✅ **Fields per fragment:** surface_id, $z$, area $A$, $(R, G, B)$, $\alpha$, *next*.
- ✅ **Two phases:** (1) Fragment collection (scan convert + append), (2) Visibility resolution (sort by depth + Carpenter blend).
- ✅ **Anti-aliasing mechanism:** fractional area $A_i$ weighted in accumulation.
- ✅ **Transparency mechanism:** opacity $\alpha_i$ attenuates back layers via $\prod(1-\alpha_j)$.
- ✅ **Mask formula:** $m_i = A_i \cdot \prod_{j<i}(1 - \alpha_j)$.
- ✅ **Accumulation formula:** $C_{out} = C_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{L} m_i \cdot (C_i - C_0)$.
- ✅ **Resolution independence:** fragments are not tied to a sub-pixel grid; area is floating point.
- ✅ **Memory trade-off:** higher storage than Z-Buffer; bounded by $N_{max}$ per pixel.
- ✅ **Speed trade-off:** two passes make it slower than Z-Buffer but feature-richer.
- ✅ **Compare in exam:** Z-Buffer (fast, simple, no AA/transparency) vs A-Buffer (rich, two-pass, full AA + transparency).
- ✅ **Sort direction:** back-to-front (farthest first) for over-compositing.
- ✅ **MUST remember:** multiply mask by $A_i$ — area is the A-Buffer's signature.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scan Line
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT414 - COMPUTER GRAPHICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Scan Line ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **scan line** is a horizontal line of pixels that a raster display device processes sequentially from top to bottom to render an image. In the context of Computer Graphics algorithms, the **Scan-Line Algorithm** is an **image-space technique** that exploits **coherence** (the principle that neighbouring pixels tend to share similar properties) to efficiently perform two fundamental operations: 1. **Polygon Filling** — computing the interior pixels of a 2D polygon. 2. **Hidden Surface Removal (HSR)** — determining which surfaces of a 3D scene are visible to the viewer. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 – Visible Surface Detection):** The Scan-Line Algorithm is one of the most important image-space methods. It is listed in KTU Module 4 alongside the Depth Buffer (z-buffer) and Depth Sort (Painter's) algorithms. It is fundamentally an extension of the 2D Scan-Line Polygon Fill algorithm, augmented with a depth comparison test. --- ### Conceptual Analogy — "Painting a Wall One Stripe at a Time" Imagine you are a painter tasked with painting a complex shape on a wall: - **Naive (Pixel-by-Pixel) Approach:** You would look at *every single point* on the wall and decide whether to paint it. This is slow and tedious — analogous to the brute-force approach in graphics where every pixel is checked independently. - **Scan-Line Approach:** You take a **horizontal stripe** of the wall, find the **two edges** where your shape's boundary crosses that stripe, and **paint the entire region between them in one sweep**. Then you move down to the next stripe. This is dramatically faster because the boundaries (edges) change very little from one horizontal level to the next — this is **scan-line coherence**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight for Exams:** The Scan-Line Algorithm trades off memory for speed. Instead of recomputing edge intersections for every pixel, it stores the polygon edges in sorted tables and updates them **only once per scan line (per row of pixels)**. The standard refresh rate of a display is **60 Hz**, meaning roughly **60 scan lines are processed per millisecond of vertical refresh** — the algorithm must be extremely efficient to keep up. ### Why Scan-Line? (Motivation) | Property | Brute-Force Pixel Method | Scan-Line Method | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Speed** | O(pixels × polygons) | O(pixels + edges) — much faster | | **Coherence Used** | None | **Scan-line coherence** (edges change slowly row-to-row) | | **Memory** | High (depth buffer per pixel) | Low (just edge tables) | | **Suitability** | Simple shapes | Complex, multi-polygon 3D scenes | --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Two Key Data Structures The algorithm's efficiency comes from two carefully designed tables: #### A. Edge Table (ET) — The "Master List" The **Edge Table** is a static, pre-computed structure containing every non-horizontal edge of the polygon, **sorted by the y-coordinate of their lowest endpoint** ($y_{min}$). > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** Horizontal edges are excluded from the ET. Why? Because they never "enter" or "leave" the polygon — they are parallel to the scan line and would cause infinite slope values. Each entry (called a **bucket**) in the ET for a scan line $y_k$ contains a list of edges whose $y_{min} = y_k$. For each edge, we store: - **$y_{max}$** — the y-coordinate of the highest endpoint. - **$x$** — the x-coordinate of the edge at the current scan line (initially $x$ at $y_{min}$). - **$1/m$** — the inverse slope of the edge. This is the **increment** added to $x$ each time we move down one scan line. - **(Optional, for HSR) Polygon ID and depth coefficients** — see Section 2.4. #### B. Active Edge Table (AET) — The "Working List" The **Active Edge Table** dynamically holds the subset of edges that **intersect the current scan line**. It is **re-sorted by x-intersection value at every scan line**, ensuring that pairs of x-values (left, right) correctly bound the polygon interior at that row. --- ### 2.2 The Scan-Line Polygon Fill Algorithm (Step-by-Step Logic) > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **core algorithm** you must master. The following logic is exactly what KTU examiners expect for 14-mark questions. 1. **Build the Edge Table (ET)** from the polygon vertices. For each edge (skipping horizontals), record $(y_{max}, x_{at\_y_{min}}, 1/m)$ and place it in bucket $ET[y_{min}]$. 2. **Initialise** $y = y_{min}$ (the lowest scan line touched by the polygon) and set the **AET = empty**. 3. **Repeat for each scan line** $y$ from $y_{min}$ to $y_{max}$: **(a) Merge:** Move all edges from $ET[y]$ into the AET. These are the edges that "enter" the polygon at this scan line. **(b) Remove:** Delete any edge from the AET whose $y_{max} = y$. These edges are about to exit the polygon. **(c) Sort:** Sort the AET in **ascending order of $x$** (current x-intersection). The AET is now a list of edges ordered left-to-right along the current scan line. **(d) Fill:** Process the AET in pairs: $(x_1, x_2), (x_3, x_4), \ldots$ — fill all pixels between $x_{2i-1}$ and $x_{2i}$. This exploits the **Parity Rule** (odd-even rule): a point is inside the polygon if a horizontal ray from it crosses an **odd** number of edges. **(e) Update:** Increment the $x$ value of every edge remaining in the AET by its $1/m$ (i.e., $x_{new} = x_{old} + 1/m$). This prepares the entries for the next scan line. **(f) Increment** $y$ by 1 and return to step (a). --- ### 2.3 Inverse Slope Formula (Critical for Exam) For an edge from $(x_1, y_1)$ to $(x_2, y_2)$ where $y_1 < y_2$: $$m = \frac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{m} = \frac{x_2 - x_1}{y_2 - y_1}$$ At each new scan line $y_{new} = y_{old} + 1$, the new x-intersection is: $$x_{new} = x_{old} + \frac{1}{m} = x_{old} + \frac{x_2 - x_1}{y_2 - y_1}$$ > [!TIP] > **Why inverse slope and not regular slope?** In the scan-line algorithm, we step the y-coordinate by **1 unit at a time** (one scan line down). It is computationally much more efficient to pre-compute $\Delta x / \Delta y$ once and add it to $x$ each step than to recompute the slope expression every pixel. This single arithmetic operation per edge per scan line is what makes the algorithm so fast. --- ### 2.4 Extension to Hidden Surface Removal (Scan-Line HSR) The 3D version enhances the ET and AET with: - **Polygon ID** — to identify which surface owns the edge. - **Plane equation coefficients $(A, B, C, D)$** — the 3D plane of the polygon: $Ax + By + Cz + D = 0$. The depth of a polygon at any screen point $(x, y)$ is computed by solving the plane equation for $z$: $$z(x, y) = -\frac{Ax + By + D}{C} \quad \text{(assuming } C \neq 0\text{)}$$ **The Cross-Product Test (Depth Comparison Between Two Polygons):** When two polygons $P$ and $Q$ overlap on a scan line between the x-intersections of their edges, we must determine which is in front. Let their depth functions be $z_P(x,y)$ and $z_Q(x,y)$. The intersection of the two planes in screen space is a line. We use a cross product to test on which side of this line each polygon's reference depth lies: Given two reference points $P_1 = (x_1, y_1, z_1)$ on polygon $P$ and $Q_1 = (x_1, y_1, z_1')$ on polygon $Q$ (same $x, y$, different $z$): $$\vec{V_1} = P_2 - P_1, \quad \vec{V_2} = Q_1 - P_1$$ $$\vec{N} = \vec{V_1} \times \vec{V_2}$$ The **sign of the z-component of $\vec{N}$** indicates which polygon is closer at the tested point. If the sign indicates polygon $P$ is in front at one side of the overlap, then $P$ is in front **throughout** that span (since surfaces are planar and their intersection is a single line). > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Scan-Line HSR is the historical foundation for **hardware graphics accelerators**. Early GPUs (1980s–1990s) like the SGI RealityEngine used scan-line architectures before the z-buffer (depth buffer) became dominant. The concept of processing one row of pixels at a time is still embedded in modern tile-based renderers used in **mobile GPUs** (ARM Mali, Apple GPU, Qualcomm Adreno). --- ### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Inverse slope | $\frac{1}{m} = \frac{x_2 - x_1}{y_2 - y_1}$ | Used to update $x$ each scan line | | 2 | x-update rule | $x_{new} = x_{old} + \frac{1}{m}$ | Applied to every edge in AET per row | | 3 | Polygon depth (from plane) | $z = -\dfrac{Ax + By + D}{C}$ | $Ax + By + Cz + D = 0$ is the plane | | 4 | Plane from 3 points | Solve the linear system of 3 plane equations | $A, B, C, D$ are constants per polygon | | 5 | Parity fill rule | Fill between $(x_1,x_2), (x_3,x_4), \ldots$ | Pairs of sorted AET x-values | | 6 | Cross-product test | $\vec{N} = (P_2 - P_1) \times (Q_1 - P_1)$ | Sign of $N_z$ determines front polygon | | 7 | ET bucket index | $ET[y_{min}]$ | $y_{min}$ is the smaller y of the edge | | 8 | AET removal | Remove edges where $y_{max} = y_{current}$ | Edges that have just exited | --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example — Scan-Line Polygon Fill (14-Mark Standard) **Problem:** Fill the polygon with vertices $V_1(2,1), V_2(8,1), V_3(10,4), V_4(4,8), V_5(2,5)$ using the Scan-Line Algorithm. Show the ET, AET updates, and the final filled pixels. #### Step 1 — Identify the Edges and Compute Parameters | Edge | From → To | Horizontal? | $y_{min}$ | $y_{max}$ | $x$ at $y_{min}$ | $1/m = \Delta x / \Delta y$ | ET Bucket | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | E1 | V1 → V2 | **Yes** ($y=1$) | — | — | — | — | **Skip** | | E2 | V2 → V3 | No | 1 | 4 | 8 | $\frac{10-8}{4-1} = \frac{2}{3}$ | $ET[1]$ | | E3 | V3 → V4 | No | 4 | 8 | 10 | $\frac{4-10}{8-4} = -\frac{3}{2}$ | $ET[4]$ | | E4 | V4 → V5 | No | 5 | 8 | 4 | $\frac{2-4}{5-8} = \frac{2}{3}$ | $ET[5]$ | | E5 | V5 → V1 | No | 1 | 5 | 2 | $\frac{2-2}{1-5} = 0$ | $ET[1]$ | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Step:** $y_{min}$ is **always the smaller y-coordinate** of the two endpoints. For E2, since $y_2=1 < y_3=4$, we record $y_{min}=1, y_{max}=4$, and $x$ at $y_{min}$ is $x_2=8$. The slope direction is from the lower vertex to the upper vertex. If you reverse this convention, your entire table will be wrong — this is a **common 2-mark deduction** in KTU valuations. #### Step 2 — Built Edge Table (ET) $$ ET = \begin{cases} ET[1] = \{E2:\ (y_{max}=4,\ x=8,\ 1/m=2/3),\ \ E5:\ (y_{max}=5,\ x=2,\ 1/m=0)\} \\ ET[4] = \{E3:\ (y_{max}=8,\ x=10,\ 1/m=-3/2)\} \\ ET[5] = \{E4:\ (y_{max}=8,\ x=4,\ 1/m=2/3)\} \\ ET[k] = \emptyset \quad \text{for all other } k \end{cases} $$ #### Step 3 — Trace AET Scan-Line by Scan-Line > **Convention:** AET entries are kept sorted by current $x$ value ascending. ##### ▶ Scan Line $y = 1$ (AET construction) - **Merge:** Add E2 and E5 from $ET[1]$. - **Remove:** None (no $y_{max} = 1$). - **AET (sorted):** E5 $(x=2, 1/m=0)$, E2 $(x=8, 1/m=2/3)$. - **Fill pairs:** $(2, 8)$ → Fill pixels at $x = 3, 4, 5, 6, 7$ (between intersection points). - **Update:** E5: $x = 2 + 0 = 2$; E2: $x = 8 + 2/3 \approx 8.67$. ##### ▶ Scan Line $y = 2$ (start of polygon interior) - **Merge:** $ET[2] = \emptyset$ → no new edges. - **Remove:** None. - **AET (sorted):** E5 $(x=2)$, E2 $(x=8.67)$. - **Fill pairs:** $(2, 8.67)$ → Fill pixels at $x = 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8$. - **Update:** E5: $x=2$; E2: $x = 8.67 + 2/3 \approx 9.33$. ##### ▶ Scan Line $y = 3$ - **Merge:** None. - **Remove:** None. - **AET:** E5 $(x=2)$, E2 $(x=9.33)$. - **Fill pairs:** $(2, 9.33)$ → Fill $x = 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9$. - **Update:** E5: $x=2$; E2: $x = 9.33 + 2/3 = 10.00$. ##### ▶ Scan Line $y = 4$ (Edge E3 enters, Edge E2 exits) - **Merge:** Add E3 $(x=10, 1/m=-3/2)$ from $ET[4]$. - **Remove:** Remove E2 (its $y_{max} = 4$). - **AET after merge & remove (sorted by x):** E5 $(x=2)$, E3 $(x=10)$. - **Fill pairs:** $(2, 10)$ → Fill $x = 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9$. - **Update:** E5: $x=2$; E3: $x = 10 + (-3/2) = 8.5$. ##### ▶ Scan Line $y = 5$ (Edge E4 enters, Edge E5 exits) - **Merge:** Add E4 $(x=4, 1/m=2/3)$ from $ET[5]$. - **Remove:** Remove E5 (its $y_{max} = 5$). - **AET after merge & remove (sorted by x):** E4 $(x=4)$, E3 $(x=8.5)$. - **Fill pairs:** $(4, 8.5)$ → Fill $x = 5, 6, 7, 8$. - **Update:** E4: $x = 4 + 2/3 \approx 4.67$; E3: $x = 8.5 + (-3/2) = 7.0$. ##### ▶ Scan Line $y = 6$ - **Merge:** None. - **Remove:** None. - **AET:** E4 $(x=4.67)$, E3 $(x=7.0)$. - **Fill pairs:** $(4.67, 7.0)$ → Fill $x = 5, 6$. - **Update:** E4: $x \approx 5.33$; E3: $x = 5.5$. ##### ▶ Scan Line $y = 7$ - **AET:** E4 $(x=5.33)$, E3 $(x=5.5)$. - **Fill pairs:** $(5.33, 5.5)$ → Fill $x = 6$ (only one pixel — narrow polygon tip). - **Update:** E4: $x = 6.0$; E3: $x = 4.0$. ##### ▶ Scan Line $y = 8$ (All edges exit) - **Merge:** None. - **Remove:** Remove E4 ($y_{max}=8$) and E3 ($y_{max}=8$). - **AET:** $\emptyset$ → **Algorithm terminates**. #### Step 4 — Resulting Pixels Filled (Summary Matrix) | Scan Line $y$ | Fill Span (x-range) | Pixels Set | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $(2, 8)$ | 3, 4, 5, 6, 7 | | 2 | $(2, 8.67)$ | 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8 | | 3 | $(2, 9.33)$ | 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9 | | 4 | $(2, 10)$ | 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9 | | 5 | $(4, 8.5)$ | 5, 6, 7, 8 | | 6 | $(4.67, 7.0)$ | 5, 6 | | 7 | $(5.33, 5.5)$ | 6 | > [!TIP] > **Exam Insight:** Notice the **$y=1$ row** has only 6 pixels filled while **$y=3, 4$** have 7. This is because E2's slope carries the right edge outward as we descend. The narrow tip at $y=7$ (only one pixel) reflects the polygon tapering to a vertex at $V_4(4,8)$. This shape-sensitivity is the **practical reason** why scan-line fill is preferred over uniform grid fill for irregular polygons. --- ### 3.2 Derivation — Cross-Product Test for Two Polygons (Hidden Surface) **Problem:** Two polygons $P$ and $Q$ overlap in screen space. We know the following depth values at three collinear scan-line intersections: - On $P$: $z_P(x_1, y_1) = 4$, $z_P(x_2, y_2) = 7$ - On $Q$: $z_Q(x_1, y_1) = 6$, $z_Q(x_2, y_2) = 3$ Determine which polygon is in front at the **midpoint** of the overlap region using the cross-product test. #### Step 1 — Set Up the Test Vectors Let $P_1 = (x_1, y_1, 4)$ (point on $P$), $P_2 = (x_2, y_2, 7)$ (another point on $P$), and $Q_1 = (x_1, y_1, 6)$ (point on $Q$ at same screen location as $P_1$). $$ \vec{V_1} = P_2 - P_1 = (x_2 - x_1,\ y_2 - y_1,\ 3) $$ $$ \vec{V_2} = Q_1 - P_1 = (0,\ 0,\ 2) $$ #### Step 2 — Compute the Cross Product $$ \vec{N} = \vec{V_1} \times \vec{V_2} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ x_2 - x_1 & y_2 - y_1 & 3 \\ 0 & 0 & 2 \end{vmatrix} $$ Expanding along the third row (most non-zero entries): $$ \vec{N} = \hat{i}\left[(y_2 - y_1)(2) - (3)(0)\right] - \hat{j}\left[(x_2 - x_1)(2) - (3)(0)\right] + \hat{k}\left[(x_2 - x_1)(0) - (y_2 - y_1)(0)\right] $$ $$ \vec{N} = 2(y_2 - y_1)\hat{i} - 2(x_2 - x_1)\hat{j} + 0\hat{k} $$ #### Step 3 — Interpret the Sign of $N_z$ $$ N_z = 0 $$ This indicates the two depth gradients have **no z-component cross product**, meaning the intersection line of the two planes is **parallel to the z-axis at this point in screen space** — i.e., the two surfaces are vertically stacked but cross at a line that, when projected, has a particular direction. Since the test relies on the **sign of $N_z$** relative to a reference orientation: - If $N_z > 0$: $P$ is in front on one side of the overlap, $Q$ on the other. - If $N_z = 0$: The two planes are **coplanar** in depth at that screen location — an ambiguous case requiring sampling at a different scan line. - If $N_z < 0$: The depth ordering is reversed. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Note:** The cross-product test works because for two planar polygons, the **set of points where they have equal depth** forms a single line in screen space. Therefore, the depth ordering of $P$ vs $Q$ is **constant throughout each span** between their edge intersections — no need to re-compare depth at every pixel. This single decision per span is the key speed advantage of scan-line HSR over the z-buffer. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Scan-Line Algorithm Architecture The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the flow of the Scan-Line Polygon Fill algorithm, from polygon input to fully rendered image: ```mermaid flowchart TD inputA["Polygon Vertices List"] --> buildET buildET["Build Edge Table ET
- For each non-horizontal edge
- Compute ymax, x at ymin, 1/m
- Insert into ET bucket at ymin"] --> setY setY["Set y = ymin of polygon
AET = empty"] --> loopStart loopStart{"For each scan line y
from ymin to ymax"} loopStart --> mergeStep mergeStep["Merge Step
Add all edges from ET y
into AET"] --> removeStep removeStep["Remove Step
Delete edges where ymax = y"] --> sortStep sortStep["Sort AET
by current x value ascending"] --> fillStep fillStep["Fill Step
For pairs x1, x2 x3, x4 ...
set pixels between x odd and x even"] --> updateStep updateStep["Update Step
For each edge in AET:
x new = x old + 1 per m"] --> incY incY["y = y + 1"] --> loopStart loopStart -->|"AET empty and ET exhausted"| outputZ outputZ["Output
Fully Filled Polygon Bitmap"] style buildET fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0 style fillStep fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100 style outputZ fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32 ``` ### 4.2 Data Structure Schematic — ET and AET Layout ```mermaid graph LR subgraph ET["Edge Table ET - Static"] ET1["Bucket y=1
E2: ymax=4, x=8, 1/m=2/3
E5: ymax=5, x=2, 1/m=0"] ET4["Bucket y=4
E3: ymax=8, x=10, 1/m=-3/2"] ET5["Bucket y=5
E4: ymax=8, x=4, 1/m=2/3"] end subgraph AET["Active Edge Table AET - Dynamic"] AETcurrent["At y=4
E5: x=2, 1/m=0
E3: x=10, 1/m=-3/2
SORTED BY x ASCENDING"] end ET1 -.merge at y=1.-> AETcurrent ET4 -.merge at y=4.-> AETcurrent ET5 -.merge at y=5.-> AETcurrent style ET fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b style AET fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#ff6f00 style AETcurrent fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100 ``` ### 4.3 Visual Intuition — Polygon with Scan Lines ```mermaid graph TB subgraph POLY["Polygon V1 2,1 to V2 8,1 to V3 10,4 to V4 4,8 to V5 2,5"] y1["y=1 ---scan line 1---"] y2["y=2 ---scan line 2---"] y3["y=3 ---scan line 3---"] y4["y=4 ---scan line 4--- E3 enters, E2 exits"] y5["y=5 ---scan line 5--- E4 enters, E5 exits"] y6["y=6 ---scan line 6---"] y7["y=7 ---scan line 7--- narrowing tip"] y8["y=8 ---scan line 8--- all edges exit"] end style y4 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c style y5 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c style y7 fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagrams:** Each horizontal box in the polygon subgraph represents one scan line. The highlighted rows ($y=4, 5$) show the critical scan lines where edges enter and exit the active set. These are the scan lines that produce the most transitions in the AET and consequently the most arithmetic — the **performance hot spots** of the algorithm. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** Define the **Edge Table (ET)** and **Active Edge Table (AET)** used in the Scan-Line polygon fill algorithm. Why are horizontal edges excluded from the ET? **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Edge Table (ET):** A static data structure that stores all non-horizontal edges of a polygon, grouped into buckets indexed by the y-coordinate of each edge's lowest endpoint ($y_{min}$). Each entry holds: (1) $y_{max}$ — the y-coordinate of the highest endpoint, (2) $x$ at $y_{min}$ — the initial x-intersection, and (3) $1/m$ — the inverse slope, used to update $x$ as the scan line moves down. **(1 mark)** - **Active Edge Table (AET):** A dynamic list holding only those edges that intersect the current scan line. It is re-sorted by x-intersection at every row and updated incrementally by adding $1/m$ to each $x$ value. **(1 mark)** - **Why horizontal edges are excluded:** Horizontal edges are parallel to the scan line, meaning they would never produce a "new" x-intersection as $y$ advances. Including them would either cause division-by-zero in the slope calculation or create ambiguous parity in the fill rule. The fill between their bounding vertices is handled implicitly by the edges incident to them. **(1 mark)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4]** What is **scan-line coherence**, and how does the Scan-Line Algorithm exploit it for hidden surface removal? **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Scan-line coherence** is the property that the visibility (depth ordering) of surfaces changes **infrequently** between adjacent rows of pixels. For planar surfaces, the depth ordering is constant across an entire span (region between two edge intersections) and only changes when the scan line crosses a new edge or the intersection line of two planes. **(1.5 marks)** - The Scan-Line Algorithm exploits this by: (1) maintaining a **depth-sorted Active Edge Table (AET)** for the current scan line, (2) using the **cross-product test** to determine which of two overlapping polygons is in front at each span, and (3) **only recomputing the depth ordering when the AET changes** (i.e., when an edge enters or exits). This avoids per-pixel depth comparisons, reducing the work to **O(edges + pixels)** instead of O(polygons × pixels). **(1.5 marks)** --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Polygon Fill Application **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Explain the **Scan-Line Polygon Fill Algorithm** with neat diagrams. Discuss the role of the **Edge Table (ET)** and **Active Edge Table (AET)** in detail. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Apply the Scan-Line Polygon Fill Algorithm to fill the polygon with vertices $A(1,1), B(6,1), C(8,5), D(4,7), E(1,4)$. Construct the **Edge Table (ET)** and show the **AET updates for at least three scan lines**, clearly indicating the filled pixels at each step. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution — Question A #### Part (a) — Algorithm Explanation (7 marks) > The complete algorithm flow, ET/AET structure, fill rule, and update mechanism are described in **Section 2.2** of these notes. The 7-mark answer should include: 1. **Definition and motivation** of the scan-line approach (coherence principle). **(1 mark)** 2. **ET structure**: bucket indexing by $y_{min}$, per-edge fields $(y_{max}, x, 1/m)$, exclusion of horizontal edges. **(1.5 marks)** 3. **AET structure and lifecycle**: merge from ET, removal at $y_{max}$, sort by x, fill pairs, update $x$ by $1/m$. **(2 marks)** 4. **Pairwise fill using parity rule** with a clear example of how x-pairs map to filled pixel ranges. **(1.5 marks)** 5. **Diagram** showing the polygon with horizontal scan lines and the resulting filled pixels. **(1 mark)** #### Part (b) — Worked Application (7 marks) **Edges (skipping horizontals):** | Edge | From → To | $y_{min}$ | $y_{max}$ | $x$ at $y_{min}$ | $1/m$ | ET Bucket | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | E1 | A(1,1) → B(6,1) | Horizontal | — | — | — | Skip | | E2 | B(6,1) → C(8,5) | 1 | 5 | 6 | $\frac{8-6}{5-1} = \frac{1}{2}$ | $ET[1]$ | | E3 | C(8,5) → D(4,7) | 5 | 7 | 8 | $\frac{4-8}{7-5} = -2$ | $ET[5]$ | | E4 | D(4,7) → E(1,4) | 4 | 7 | 1 | $\frac{1-4}{4-7} = 1$ | $ET[4]$ | | E5 | E(1,4) → A(1,1) | 1 | 4 | 1 | $\frac{1-1}{1-4} = 0$ | $ET[1]$ | **Edge Table:** $$ ET[1] = \{E2, E5\}, \quad ET[4] = \{E4\}, \quad ET[5] = \{E3\} $$ **AET Trace (Selected Scan Lines):** ##### ▶ $y = 1$ - **Merge:** E2, E5 from $ET[1]$. - **AET (sorted):** E5 $(x=1)$, E2 $(x=6)$. - **Fill:** $(1, 6)$ → Pixels $x = 2, 3, 4, 5$. **(1 mark)** - **Update:** E5: $x = 1 + 0 = 1$; E2: $x = 6 + 1/2 = 6.5$. ##### ▶ $y = 2$ - **AET (sorted):** E5 $(x=1)$, E2 $(x=6.5)$. - **Fill:** $(1, 6.5)$ → Pixels $x = 2, 3, 4, 5, 6$. **(1 mark)** - **Update:** E5: $x=1$; E2: $x = 6.5 + 0.5 = 7.0$. ##### ▶ $y = 3$ - **AET (sorted):** E5 $(x=1)$, E2 $(x=7.0)$. - **Fill:** $(1, 7.0)$ → Pixels $x = 2, 3, 4, 5, 6$. **(1 mark)** - **Update:** E5: $x=1$; E2: $x = 7.5$. ##### ▶ $y = 4$ (E4 enters, E5 exits) - **Merge:** E4 $(x=1, 1/m=1)$ from $ET[4]$. - **Remove:** E5 ($y_{max}=4$). - **AET (sorted):** E2 $(x=7.5)$, E4 $(x=1)$ → after sort: E4 $(x=1)$, E2 $(x=7.5)$. - **Fill:** $(1, 7.5)$ → Pixels $x = 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7$. **(1 mark)** - **Update:** E4: $x = 1 + 1 = 2$; E2: $x = 7.5 + 0.5 = 8.0$. ##### ▶ $y = 5$ (E3 enters, E2 exits) - **Merge:** E3 $(x=8, 1/m=-2)$ from $ET[5]$. - **Remove:** E2 ($y_{max}=5$). - **AET (sorted):** E4 $(x=2)$, E3 $(x=8)$. - **Fill:** $(2, 8)$ → Pixels $x = 3, 4, 5, 6, 7$. **(1 mark)** - **Update:** E4: $x = 3$; E3: $x = 8 - 2 = 6$. ##### ▶ $y = 6$ - **AET (sorted):** E4 $(x=3)$, E3 $(x=6)$. - **Fill:** $(3, 6)$ → Pixels $x = 4, 5$. **(1 mark)** ##### ▶ $y = 7$ (All edges exit) - **AET:** $\emptyset$ → Terminate. **Summary Table of Filled Pixels (1 mark):** | Scan Line $y$ | Fill Span | Pixels | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $(1, 6)$ | 2, 3, 4, 5 | | 2 | $(1, 6.5)$ | 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 | | 3 | $(1, 7.0)$ | 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 | | 4 | $(1, 7.5)$ | 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7 | | 5 | $(2, 8)$ | 3, 4, 5, 6, 7 | | 6 | $(3, 6)$ | 4, 5 | --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Hidden Surface Removal Application **[KTU University Exam – June 2023 / Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Describe the **Scan-Line Algorithm for Hidden Surface Removal**. Explain the structure of the augmented ET/AET with polygon identifiers and depth information. How does the **cross-product test** resolve depth conflicts between two overlapping polygons? **(7 marks)** **(b)** Two planar polygons $P$ and $Q$ overlap on a scan line between $x = 2$ and $x = 6$. Their depth values are: $$ z_P(2, y) = 10, \quad z_P(6, y) = 14, \quad z_Q(2, y) = 12, \quad z_Q(6, y) = 8 $$ Apply the **cross-product test** to determine which polygon is in front (visible) throughout the overlap span. Show all steps. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution — Question B #### Part (a) — Algorithm Explanation (7 marks) The Scan-Line Algorithm for HSR extends the polygon fill algorithm with: 1. **Augmented ET entry per edge:** $(y_{max}, x_{at\_y_{min}}, 1/m, \text{polygon\_ID}, \text{plane coefficients } A, B, C, D)$. **(1.5 marks)** 2. **Depth-sorted AET:** At each scan line, the AET is sorted by both $x$-intersection **and** the polygon ID / depth flag, so that when two polygons' edge x-values are interleaved, the front polygon's pixels are filled first. **(1.5 marks)** 3. **Span-by-span depth check:** For each pair of adjacent AET entries spanning a region, compute the depth of the two owning polygons at the mid-point (or any reference point) and determine which is closer. **(1 mark)** 4. **Cross-product test:** When two polygons span the same region, use the test to confirm that the depth ordering is **constant throughout the span** (no re-evaluation needed). The test: form $\vec{N} = (P_2 - P_1) \times (Q_1 - P_1)$; the sign of the z-component of $\vec{N}$ indicates the front polygon on the tested side. **(2 marks)** 5. **Update and iterate** the same way as polygon fill, plus handling of surface flag changes when the AET is re-sorted. **(1 mark)** #### Part (b) — Cross-Product Test Application (7 marks) **Given:** - $P_1 = (2, y, 10)$ — point on $P$ at left edge of overlap. - $P_2 = (6, y, 14)$ — point on $P$ at right edge of overlap. - $Q_1 = (2, y, 12)$ — point on $Q$ at left edge of overlap (same $x, y$ as $P_1$). **Step 1 — Construct the test vectors:** $$ \vec{V_1} = P_2 - P_1 = (6 - 2,\ 0,\ 14 - 10) = (4,\ 0,\ 4) $$ $$ \vec{V_2} = Q_1 - P_1 = (2 - 2,\ 0,\ 12 - 10) = (0,\ 0,\ 2) $$ **Step 2 — Compute the cross product $\vec{N} = \vec{V_1} \times \vec{V_2}$:** $$ \vec{N} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 4 & 0 & 4 \\ 0 & 0 & 2 \end{vmatrix} $$ Expanding along the first row: $$ N_x = (0)(2) - (4)(0) = 0 $$ $$ N_y = -\left[(4)(2) - (4)(0)\right] = -8 $$ $$ N_z = (4)(0) - (0)(0) = 0 $$ So $\vec{N} = (0, -8, 0)$. **(3 marks)** **Step 3 — Interpret the result:** The $z$-component of $\vec{N}$ is **zero**, meaning the two planes' intersection line in screen space is **perpendicular to the $y$-axis** and the test at this specific point is ambiguous. **(1 mark)** **Step 4 — Test the other edge instead (at $x = 6$):** At $x = 6$, the screen point lies on both $P$ and $Q$ (assuming their projections overlap exactly at this $x$): - On $P$: $z_P(6, y) = 14$ - On $Q$: $z_Q(6, y) = 8$ Comparing: at $x = 6$, $z_Q < z_P$, so **$Q$ is in front at the right edge of the overlap**. At $x = 2$, $z_P(2, y) = 10 < z_Q(2, y) = 12$, so **$P$ is in front at the left edge of the overlap**. **Step 5 — Conclusion:** The two polygons must therefore **cross** within the overlap region. The intersection of their depth planes occurs where $z_P(x, y) = z_Q(x, y)$. Since both are linear in $x$ (on this scan line, $y$ is fixed): $$ 10 + (14 - 10) \cdot \frac{x - 2}{4} = 12 + (8 - 12) \cdot \frac{x - 2}{4} $$ $$ 10 + (x - 2) = 12 - (x - 2) $$ $$ 10 + x - 2 = 12 - x + 2 $$ $$ x + 8 = 14 - x $$ $$ 2x = 6 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = 3 $$ So the depth ordering **switches at $x = 3$ on this scan line**: - For $2 \leq x < 3$: **Polygon $P$ is in front** (depth $= 10$ at $x=2$, decreasing toward equality at $x=3$). - For $3 < x \leq 6$: **Polygon $Q$ is in front** (depth $= 8$ at $x=6$, increasing toward equality at $x=3$). - At $x = 3$: Both polygons have the same depth, so the choice is arbitrary (or a tie-breaking rule applies). **(2 marks)** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Including horizontal edges in the ET** — This is the most common mistake. Horizontal edges produce undefined slopes ($m = 0$ or $\infty$) and break the parity rule. Always **explicitly state** in your answer that horizontal edges are skipped. Deducted: **0.5–1 mark**. > 2. **Confusing $y_{min}$ and $y_{max}$** — The ET bucket is indexed by $y_{min}$, not $y_{max}$. Many students place an edge in $ET[y_{max}]$, causing the algorithm to add it to the AET one scan line too late. Deducted: **1 mark**. > 3. **Forgetting the x-update step** — The x-intersection at the next scan line is $x + 1/m$, **not** $x + m$. Mixing up slope and inverse slope is a frequent sign error. Deducted: **1 mark**. > 4. **Not sorting the AET after every merge/remove** — The fill pairs depend on the AET being sorted by current $x$. Skipping the re-sort causes pairs of x-values to be mismatched. Deducted: **1 mark**. > 5. **For the cross-product test:** Students often compute the determinant but forget to **interpret the sign of $N_z$** in the context of which polygon is in front. A numerical answer without the interpretation loses **1.5–2 marks**. > 6. **Overfilling at the boundary** — Many students include the boundary pixels (the x-intersection points themselves) in the fill. By the parity rule, the interior of the polygon is filled, and the edges themselves are typically left unfilled (or filled with a separate color). Deducted: **0.5 mark**. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute Exam Prep):** - **Scan Line Definition:** A horizontal line of pixels processed in sequence. The Scan-Line Algorithm exploits **scan-line coherence** — adjacent rows have similar visibility/fill properties. - **Two Data Structures:** - **ET (Edge Table):** Static, sorted by $y_{min}$ of each edge. Holds $(y_{max}, x_{at\_y_{min}}, 1/m)$. **Horizontal edges are excluded.** - **AET (Active Edge Table):** Dynamic, holds only edges crossing the current scan line. **Re-sorted by x** at every row. - **Five-Step Per-Scan-Line Procedure:** **M**erge (add from ET) → **R**emove (edges with $y_{max} = y$) → **S**ort (by x) → **F**ill (pairs of x) → **U**pdate ($x \leftarrow x + 1/m$). - **Inverse Slope:** $\frac{1}{m} = \frac{x_2 - x_1}{y_2 - y_1}$. Compute once per edge; add to $x$ each scan line. - **Fill Rule:** Parity rule — fill between $(x_1, x_2), (x_3, x_4), \ldots$ sorted pairs. A point is inside the polygon if a horizontal ray crosses an **odd** number of edges. - **Hidden Surface Extension:** Add polygon ID and plane coefficients $(A, B, C, D)$ to each entry. Compute depth as $z = -(Ax + By + D)/C$. - **Cross-Product Test:** $\vec{N} = (P_2 - P_1) \times (Q_1 - P_1)$. The **sign of $N_z$** determines which polygon is in front. For planar surfaces, this ordering is **constant across each span**. - **Why It's Fast:** Only **O(edges + pixels)** work. The AET update is O(edges) per scan line, and the fill is O(pixels) per scan line — total O(edges × scan lines + pixels). - **Why Horizontal Edges Are Excluded:** They are parallel to scan lines, causing zero or infinite slope. The fill is naturally handled by the non-horizontal edges bounding them. - **Time Complexity:** $O(n \cdot h)$ where $n$ is the number of edges and $h$ is the height in scan lines. With the incremental $x$-update, it becomes $O(n + h)$ per row. - **Common Exam Traps:** $y_{min}$ vs $y_{max}$ confusion, $1/m$ vs $m$ swap, missing re-sort, including horizontal edges, forgetting to interpret $N_z$ sign. - **Real-World Use:** Historical SGI graphics hardware, modern **tile-based mobile GPUs** (Mali, Adreno, Apple GPU), and the conceptual foundation for **scanline rendering in game engines**. ---

topics.title: Depth Sorting Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT414 - COMPUTER GRAPHICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Depth Sorting Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Depth Sorting Methods?

**Depth Sorting Methods** are a class of **Visible Surface Detection (VSD)** algorithms in computer graphics used to determine which surfaces (or parts of surfaces) of a 3D scene are visible to the viewer (camera) and which are hidden behind opaque objects, so that only the front-most surfaces are rendered (painted) onto the 2D display screen.

In formal KTU 2024 terminology:

> **Definition:** Depth Sorting is the process of ordering the surfaces (polygons) in a 3D scene according to their distance (depth) from the viewing plane (or viewer), so that surfaces farther from the viewer are drawn first and overwritten by closer surfaces, producing a correctly shaded image.

The fundamental depth measure used is the **z-coordinate** in the **Viewing Coordinate System (VCS)**, typically measured along the negative z-axis of the camera, with values ranging between the **near plane (z = -z_near)** and the **far plane (z = -z_far)**.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy: The Stack of OHP Transparencies**
>
> Imagine a teacher preparing a lecture on an **Overhead Projector (OHP)**. Each transparency sheet has part of a diagram drawn on it (a star, a circle, a square, a triangle). Now consider two scenarios:
>
> 1. **No Sorting (Wrong Result):** The teacher randomly places the transparencies on the OHP. Whichever transparency is on top will hide parts of the others — the result looks distorted and incorrect.
> 2. **Depth Sorting (Painter's Algorithm):** The teacher carefully sorts the transparencies based on **distance from the projector lamp**: the sheet drawn with the **background star** goes down first, then the **middle-layer circle**, and finally the **front-layer triangle** on the very top. The projected image now shows the correct 3D scene!
>
> The projector lamp plays the role of the **viewer's eye**, and each transparency is a polygon. Sorting by depth = stacking from farthest to closest.

The classic algorithms implementing this are:
- **Painter's Algorithm** (naive)
- **Depth Sort Algorithm** (Newell, Newell, Sancha, 1972) — refined
- **Binary Space Partitioning (BSP) Trees**
- **Z-Buffer Algorithm** (pixel-precision)
- **Scan-Line Z-Buffer**

---

## 1.3 Why Depth Sorting is Needed

Without resolving visibility, the rendered image suffers from artifacts:

| Artifact | Cause |
|---|---|
| **Surface Ambiguity** | Two overlapping surfaces whose visibility cannot be decided locally |
| **Penetration Effect** | Intersecting polygons producing incorrect patches |
| **Ink-Saving Visualization Errors** | Far objects drawn over near objects (reverse depth) |
| **Cyclic Overlap** | A, B, C mutually overlap (Painter's ambiguity) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:**
> KTU Module 4 — *Visible Surface Detection* — explicitly lists **Depth Sorting Methods (Painter's Algorithm, Depth Sort, BSP Trees)** as core topics. Students must be able to describe the algorithm, perform a manual sort, and identify failure cases.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Depth Sorting Methods

Depth sorting algorithms fall into two broad families:

**A) Object-Space Methods (operate on polygons)**
- Painter's Algorithm
- Depth Sort Algorithm
- BSP Trees
- Subdivision Methods (Warnock, Weiler-Atherton)

**B) Image-Space Methods (operate on pixels)**
- Z-Buffer (Depth Buffer)
- Scan-Line Z-Buffer
- A-Buffer (anti-aliased)

> [!NOTE]
> Object-space methods are also called **list-priority methods** because they establish a rendering order (priority list) of objects before any pixel is drawn.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Painter's Algorithm (Basic Depth Sorting)

**Conceptual Steps:**
1. Compute the **z-coordinate** (depth) of every polygon in the scene, using the polygon's farthest vertex (or the polygon's centroid, or its maximum z).
2. **Sort** all polygons in **decreasing order of depth** (farthest first).
3. Render (paint) polygons one by one starting from the farthest. Each newly drawn polygon **overwrites** the pixels of previously drawn ones.
4. The result is a correctly visible-surface image — analogous to a painter painting the background first, then the foreground on top.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "overwrite" works:** Since each new polygon is closer to the viewer, it must obscure whatever is behind it. Overwriting in this order correctly implements the visibility ordering.

### 2.1.1 The Three Failure Cases of the Painter's Algorithm

The basic algorithm fails when sorting polygons by a single depth value is not sufficient:

1. **Case 1: Cyclic Overlap**
   - Polygons A, B, C overlap cyclically: A overlaps B, B overlaps C, C overlaps A.
   - Sorting by z alone cannot resolve the order.

2. **Case 2: Mutual Penetration**
   - Two polygons intersect (penetrate) each other, so neither is fully in front of the other.

3. **Case 3: Containment**
   - A smaller polygon is entirely inside a larger polygon but appears in front of part of the larger polygon and behind another part.

---

## 2.2 The Depth Sort Algorithm (Newell, Newell, Sancha — 1972)

This algorithm fixes Painter's problems by performing tests to **verify or refine** the depth order. The goal is to produce a **strictly ordered list** of polygons from back to front.

### 2.2.1 Algorithm Outline

1. Compute the **z-extent (z_min, z_max)** of every polygon.
2. Sort polygons by the **smallest z-coordinate (z_min)** of each polygon — the one with the **smallest z_min** is initially treated as the "farthest."
3. For each polygon **P** in the sorted list, test it against all polygons **Q** that are currently after it (i.e., closer).
4. If any test **fails**, swap P and Q, and restart the swap-test cycle.
5. Stop when all polygons pass all tests.

### 2.2.2 The Five Depth-Sort Tests

For polygons **P** (claimed farther) and **Q** (claimed closer), all four tests must pass before the order is confirmed. If **any** test fails, the order must be reversed for that pair:

> **Test 1: Bounding Spheres Do Not Overlap in x or y**
> The 2D projections (in screen space) of P and Q do not overlap. Then z-order is irrelevant — they don't hide each other.

> **Test 2: P is Entirely Behind Q's Bounding Plane (Min-Max Test)**
> $$\boxed{\;z_{\min}(P) \;\geq\; z_{\max}(Q)\;}$$
> If true, polygon P is entirely on the far side of polygon Q, so P is correctly placed behind Q.

> **Test 3: P is Entirely in Front of Q's Bounding Plane**
> $$\boxed{\;z_{\max}(P) \;\leq\; z_{\min}(Q)\;}$$
> The bounding boxes in z do not overlap at all.

> **Test 4: Polygons Do Not Overlap in the xy-Plane (Projected on Screen)**
> The 2D projections of P and Q are disjoint on the image plane. They cannot occlude each other.

> **Test 5: P Does Not Intersect Q**
> A line-polygon or polygon-polygon intersection test in 3D confirms no geometric intersection. (This is the most expensive test.)

If all five tests pass for every adjacent pair, the order is correct.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Point:** If any of the five tests fails, the algorithm **swaps** the order of P and Q and **restarts** testing from that point. The algorithm terminates when no swaps are required (a stable list is reached).

---

## 2.3 Binary Space Partitioning (BSP) Trees

The BSP tree is a **pre-processing data structure** that organizes polygons for fast visibility queries.

### 2.3.1 BSP Tree Construction

1. Pick a **partitioning plane** defined by a polygon in the scene.
2. Classify every other polygon as **in-front of**, **behind**, or **intersecting** the plane.
3. For intersecting polygons, **split** them along the partition plane into two new polygons.
4. Recursively build BSP sub-trees for the in-front set and the behind set.

### 2.3.2 Visibility Ordering Using BSP Trees

Given the viewer's position:
- A **back-to-front traversal** is used for the Painter's-style painting:
  - Recursively traverse the sub-tree on the **far side** of the partition first.
  - Then render the root polygon.
  - Then recursively traverse the sub-tree on the **near side**.
- A **front-to-back traversal** is used with a Z-buffer:
  - Traverse near first, then far, allowing early z-rejection of pixels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BSP Trees — Key Advantage:** They provide O(n²) worst-case construction but enable **O(n) average visibility ordering per frame**, making them excellent for **static scenes** like indoor walk-throughs (e.g., *Doom*, 1993).

---

## 2.4 The Z-Buffer Algorithm (Depth Buffer)

The Z-buffer is a modern, hardware-accelerated image-space method that eliminates many of the problems of object-space depth sorting.

### 2.4.1 Data Structures

- **Frame Buffer (F):** stores the color/intensity for every pixel `(x, y)`.
- **Depth Buffer (Z):** stores the depth (z-value) of the closest visible surface at each pixel `(x, y)`. Initialized to **-∞ (or most negative z, the far plane)**.

### 2.4.2 Algorithm

For every polygon, for every pixel `(x, y)` of the polygon:

$$z(x, y) = \frac{-A x - B y - D}{C}$$

Then perform the test:

$$\boxed{\; \text{if } z(x,y) < Z(x,y) \;\text{ then } \bigl\{ Z(x,y) \leftarrow z(x,y);\ \ F(x,y) \leftarrow \text{intensity} \bigr\}\;}$$

### 2.4.3 Key Properties

- **Resolution:** 1/z is used in hardware to enable linear interpolation. The actual stored value is often
  $$z' = \frac{1}{z}$$
  so that linear interpolation in screen space corresponds to perspective-correct depth.
- **Memory cost:** requires an additional buffer of `width × height × bits_per_z` (e.g., 24-bit or 32-bit depth).
- **Speed:** O(N·P) where N is the number of polygons and P is the polygon pixel count.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **THE KTU 2024 EXAM CHEAT SHEET — DEPTH SORTING METHODS**

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Condition / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Depth of a Plane (Point-Slope)** | $$Ax + By + Cz + D = 0$$ | $C \neq 0$ for the view direction |
| **Solve z at pixel (x, y)** | $$z(x,y) = \frac{-A x - B y - D}{C}$$ | Used in Z-buffer for each scan-line |
| **Z-Buffer Test** | $\text{if } z_{\text{new}} < Z(x,y) \text{ then overwrite}$ | Nearest pixel wins |
| **Min-Z Test (Depth Sort, T2)** | $z_{\min}(P) \geq z_{\max}(Q)$ | P is entirely behind Q |
| **Max-Z Test (Depth Sort, T3)** | $z_{\max}(P) \leq z_{\min}(Q)$ | P is entirely in front of Q |
| **Planar Equation Constants (3 pts)** | $\mathbf{n} = (P_2 - P_1) \times (P_3 - P_1)$ | $A, B, C$ are components of $\mathbf{n}$ |
| **Plane Constant D** | $D = -A x_1 - B y_1 - C z_1$ | For any vertex $(x_1, y_1, z_1)$ on the plane |
| **Resolution of Depth Buffer** | $n \text{ bits} \Rightarrow 2^n \text{ depth levels}$ | Typical: 16, 24, or 32 bits |
| **Number of Polygons Complexity** | $O(n \log n)$ — Painter's / Depth Sort | $O(n)$ per query — BSP |
| **BSP Traversal** | Far-subtree → Root → Near-subtree | Painter's mode |
| **Z-Buffer Memory** | $\text{width} \times \text{height} \times n$ bits | E.g., 1920×1080×24 = ~6 MB |

> **Notation:** $\vert x \vert$ means absolute value; the bounding extent of a polygon is $[z_{\min}, z_{\max}]$.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Video Games:** BSP trees in *Doom/Quake* era; Z-buffer in every modern GPU.
- **CAD/Engineering Visualization:** Hidden-line removal in mechanical assemblies.
- **Medical Imaging (CT/MRI):** Volume rendering with depth compositing.
- **Augmented Reality (AR):** Occlusion handling — virtual objects properly hidden by real ones.
- **3D Printing Slicers:** Depth sorting of slice polygons before generating G-code.
- **Flight Simulators & GIS:** Terrain rendering requires efficient culling of occluded surfaces.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Computing z(x, y) from the Planar Equation

**Given:** A polygon lies on the plane
$$Ax + By + Cz + D = 0$$
where $A, B, C, D$ are real constants.

**Step 1 — Isolate z:**
$$Cz = -Ax - By - D$$

**Step 2 — Solve for z:**
$$z = \frac{-Ax - By - D}{C}, \quad C \neq 0$$

This is the **z-coordinate of any point on the plane** given its screen projection $(x, y)$.

> **Conversion Logic:** We treat $A, B, C$ as derived from the plane's normal vector and $D$ from a known point on the plane. For a polygon, this is computed once and then evaluated at every pixel $(x, y)$ covered by the polygon.

**Step 3 — Use in Z-Buffer Update:**

For each pixel $(x, y)$ of the polygon:
- Compute $z_{\text{candidate}}(x, y)$ using the formula above.
- If $z_{\text{candidate}} < Z(x, y)$ (i.e., the candidate is closer to the viewer), update the frame and depth buffers.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: The Plane Coefficients from Three 3D Points

**Given:** Three non-collinear vertices
$$P_1 = (x_1, y_1, z_1), \quad P_2 = (x_2, y_2, z_2), \quad P_3 = (x_3, y_3, z_3)$$

**Step 1 — Form two edge vectors:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{u} &= P_2 - P_1 = (x_2 - x_1,\ y_2 - y_1,\ z_2 - z_1) \\
\mathbf{v} &= P_3 - P_1 = (x_3 - x_1,\ y_3 - y_1,\ z_3 - z_1)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the normal vector via the cross product:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{n} = \mathbf{u} \times \mathbf{v} &=
\begin{vmatrix}
\mathbf{i} & \mathbf{j} & \mathbf{k} \\
x_2 - x_1 & y_2 - y_1 & z_2 - z_1 \\
x_3 - x_1 & y_3 - y_1 & z_3 - z_1
\end{vmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

Expanding:

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= (y_2 - y_1)(z_3 - z_1) - (z_2 - z_1)(y_3 - y_1) \\
B &= (z_2 - z_1)(x_3 - x_1) - (x_2 - x_1)(z_3 - z_1) \\
C &= (x_2 - x_1)(y_3 - y_1) - (y_2 - y_1)(x_3 - x_1)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute D using any of the three points** (e.g., $P_1$):

$$D = -(A x_1 + B y_1 + C z_1)$$

**Conversion Logic:** The normal $\mathbf{n} = (A, B, C)$ points perpendicular to the polygon's plane. Plugging $P_1$ into $Ax + By + Cz$ gives the value that must be cancelled by $D$ for the plane equation to hold.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Manual Depth Sort on Three Polygons

**Scene:**

| Polygon | Vertices (VCS) | $z_{\min}$ | $z_{\max}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| P (square) | $(0,0,-2),\ (2,0,-2),\ (2,2,-2),\ (0,2,-2)$ | $-2$ | $-2$ |
| Q (triangle) | $(1,1,-4),\ (3,1,-4),\ (2,3,-4)$ | $-4$ | $-4$ |
| R (rectangle) | $(1,0,-3),\ (3,0,-3),\ (3,2,-3),\ (1,2,-3)$ | $-3$ | $-3$ |

(More negative z = farther from viewer; viewer at origin looking down -z axis.)

**Step 1 — Sort by $z_{\min}$ ascending (farthest first):**

$$\text{Q} \;(z_{\min}=-4) \;\to\; \text{R} \;(z_{\min}=-3) \;\to\; \text{P} \;(z_{\min}=-2)$$

**Step 2 — Apply Depth Sort tests:**

- **Q vs R:** $z_{\max}(Q) = -4 \;\leq\; z_{\min}(R) = -3$. Test 3 passes ✓
- **R vs P:** $z_{\max}(R) = -3 \;\leq\; z_{\min}(P) = -2$. Test 3 passes ✓

**Step 3 — Final paint order:** Q → R → P.

> **Conversion Logic:** Q is the farthest rectangle (the back wall), R is the middle rectangle, and P is the front square. Painting in this order produces the correct visible surface.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Depth Sorting Methods

| Criterion | Painter's Algorithm | Depth Sort | BSP Tree | Z-Buffer |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Object-space | Object-space | Object-space (preprocess) | Image-space |
| **Complexity** | $O(n \log n)$ sort | $O(n^2)$ worst case | $O(n^2)$ build, $O(n)$ query | $O(N \cdot P)$ per frame |
| **Handles Overlaps?** | ❌ Fails on cyclic/penetration | ✅ With 5 tests | ✅ Yes | ✅ Always |
| **Handles Intersections?** | ❌ | ⚠️ Test 5 needed | ✅ Splits polygons | ✅ Always |
| **Memory** | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n^2)$ worst case | $O(W \cdot H)$ frame |
| **Hardware Support** | None (CPU only) | None | None | ✅ Built into every modern GPU |
| **Best for** | Simple static scenes | Few objects, no intersections | Static indoor scenes | Real-time 3D graphics |
| **Year Introduced** | 1970s | 1972 (Newell et al.) | 1969 (Schumacker) | 1974 (Catmull) |
| **Z-fighting** | N/A | N/A | N/A | ⚠️ Possible at near-coplanar surfaces |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **N = number of polygons; P = pixels per polygon; n = total scene polygons; W, H = framebuffer width & height.**

---

## 3.5 Derivations — Z-Buffer Resolution and Z-Fighting

**The Z-Buffer Resolution Question:**

A depth buffer with $n$ bits stores $2^n$ discrete depth levels. If the **near plane** is at distance $z_{\text{near}}$ and the **far plane** at distance $z_{\text{far}}$ (in view space), the depth precision is **non-linear**:

$$z_{\text{precision}} \;\propto\; \frac{z^2}{z_{\text{far}} - z_{\text{near}}}$$

**Conversion Logic:** The stored value is the **non-linear** mapping

$$z' = \frac{1/z - 1/z_{\text{near}}}{1/z_{\text{far}} - 1/z_{\text{near}}}$$

Hence, precision (the world-space distance per discrete z' step) **shrinks quadratically with view-space z**. At large $z$ (far surfaces), many z' levels correspond to a large world distance, but at small $z$ (near surfaces), one z' level corresponds to a tiny world distance.

> **This explains z-fighting:** two nearly-coplanar surfaces at large z fall into the **same discrete z' bin**, and the GPU cannot decide which is in front — flickering ("z-fighting") occurs.

**Remedies:**
- Bring $z_{\text{near}}$ closer to the eye.
- Move $z_{\text{far}}$ closer (smaller far plane).
- Use **24-bit or 32-bit** depth buffers.
- **Logarithmic depth buffer** (advanced technique).

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Depth Sorting Pipeline

The following Mermaid block renders a **block-level functional architecture flow** that captures the entire depth sorting pipeline from input polygon list to final image output. This is a system-level schematic rather than a physical drawing.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputPolyList["INPUT POLYGON LIST"] --> stage1
    subgraph stage1["STAGE 1: GEOMETRY PRE-PROCESSING"]
        dirA["Project to View Coordinates VCS"]
        dirB["Compute Plane Coeff A B C D per polygon"]
        dirC["Compute z_min and z_max per polygon"]
        dirA --> dirB --> dirC
    end
    stage1 --> stage2
    subgraph stage2["STAGE 2: SORTING ENGINE"]
        sorterA["Initial Sort by z_min descending"]
        sorterB["Apply 5 Depth-Sort Tests"]
        sorterC["Detect Cycles Intersections Containment"]
        sorterD["Swap Out-of-Order Pairs"]
        sorterE["Re-Test until Stable"]
        sorterA --> sorterB --> sorterC --> sorterD --> sorterE
    end
    stage2 --> stage3
    subgraph stage3["STAGE 3: RENDERER BACKEND"]
        renderA{"Method Selected"}
        renderB["Painter's Paint Back-to-Front"]
        renderC["BSP Tree Traversal Far-First"]
        renderD["Z-Buffer Pixel-by-Pixel Test"]
        renderE["Scan-Line Z-Buffer"]
    end
    renderA --> renderB
    renderA --> renderC
    renderA --> renderD
    renderA --> renderE
    stage3 --> stage4
    subgraph stage4["STAGE 4: OUTPUT"]
        outA["Frame Buffer Colors"]
        outB["Depth Buffer Z Map"]
        outC["Final Visible Image on Display"]
        outA --> outC
        outB --> outC
    end
    style inputPolyList fill:#fde,stroke:#900
    style outC fill:#cfe,stroke:#060
    style renderA fill:#ffe,stroke:#aa0
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Define Depth Sorting. List any two depth sorting methods.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Depth Sorting** is the process of ordering the surfaces of a 3D scene according to their distance from the viewer so that hidden surfaces can be eliminated and the visible ones painted in the correct order.
>
> **Two depth sorting methods:**
> 1. **Painter's Algorithm** (object-space, back-to-front paint)
> 2. **Z-Buffer Algorithm** (image-space, per-pixel depth comparison)

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`
**What is the difference between object-space and image-space methods of visible surface detection?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Object-Space | Image-Space |
|---|---|---|
| **Unit of processing** | Whole polygons | Individual pixels |
| **Precision** | Continuous (analytic) | Discrete (resolution-limited) |
| **Speed** | Slower, scene-complexity bound | Faster with hardware (GPU) |
| **Examples** | Painter's, Depth Sort, BSP | Z-Buffer, Scan-line Z-Buffer, A-Buffer |
| **Resolution Independence** | Yes | No (depends on framebuffer size) |

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, usually split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Internal choice is given — both alternatives are provided below.

---

### Question A: Painter's Algorithm and Depth Sort — 14 Marks

> **Part (a) [7 Marks]** — Explain the **Painter's Algorithm** in detail. Discuss its three failure cases with neat sketches.

**Model Solution:**

1. **Painter's Algorithm Overview (2 marks):**
   - The algorithm sorts all polygons in the scene in **descending order of depth** (farthest first), then paints them sequentially onto the framebuffer. Each new polygon overwrites the previous ones, ensuring that closer polygons obscure farther ones.

2. **Procedure (2 marks):**
   - Compute the depth (z) of every polygon (often using the polygon's farthest z or its centroid z).
   - Sort polygons in decreasing z order.
   - Paint polygons from farthest to nearest.

3. **Three Failure Cases (3 marks):**

   - **Case 1 — Cyclic Overlap:** Three polygons A, B, C such that A overlaps B, B overlaps C, and C overlaps A. Sorting by z cannot resolve the cycle.

   - **Case 2 — Mutual Penetration:** Two polygons intersect geometrically. Neither is fully in front of the other; neither is fully behind.

   - **Case 3 — Containment:** A small polygon is completely inside a larger one but appears partly in front and partly behind the larger polygon.

> **Part (b) [7 Marks]** — Describe the **Depth Sort Algorithm** (Newell-Newell-Sancha). List the five tests it performs.

**Model Solution:**

1. **Depth Sort Introduction (2 marks):**
   - Proposed by **Newell, Newell, and Sancha in 1972** to address the Painter's algorithm's failure cases. It produces a strictly back-to-front ordered list of polygons by **repeatedly testing and swapping**.

2. **Algorithm Steps (2 marks):**
   - Sort polygons by $z_{\min}$ initially.
   - For each polygon P (claimed farther), test it against the next polygon Q.
   - If any test fails, **swap** P and Q and re-test from that position.
   - Terminate when no swaps are needed.

3. **The Five Tests (3 marks):**
   1. **Bounding Spheres Do Not Overlap in x or y** — 2D projections are disjoint.
   2. **P Entirely Behind Q** — $z_{\min}(P) \geq z_{\max}(Q)$.
   3. **P Entirely in Front of Q** — $z_{\max}(P) \leq z_{\min}(Q)$.
   4. **No xy-Plane Overlap** — projected 2D silhouettes disjoint.
   5. **No 3D Intersection** — geometric intersection test (most expensive).

If **all five tests pass**, the order is correct. If any fails, the order is **reversed** for that pair.

---

### Question B: Z-Buffer and BSP Trees — 14 Marks

> **Part (a) [7 Marks]** — Explain the **Z-Buffer Algorithm** for visible surface detection. Mention its advantages and disadvantages.

**Model Solution:**

1. **Data Structures (1 mark):**
   - **Frame Buffer F(x, y):** stores the color/intensity per pixel.
   - **Depth Buffer Z(x, y):** stores the z-value of the closest visible surface, initialized to **-∞** (far plane).

2. **Algorithm (3 marks):**
   - For each polygon and for each pixel $(x, y)$ in the polygon's projection:
     - Compute depth $z(x, y) = \frac{-Ax - By - D}{C}$.
     - If $z(x, y) < Z(x, y)$ (closer than stored), then:
       - Update: $Z(x, y) \leftarrow z(x, y)$ and $F(x, y) \leftarrow \text{intensity}$.
   - Continue until all polygons are processed.

3. **Advantages (1.5 marks):**
   - Handles all overlap cases, intersections, and containments correctly.
   - Simple to implement in hardware.
   - $O(N \cdot P)$ per frame; trivially parallelizable.

4. **Disadvantages (1.5 marks):**
   - Requires extra **depth buffer memory** (e.g., 24 bits × resolution).
   - Subject to **z-fighting** on near-coplanar surfaces.
   - Wastes computation on hidden surfaces.

> **Part (b) [7 Marks]** — Describe the **Binary Space Partitioning (BSP) Tree** technique. How does it resolve visibility?

**Model Solution:**

1. **BSP Tree Concept (2 marks):**
   - A BSP tree is a **recursive subdivision of 3D space** using planes derived from polygons. Each internal node stores a polygon; the children represent the two half-spaces on either side of its plane.

2. **Construction (2 marks):**
   - Pick a polygon as the root's partition plane.
   - Classify other polygons as **in-front**, **behind**, or **intersecting** the plane.
   - **Split** intersecting polygons along the plane.
   - Recursively build sub-trees.

3. **Visibility Ordering (2 marks):**
   - Given the viewer's position, perform a **back-to-front traversal**: first the far sub-tree, then the root polygon, then the near sub-tree.
   - The resulting order is used to paint surfaces correctly.

4. **Advantages (1 mark):**
   - $O(n)$ visibility query per frame after $O(n^2)$ preprocessing.
   - Ideal for static indoor scenes (e.g., *Doom*, 1993).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the swap-and-retest logic** in the Depth Sort algorithm — many students just "sort once and paint." The depth sort **repeatedly swaps** until stable. Failure to mention this loses **2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing Painter's algorithm with Depth Sort.** Painter's is the simple version; Depth Sort is the corrected version with five tests. KTU values distinguishing the two.
> 3. **Writing "Z-buffer is just a frame buffer"** — No! It is a **separate depth buffer** that stores z-values per pixel.
> 4. **Ignoring z-fighting** in Z-Buffer answers — mention **coplanar surfaces** and how 24/32-bit depth mitigates it.
> 5. **Skipping plane coefficient derivation** — if the question asks for $A, B, C, D$ from 3 vertices, show the **cross product**, not just the formula.
> 6. **Not drawing the failure-case diagrams** of Painter's algorithm. KTU awards marks for neat sketches.
> 7. **Confusing Painter's with BSP tree traversal order** — Painter's is far-to-near in *list order*; BSP gives the *traversal order* via tree recursion.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — DEPTH SORTING METHODS**

- **Visible Surface Detection (VSD)** is the core problem: which surface is in front?
- **Object-space** methods = work on polygons; **image-space** methods = work on pixels.
- **Painter's Algorithm** — sort by z, paint back-to-front; **three failures:** cyclic, penetration, containment.
- **Depth Sort (1972)** — same idea, but with **5 verification tests** + swap-and-retest.
- **Five Tests of Depth Sort:** disjoint projections, P-behind-Q, P-front-of-Q, no-xy-overlap, no-3D-intersection.
- **BSP Tree** — recursive spatial subdivision; back-to-front traversal for Painter's mode; **static scenes only**.
- **Z-Buffer** — pixel-precise, hardware-friendly, requires separate depth memory.
- **Plane equation:** $Ax + By + Cz + D = 0$.
- **Solve for z at pixel:** $z(x,y) = \frac{-Ax - By - D}{C}$.
- **Z-Buffer update rule:** $\text{if } z_{\text{new}} < Z(x,y), \text{ overwrite}$.
- **Initial value of Z(x, y):** **-∞** (or the most negative / farthest depth).
- **Z-fighting** = coplanar surfaces flickering; mitigated by deeper depth buffer, tighter near/far planes, or logarithmic depth.
- **BSP complexity:** $O(n^2)$ build, $O(n)$ per frame traversal.
- **Z-Buffer complexity:** $O(N \cdot P)$ per frame; easily parallelized on GPU.
- **Modern GPUs always use Z-buffer**; BSP is now mainly a historical/educational technique.
- **KTU Past Year Frequency:** Depth sorting is asked almost **every semester** — Painter's, Depth Sort, and Z-Buffer are the "must-know" trio.
- **Memory formula for depth buffer:** $\text{Width} \times \text{Height} \times n_{\text{bits}}$.
- **Depth precision varies with $z^2$** — closer objects get more precision.
- **Tree traversal for back-to-front painting:** far-subtree → root → near-subtree.

> **End of Notes — Depth Sorting Methods, Module 4, PEITT414 (Computer Graphics), KTU 2024 Scheme.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
