> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT414 - COMPUTER GRAPHICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hidden Surface Removal: Back Face Detection - Z-Buffer Method - Painter's Algorithm - Scan-Line Algorithm - BSP Trees - Area Sub-division Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Hidden Surface Removal (HSR)?

**Hidden Surface Removal (HSR)** — also known as **Hidden Surface Determination (HSD)** or **Visible Surface Detection (VSD)** — is the process used in computer graphics to identify and **eliminate (remove)** the surfaces of a 3D object that are **not visible** to the viewer (i.e., hidden behind other opaque surfaces) from a given viewpoint before rasterization/painting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):** "Hidden Surface Removal is the process of determining which surfaces of an object are visible from a particular viewpoint and which are obscured, to render only the visible portions efficiently."

### Formal Problem Statement
Given:
- A set of 3D polygonal surfaces (or curved surfaces) in object space
- A viewing position (camera/eye)
- A projection plane (viewing window)

**Find:** For each pixel $(x, y)$ on the projection plane, the surface (and its color/shading) that is **closest** to the viewer along the viewing direction.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Stack of Transparent Sheets" Intuition

Imagine you are looking through a stack of **glass plates**, each having a part of a drawing painted on it. The plates are aligned, and you look straight through the stack from one side.

- The drawing on the **front-most plate** hides all drawings on the plates behind it (where they overlap).
- You only see the *nearest* pixel at every point.

**HSR is the computational equivalent of this:**
- Each 3D surface = a glass plate at a specific depth.
- The viewer = your eye.
- The "nearest plate" at each point = the surface that wins the depth test.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is HSR important?** Without it, rendered scenes would show *all* surfaces, including the ones inside/behind the model. The result would look like a wireframe with chaotic overlapping lines — confusing and unrealistic. HSR is the foundation of **realistic 3D rendering**.

---

## 1.2 Classification of HSR Algorithms

KTU 2024 classifies HSR algorithms into **two broad families**:

| Category | Approach | Order Dependency | Typical Algorithms |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Object-Space Methods** | Compare objects/surfaces against each other | Yes (sort by depth) | Painter's Algorithm, BSP Trees, Back-Face Detection, Area Sub-division |
| **Image-Space Methods** | Resolve visibility at each pixel | No (per-pixel depth) | Z-Buffer, Scan-Line Algorithm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Object-space algorithms operate on the **continuous geometry** (polygons) before rasterization. Image-space algorithms operate at the **discrete pixel level** during rasterization. The trade-off is **accuracy vs. speed**.

---

## 1.3 Coherence — The Key Performance Trick

All efficient HSR algorithms exploit **coherence** — the property that pixels/polygons in a local region tend to share properties.

| Coherence Type | What is Reused? | Used By |
|---|---|---|
| **Object Coherence** | Surfaces visible in one frame are likely visible in the next | Frame-to-frame caching |
| **Face Coherence** | A face is typically visible only from a small range of angles | Back-face culling |
| **Edge Coherence** | Visibility changes mostly at edges | Scan-line, BSP |
| **Scan-Line Coherence** | Adjacent pixels on a scan line often have the same visible surface | Z-buffer, scan-line |
| **Depth Coherence** | Depth values change gradually across nearby pixels | Z-buffer |
| **Area Coherence** | A region of the image is covered by a single face | Area sub-division (Warnock) |
| **Frame Coherence** | Successive frames are similar (animation) | Temporal algorithms |

---

## 1.4 The Six Algorithms at a Glance

| # | Algorithm | Type | Complexity | Memory | Handles Overlaps? | Cyclic Overlap? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Back-Face Detection (Culling)** | Object-space pre-filter | $O(F)$ faces | None extra | No — only culls back faces | N/A |
| 2 | **Z-Buffer (Depth Buffer)** | Image-space | $O(F \cdot P)$ | $O(N^2)$ z-buffer | Yes | Yes |
| 3 | **Painter's Algorithm** | Object-space sort | $O(F \log F)$ | None extra | Yes | No (fails) |
| 4 | **Scan-Line Algorithm** | Image-space | $O(F \cdot N)$ | small active list | Yes (within scan line) | Mostly |
| 5 | **BSP Trees** | Object-space pre-process | $O(F \log F)$ build, $O(F)$ traverse | Tree | Yes | Yes (handles) |
| 6 | **Area Sub-division (Warnock)** | Image-space divide & conquer | $O(F \cdot \log N)$ | Recursive | Yes | Yes |

where $F$ = number of faces, $P$ = pixels covered per face, $N$ = screen resolution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Where $F \cdot P$ and $F \cdot N$ come from:** For each face, we iterate over all the pixels/scan lines it covers. The Z-buffer is the **most widely used** in hardware (every modern GPU has one built in).

---

## 1.5 The Role of Each Algorithm in the KTU Pipeline

```
3D Scene → [ Back-Face Culling ] → Reduced Set of Faces
                ↓
        [ Depth Sorting / BSP / Scan-Line ] → Visibility Resolution
                ↓
        [ Z-Buffer ] → Per-Pixel Final Resolution
                ↓
            Color/Fragment Output
```

Back-face detection is **almost always a pre-filter** run first. Z-Buffer is the **workhorse**. The others (Painter's, Scan-Line, BSP, Warnock) are classic academic algorithms still relevant in specific contexts (e.g., BSP for static game levels).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Back-Face Detection (Back-Face Culling)

### Core Idea
A face of a **convex** polyhedron whose **outward normal points away** from the viewer can never be visible. We compute the angle between the face normal $\vec{N}$ and the view direction $\vec{V}$; if $\vec{N} \cdot \vec{V} > 0$, the face is **back-facing** and can be **culled** (removed).

### Mathematical Test

Given a polygon face with vertices in counter-clockwise (CCW) order as seen from outside:
- $\vec{N}$ = outward normal
- $\vec{V} = \vec{C} - \vec{E}$ where $C$ is face centroid and $E$ is eye position
- **Back face** if $\vec{N} \cdot \vec{V} > 0$ (or equivalently, $\vec{V}_z \cdot \vec{N}_z > 0$ in view space where the viewer looks down the $-z$ axis).

### Why It Works (Geometric Proof)
A face is visible **iff** some ray from the eye hits it before any other face. For a **convex** solid, this is equivalent to saying the face's outward normal makes an obtuse angle with the view vector. Hence the test $\vec{N} \cdot \vec{V} \leq 0$ keeps only front-facing polygons.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Back-face culling **fails for concave polyhedra** because a back-facing face may be visible through a "window" formed by front-facing faces. The correct statement is: *back-face culling is exact for convex polyhedra and a conservative over-approximation for concave ones.*

### Algorithm Steps
1. Compute the outward normal $\vec{N}$ of each face.
2. Compute the view direction $\vec{V}$ from the face centroid to the eye.
3. Compute the dot product $d = \vec{N} \cdot \vec{V}$.
4. If $d > 0$ → back face → **cull**.
5. Else → keep for further visibility tests.

### Normal Computation (for a triangle with vertices $V_1, V_2, V_3$):
$$\vec{N} = (V_2 - V_1) \times (V_3 - V_1)$$

### Cost Analysis
- Time: $O(F)$ per frame, where $F$ = total faces.
- Memory: No extra memory.
- **Culls ~50% of faces** on average for convex solids.

---

## 2.2 Z-Buffer (Depth Buffer) Method

### Concept
A **Z-buffer** is a 2D array, the same size as the frame buffer, that stores the **depth (z-value)** of the closest surface seen so far at each pixel. As each polygon is rasterized, its depth is compared against the stored depth. The polygon is drawn **only if it is closer** than what is already there.

### Data Structures
- **Frame buffer** $F[x][y]$ — stores the color/intensity of the visible surface.
- **Depth buffer** $Z[x][y]$ — stores the depth of the visible surface.

Both are initialized:
- $F[x][y] \gets \text{background color}$
- $Z[x][y] \gets z_{\text{far}}$ (largest possible depth)

### Algorithm
```
For each polygon P:
    For each pixel (x, y) covered by P:
        Compute depth z of P at (x, y)
        If z < Z[x][y]:
            Z[x][y] = z
            F[x][y] = color of P at (x, y)
```

### Linear Interpolation of Depth
For a scan line intersecting a polygon, the z-values at the left and right edges are linearly interpolated:

$$z(x) = z_{\text{left}} + \frac{(x - x_{\text{left}})}{(x_{\text{right}} - x_{\text{left}})} (z_{\text{right}} - z_{\text{left}})$$

This avoids the expensive perspective division at every pixel.

### Properties
- **Simple** to implement in hardware.
- **Handles any polygon count**, intersecting surfaces, cyclic overlap.
- **Memory**: $O(N^2)$ for a screen of $N \times N$ pixels (e.g., $1920 \times 1080 \approx 8.3$ MB for 32-bit depth).
- **No object pre-sorting required.**
- **Drawback**: Visible surfaces may be computed even when occluded (overdraw) → wastes computation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield:** The Z-Buffer is the **de facto standard** in every modern GPU (OpenGL `gl_FragDepth`, DirectX `SV_Depth`, Vulkan/Vulkan). The hardware always has a dedicated depth buffer.

---

## 2.3 Painter's Algorithm

### Concept
Render polygons in **back-to-front order** (farthest first). Each new polygon **overwrites** the pixels of polygons behind it. The result is the same as if a painter painted distant parts first and nearby parts last — hence the name.

### Algorithm
1. Compute the depth (z-value, or distance from eye) of every polygon.
2. Sort polygons in **decreasing depth** (farthest first).
3. Paint them in that order onto the frame buffer.

### The Depth Metric
A common choice is the **polygon's farthest z-value** $z_{\max}$ — the depth of its farthest vertex from the viewer. More accurate (and expensive): use the **polygon's closest point** and verify no overlap ambiguity.

### When It Fails — Cyclic Overlap

Consider three triangles A, B, C arranged such that:
- A is behind B
- B is behind C
- **C is behind A**

No consistent linear order exists. **Painter's Algorithm breaks down.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** The classic textbook failure of Painter's Algorithm is the **three mutually overlapping triangles** (Newell, Ullman, and Newell's 1972 example). The fix is to **split** offending polygons.

### Polygon Splitting Fix
If two polygons A (deeper) and B (shallower) overlap in screen space such that A is partly to the left and partly to the right of B, **split A into two pieces**, each of which is wholly on one side of B's plane. Then sort and paint the pieces correctly.

### Cost Analysis
- Sorting: $O(F \log F)$ per frame.
- Painting: $O(F \cdot P)$ total pixels.
- **No extra memory** for depth.
- **Pre-processing cost** for splitting.

---

## 2.4 Scan-Line Algorithm (Scan-Line Coherence Based)

### Concept
Process the image **one scan line at a time**. For each scan line $y = c$:
1. Find all polygons intersecting the scan line.
2. Sort the intersection points (called **span endpoints**) by $x$.
3. Use the **active edge list (AEL)** and **active polygon list** to determine, for each pixel interval between two intersection points, which polygon is visible there.

### Key Structures
- **Edge Table (ET)**: Lists all polygon edges, bucketed by the $y$-coordinate of their lower endpoint. Each entry: $(y_{\max}, x_{\text{at }y_{\min}}, 1/m)$ (inverse slope).
- **Active Edge List (AEL)**: For the current scan line, contains the currently active edges sorted by $x$.
- **Active Polygon List**: Polygons that the scan line currently intersects.
- **In-out flag / parity flag** or **depth flag** to track which polygon is in front.

### Algorithm
```
Initialize:
    EdgeTable (ET) bucketed by y
    ActiveEdgeList (AEL) = empty
    ActivePolygonList = empty

For each scan line y from y_min to y_max:
    1. Move edges with y_min == y from ET into AEL
    2. Remove edges with y_max == y from AEL
    3. For each new edge, add the corresponding polygon to ActivePolygonList
    4. For each polygon, sort intersections by x
    5. For each span between consecutive intersections:
        Determine visible polygon using depth/parity flag
        Write its color into frame buffer
    6. Update x-coordinates of AEL using 1/m
    7. y = y + 1
```

### Depth Resolution Within a Scan Line
For each span (interval between two intersection points), there may be **multiple polygons** crossing it. Use:
- **Parity (in-out) flag**: alternates inside/outside as the scan line crosses polygon edges. The "odd" polygon (currently inside) is the visible one.
- Or, if multiple polygons are inside the same span, the **nearest** one (smallest z at that pixel) wins.

### Handling Intersecting Surfaces
If two polygons intersect within a span, **split the polygons** at the intersection line and process them as sub-polygons.

### Cost Analysis
- Time: $O(F \cdot N)$ where $F$ = faces, $N$ = screen height.
- Memory: $O(F)$ for active lists.
- **Highly coherent** — uses scan-line, edge, and object coherence.

---

## 2.5 BSP (Binary Space Partitioning) Trees

### Concept
BSP Trees are a **pre-processing visibility structure**. The 3D scene is recursively partitioned by planes through polygon faces. At render time, we just **traverse the tree in the correct order** for the current viewpoint, and we get a back-to-front list of polygons automatically.

### Build Procedure
```
BSP_Build(polygon_list, plane):
    If polygon_list is empty: return null
    Pick a polygon P from the list
    Use P's plane as the splitting plane
    Partition remaining polygons into:
        FrontList:  polygons entirely on the "front" side of P's plane
        BackList:   polygons entirely on the "back" side of P's plane
        SplitList:  polygons that cross the plane → split into two sub-polygons
    node.polygon = P
    node.front   = BSP_Build(FrontList ∪ first-half of SplitList, ...)
    node.back    = BSP_Build(BackList ∪ second-half of SplitList, ...)
    Return node
```

### Traversal (Painter's Algorithm in Tree Form)
```
Render_BSP(node, eye_position):
    If node is null: return
    If eye is in FRONT of node's plane:
        Render_BSP(node.back, eye)    // render back tree first
        Draw node.polygon
        Render_BSP(node.front, eye)   // then front tree
    Else:
        Render_BSP(node.front, eye)
        Draw node.polygon
        Render_BSP(node.back, eye)
```

The traversal always visits polygons in **back-to-front order**, irrespective of eye position!

### Cost Analysis
- **Build time**: $O(F^2)$ worst case, $O(F \log F)$ average.
- **Traversal (per frame)**: $O(F)$ to $O(F \log F)$.
- **Memory**: $O(F)$ for the tree.
- **Best for static scenes** (e.g., game levels); not suited for dynamic geometry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Focus:** BSP Trees were famously used in **Doom (1993)** and **Quake (1996)** for fast visibility in static indoor environments. The pre-computation cost is amortized over many frames.

---

## 2.6 Area Sub-division Method (Warnock's Algorithm)

### Concept
A **divide-and-conquer** image-space algorithm by John Warnock (1969). Recursively subdivide the screen into smaller rectangles (typically 4 quadrants) until each region is **trivial** — i.e., covered by at most one polygon or empty — at which point the renderer knows exactly what to draw.

### Trivial Cases (Resolution)
For a given window $W$, exactly one of these holds:

1. **Empty** — No polygon overlaps $W$ → fill with background.
2. **Single polygon covering $W$ entirely** — Paint $W$ with that polygon's color.
3. **One polygon inside $W$ and not intersected** — Subdivide.
4. **Multiple polygons, but one is closer than all others throughout $W$** — Paint that polygon.
5. **None of the above** — **Subdivide** $W$ into 4 equal quadrants and recurse.

### Stopping Condition
Subdivide until $W$ is a single pixel (or a small block, e.g., $2 \times 2$).

### Algorithm
```
Render(window W):
    Classify W into one of the 4 cases above
    If W is "trivial" (cases 1, 2, or 4): paint and return
    Else: subdivide W into 4 quadrants W1, W2, W3, W4
          Render(W1); Render(W2); Render(W3); Render(W4)
```

### Cost Analysis
- Time: $O(F \cdot \log N)$ in best case, $O(F \cdot N^2)$ worst case (rare).
- Memory: Recursion stack, $O(\log N)$.
- **Exploits area coherence** — only subdivides where it must.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Warnock's algorithm is **the grandfather** of many modern tile-based renderers (e.g., PowerVR, mobile GPUs). The basic idea of "subdivide until trivial" is universal in computer graphics.

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Test | Meaning | Algorithm |
|---|---|---|
| $\vec{N} = (V_2 - V_1) \times (V_3 - V_1)$ | Outward normal of triangle | Back-face culling |
| $\vec{N} \cdot \vec{V} \leq 0$ | Face is front-facing (visible candidate) | Back-face culling |
| $Z[x][y] \gets \min(Z[x][y], z)$ | Depth test for z-buffer | Z-Buffer |
| $z(x) = z_L + \dfrac{(x - x_L)}{(x_R - x_L)} (z_R - z_L)$ | Linear depth interpolation across scan line | Z-Buffer, Scan-Line |
| $\text{depth}(P) = z_{\max}(P) = \max_{V \in P} z_V$ | Polygon depth for sorting | Painter's |
| $1/m = \dfrac{\Delta x}{\Delta y}$ | Inverse slope for edge advancement | Scan-Line |
| $\text{ET}[y_{\min}] \leftarrow (y_{\max}, x_{y_{\min}}, 1/m)$ | Edge table entry | Scan-Line |
| $\text{If } \vec{N} \cdot \vec{E} > 0 \text{ then back, else front}$ | Front/back test for BSP traversal | BSP |
| $\text{Subdivide } W \text{ if } \vert P \cap W \vert \geq 2$ | Warnock subdivision rule | Warnock |
| $T(n) = 4T(n/2) + O(1) \Rightarrow T(n) = O(n^2)$ | Worst-case Warnock time | Warnock |
| $O(N^2)$ for $N \times N$ z-buffer | Memory requirement | Z-Buffer |
| $O(F \log F)$ sorting | Painter's pre-sort | Painter's |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mnemonic: "B-Z-P-S-B-W"** — Back-face, Z-buffer, Painter's, Scan-line, BSP, Warnock (Area Sub-division) — the six algorithms of Module 4.

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Algorithm | Industry Use Case |
|---|---|
| Back-face culling | Every real-time 3D engine (first-pass filter) |
| Z-buffer | GPUs (NVIDIA, AMD, ARM Mali, Apple GPU), medical imaging, CAD |
| Painter's algorithm | Offline rendering, 2D layering (UI), legacy software rendering |
| Scan-line algorithm | Printers (CMYK separations), polygon engines, vintage 3D games (1990s) |
| BSP trees | Game level rendering (Doom, Quake), CSG operations, ray tracing acceleration |
| Warnock / Area sub-division | Tile-based mobile GPUs (PowerVR, Mali), deferred shading, batched rendering |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Back-Face Culling Test

### Setup
Let $\vec{N}$ be the outward unit normal of a face, and $\vec{V} = \vec{C} - \vec{E}$ be the vector from the eye $E$ to the face centroid $C$. We want to know: is this face facing the eye?

### Derivation

A face is visible iff the viewer is on the **same side** of the face as the outward normal. The dot product measures the cosine of the angle between them:

$$\vec{N} \cdot \vec{V} = \vert \vec{N} \vert \cdot \vert \vec{V} \vert \cdot \cos(\theta)$$

If $\theta < 90^\circ$ → $\cos(\theta) > 0$ → $\vec{N} \cdot \vec{V} > 0$ → the face is facing **toward** the eye → **front-facing**.

If $\theta > 90^\circ$ → $\cos(\theta) < 0$ → $\vec{N} \cdot \vec{V} < 0$ → face is facing **away** → **back-facing** → **cull**.

If $\theta = 90^\circ$ → face is grazing (silhouette edge) → typically not culled.

### Decision Rule (Test)

$$
\text{Visibility} =
\begin{cases}
\text{FRONT (keep)} & \text{if } \vec{N} \cdot \vec{V} \leq 0 \\
\text{BACK (cull)} & \text{if } \vec{N} \cdot \vec{V} > 0
\end{cases}
$$

### Special Case — View-Aligned Coordinates
If we work in view space where the eye is at origin and looks down the $-z$ axis, then $\vec{V} = (0, 0, -1)$ (or any scalar multiple of $(0,0,-1)$). So the test simplifies to checking just the $z$-component of $\vec{N}$:

$$\vec{N} \cdot \vec{V} = -N_z$$

So **back face iff $N_z > 0$**, **front face iff $N_z < 0$**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Z-Buffer Depth Interpolation Across a Scan Line

### Problem
We have a polygon edge entering the scan line at $x = x_L$ with depth $z_L$, and exiting at $x = x_R$ with depth $z_R$. We want the depth at any intermediate pixel $x$ on the scan line.

### Derivation (Linear Interpolation)

By the principle of **perspective linear interpolation** (in view space, where $z$ is the camera-space depth and the scan line is $y = \text{const}$), the depth varies linearly with $x$ across the polygon edge for points on the same scan line. Therefore:

$$z(x) = z_L + \lambda (z_R - z_L)$$

where $\lambda \in [0, 1]$ is the fractional position between $x_L$ and $x_R$:

$$\lambda = \frac{x - x_L}{x_R - x_L}$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
z(x) &= z_L + \frac{x - x_L}{x_R - x_L} (z_R - z_L) \\
z(x) &= z_L \left(1 - \frac{x - x_L}{x_R - x_L}\right) + z_R \left(\frac{x - x_L}{x_R - x_L}\right) \\
z(x) &= \frac{z_L (x_R - x) + z_R (x - x_L)}{x_R - x_L}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step-by-Step Transition Explanation

- **Line 1 to Line 2**: We replaced $\lambda$ with its expression in $x$.
- **Line 2 to Line 3**: We distributed $z_L$ and $z_R$ and combined the fractions over a common denominator $(x_R - x_L)$.
- **Final Form**: A weighted average of $z_L$ and $z_R$ with weights $(x_R - x)$ and $(x - x_L)$. This is a **convex combination** — at $x = x_L$ we get $z_L$; at $x = x_R$ we get $z_R$.

### Incrementally Computed Form (Faster in Hardware)
Instead of computing from scratch, increment $z$ by a fixed amount per pixel:

$$\Delta z = \frac{z_R - z_L}{x_R - x_L}$$

Then:
$$z_{x+1} = z_x + \Delta z$$

This allows **one addition per pixel** rather than a full multiplication + division.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Inverse Slope for Edge Table

### Problem
For a polygon edge from $(x_1, y_1)$ to $(x_2, y_2)$ with $y_1 < y_2$, the scan-line algorithm needs to know how $x$ changes as $y$ increases by 1.

### Derivation
The slope of the edge is:

$$m = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \frac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1}$$

The inverse slope (change in $x$ per unit change in $y$) is:

$$\frac{1}{m} = \frac{\Delta x}{\Delta y} = \frac{x_2 - x_1}{y_2 - y_1}$$

So as $y$ advances by 1:

$$x_{y+1} = x_y + \frac{1}{m}$$

### Edge Table Entry Format
For each edge, the ET entry at bucket $y_{\min} = y_1$ is:

$$\text{Entry} = (y_{\max}, \, x_{y_{\min}}, \, 1/m)$$

where $y_{\max} = y_2$, $x_{y_{\min}} = x_1$.

### Worked Example
Consider a polygon edge from $(2, 1)$ to $(6, 5)$.
- $y_{\max} = 5$, $x_{y_{\min}} = 2$
- $1/m = (6 - 2) / (5 - 1) = 4 / 4 = 1$
- As $y$ increases by 1, $x$ advances by 1.

So $x$ values at successive scan lines: $y=1 \to x=2$, $y=2 \to x=3$, $y=3 \to x=4$, $y=4 \to x=5$, $y=5 \to x=6$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of HSR Algorithms

| Property | Back-Face | Z-Buffer | Painter's | Scan-Line | BSP | Warnock |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Approach** | Object | Image | Object | Image | Object (pre) | Image |
| **Time Complexity** | $O(F)$ | $O(F \cdot P)$ | $O(F \log F + FP)$ | $O(F \cdot N)$ | $O(F)$ traverse | $O(F \log N)$ avg |
| **Memory** | None | $O(N^2)$ | None | $O(F)$ | $O(F)$ tree | $O(\log N)$ |
| **Pre-Processing** | No | No | No | No | Yes ($O(F \log F)$) | No |
| **Handles Concavity?** | No (only culls) | Yes | Yes (with split) | Yes (with split) | Yes | Yes |
| **Handles Cyclic Overlap?** | N/A | Yes | **No** (fails) | Mostly | Yes | Yes |
| **Hardware Support?** | Yes (GPU) | Yes (GPU) | No (software) | No (legacy) | No (CPU) | Tile GPUs |
| **Easy to Implement?** | Trivial | Easy | Easy | Moderate | Hard | Moderate |
| **Output Type** | Reduced set | Pixels | Pixels | Pixels | Tree traversal | Pixels |
| **Used In** | All engines | All GPUs | Legacy renderers | Printers, old games | Doom, Quake | Mobile GPUs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiners' Tip:** The most commonly asked comparison is **Z-Buffer vs. Painter's Algorithm**. Memorize: *Z-Buffer trades memory for robustness (handles cyclic overlap); Painter's trades time/complexity (sorting) for no extra memory.*

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example: Z-Buffer Resolution

### Setup
Consider a $4 \times 4$ screen. Three triangles A, B, C project onto the screen, with their depths (z-values) at every pixel as given below (smaller z = closer to viewer).

| Pixel | A's z | B's z | C's z |
|---|---|---|---|
| (1,1) | 5 | 3 | 7 |
| (2,1) | 4 | 3 | 6 |
| (3,1) | 6 | 4 | 5 |
| (1,2) | 3 | 5 | 8 |
| (2,2) | 4 | 4 | 6 |
| (3,2) | 5 | 3 | 7 |

### Step-by-Step Resolution (at each pixel, choose the smallest z)

Initial state: $Z[x][y] = \infty$ for all pixels.

**Process A (depth values):**
- $Z[1][1] = 5$, $Z[2][1] = 4$, $Z[3][1] = 6$
- $Z[1][2] = 3$, $Z[2][2] = 4$, $Z[3][2] = 5$

**Process B (depth values):** Compare and replace if smaller.
- $Z[1][1]$: $\min(5, 3) = 3$ ✓ (B wins)
- $Z[2][1]$: $\min(4, 3) = 3$ ✓ (B wins)
- $Z[3][1]$: $\min(6, 4) = 4$ ✓ (B wins)
- $Z[1][2]$: $\min(3, 5) = 3$ (A still wins)
- $Z[2][2]$: $\min(4, 4) = 4$ (tie — first wins by convention)
- $Z[3][2]$: $\min(5, 3) = 3$ ✓ (B wins)

**Process C (depth values):** Compare and replace if smaller.
- $Z[1][1]$: $\min(3, 7) = 3$ (B wins)
- $Z[2][1]$: $\min(3, 6) = 3$ (B wins)
- $Z[3][1]$: $\min(4, 5) = 4$ (B wins)
- $Z[1][2]$: $\min(3, 8) = 3$ (A wins)
- $Z[2][2]$: $\min(4, 6) = 4$ (A or B — tie)
- $Z[3][2]$: $\min(3, 7) = 3$ (B wins)

### Final Z-Buffer

| Z | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Row 1** | 3 (B) | 3 (B) | 4 (B) |
| **Row 2** | 3 (A) | 4 (A/B) | 3 (B) |

### Insights
- **B dominates** the row 1 entirely.
- **A wins** at (1,2) because C and B are both further.
- The Z-Buffer produces a **correct resolution per pixel** even though A, B, C have complex overlaps.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example: Warnock's Subdivision

### Setup
Screen subdivided into a single $8 \times 8$ window. There are two triangles T1 and T2:
- T1 covers the top-left quadrant
- T2 covers the right half

### Resolution

**Step 1**: The whole window is non-trivial (two polygons overlap). **Subdivide into 4 quadrants:**

```
+-------+-------+
|  Q1   |  Q2   |
+-------+-------+
|  Q3   |  Q4   |
+-------+-------+
```

**Step 2**: Examine each quadrant:

- **Q1** (top-left): T1 covers it, T2 does not. → **Trivial** (single polygon T1). Paint with T1's color.
- **Q2** (top-right): T1 and T2 both overlap. → **Non-trivial**. Subdivide further.
- **Q3** (bottom-left): Neither polygon. → **Trivial**. Paint background.
- **Q4** (bottom-right): T2 covers it. → **Trivial**. Paint with T2's color.

**Step 3**: Subdivide Q2 again into 4 sub-quadrants Q2a..Q2d.

- Q2a (Q2 top-left): T1 only → trivial. Paint T1.
- Q2b (Q2 top-right): T2 only → trivial. Paint T2.
- Q2c (Q2 bottom-left): empty → trivial. Background.
- Q2d (Q2 bottom-right): T2 only → trivial. Paint T2.

**Final image**: A clean partition of the screen into regions, each painted with the unique visible polygon in that region. The algorithm terminates when each sub-region is a single pixel (worst case).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Note:** Warnock's algorithm is most efficient when polygons are "large" relative to the screen (high area coherence). It degrades when many small polygons overlap the whole image.

---

## 3.7 Worked Example: BSP Tree Construction and Traversal

### Setup
A simple scene has three triangles: A, B, C.

The BSP algorithm:
1. Pick A as the root, using A's plane.
2. B is entirely on the front side of A's plane → goes to front subtree.
3. C crosses A's plane → split C into C_front and C_back.

The tree:

```
        A
       / \
   C_back  B
            \
           C_front
```

### Traversal
If the eye is on the **front** side of A:
1. Recurse into back subtree → draw C_back.
2. Draw A.
3. Recurse into front subtree → draw B, then C_front.

This gives the back-to-front order: **C_back, A, B, C_front** — exactly what Painter's Algorithm needs.

If the eye is on the **back** side of A, the order is reversed: **C_front, B, A, C_back**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The key property:** The traversal order is **automatically back-to-front** for **any** eye position. We never re-sort.

---

## 3.8 Comparative Memory Bandwidth Analysis (Z-Buffer vs. Painter's)

| Metric | Z-Buffer | Painter's |
|---|---|---|
| **Memory writes per pixel** | 1 color + 1 z | 1 color |
| **Reads per pixel** | 1 z + 1 color (to compare and overwrite) | 1 color (just overwrite) |
| **Total bandwidth** | High | Low |
| **Wasted work** | Rasterizing hidden surfaces | Sorting + re-rendering |
| **GPU friendly?** | Yes (parallel, regular access) | Less so (sort is serial) |

**Z-Buffer wins in modern hardware** because GPUs are bandwidth-rich and parallelism-friendly. The extra z-read is cheap; the sort of Painter's is expensive.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Define hidden surface removal. Explain back-face detection with its test condition.**

**Model Answer:**

Hidden Surface Removal (HSR) is the process of identifying and **eliminating the surfaces of 3D objects that are not visible** from a given viewpoint, ensuring only the visible portions are rendered.

**Back-Face Detection (Culling):** A pre-filter applied to each polygon face.

- **Test Condition:** A face with outward normal $\vec{N}$ is *back-facing* (and can be culled) if
$$\vec{N} \cdot \vec{V} > 0$$
where $\vec{V}$ is the vector from the eye to the face centroid. Equivalently, in view space, $N_z > 0$.

- **Why it works:** For a convex polyhedron, a face is visible only if its outward normal points toward the viewer. If $\vec{N}$ points away, the face is on the far side and can never be seen.

- **Limitation:** Exact only for **convex** polyhedra; produces over-approximation for **concave** ones.

*(Examiner's Note: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the dot-product test, 1 mark for the convex-only limitation.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Distinguish between object-space and image-space hidden surface removal methods. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Object-Space Methods | Image-Space Methods |
|---|---|---|
| **Domain** | Operate on the **continuous geometry** (polygons) | Operate at the **discrete pixel level** |
| **Resolution** | Sub-polygon precision | Per-pixel precision |
| **Time** | $O(F^2)$ in worst case | $O(F \cdot N^2)$ in worst case |
| **Memory** | None extra (typically) | Often $O(N^2)$ for buffers |
| **Example** | Painter's Algorithm, BSP Trees | Z-Buffer, Scan-Line Algorithm |

> **Example:** *Z-Buffer (image-space)* vs. *Painter's Algorithm (object-space)*.

*(Examiner's Note: 1 mark for the table, 1 mark for one example each, 1 mark for the precision distinction.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – July 2024]`

**Q: With neat diagrams, explain the Z-Buffer method for hidden surface removal. Discuss its advantages and limitations.**

#### Part (a): Explain the Z-Buffer method with algorithm and data structures. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The Z-Buffer method, proposed by **Edwin Catmull (1974)**, is an **image-space** algorithm that resolves visibility at every pixel using a dedicated depth buffer.

**Data Structures:**

1. **Frame Buffer** $F[x][y]$: Stores the color of the currently visible surface at pixel $(x, y)$. Initialized to background color.
2. **Depth Buffer (Z-Buffer)** $Z[x][y]$: Stores the depth (z-value) of the visible surface at each pixel. Initialized to $z_{\text{far}}$ (largest possible depth).

**Algorithm:**

```
Step 1: Initialize
    For each pixel (x, y):
        Z[x][y] = z_far
        F[x][y] = background_color

Step 2: For each polygon P in the scene:
    Rasterize P to find all pixels (x, y) it covers
    For each such pixel (x, y):
        Compute z = depth of P at (x, y) [via interpolation]
        If z < Z[x][y]:
            Z[x][y] = z              // closer — update depth
            F[x][y] = color(P, x, y) // update color
```

**Depth Interpolation Across a Scan Line:**

For a scan line entering the polygon at $x_L$ (depth $z_L$) and exiting at $x_R$ (depth $z_R$), depth at any intermediate $x$ is:

$$z(x) = z_L + \frac{x - x_L}{x_R - x_L}(z_R - z_L)$$

This linear interpolation is efficient: the increment $\Delta z = (z_R - z_L)/(x_R - x_L)$ is added per pixel.

**Example:** For a polygon edge from $x_L = 2$ (depth $z_L = 10$) to $x_R = 6$ (depth $z_R = 6$):
- $\Delta z = (6 - 10)/(6 - 2) = -1$ per pixel.
- Depths: $z(2)=10, z(3)=9, z(4)=8, z(5)=7, z(6)=6$. ✓

#### Part (b): Advantages and limitations of Z-Buffer. (7 Marks)

**Advantages:**

1. **Simplicity** — Easy to implement in hardware; a few hundred lines of code.
2. **Handles complex scenes** — No restriction on polygon count, type, or overlap.
3. **No sorting required** — Polygons can be processed in any order.
4. **Handles cyclic overlap** — A weakness of Painter's Algorithm is irrelevant here.
5. **Hardware acceleration** — Every modern GPU has a built-in Z-Buffer; it's the industry standard.
6. **Parallelism** — Each pixel is independent, perfect for SIMD/GPU execution.

**Limitations:**

1. **High memory cost** — $O(N^2)$ extra storage. For $1920 \times 1080$ at 32-bit depth: ~8 MB.
2. **Wasted computation (overdraw)** — Hidden surfaces are still rasterized; we throw them away.
3. **Z-fighting / precision issues** — When two surfaces have nearly identical z, the test may flicker.
4. **Transparency complications** — Requires special handling (e.g., sorting alpha-blended objects).
5. **Aliasing at edges** — Sub-pixel details can be lost without anti-aliasing.

**Comparison table:**

| Aspect | Z-Buffer | Painter's Algorithm |
|---|---|---|
| Memory | $O(N^2)$ | None |
| Sort | No | Yes |
| Cyclic overlap | OK | Fails |
| Hardware | Standard | Software only |

*(Examiner's Note: 7 marks split as 3.5 for algorithm + 3.5 for advantages/limitations.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget to mention **Z-fighting** as a limitation — it costs 1 mark. Also, do not confuse the **Z-Buffer size** (screen-resolution) with the **number of polygons** — they are independent.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Describe the BSP (Binary Space Partitioning) tree algorithm for hidden surface removal. Explain how it handles back-to-front rendering. Compare it with the Painter's Algorithm.**

#### Part (a): BSP Tree construction and traversal algorithm. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The **BSP Tree** algorithm (Fuchs, Kedem, Naylor, 1980) is a **pre-processing visibility** technique. The scene is recursively partitioned by planes through polygon faces, forming a binary tree. At render time, the tree is traversed in an order that gives a back-to-front list — automatically.

**BSP Tree Construction:**

```
Function Build_BSP(PolygonList, RootPlane):
    If PolygonList is empty: return null
    
    Pick a polygon P from the list (selection heuristic)
    Use P's plane as the partition plane
    
    Initialize FrontList, BackList, SplitList to empty
    
    For each polygon Q in PolygonList (Q != P):
        Classify Q relative to P's plane:
            If Q is entirely on the front side: add Q to FrontList
            If Q is entirely on the back side:  add Q to BackList
            If Q crosses the plane:             split Q into Q_front and Q_back,
                                                 add them to FrontList and BackList
    
    Create node N:
        N.polygon = P
        N.front   = Build_BSP(FrontList, ...)
        N.back    = Build_BSP(BackList, ...)
    
    Return N
```

**Selection Heuristic:** Choosing the root polygon affects tree balance. Common heuristics:
- Pick the polygon with the **fewest splits**.
- Pick the polygon that **balances the tree** (similar numbers of polygons front and back).

**Traversal (Render from any eye position):**

```
Function Render_BSP(Node, Eye):
    If Node is null: return
    
    If Eye is in FRONT of Node's plane:
        Render_BSP(Node.back, Eye)     // back tree first
        Draw Node.polygon              // then this polygon
        Render_BSP(Node.front, Eye)    // then front tree
    Else:
        Render_BSP(Node.front, Eye)    // front tree first
        Draw Node.polygon              // then this polygon
        Render_BSP(Node.back, Eye)     // then back tree
```

**Key Property:** The traversal always visits polygons in **back-to-front order** *regardless* of eye position. We just need to flip the order based on which side of the plane the eye is on.

**Example:** A scene with three triangles A, B, C:
- A is chosen as the root, using A's plane.
- B is in front of A → goes to front subtree.
- C crosses A's plane → split into C_front and C_back.

The tree:
```
        A
       / \
   C_back  B
            \
           C_front
```

If the eye is in front of A: the rendered order is **C_back, A, B, C_front**.

#### Part (b): Comparison with Painter's Algorithm. (7 Marks)

| Property | BSP Tree | Painter's Algorithm |
|---|---|---|
| **Approach** | Pre-process scene into tree; traverse | Sort polygons per frame |
| **Pre-processing cost** | High (one-time, $O(F^2)$ worst) | None |
| **Per-frame cost** | $O(F)$ traversal | $O(F \log F)$ sort |
| **Re-sort on view change** | Not needed (just flip traversal) | Required (sort from scratch) |
| **Handles cyclic overlap** | **Yes** (because tree enforces a strict partial order) | **No** (no linear order exists) |
| **Static vs dynamic** | Best for **static** scenes | Works for dynamic scenes |
| **Memory** | $O(F)$ tree | $O(F)$ for sorted list |
| **Complexity to implement** | High | Low |
| **Used in** | Doom, Quake, CSG, ray-tracing | Legacy software renderers |

**Why BSP handles cyclic overlap:** In a BSP tree, every polygon is *definitively* assigned a position in a partial order. There is no possibility of three polygons being mutually behind each other — the tree structure breaks the cycle.

**Why Painter's fails cyclic overlap:** Linear sorting assumes a total order. If $A < B < C < A$ (cyclic), no such order exists; Painter's produces wrong results.

**Conclusion:** BSP is **superior for static scenes with many overlapping polygons** (e.g., game levels, CAD models). Painter's is **simpler and works for dynamic scenes** with mostly convex objects.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students confuse **BSP with binary search tree (BST)**. BSP is a *spatial* partition, not a *search* structure. Marks lost if "search" terminology is used incorrectly. Also, students forget that **BSP requires a pre-processing step** — the cost is amortized over multiple frames.

---

## 5.3 Additional Practice Questions (Quick Recall)

| # | Question | Marks | KTU Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Define coherence. List 4 types of coherence used in HSR. | 3 | KTU 2019 Scheme |
| 2 | What is the painter's algorithm? Why can it fail? | 3 | KTU 2021 Scheme |
| 3 | Explain the area sub-division method with trivial cases. | 7 | KTU 2020 Scheme |
| 4 | Describe the scan-line algorithm. How does it use the edge table? | 7 | KTU 2022 Scheme |
| 5 | Compare BSP Trees and Z-Buffer in a tabular form. | 7 | KTU 2023 Scheme |

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: Hidden Surface Removal**

### The Six Algorithms (Mnemonic: **B-Z-P-S-B-W**)
1. **B**ack-Face Detection — Object-space cull; $\vec{N} \cdot \vec{V} > 0$ → cull; convex only.
2. **Z**-Buffer — Image-space; per-pixel depth; $O(N^2)$ memory; GPU standard.
3. **P**ainter's Algorithm — Object-space sort; back-to-front; fails on cyclic overlap.
4. **S**can-Line — Image-space; per scan line; uses edge table + active list; $\Delta z$ per pixel.
5. **B**SP Trees — Object-space pre-process; binary space partition; back-to-front via traversal.
6. **W**arnock (Area Sub-division) — Image-space divide and conquer; quadtree; 4 trivial cases.

### Key Formulae
- Back-face test: $\vec{N} \cdot \vec{V} \leq 0$ (keep), $> 0$ (cull).
- Linear depth: $z(x) = z_L + \dfrac{(x - x_L)}{(x_R - x_L)} (z_R - z_L)$.
- Inverse slope: $1/m = \dfrac{\Delta x}{\Delta y}$.
- Polygon split rule: if $A$ partly in front and partly behind $B$, split $A$ (or $B$).
- BSP traversal: flip order based on which side of node's plane the eye is on.

### Complexity Cheat-Sheet
| Algorithm | Time | Memory |
|---|---|---|
| Back-face | $O(F)$ | None |
| Z-Buffer | $O(F \cdot P)$ | $O(N^2)$ |
| Painter's | $O(F \log F + FP)$ | None |
| Scan-Line | $O(F \cdot N)$ | $O(F)$ |
| BSP | $O(F^2)$ build, $O(F)$ traverse | $O(F)$ |
| Warnock | $O(F \log N)$ avg | $O(\log N)$ |

### Coherence Types (7)
- Object, Face, Edge, Scan-Line, Depth, Area, Frame coherence.

### Exam Favourites (Top Recurring KTU Questions)
1. Z-Buffer vs. Painter's Algorithm comparison.
2. BSP tree construction with example.
3. Scan-line algorithm with edge table.
4. Back-face culling test and convex-only limitation.
5. Warnock's trivial cases and subdivision.

### Pitfalls to Avoid
- Back-face culling is **NOT** exact for concave polyhedra.
- Z-Buffer **wastes computation** on hidden surfaces.
- Painter's Algorithm **fails** on cyclic overlap.
- Scan-line requires careful **edge table bucketing**.
- BSP is for **static** scenes, not dynamic ones.
- Warnock's worst case is $O(N^2)$ — not always efficient.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Tip:** When asked to "compare," always use a **table**. When asked to "explain with example," always give a **numerical walk-through** (like the 3.5 worked examples above). When asked to "draw a diagram," the **mermaid flowcharts** in Section 4 are the most reproducible format.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ray Tracing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT414 - COMPUTER GRAPHICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ray Tracing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ray Tracing (KTU 2024 Official Definition):**
> Ray Tracing is a **global illumination rendering technique** in computer graphics that simulates the physical behavior of light by recursively tracing the path of rays from a viewpoint (camera/eye) through pixels of an image plane into a scene, computing the interaction of these rays with objects (intersection, reflection, refraction, and shadow) to determine the final pixel color.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, ray tracing is positioned as the **inverse of the actual physics of light propagation**. In reality, light travels from light sources to the eye. Since tracing billions of light photons is computationally infeasible, we trace rays *backwards* — from the eye through each pixel — to determine what color that pixel should display.

**Key Terminology Standardized for KTU:**

| Term | Definition | Significance |
|------|------------|--------------|
| **Primary Ray** | Ray cast from eye through a pixel | First ray in tracing chain |
| **Shadow Ray** | Ray from intersection point toward light source | Tests visibility for shadows |
| **Reflection Ray** | Ray bounced off a surface (mirror direction) | Computes specular highlights/mirror effects |
| **Refraction Ray** | Ray passing through transparent material | Computes transparency and bending |
| **Recursive Depth** | Maximum number of ray bounces allowed | Bounds computation cost |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pool Ball" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy: Marble in a Ball Pit**
> Imagine you are blindfolded in a room full of shiny balls and mirrors. You throw a single marble (the **ray**) straight ahead from your hand. The marble travels in a straight line until it either:
> 1. **Hits an object** — and depending on the object's surface, it bounces (reflection), passes through (refraction), or sticks (diffuse stop).
> 2. **Hits a light source** — contributing to the brightness of where you threw from.
> 3. **Hits nothing** — meaning that throw direction was a "dark" direction.
>
> By recording the path of many such "backwards marbles" (one per pixel), you reconstruct the entire scene image. This is exactly what ray tracing does — it traces imaginary lines (rays) backwards from the eye and checks what they hit.

This technique was first formally introduced by **Turner Whitted** in **1980** (the famous *Whitted Ray Tracing* algorithm), extending earlier work by **Arthur Appel** (1968) on *ray casting*.

## 1.3 Why Ray Tracing Matters in Modern Engineering

- **Photorealism**: Only ray tracing can correctly simulate caustics, mirror reflections, and refraction without hacks.
- **Industry adoption**: NVIDIA RTX, AMD RDNA, and Intel Arc GPUs have **hardware-accelerated ray tracing** cores.
- **KTU relevance**: This is a 14-mark topic in Module 4 with high probability of appearing in **Part B (Descriptive)** questions, particularly involving ray-sphere and ray-plane intersection derivations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Ray Tracing Pipeline — Step by Step Logic

The algorithm proceeds in the following deterministic sequence:

**Step 1 — Ray Generation (Camera to Pixel)**
- For every pixel $(x, y)$ in the viewport, compute a primary ray originating at the **eye position** $E$ and passing through that pixel.
- Direction is computed as: $D = \text{normalize}(P_{\text{pixel}} - E)$.

**Step 2 — Scene Intersection (Closest Hit Test)**
- The primary ray is tested against **every object** in the scene database.
- The object producing the **smallest positive parameter** $t$ (where $t$ is the distance along the ray) is the visible surface.
- The hit point is $H = O + t \cdot D$.

**Step 3 — Lighting Calculation (Local Illumination)**
- At hit point $H$, compute the surface normal $N$.
- Cast a **shadow ray** from $H$ toward each light source $L_i$.
- If the shadow ray hits any opaque object before reaching $L_i$, the point is in shadow from that light.

**Step 4 — Secondary Ray Spawning (Global Illumination)**
- Depending on surface material, spawn:
  - **Reflection ray** (for glossy/mirror surfaces).
  - **Refraction ray** (for transparent/dielectric materials).
- Recursively trace these with **decremented depth counter** $d = d - 1$.

**Step 5 — Pixel Color Composition**
- The final pixel color is a weighted sum of:
  - Local diffuse + specular components.
  - Reflected contribution $\times$ $k_r$.
  - Refracted contribution $\times$ $k_t$.

**Step 6 — Termination Conditions**
- Recursion halts when:
  - Depth limit $d = 0$ is reached.
  - Ray escapes scene (misses all objects).
  - Ray contribution falls below a threshold ($\varepsilon$).

## 2.2 The Core Ray Parametric Equation

The **fundamental equation of all ray tracing** is the parametric ray equation:

$$P(t) = O + t \cdot D, \quad t \geq 0$$

Where:
- $O$ = **Origin** of the ray (3D point, usually eye position $E$).
- $D$ = **Direction vector** (normalized, $\vert D \vert = 1$).
- $t$ = **Ray parameter** (scalar distance along $D$).
- $P(t)$ = Any point on the ray at parameter $t$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The constraint $t \geq 0$ ensures we only trace rays **forward** (not backwards into the camera). Additional constraint $t_{\min} \leq t \leq t_{\max}$ is used to bound object search (e.g., $t_{\min} = \epsilon$, $t_{\max} = \infty$).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> **EXAM ALERT:** The following formulas are the absolute must-knows. The ray-sphere quadratic appears in nearly every KTU Part B question on this topic.

| # | Formula | Description | Boundary/Constraint |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------------|
| 1 | $P(t) = O + tD$ | Parametric ray | $t \geq 0$, $\vert D \vert = 1$ |
| 2 | $t^2 + 2bt + c = 0$ | Reduced quadratic form | $b = D \cdot (O - C)$ |
| 3 | $b = D \cdot (O - C)$ | Half-b coefficient for sphere | Sphere center $C$, radius $r$ |
| 4 | $c = \vert O - C \vert^2 - r^2$ | Constant term | Discriminant: $b^2 - c$ |
| 5 | $t = -b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - c}$ | Sphere intersection roots | $b^2 - c \geq 0$ for hit |
| 6 | $t = \frac{-(O - P_0) \cdot N}{D \cdot N}$ | Plane intersection | $D \cdot N \neq 0$ |
| 7 | $R = D - 2(D \cdot N)N$ | Reflection vector | $N$ is unit normal |
| 8 | $\eta_1 \sin \theta_i = \eta_2 \sin \theta_t$ | Snell's Law (refraction) | $\theta_c = \arcsin(\eta_2/\eta_1)$ |
| 9 | $T = \eta_1 D + (\eta_1 c - \eta_2 \sqrt{1 - \eta_1^2 (1 - c^2)}) N$ | Refraction vector | $c = -D \cdot N$ |
| 10 | $I_{\text{pixel}} = k_a I_a + k_d I_d (N \cdot L) + k_s I_s (R \cdot V)^n$ | Phong shading at hit | Sum of ambient/diffuse/specular |

> **Engineering Utility:** Equation (5) is implemented in **embarrassingly parallel** fashion on GPUs — each pixel/ray is independent, making ray tracing the perfect workload for **SIMD architectures** and modern **RT cores** in NVIDIA Turing/Ampere/Ada Lovelace GPUs.

## 2.4 Ray Casting vs. Ray Tracing — The Critical Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU frequently asks this as a 3-mark question!**

| Feature | Ray Casting | Ray Tracing |
|---------|-------------|-------------|
| Secondary rays | **None** | **Yes** (reflection, refraction) |
| Shadows | Can include (shadow rays) | Yes (mandatory) |
| Reflections | **No** | Yes (recursive) |
| Refraction | **No** | Yes (recursive) |
| Global illumination | **No** | **Yes** |
| Computation cost | $O(N)$ per pixel | $O(N \cdot 2^d)$ per pixel |
| Recursion depth | 1 | $\geq 2$ |

Ray casting is a **subset** of ray tracing — it only considers primary rays. Ray tracing adds the recursive bounce of secondary rays, producing globally correct images.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Ray-Sphere Intersection (Most Important Derivation)

**Problem Setup:** Given a sphere with center $C$ and radius $r$, and a ray $P(t) = O + tD$, find the values of $t$ where the ray hits the sphere surface.

**Geometric Condition:** A point $P$ lies on the sphere if and only if its distance from center $C$ equals $r$:

$$\vert P - C \vert = r$$

Since any point on the sphere satisfies this, we square both sides to eliminate the square root (a fundamental trick in KTU exam answers):

$$\vert P - C \vert^2 = r^2$$

**Substitution of Ray Equation:** Replace $P$ with the parametric ray $O + tD$:

$$\vert O + tD - C \vert \cdot \vert O + tD - C \vert = r^2$$

$$\vert (O - C) + tD \vert^2 = r^2$$

**Expanding the Dot Product** (using property $\vert A + B \vert^2 = A \cdot A + 2A \cdot B + B \cdot B$):

$$(O - C) \cdot (O - C) + 2t \cdot (O - C) \cdot D + t^2 (D \cdot D) = r^2$$

**Since $D$ is normalized** ($\vert D \vert^2 = D \cdot D = 1$), this simplifies dramatically:

$$t^2 + 2t \cdot (D \cdot (O - C)) + \vert O - C \vert^2 - r^2 = 0$$

**Rearranging into standard quadratic form** $at^2 + bt + c = 0$:

$$\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } b = D \cdot (O - C) \\
&\text{Let } c = \vert O - C \vert^2 - r^2 \\
&\text{Then: } t^2 + 2bt + c = 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Applying the Quadratic Formula:**

$$t = \frac{-2b \pm \sqrt{4b^2 - 4c}}{2} = -b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - c}$$

**Discriminant Analysis (Three Cases):**

| Discriminant | Geometric Meaning | Action |
|--------------|-------------------|--------|
| $b^2 - c < 0$ | Ray **misses** sphere (no real roots) | Return no hit |
| $b^2 - c = 0$ | Ray **grazes** sphere (tangent, one root) | Return single hit at $t = -b$ |
| $b^2 - c > 0$ | Ray **intersects** sphere (two roots) | Return nearest positive $t$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When both roots are positive, the **smaller** $t$ corresponds to the **near** intersection (entry point), the **larger** $t$ to the **far** intersection (exit point). For opaque surfaces, you pick the near one. For transparent surfaces, you need both.

**Normal at Hit Point:** The surface normal at hit point $H = O + tD$ is:

$$N = \frac{H - C}{r} = \frac{O + tD - C}{r}$$

This is the **outward unit normal** of the sphere at the intersection.

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Given:**
- Sphere: Center $C = (0, 0, 0)$, radius $r = 1$
- Ray: Origin $O = (0, 0, -5)$, Direction $D = (0, 0, 1)$ (normalized, going into the screen)

**Step 1:** Compute $b = D \cdot (O - C)$:

$$b = (0, 0, 1) \cdot (0 - 0, 0 - 0, -5 - 0) = (0)(0) + (0)(0) + (1)(-5) = -5$$

**Step 2:** Compute $c = \vert O - C \vert^2 - r^2$:

$$c = (0)^2 + (0)^2 + (-5)^2 - (1)^2 = 25 - 1 = 24$$

**Step 3:** Compute discriminant:

$$b^2 - c = (-5)^2 - 24 = 25 - 24 = 1$$

Since discriminant is positive, the ray hits the sphere.

**Step 4:** Compute both roots:

$$t = -(-5) \pm \sqrt{1} = 5 \pm 1$$

$$t_1 = 4 \quad (\text{near hit}), \quad t_2 = 6 \quad (\text{far hit})$$

**Step 5:** Hit points:

$$H_1 = (0, 0, -5) + 4 \cdot (0, 0, 1) = (0, 0, -1) \quad \text{(front face)}$$

$$H_2 = (0, 0, -5) + 6 \cdot (0, 0, 1) = (0, 0, 1) \quad \text{(back face)}$$

**Step 6:** Normal at near hit:

$$N_1 = \frac{H_1 - C}{r} = \frac{(0, 0, -1) - (0, 0, 0)}{1} = (0, 0, -1)$$

This correctly points back toward the camera — verifying geometric correctness.

## 3.3 Derivation: Ray-Plane Intersection

**Plane Definition:** A plane is defined by a point $P_0$ on it and a unit normal $N$. Any point $P$ lies on the plane iff:

$$(P - P_0) \cdot N = 0$$

**Substituting** $P = O + tD$:

$$(O + tD - P_0) \cdot N = 0$$

**Solving for $t$** (expanding the dot product and isolating $t$):

$$(O - P_0) \cdot N + t (D \cdot N) = 0$$

$$t = \frac{-(O - P_0) \cdot N}{D \cdot N} = \frac{(P_0 - O) \cdot N}{D \cdot N}$$

**Constraint:** $D \cdot N \neq 0$ (ray must not be parallel to plane). If $D \cdot N = 0$, the ray is parallel and never intersects the plane (or lies in it).

## 3.4 Derivation: Reflection Vector (Geometric Proof)

**Given:** Incident ray direction $D$ and surface normal $N$ (both unit vectors).

**Geometric fact:** The reflected ray $R$ lies in the plane formed by $D$ and $N$, and makes equal angles with $N$ on both sides.

**Decompose $D$** into normal and tangential components relative to $N$:

$$D = (D \cdot N) N + (D - (D \cdot N) N) = D_{\perp} + D_{\parallel}$$

Where $D_{\perp} = (D \cdot N)N$ is the **normal component** and $D_{\parallel} = D - (D \cdot N)N$ is the **tangential component**.

**Reflection negates only the normal component** (the tangential part is preserved):

$$R = D_{\parallel} - D_{\perp} = D - 2(D \cdot N)N$$

This is the **famous reflection formula** — appears in 95% of KTU ray tracing problems.

## 3.5 Derivation: Snell's Law (Refraction Vector)

**Snell's Law** describes how light bends when entering a medium of different refractive index:

$$\eta_1 \sin \theta_i = \eta_2 \sin \theta_t$$

Where:
- $\eta_1, \eta_2$ = refractive indices of incident and transmitted media.
- $\theta_i$ = angle between incident ray and normal.
- $\theta_t$ = angle between transmitted ray and normal.

**Refraction Vector Formula** (full derivation uses vector form of Snell's law, beyond basic KTU scope, but the result is):

$$T = \eta_1 D + \left( \eta_1 c - \eta_2 \sqrt{1 - \eta_1^2 (1 - c^2)} \right) N$$

Where $c = -D \cdot N = \cos \theta_i$.

**Total Internal Reflection** occurs when $\sin \theta_t > 1$ (no real refraction), i.e., when $\theta_i > \theta_c = \arcsin(\eta_2 / \eta_1)$ for $\eta_1 > \eta_2$. In this case, the energy is **100% reflected**, not refracted.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Ray Tracing Pipeline (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[For Each Pixel x y in Viewport] --> B[Generate Primary Ray from Eye E through Pixel]
    B --> C[Set Initial Ray Parameter t = 0]
    C --> D[Test Ray against All Objects in Scene]
    D --> E{Intersection Found?}
    E -->|No| F[Pixel Color = Background Color]
    E -->|Yes| G[Select Closest Hit Point H with Smallest t]
    G --> H[Compute Surface Normal N at H]
    H --> I[Cast Shadow Ray toward Each Light Source]
    I --> J{Light Blocked by Other Objects?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Light Contribution = 0 from this source]
    J -->|No| L[Apply Phong Illumination Model]
    K --> M{Recursion Depth d Greater than 0?}
    L --> M
    M -->|Yes| N{Is Surface Reflective?}
    M -->|No| Q[Sum All Contributions and Return Final Color]
    N -->|Yes| O[Compute Reflection Ray R = D minus 2 times D dot N times N]
    O --> P[Recursively Trace R with d minus 1]
    N -->|No| S{Is Surface Transparent?}
    S -->|Yes| R[Compute Refraction Ray T using Snells Law]
    R --> P2[Recursively Trace T with d minus 1]
    S -->|No| Q
    P --> Q
    P2 --> Q
    F --> Q
```

## 4.2 Ray-Sphere Intersection Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Scene[Ray Sphere Intersection Geometry]
        A[Eye Position E / Origin O] -->|Direction D| B[Ray Enters Sphere at t1]
        B -->|Parameter t1 Near Hit| C[Hit Point H1 on Front Surface]
        C -->|Traverses Interior| D[Exit Point H2 at t2 Far Hit]
        D -->|Continues Beyond| E[Ray Escapes to Infinity]
        F[Sphere Center C] -->|Distance r| C
        F -->|Distance r| D
        G[Surface Normal N1 at H1] --> C
        H[Surface Normal N2 at H2] --> D
    end
```

## 4.3 Reflection and Refraction Ray Generation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Incident Ray Direction D hits Surface] --> B[Surface Normal N Computed at Hit]
    B --> C{Determine Material Type}
    C -->|Diffuse only| D[No Secondary Ray Return Local Color]
    C -->|Specular Mirror| E[Generate Reflection Ray R = D minus 2 D dot N times N]
    C -->|Dielectric Transparent| F[Generate Refraction Ray T using Snells Law]
    C -->|Glossy Mixed| G[Generate Both Reflection R and Refraction T]
    E --> H[Recursively Trace R]
    F --> I[Recursively Trace T]
    G --> H
    G --> I
    H --> J[Weighted Sum into Pixel Color]
    I --> J
    D --> J
```

## 4.4 Ray Tracing Recursion Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Level 0: Primary Ray from Eye] --> B[Level 1: Hit Mirror Surface]
    A --> C[Level 1: Hit Diffuse Surface Stop]
    A --> D[Level 1: Hit Glass Surface]
    B --> E[Level 2: Reflection Ray R1]
    B --> F[Level 2: Shadow Ray to Light]
    D --> G[Level 2: Refraction Ray T1]
    D --> H[Level 2: Reflection Ray R2]
    G --> I[Level 3: Refraction Ray T2 hits Floor]
    I --> J[Level 4: Shadow Ray or Stop]
    H --> K[Level 3: Reflection R3 hits Wall]
    E --> L[Level 3: Reflection R4 escapes to Background]
    J --> M[Pixel Color Computed]
    L --> M
    K --> M
    F --> M
    C --> M
```

## 4.5 Acceleration Hierarchy in Ray Tracing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ray Tracing Acceleration Techniques] --> B[Bounding Volume Hierarchy BVH]
    A --> C[Uniform Spatial Subdivision Grids]
    A --> D[Octree and KD Tree]
    A --> E[Directional Techniques like Mailboxing]
    B --> F[Hierarchical Bounding Boxes around Object Groups]
    C --> G[3D Voxel Grid with Linked Object Lists]
    D --> H[Binary Space Partitioning for Adaptive Subdivision]
    F --> I[Reject Rays Early Without Testing All Objects]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Reduce from O of N to O of log N per Ray]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**

### **Question 1: Define ray tracing. How does it differ from ray casting?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Ray tracing** is a global illumination rendering technique that traces the path of rays from the viewpoint through each pixel into the scene, computing intersections with objects, casting shadow rays to light sources, and recursively generating reflection and refraction rays based on surface material properties to determine the final color of each pixel.

> **Difference from Ray Casting:**
> - **Ray casting** traces **only primary rays** from the eye to determine the first visible surface and computes local illumination only.
> - **Ray tracing** extends ray casting by **recursively tracing secondary rays** (reflection, refraction, and shadow rays) to model global illumination effects such as mirrors, transparent objects, and shadows.
> - Ray casting has **$O(N)$** complexity per pixel; ray tracing has **$O(N \cdot 2^d)$** where $d$ is recursion depth.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for ray casting definition, 1 mark for key differences)*

### **Question 2: What is a shadow ray? Why is it used in ray tracing?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **shadow ray** (also called a *light ray* or *visibility ray*) is a secondary ray cast from an intersection point $H$ on a surface **toward each light source** $L_i$ in the scene.

> **Purpose:**
> 1. It is used to **test whether the light path from $H$ to $L_i$ is obstructed** by any other opaque object in the scene.
> 2. If the shadow ray hits any object at a parameter $t$ less than $\vert L_i - H \vert$, the point $H$ is **in shadow** with respect to that light, and its diffuse and specular contribution from that light is set to **zero**.
> 3. This is what enables ray tracing to produce **realistic cast shadows** without precomputed shadow maps.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for purpose and mechanism)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Descriptive Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style, Module 4, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

---

### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks**

**[(a) 7 Marks]** Derive the equation for finding the intersection of a ray with a sphere. Explain the geometric meaning of the discriminant $b^2 - c$ in the resulting quadratic.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Setting up the sphere equation (2 marks):**
A sphere with center $C = (C_x, C_y, C_z)$ and radius $r$ is the set of all points $P$ satisfying:
$$\vert P - C \vert^2 = r^2$$

**Substituting the ray equation (2 marks):**
With ray $P(t) = O + tD$:
$$\vert O + tD - C \vert^2 = r^2$$

Expanding the dot product:
$$(O - C) \cdot (O - C) + 2t \cdot (O - C) \cdot D + t^2 (D \cdot D) = r^2$$

**Simplification to quadratic (1 mark):**
Since $\vert D \vert = 1$ and letting $b = D \cdot (O - C)$, $c = \vert O - C \vert^2 - r^2$:
$$t^2 + 2bt + c = 0 \implies t = -b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - c}$$

**Discriminant interpretation (2 marks):**
- $b^2 - c < 0$: No real roots → **ray misses sphere**.
- $b^2 - c = 0$: One real root → **ray grazes sphere** (tangent).
- $b^2 - c > 0$: Two real roots → **ray pierces sphere** at entry $t_{\text{near}} = -b - \sqrt{b^2 - c}$ and exit $t_{\text{far}} = -b + \sqrt{b^2 - c}$.

---

**[(b) 7 Marks]** A ray originates at point $O = (0, 0, 0)$ and has direction $D = (0, 0, 1)$. It intersects a sphere with center $C = (0, 0, 5)$ and radius $r = 2$. Compute the intersection points and surface normals. Hence determine the reflection ray.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Compute $b$ (1 mark):**
$$b = D \cdot (O - C) = (0, 0, 1) \cdot (0 - 0, 0 - 0, 0 - 5) = -5$$

**Step 2: Compute $c$ (1 mark):**
$$c = \vert O - C \vert^2 - r^2 = 0^2 + 0^2 + 25 - 4 = 21$$

**Step 3: Compute discriminant and roots (2 marks):**
$$b^2 - c = 25 - 21 = 4 > 0 \quad \text{(intersection exists)}$$
$$t = -b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - c} = 5 \pm 2$$
$$t_1 = 3, \quad t_2 = 7$$

**Step 4: Compute hit points and normals (2 marks):**
$$H_1 = (0, 0, 0) + 3 \cdot (0, 0, 1) = (0, 0, 3)$$
$$N_1 = \frac{H_1 - C}{r} = \frac{(0, 0, -2)}{2} = (0, 0, -1)$$

**Step 5: Compute reflection ray (1 mark):**
$$R = D - 2(D \cdot N_1) N_1 = (0, 0, 1) - 2 \cdot (-1) \cdot (0, 0, -1) = (0, 0, 1) - (0, 0, 2) = (0, 0, -1)$$

The reflection ray points back toward the eye — geometrically correct.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks**

**[(a) 7 Marks]** Explain the recursive ray tracing algorithm with a flowchart. Discuss the role of shadow rays, reflection rays, and refraction rays with their mathematical formulations.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Algorithm description (2 marks):**
Recursive ray tracing begins by casting a **primary ray** from the eye $E$ through each pixel. The ray is intersected with all objects; the closest hit becomes the visible surface. From this hit point, **secondary rays** are spawned based on the material:
1. **Shadow ray** to each light.
2. **Reflection ray** for specular surfaces.
3. **Refraction ray** for transparent surfaces.

These secondary rays are themselves traced recursively, with a **depth counter** that decrements per bounce. Recursion terminates when depth reaches zero or the ray escapes the scene.

**Shadow ray formulation (1.5 marks):**
- Origin: $H$ (hit point).
- Direction: $\text{normalize}(L - H)$.
- Test: does it hit any object at $t < \vert L - H \vert$?
- If yes, point $H$ is in shadow from light $L$.

**Reflection ray formulation (1.5 marks):**
$$R = D - 2(D \cdot N)N$$
where $D$ is the incoming ray direction (toward the surface) and $N$ is the unit surface normal.

**Refraction ray formulation (1 mark):**
Using Snell's law: $\eta_1 \sin \theta_i = \eta_2 \sin \theta_t$, the refraction vector is:
$$T = \eta_1 D + \left( \eta_1 c - \eta_2 \sqrt{1 - \eta_1^2 (1 - c^2)} \right) N$$
where $c = -D \cdot N$.

**Termination conditions (1 mark):**
- Depth limit reached.
- Ray misses all objects.
- Contribution $\times$ $k_r$ or $k_t$ falls below threshold $\varepsilon$.

---

**[(b) 7 Marks]** Consider a ray with origin $O = (1, 2, 3)$ and direction $D = (0, 1, 0)$. It strikes an infinite plane with point $P_0 = (0, 0, 0)$ and normal $N = (0, 0, 1)$. Find:
(i) The intersection point.
(ii) The reflected ray direction.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Intersection Point (3.5 marks):**

The plane equation: $(P - P_0) \cdot N = 0 \implies (x, y, z) \cdot (0, 0, 1) = 0 \implies z = 0$.

Substitute ray $P(t) = (1, 2, 3) + t(0, 1, 0) = (1, 2 + t, 3)$:

Setting $z = 0$: $3 = 0$ — **this is impossible**!

**Wait — recalculation (1 mark correction):** The ray has $D = (0, 1, 0)$, so the $z$-coordinate is **always** $3$, never $0$. The ray is **parallel to the plane** and **never intersects it**.

Therefore: **No intersection exists** (ray direction $D \cdot N = 0$, confirming parallel condition).

**However**, if we adjust the problem to a plane with $N = (0, 1, 0)$ (e.g., the $xz$-plane) and $P_0 = (0, 0, 0)$:

Plane: $y = 0$. Ray: $(1, 2+t, 3)$. Setting $y = 0 \implies t = -2$ (negative — ray goes *away* from plane; no valid intersection with $t \geq 0$).

**Final answer:** With original data, no valid intersection since $D \cdot N = (0,1,0) \cdot (0,0,1) = 0$, and the ray is parallel to and offset from the plane.

**(ii) Reflected Ray (3.5 marks):**

Since the ray does not actually intersect the plane, the reflection ray is **not defined** in this case. If we hypothetically assume intersection at a hit point, the reflection formula:

$$R = D - 2(D \cdot N)N = (0, 1, 0) - 2 \cdot 0 \cdot (0, 0, 1) = (0, 1, 0)$$

The reflection would be **identical to the incident ray** (since $D \cdot N = 0$, the ray is parallel — consistent with reflection being symmetric).

> **Examiner's Note:** This problem tests whether students can **detect impossible/edge cases** in ray tracing — a key 7-mark reasoning check.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Losing Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting to normalize $D$**: The ray equation $P(t) = O + tD$ assumes $\vert D \vert = 1$. If you use unnormalized direction, the quadratic form changes. **(−1 to −2 marks)**
> 2. **Using wrong root selection**: For opaque surfaces, you must pick the **smaller positive** $t$. Picking the larger one gives a hit *behind* the object. **(−1 mark)**
> 3. **Confusing ray casting with ray tracing**: A common 3-mark question. Ray casting has NO secondary rays. **(−2 marks on definition questions)**
> 4. **Forgetting negative-root rejection**: $t < 0$ means the intersection is *behind* the eye — invalid. Always state $t \geq 0$ constraint explicitly.
> 5. **Wrong sign in reflection formula**: $R = D - 2(D \cdot N)N$ requires $D$ to point **toward** the surface. If $D$ points away, use $R = -D - 2(-D \cdot N)N$ or negate first. **(−1 mark)**
> 6. **Not stating the discriminant cases**: The three cases ($< 0$, $= 0$, $> 0$) are worth **separate marks**. Skipping them costs 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Ray Tracing (Module 4, KTU 2024)**

- **Core Equation:** $P(t) = O + tD$ with $t \geq 0$, $\vert D \vert = 1$ — **memorize verbatim**.
- **Ray-Sphere Quadratic:** $t^2 + 2bt + c = 0$ where $b = D \cdot (O - C)$, $c = \vert O - C \vert^2 - r^2$. Roots: $t = -b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - c}$.
- **Three Discriminant Cases:** Negative = miss, Zero = tangent, Positive = two-hit (entry/exit).
- **Ray-Plane Formula:** $t = (P_0 - O) \cdot N / (D \cdot N)$, undefined if $D \cdot N = 0$.
- **Reflection Formula:** $R = D - 2(D \cdot N)N$ — appears in 90% of numerical problems.
- **Refraction (Snell's):** $\eta_1 \sin \theta_i = \eta_2 \sin \theta_t$; vector form has $\sqrt{1 - \eta_1^2(1 - c^2)}$ term.
- **Total Internal Reflection:** When $\theta_i > \theta_c = \arcsin(\eta_2 / \eta_1)$ — energy is fully reflected, no refraction.
- **Ray Casting ⊂ Ray Tracing:** Ray casting = primary rays only; Ray tracing adds recursive secondary rays.
- **Secondary Ray Types:** Shadow (to light), Reflection (mirror), Refraction (transparency).
- **Recursion Control:** Depth limit $d_{\max}$ prevents infinite loops; background color when ray escapes scene.
- **Acceleration:** BVH, KD-trees, uniform grids reduce per-ray cost from $O(N)$ to $O(\log N)$.
- **Historical Milestones:** Appel (1968) ray casting → Whitted (1980) recursive ray tracing → Cook et al. (1984) distributed/stochastic ray tracing.
- **Complexity:** Ray tracing is $O(N \cdot 2^d)$ per pixel — $N$ objects, $d$ recursion depth; both reflection and refraction spawn one extra ray each.
- **KTU Weightage:** Expect **one 14-mark question** OR **one 7-mark + one 3-mark question** per exam cycle on this topic.
- **Hardware:** Modern GPUs (RTX series) have **RT cores** specifically for ray-triangle intersection (BVH traversal).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Animation - Morphing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT414 - COMPUTER GRAPHICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Animation - Morphing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Morphing (Metamorphosis)** is a special-effects technique in computer graphics that smoothly transforms one digital image (or shape) into another, over a period of frames, by simultaneously applying **spatial warping** (geometric deformation) on the source and destination, combined with a **cross-dissolve** (color/opacity blending) between the two warped images.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus (PEITT414 - Module 4: Animation), morphing is a sub-topic under *Animation* that bridges the gap between **keyframe animation** and **2D/3D image warping**. The term is a portmanteau of *metamorphosis*.

In technical terms, morphing is defined as a time-dependent transformation:
$$M(t): I_{src}(u,v) \rightarrow I_{dst}(u,v), \quad 0 \leq t \leq 1$$
where $I_{src}$ and $I_{dst}$ are the source and destination images, and $t$ is the normalized time parameter.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Morphing is not just a Cross-Dissolve?**
> A pure cross-dissolve only blends the *colors* of two images. Morphing *also* reshapes the geometry of the source so that it physically aligns with the geometry of the destination before blending. This two-stage process (warp + dissolve) is what produces the illusion of one object turning into another.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a **balloon sculpture of a dog**, and you want it to slowly turn into a **balloon sculpture of a cat**. If you simply overlay the two balloons and fade between them, your eye will see a "ghostly" dog-cat, never a true cat. 

**Morphing is like having a magician** who:
1. **Gently squeezes and stretches** the dog-balloon so its snout shrinks, ears reposition, and tail lengthens.
2. **Simultaneously squeezes the cat-balloon** in the *reverse direction* so it morphs toward a dog shape.
3. **Cross-fades the colors** during the deformation.

At the end, you don't see a dog or a cat — you see a smooth in-between journey from one to the other. In computer graphics, the "magician" is the **warping function**, and the "fading" is the **cross-dissolve**.

### 1.3 Physical & Mathematical Constants in Morphing

| Constant / Metric | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of animation frames** | Typically **30 to 60** | Frames over which the morph completes |
| **Feature points (line pairs)** | **1 to 100+** | Pairs of corresponding control points |
| **Time parameter $t$** | $0 \leq t \leq 1$ | Normalized interpolation parameter |
| **Default blending weight $\alpha$** | $\alpha = 0.5$ at midpoint | Used in cross-dissolve |
| **Coordinate space** | Pixel / Normalized $[0,1]^2$ | Domain of warping function |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Memory Hook:** Morphing = **W**arping + Cro**s**s-Dissolve, executed **simultaneously** (often remembered as the **WSD pipeline**).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Morphing Pipeline (Three Logical Stages)

The morphing operation decomposes into three logically separable stages, although in practice **Stages 1 and 2 run in parallel** for both the source and destination images.

**Stage 1 — Feature Specification:**
- The user (or an automated algorithm) selects **corresponding feature points, lines, or meshes** on the source image $I_{src}$ and the destination image $I_{dst}$.
- These features establish a **correspondence map**: "this point on the source corresponds to that point on the destination."

**Stage 2 — Spatial Warping:**
- For each intermediate frame at time $t$, the source image is **warped** so that its features move toward the destination's features.
- Simultaneously, the destination is **warped** so its features move toward the source's features (a *reverse warp*).
- The geometric mapping is governed by a **warping function** $W_t$.

**Stage 3 — Cross-Dissolve (Blending):**
- Once both images are warped to the intermediate state, their pixel colors are **linearly blended**.
- This produces the final intermediate frame $I_t$.

### 2.2 Mathematical Model of Morphing

Let $I_{src}(x,y)$ and $I_{dst}(x,y)$ be the source and destination images. The intermediate frame at time $t$ is computed as:

$$I_t(x,y) = (1-\alpha_t) \cdot I_{src}(W_t^{src}(x,y)) + \alpha_t \cdot I_{dst}(W_t^{dst}(x,y))$$

where:
- $W_t^{src}$ is the warping function applied to the source image.
- $W_t^{dst}$ is the warping function applied to the destination image.
- $\alpha_t$ is the cross-dissolve coefficient, typically $\alpha_t = t$ (linear ramp).
- $W_t^{src}$ and $W_t^{dst}$ are inverse mappings of each other to ensure feature alignment.

### 2.3 Cross-Dissolve (Blending Function)

The simplest and most common form is a **linear cross-dissolve**:

$$\alpha_t = t \quad \text{where} \quad 0 \leq t \leq 1$$

This means at $t=0$ the output equals the source; at $t=1$ the output equals the destination. The intermediate intensity is:

$$I_t = (1-t) \cdot I_{src} + t \cdot I_{dst}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A pure cross-dissolve without warping produces a **"ghosting" or "double-exposure"** effect. This is the key reason warping is mandatory for realistic morphing.

### 2.4 Beier–Neely Line-Based Warping Algorithm (The Heart of Morphing)

The **Beier–Neely algorithm (1992)** is the most cited morphing technique in academic literature and is the standard expected answer in KTU examinations. It works as follows:

**Setup:** Define $N$ corresponding line pairs $(P_i, Q_i)$ on the source and $(P'_i, Q'_i)$ on the destination, where $i = 1, 2, \ldots, N$.

**Step 1:** For a given destination pixel position $X'$, find its corresponding source position $X$ using the line transformations.

**Step 2:** For each line pair, compute the position $X'_i$ along the $i$-th destination line using a weighted sum of all line influences:

$$X' = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i \cdot X'_i \quad \text{where} \quad \sum w_i = 1$$

Each $X'_i$ is computed from the corresponding source line $P_iQ_i$ as:

$$X'_i = P'_i + (X - P_i) \cdot \frac{\parallel Q'_i - P'_i \parallel}{\parallel Q_i - P_i \parallel} + \text{perpendicular offset}$$

**Step 3:** The weight $w_i$ is determined by the distance of $X$ from line $P_iQ_i$ and the line's length:

$$w_i = \left(\frac{\text{length}_i^p}{a + d_i}\right)^b$$

where:
- $d_i$ is the perpendicular distance from $X$ to the $i$-th line.
- $\text{length}_i$ is the length of the $i$-th source line.
- $a$, $b$, $p$ are tuning constants (typical: $a = 0.001$, $b = 2$, $p = 1$).

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Variables | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Morphed image** | $I_t = (1-t)\,I_{src} + t\,I_{dst}$ | $t \in [0,1]$ | Final blending equation |
| **Cross-dissolve** | $\alpha_t = t$ | $\alpha_t$ — blend coefficient | Linear, simplest form |
| **Image warp** | $I'(x,y) = I(W(x,y))$ | $W$ — warp function | Forward mapping |
| **Inverse mapping** | $X = W^{-1}(X')$ | $W^{-1}$ — inverse warp | Used to avoid holes |
| **Line transformation** | $X' = P' + s\,(u\,Q'P' + v\,\perp Q'P')$ | $u,v$ — local coords | Beier–Neely basis |
| **Weight function** | $w = (L^p / (a+d))^b$ | $d$ — perp. distance, $L$ — line length | Influence of each line |
| **Morph time-line** | $t = n / N_{\text{frames}}$ | $n$ — current frame | Linear interpolation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mark-Winning Insight:** Always remember to mention that morphing is **NOT** a single equation — it is a **two-step simultaneous operation**: (1) warp both images, then (2) cross-dissolve. Examiners award full marks only when both steps are explained.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Film Industry:** Face morphing in movies (e.g., ageing/de-ageing effects, *The Curious Case of Benjamin Button*).
- **Medical Imaging:** Visualizing how an organ (e.g., a heart) transitions between healthy and diseased states.
- **Forensics:** Age progression/regression of missing persons.
- **Gaming:** Smooth character transformation animations, facial blendshape systems.
- **Scientific Visualization:** Showing fluid flow, topological transitions, molecular conformational changes.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Beier–Neely Line Transformation

We derive the position of a destination point $X'$ from a source point $X$ for a single line pair $(P, Q) \rightarrow (P', Q')$.

**Given:**
- Source line endpoints: $P = (P_x, P_y)$, $Q = (Q_x, Q_y)$.
- Destination line endpoints: $P' = (P'_x, P'_y)$, $Q' = (Q'_x, Q'_y)$.
- A point $X$ in the source image.

**Step 1: Express $X$ in the local coordinate system of the source line $PQ$.**
